40
ASHRAE Journal ashrae.org
January 2003
By Yunho Hwang, Member ASHRAE, Amr Gado, Student Member ASHRAE, and Reinhard Radermacher, Member ASHRAE
Comparing R-290
With R-22 in Heat Pumps
T
he contribution of hydrofluorocarbons to global warming has led to the reinvestigation of hydrocarbons (HCs).
Despite the flammability of HCs, some manufacturers of refrigerators and residential air conditioners (especially in
the European Union and Japan) have begun to use HCs as refrigerants in small capacity equipment.1,2 The increased
use of HCs in refrigerators brings up the applicability of HCs in residential air-conditioning units and heat pumps. Among
the HCs, R-290 has a similar vapor pressure to that of R-22. To contribute to a clearer understanding of the relative
performance potential of R-290 as compared to R-22, the per-
formance of both refrigerants was measured according to
ASHRAE standards3,4 in a residential, state-of-the-art, split-sys-
tem air-conditioner heat pump using psychrometric chambers
at the Center for Environmental Energy Engineering at the
University of Maryland.
The test unit, with a nominal capacity of 9 kW (2.5 tons),
uses a scroll-type compressor and a thermostatic expansion
valve that was originally designed for R-22. In the R-290 com-
parison tests, a compressor of equal nominal efficiency to the
R-22 compressor (with a displacement volume increased by
11%) was used to approach R-22’s capacity. In addition to the
compressor change, a metering valve was used to match the
degree of a superheating at the evaporator outlet to simulate
the thermostatic expansion valve appropriately designed for
R-290. The purity of R-290 was 99.5%.
Cooling Test Results
R-290 showed 3% to 6% lower cooling capacity and 5%
lower coefficient of performance (COP) than R-22. R-290
showed a better cyclic performance than R-22 by having 5%
lower cycling efficiency loss factors as compared to R-22.
The compressor inlet temperature of R-290 was as much as
13 K lower than that of R-22, which contributed a colder
evaporator temperature. Additionally, the lower suction tem-
perature and higher specific heat of R-290 resulted in a lower
discharge temperature than that of R-22. Based on the steady-
state and cyclic cooling test results, the SEERs were calcu-
lated for both refrigerants. As was similar to the steady state
cooling performance results, the SEER of R-290 was lower
than that of R-22.
Heating Test Results
The heating performance of R-22 and R-290 was evaluated
at various ambient temperatures. Test results showed that R-
290 had equal lower heating capacity and COP than R-22
when the ambient temperature was –8°C (17°F) or higher. The
capacity improvement of R-290 at lower ambient temperature
are consistent with the relative increase in vapor density and
increasingly favorable transport properties of R-290 as com-
pared to R-22 as ambient temperature decreases.
References
1. Greenpeace. 1997. www. greenpeace.org/~ozone/.
2. Japan Air Conditioning, Heating & Refrigeration News.
2002. www.jarn.co.jp.
3. ANSI/ASHRAE Standard 37-1988, Methods of Testing
for Rating Unitary Air-Conditioning and Heat Pump Equip-
ment.
4. ANSI/ASHRAE Standard 116-1995, Methods of Testing
for Rating Seasonal Efficiency of Unitary Air Conditioners
and Heat Pumps.
Yunho Hwang is an assistant research scientist, Amr Gado
is a graduate research assistant, and Reinhard Radermacher
is the director of the Center for Environmental Energy Engi-
neering, Department of Mechanical Engineering, University
of Maryland, College Park, Md.

ASHRAE Journal
May 2003
n schools, the combination of small classroom size, large ventilation
air requirements, and a humid climate creates the potential for high
indoor humidity and indoor air quality problems. To prevent these prob-
lems, while keeping energy use as low as possible, EPRI and Florida
Power and Light teamed with Brevard County School District to dem-
onstrate the effectiveness of a dual-path, low temperature air-distribu-
tion system used in conjunction with thermal energy storage (TES).
with a dedicated coil, and delivers the
conditioned air to the space. The venti-
lation air quantity is controlled indepen-
dently of the recirculation or total airflow.
Dampers in the ventilation and return
airstreams adjust ventilation airflow to
the desired level.
The cooling and dehumidification
coil for outdoor air is designed to meet
virtually the entire building latent load
(moisture from ventilation, infiltration,
occupants and any internal generation),
while the coil for the recirculation air-
stream primarily is devoted to sensible
cooling. By continuously dehumidify-
ing all incoming air, excellent humidity
control is achieved at all part-load con-
ditions. Khattar and Brandemuehl de-
scribe the dual-path HVAC system in
further detail.1
Thermal Storage Integration
Three challenges exist in the design
and control of the cooling and dehumidi-
fication coil and the air-conditioning sys-
tem for the ventilation air path. First, the
coil must continuously cool the air to a
low dew-point temperature to wring out
the moisture. Since ventilation air must
be cooled to a low temperature (~43°F
[6°C] instead of typical 55°F [13°C]) for
moisture removal, the project team capi-
talized on this opportunity to supply low
temperature air to the conditioned spaces,
which significantly reduced fan power and
energy as well as the installed first cost of
the air-distribution system.
Second, the cooling and dehumidifica-
tion load on this coil varies significantly
with ambient conditions, and good coil
capacity modulation is required to ensure
the low supply air temperature at all con-
ditions. Such capacity modulation intro-
duces complexity in direct expansion
The district’s state-of-the-art system
was a chilled water system with two sepa-
rate air-handling units—one dedicated
to conditioning outdoor ventilation air
to 55°F (13°C) for distribution directly
to the classrooms, and the second either
a constant- or a variable-air-volume unit
dedicated to conditioning the return air.
One school was retrofitted with a TES
system and provided valuable data for
comparison with the dual path system
with TES.
The demonstration project at Ralph
Williams Jr. Elementary School in east
central Florida improved upon the
district’s state-of-the-art system by using
a dual path approach to eliminate the de-
livery of any unconditioned ventilation
air to the space (Figure 1). It continu-
ously dehumidifies the ventilation air
About the Authors
Mukesh Khattar, Ph.D., is energy director with
Oracle Corporation in Redwood Shores, Calif.
This project was started when he was with EPRI.
Don Shirey III is program director and
Richard Raustad is senior research engineer with
the Florida Solar Energy Center, Cocoa, Fla.
By Mukesh Khattar, Ph.D., P.E., Member ASHRAE, Don Shirey III, Member ASHRAE, and Richard Raustad
I
The following article was published in ASHRAE Journal, May 2003. © Copyright 2003 American Society of Heating,
Refrigerating and Air-Conditioning Engineers, Inc. It is presented for educational purposes only. This article may not
be copied and/or distributed electronically or in paper form without permission of ASHRAE.
May 2003
ASHRAE Journal
59
refrigerant system’s design and operation, and such systems are
not available as unitary packages. Therefore, the project team
used a chiller with its inherent chilled water loop thermal mass
(to avoid excessive cycling at part load) to provide low supply
fluid temperature (36°F [2°C]) to the cooling coils.
Third, the project team used the synergy of ice storage, which
provides steady 36°F (2°C) chilled fluid at all loads without
requiring any additional energy. TES further leveraged the util-
ity rate structure due to the a relatively small size of storage
required because of limited utility on-peak hours of operation.
Demonstration System Description
The classrooms and other common areas in the 86,000 ft2
(8000 m2) main building are conditioned with six dual-path
air-handling units. As described previously, the outdoor venti-
lation air is conditioned to a dry-bulb temperature of 43°F
(6°C) regardless of the ambient air conditions entering the
coil. The recirculation air coil is also sized to cool the air to
43°F (6°C) at design load. The VAV
air-handling units deliver cold air
(45°F [7°]) to the terminal units. High
induction cold air diffusers properly
distribute the air to the conditioned
space over a range of supply airflow
rates. Chilled brine (33% propylene
glycol, 67% water) is supplied to the
air handlers at 36°F [2°C] using a two-
speed primary and variable-speed
secondary pumping arrangement.
During periods of peak electric use,
the chillers are shut off and 16 ice storage tanks (internal melt,
ice-on-coil) provide cooling for the building. The air-cooled
chillers recharge the ice tanks at night and on weekends when
off-peak electricity rates are low.
The winter heating loads are very small in central Florida and
resistance heaters are located in the air handlers to temper the
supply air to about 70°F (21°C). Additional heating elements
are also located in the VAV terminal boxes to meet any remain-
ing heating load as well as tempering air at very low cooling
load conditions. Supply air temperature (SAT) reset is used at
low load conditions to minimize overcooling and reheat. To
maintain indoor humidity control, the return air-cooling coil
SAT is fully reset before outdoor air coil SAT reset begins.
System Performance
System performance at the demonstration project school and
two other schools, with identical building layouts but with dif-
ferent HVAC systems, were selected for comparison. The HVAC
air-distribution systems for the other schools consisted of dedi-
cated air-handling units to condition all ventilation air to 55°F
(13°C) and delivering it directly to the conditioned spaces. Ad-
ditional separate variable and constant volume air-handling units
with design supply temperatures of 55°F (13°C) were used to
condition the recirculation air. The comparison schools used
air-cooled chillers to provide 45°F (7°C) water to cooling coils
via constant speed pumps. One of the two schools (West Side)
was retrofitted with TES after one year of operation under a
performance contract. Data were obtained on occupancy, oper-
ating hours, electricity use and billing history for each school.
While detailed performance data were collected at Ralph Will-
iams, only electric utility billing data and spot measurements of
space conditions were available for the other schools.
Indoor Humidity, Air Quality and Thermal Comfort
The school district typically uses a 74°F (23°C) cooling
setpoint temperature in its schools. As explained previously, the
cooling coils at the demonstration school were designed to pro-
vide discharge air at a dew-point temperature of approximately
42°F (6°C) at design conditions. The project team expected that
the dew-point temperature of the air in the conditioned spaces
would be within 5°F (3°C) of the dew-point temperature of the
air leaving the air-handling units (e.g.,
47°F [8°C]). With an indoor air dry-bulb
temperature of 74°F (23°C) and a dew-
point temperature of 47°F (8°C), the
project team expected indoor humid-
ity levels to be around 40% RH.
Figure 2 shows measured air tempera-
ture and relative humidity levels for a
typical August day. The air temperature
and humidity sensors located in the air
handler return airstreams (RA) are good
indicators of average conditions in the
occupied spaces when the air handlers were operating from Hour
7 to Hour 18. The return air gains some heat from lights, but the
return air plenum is located within the thermal envelope of the
building, so further heat gain is only modest. The dry-bulb tem-
perature ranged from 74°F to 78°F (23°C to 26°C) during hours
when the air handlers operated. The RH level varied from 48%
when the system started at 7 a.m. to around 38% RH when the
outdoor ventilation air was reduced from 3 to 6 p.m.
In comparison, other area schools with conventional air-
conditioning systems (i.e., 55°F [13°C] supply air tempera-
ture) maintain indoor air between 55% RH to just over 60%
RH. Clearly the cold air distribution system is able to maintain
much lower indoor humidity levels and very comfortable con-
ditions within the school.
The project team also monitored carbon dioxide (CO2) con-
centrations in the return airstream of one air handler and in a
typical classroom served by that air handler to provide an indica-
tion of how well the ventilation air was being distributed. Steady-
state CO2 concentrations in the classroom were typically 500 to
600 ppm above the outdoor air CO2 level, just as expected from
theoretical calculations after considering actual classroom oc-
cupancy and metabolic rate of the occupants, indicating that the
outdoor ventilation air is being distributed to the occupied zones.

Operation and Maintenance
After a brief commissioning period to adjust hardware and
control settings, the new system has lived up to expectations.
The operation of this system is similar to other conventional
chilled water and thermal energy storage systems that are used
at other schools within this district. Initial adjustments were
required to properly schedule chiller operation for building cool-
ing during the daytime and ice building at night. The supply air
temperature reset strategy was refined to minimize overcooling
in the spaces and terminal reheating. System maintenance does
not seem to be significantly different from other conventional
chilled water VAV systems. The school district is tracking sys-
tem maintenance costs for comparison with similar area schools
with more conventional HVAC systems.
The project team developed a simple method for identify-
ing problems with chiller op-
eration that might lead to a
shortfall of nighttime ice mak-
ing. This method checks
chiller operation by tracking
its electric current draw and
leads to quick identification
and correction of problems by
operations personnel before
on-peak chiller usage is nec-
essary. Even brief operation of
chillers during on-peak peri-
ods can erode energy cost sav-
ings. This project innovation
can be applied to all TES sys-
tems, and is important be-
cause it addresses a common industry complaint that many
TES systems are not able to save energy costs. Investigations
often find improper system operations that were not detected
in time.
Energy Use
Ralph Williams used only4.3% more energy than Long Leaf
(a school with conventional temperature air distribution with
no TES). However, the differences in the energy use between the
two turn out to be insignificant once the 3.9% greater occu-
pancy at Ralph Williams is taken into account. West Side, with
its conventional temperature air-distribution system and retro-
fitted TES, used the most energy (9.3% more than Long Leaf).
The significance of the integration of TES was clearly demon-
strated. First, Ralph Williams’ TES system provided much lower
humidity. Second, Ralph Williams did not materially increase
energy use compared to Long Leaf. Third, the TES at West Side
without integration substantially increased energy use.
Demand Reduction and Energy Cost Savings
The energy costs for the dual-path, low-temperature air-distri-
bution system at Ralph Williams were significantly lower than
for Long Leaf, which did not use low-temperature air or thermal
storage. The TES system is able to shift more than 300 kW of
electric demand from the utility’s on-peak period. This on-peak
demand reduction combined with the time-of-use rate schedule
resulted in 22% annual energy cost savings for this school.
First Cost Effectiveness
The installed first cost for the new system was also compa-
rable—only 1% more, $15.35 vs. $15.19 per square foot ($165
vs. $163 per
square
meter)—as compared to
Long Leaf that did not use
thermal energy storage or
low temperature air distri-
bution. The net first cost,
however, was about 7%
lower once the local power
company incentives for us-
ing TES and cold air distri-
bution were taken into
account.
Conclusions
This field demonstration
project confirmed that a
dual-path air-distribution
system with low temperature air can maintain lower and more
comfortable humidity levels as well as improved air quality in
the conditioned spaces, even in humid locations such as Florida.
The integration with TES resulted in a system that used about
the same amount of energy as a state-of-the-art HVAC system
without TES and low-temperature air distribution. The first cost
for the new system was within 1% of the state-of-the art system.
References
1. Khattar, M.K. and M.J. Brandemuehl. 2002. “Separating the V in
HVAC: a dual-path approach.” ASHRAE Journal 44(5):37–43.

butions of Mr. Larry S. Nelson, P.E., of Florida Power & Light Co.; Mr.
Brian M. Blenis, P.E., Brevard County School District; and Mr. Keith
Couch, P.E., Tilden, Lobnitz & Cooper.

W
By Matt Lawrence, Associate Member ASHRAE
Controls
For Critical
Cooling
hen computer and electronic equipment or process loads cannot
tolerate loss of cooling, high reliability cooling systems are required.
These systems, with backup air-handling systems, chillers, pumps and cooling
towers and layers of controls provide diverse means of cooling when equip-
ment or controls fail. This article provides guidance for control systems
that maximize the reliability of these elaborate cooling systems.
Some examples of high reliability sys-
tems include:
• Low-cost systems with staged equip-
ment and a control system designed to
allow some cooling to be on by default
upon a control system failure.
• Moderate-cost systems with fully re-
dundant equipment with ordinary or fully
redundant controls.
• High-cost systems with staged or re-
dundant equipment with fault tolerant
controls.
Where a project fits along this spec-
trum drives the controls planning and
testing process. With a staged mechani-
cal system, the control system must vary
the cooling capacity with the load. A
staged system might have two 150-ton
(530 kW) chillers to meet a 200-ton (700
kW) maximum load. Staging is regularly
applied to air handlers, pumps, cooling
towers and chillers.
Additional modes of operation, such as
humidity control modes, load manage-
ment modes during generator operation
and other modes merge into the staging
sequence, creating complexity. With such
systems, when the digital controls fail,
some equipment may be wired to fail “ON”
so the system can operate in a limp mode
until it can be repaired. The benefit to this
design is that relatively less capacity sits
idle during normal operation so equip-
ment and maintenance costs are lower.
In a fully redundant system, single
chillers, pumps, towers and air handlers
modulate to meet loads. The controls re-
quired often are factory-installed
onboard the equipment. When the equip-
ment fails, its lag counterpart starts and
takes over. There may be a secondary layer
of controls that operate during a primary
control system failure. In this design, re-
dundant capacity is purchased and main-
tained to sit idle until a failure of the
primary equipment. For example, two
200-ton (700 kW) chillers might be se-
lected for a 200-ton (700 kW) load. This
is a more costly system to own, but the
controls and testing are less complicated.
A system with staged or redundant
Server rooms can’t tolerate cooling loss.
The following article was published in ASHRAE Journal, January 2003. © Copyright 2003 American
Society of Heating, Refrigerating and Air-Conditioning Engineers, Inc. It is presented for educational
purposes only. This article may not be copied and/or distributed electronically or in paper form
without permission of ASHRAE.
Controls
January 2003
ASHRAE Journal
33
About the Author
equipment and fault tolerant controls
operates similarly to the staged system
but with another layer of controls. The
task of controlling the plant is split
among multiple controllers and diagnos-
tic software monitors the health of each
controller. When a controller fails, the
remaining controllers start executing
dormant software to operate equipment
around the failed controller. Testing is
complicated and costs are high. How-
ever, with proper attention and skills, this
design is very robust.
The Controls Plan
If a power surge or other unplanned
event causes a controller to lose its pro-
Matt Lawrence is manager of technical sup-
port for BellSouth Telecommunications in Jack-
sonville, Fla.
gramming and point definitions, some
plan for restoration is required. In high
reliability designs, such as telecommu-
nications networks, a central site may
store the programming and point defi-
nitions. Controllers can be configured
to “boot up” with a call through a se-
cure network to the central site to re-
load programming and definitions. In
the absence of such a network, another
automatic restoration plan must be part
of the controls design.
Control Programming
Once the larger control plan is com-
plete, the plans begin for the building
control system. Diverse control power
sources must be provided, and a robust
sequence should be described that maxi-
mizes available equipment. The control
programming to implement that se-
quence should be reviewed to ensure the
sequence has been communicated fully
and programmed accurately. Software
should operate the equipment properly
during valid conditions (implement the
sequence), handle invalid conditions, and
handle internal errors. Software should
anticipate failed temperature and status
sensors, failed equipment, and hand op-
eration of equipment. To provide for main-
tainability, material comments should be
included in the programming code if the
control system supports that feature.
Owners of high reliability sites often
employ disaster control consultants to
execute a carefully planned attack on the
cooling system to assess its vulnerabil-
ity. The plan used for that attack is a good
resource for a software review.
A software review verifies the program:
• Accomplishes every detail of the re-
quired sequence of operation.
• Is appropriate during the startup of
the system (all equipment initially off),
and with all sensing in normal ranges.
• Is appropriate for recovery from a
control power failure (program starts up
with some equipment already operating).
• Is appropriate to operate around
hand-operated equipment.
• Validates its input sensing.
• Appropriately brings on all equip-
ment when loads require.
• Appropriately shuts down all equip-
ment.
• Has no multiple instances of the same
programming for the same sequence (uses
subroutines for programming used more
than once).
• Has only one place in the software
where each piece of equipment is started
and only one place where it is stopped
to prevent toggling the equipment on
and off.
• Recovers appropriately when re-
loaded after a loss of programming, or
point definitions.
For fault tolerant controls, redundant
software should be installed in each con-
troller and should be reviewed in con-
text with the primary software. When a
controller fails, other controllers in the
control system must operate the mechani-
cal equipment around the failed control-
‘Owners of high reliability sites often employ disaster
control consultants to execute a carefully planned attack
on the cooling system to assess its vulnerability. The plan
for that attack is a good resource for a software review.’
They require a high reliability cooling system.
34
ASHRAE Journal ashrae.org
January 2003
ler. The failed controller may have its equipment wired to be in
a default state upon failure. Status signals connected to mul-
tiple controllers allow the remaining operational controllers
to operate equipment around the failed controller and main-
tain cooling. Each facility’s plan for fault tolerance is unique.
The software must be reviewed to ensure that each controller’s
programming coordinates solid fault tolerance.
Because faulty software can create situations that cannot
occur naturally, the test plan should trace through every deci-
sion branch in the program. Test conditions should be planned
to operate each branch of each decision statement. This pro-
vides an examination of each state of each sensing input in
each mode.
Component Verification
Testing the control system starts with
the physical components, power supply
and grounding. Components should be
installed, verified as functional, calibrated
and properly documented. A digital con-
trol system is more dependent on its
grounding than a conventional system.
Separate grounds should be traced from a
ground source to the control panels and
checked for continuity. Surge protection
should be verified.
Operation Verification
The first step in designing a test plan is
to review the operational sequence. Ob-
serve the normal equipment startup and
shutdown; normal equipment modula-
tion for loads; and normal equipment
modes. Common modes of operation to
be tested include refrigerated cooling,
outdoor air cooling (air economizer), re-
frigeration-assisted outdoor air cooling,
waterside economizer, humidity control
for high, moderate and low humidity con-
ditions, generator load control, low outdoor temperature modes
for freeze protection and minimum water temperature control,
and high temperature recovery mode when cooling has failed
and is restored to manage the rate of cool down.
Depending on the software design, these modes may re-
quire testing multiple times. Software can be implemented
with routines coded repetitively instead of using subroutines.
Testing should be repeated to ensure a typing error does not
cause a problem.
For example, with multiple fans staging to meet a heat load,
software is designed sometimes so that the fan staging and
lead lag is coded once for occupied operation and again for
unoccupied. An error could cause the fan staging to work prop-
erly when testing during occupied operation while it fails dur-
ing unoccupied operation. Requiring a subroutine prevents
this control system weakness.
Lead-lag changes should be demonstrated when all equip-
ment is operational and when the equipment has failed. When
all equipment is operational, a lead change simply coordi-
nates bringing on a new piece of equipment and shutting down
the lag equipment. If some equipment has failed, switching
failed equipment into the lead usually is inappropriate. Sched-
ule-based operation should be demonstrated. Operation for
occupied and unoccupied hours should be observed.
Signals indicating equipment status need to be reliable and
should not report falsely. Fans often are monitored through
differential pressure switches or current sensors. When vari-
able frequency drives are used, slow-speed operation can ap-
pear to be a failure to these sensing
devices. Therefore, careful calibration
is necessary. Each status-indicating sig-
nal should be similarly tested for weak-
ness of design and installation.
Alarm testing should demonstrate that
alarms are properly generated and re-
ceived at the correct monitoring station.
A list of equipment that is to be alarmed
should be prepared and the equipment
alarming should be tested. When an
alarm is received at the monitoring loca-
tion, its correct priority and appropriate
description or text message should be
verified. Alarm interaction and interlocks
should be tested. Fire alarm operation
usually impacts fan operation, and other
interlocks that are required should be
demonstrated to work. If the site has
standby power generation, proper equip-
ment operation and alarm functionality
during transfer to and from the generator
should be demonstrated.
The controls should appropriately
handle control system internal malfunc-
tions. In a distributed control system, panels should be discon-
nected from the network to ensure that appropriate alarms occur
for communications failure and equipment operation, and to
test appropriate equipment operation without communications.
Power failure at the controllers should be tested to verify that
appropriate equipment operation occurs when the primary con-
trol system fails. Sensor failures should be tested to ensure
robust response to erroneous sensor input. For example, failure
of an outdoor sensor could cause problems with cooling tower
operation if this failure has not been anticipated.
When programming and point definitions in the controllers
are lost, automatic reload from a remote host should be dem-
onstrated if the system has this capability.
When these tests are enumerated on a test plan, they should
Figure 1: Control panel with manual
override controls and backup pneu-
matic control.
Controls
January 2003
ASHRAE Journal
35
be compared to the list of decisions
in the software to ensure every de-
cision branch is exercised. Tests
should include those necessary to
exercise each decision branch in
the software and to demonstrate
the systems ability to respond to
dynamics of occupancy including
incremental setpoint changes.
Some testing activity fulfills the
requirements of more than one test.
Other testing has less impact on an
occupied building when per-
time-based history can be a time-weighted average, a maxi-
mum, a minimum or a snapshot at the end of the time interval.
Each of these has different implications in testing.
A good commissioning process requires at least 30 days of
change-of-state trends. These should show no short cycling of
equipment and no inappropriate alarm conditions. Time-based
trends should show temperatures and pressures are maintained.
These should be reviewed for a weekday and a weekend or
whatever length will demonstrate proper control through all
modes of operation and occupancy.
Using the change-of-state history, a time chart can be cre-
ated to validate that the proper sequence of events occurs in
sequence-sensitive equipment operation. The controller must
record history with each pass of the programming or otherwise
provide discrete changes of state of the controlled equipment.
For digital values, this is every on/off state change. For analog
information, specify the threshold for the controller to record
the change of value (every 0.1 degree change, every 10%
change) to provide a basis for accurate assessment of perfor-
mance.
Table 1 shows a time chart for a chiller system start. In this
instance, starting a lag chiller requires that a pump starts, an
isolation valve opens, then the chiller starts. The time chart is
a good tool to show if this sequence occurs correctly. Proper
operation looks like a waterfall and problems stand out. In the
table, the proper start sequence of Chiller 1 has the waterfall
appearance. The improper start sequence of Chiller 2 does not.
The chart indicates that opening the isolation valve before
starting the related pump caused a failure on lack of flow. The
chillers restarted when the pump started. This activity ulti-
mately caused the failure of a compressor on the lag chiller.
For temperature and pressure control, graphs are an excel-
lent way to assess control operation. A graph indicates correla-
tion between control devices and the controlled pressure or
temperature. Popular spreadsheet programs facilitate graphing
the historical data. Evaluating the graphs primarily assesses
tuning of proportional control loops, but also may reveal other
control problems. In Figure 2, a trend of a cooling tower’s
operation shows a continuously open bypass valve and high
fan speed. To determine the cause, the fill performance must be

Table 1: Waterfall start sequence of the first chiller, and a faulty start on the lag chiller.
formed back-to-back with another test. Sometimes testing nec-
essarily impacts the building’s operation. A quiet time should
be allowed for operation to stabilize after the test so that sup-
port of the building’s business functions can continue. After
all of these factors have been considered, the test plan is ready
for execution.
Historical Records
A control system responds to countless inputs from its sen-
sors throughout a day, a week, a month. Standing by to ob-
serve that every response is appropriate is nearly impossible.
To overcome this, a digital control system should record and
make available historical information on what was sensed and
what was operated. Historical information is most useful if the
control equipment can record data based on changes in state
of an input or output and based on time.
To monitor equipment short cycling, change-of-state in-
formation should be reviewed. To monitor that a fan system
and dampers are keeping a space at design conditions, use
time-based and change-of-state information. Pump speed usu-
ally is adjusted to yield a consistent pressure in a system, so
a time-based history of pressure and speed validates that.
Observing the speed signal history based on change of state
validates that the speed control is not hunting and changing
excessively.
The control system may have functionality to highlight
dysfunctional analog control in its history collection. More
often, a graph of the data and review of discrete changes of
value in control output is required. When the data is trans-
ferred to a spreadsheet, formulas (such as standard deviation)
can be used to see that control output is correlating with
controlled conditions.
What actually is recorded is specific to the control system
manufacturer and must be recognized so that testing can con-
firm proper operation. A change-of-state record for a digital
value (on-off) that indicates a time-weighted average and one
that indicates every actual change of state discretely, provide
different information. If the digital input and outputs are re-
corded based on a time interval, very short time intervals are
necessary for the data to have value. Analog information with
36
ASHRAE Journal ashrae.org
January 2003
assessed, and operation of the water distribution
and flow volume need to be examined.
Conclusion
High reliability cooling systems present ad-
ditional challenges for the controls system with
their special equipment, layers of controls and
control sequences that respond to failures of
equipment and controls components. Devel-
oping control software requires programming
skills, knowledge of mechanical systems and
experience in the operation and maintenance
of mechanical equipment. Detailed planning,
and software review should precede these con-
trols installations. The commissioning process
should include exhaustive testing of equip-
ment operation and detailed review of the con-
trol system’s operation history. The reward of
this effort is high reliability in cooling systems serving sen-
sitive loads.
Bibliography
ASHRAE Guideline 1-1996, The HVAC Commissioning Process.

January 2003
ASHRAE Journal
17
Sizing
H
By Bradley Kurtz, Member ASHRAE
Being Size Wise
ow does one select a summer conditioning system that provides
good thermal comfort, operates at a reasonable cost and addresses
high indoor moisture levels? The answer is design objectives will be
met if the system size is based on an accurate heat gain calculation,
while focusing on the system’s latent capacity during the selection pro-
cess. Easy enough. The question and its answer are not new. How-
ever, the subject is newly significant.
Over the last 20 years or more, resi-
dential insulation values have increased.
Techniques for sealing the structural en-
velope have been improved.
Homeowners are more informed and
aware of energy conservation and high
energy costs. They have raised their ther-
mostat setpoints in summer and lowered
them in winter. As a result, some resi-
dences suffer from bad indoor air that
includes problems such as high indoor
moisture levels and the HVAC industry’s
new four-letter-word, mold. Be assured
that homeowners are aware of, and con-
cerned about, the quality of the air in
their homes. In addition, personal com-
fort is still high on their priority list.
All of these factors demand that today’s
installing contractors use the residential
heat gain calculation, and at the same
time, revise their interpretation of the to-
tal heat load. The contractor needs to have
a clear understanding of the heat gain cal-
culation and how it relates to part-load
and design conditions and how to apply
equipment in keeping with this under-
standing. Sizing and selecting systems
based on number of Btus per square foot
or tons per bedroom or doubling the total
heat gain, if a calculation was performed,
will not provide acceptable indoor com-
fort at reasonable operating costs.
It is certain that the use of these 1970s
rules-of-thumb result in wide indoor tem-
perature swings and elevated humidity
levels along with potentially high oper-
ating costs, poor system performance and
dissatisfied customers. Long-time HVAC
industry wise man, John Malloy, has writ-
ten that equipment sizing and selection
(application) is an art form. He suggests
Residential Systems
About the Author
Bradley Kurtz works as a business consultant
and serves on the Board of Directors, ACCA
Akron-Canton Chapter, Canton, Ohio.
The following article was published in ASHRAE Journal, January 2003. © Copyright 2003 American Society of Heating, Refrigerating and Air-
Conditioning Engineers, Inc. It is presented for educational purposes only. This article may not be copied and/or distributed electronically or in
paper form without permission of ASHRAE.
18
ASHRAE Journal ashrae.org
January 2003
that the contractor should use accepted design criteria, use the
mathematics and all of the procedures and then “use your
head.” The intent of this article is to stimulate this revision of
the thought process in the HVAC contracting community and
maybe raise more questions than are answered.
Math and Procedures
The residential heat gain calculation usually is based on the
venerable, probably irreplaceable, and recently revised Air
Conditioning Contractors of America (ACCA) Manual J, Resi-
dential Load Calculation. The procedure involves calculat-
ing square footage of exposed walls, ceilings, doors, glass doors
and windows by their compass orientation
and multiplying these values by their pub-
lished heat transfer multiplier. Heat gain
values for internal loads, duct gain, infil-
tration and ventilation are calculated and
added to the sum of all of the structural
products. The result is a total heat gain.
The total heat gain is based on historic
weather data or a set of outdoor conditions
referred to as design conditions.
In the Akron-Canton region of Ohio,
summer design conditions are 86°F (30°C)
at 71°F (22°C) wet bulb, and the heat trans-
fer multiplier usually is based on a tem-
perature difference of 15°F (8°C). The 15°F
(8°C) temperature difference is selected
simply because it is securely part of HVAC
dogma (at least in Northeast Ohio), and
because 86°F (30°C) minus 15°F (8°C)
gives us 71°F (22°C) (or is it 90°F [30°C]
minus 75°F [24°C]?)—a good guess at a
thermostat setpoint for a mostly unknown
customer.
What this means in its simplest form, is
if a structure has a total heat gain of 24,000
Btu/h (7034 W) at a 15°F (8°C) outdoor
to indoor temperature difference (15°F
∆T), a system with a total capacity of
24,000 Btu/h (7034 W), will be running continuously while
conditioning that structure, if the space thermostat is set at
71°F (22°C) at an outdoor temperature of 86°F (30°C), or at a
15°F ∆T. In reality it isn’t this simple, but it is what the heat
gain calculation represents.
Traditionally, those who size and select summer condition-
ing equipment have been taught to “run the numbers” and
select up. Thus, if a cooling load comes to 25,000 Btu/h (7327
W) a 2.5 ton (9 kW) system is prescribed. Therefore, heat gain
calculation slop identification is the logical starting point for
this discussion and will be referenced to the Akron-Canton
area of Ohio throughout.
Perspective on the Numbers
Only a casual examination of the bin weather data is re-
quired to learn that the temperature in the Akron-Canton, Ohio
area is most likely to be 65°F to 75°F (18°C to 24°C) when
residential summer conditioning may be required or expected.
Only a slightly more involved look indicates that on average,
design conditions or above only occur 6% of the time that
residential summer conditioning may be required or expected.
In an effort to give this same statement more impact, it is fair to
assume that a system (selected and installed per generally ac-
cepted procedures) operates during part-load conditions 94%
of the time. To take this one step further, that same system can
be considered grossly oversized 66% of
the time that it operates during those part-
load conditions.1
Gross oversizing is amplified if the
equipment selection process involves
adding capacity to include a system safety
factor (which it often does). In addition,
all of this is magnified by the outdoor to
indoor temperature difference that is used
to arrive at the heat transfer multipliers. As
previously stated, 15°F ∆T represents a
space thermostat setting of 71°F at 86°F
(22°C at 30°C) outdoor temperature. In my
experience, summer conditioning
setpoints in residences are more often than
not 74°F to 76°F (23°C to 24°C), the
higher temperature setting being rather
common. Understand that the thermostat
sizes the system. The 24,000 Btu/h (7034
W) (at 15°F ∆T) system in the earlier ex-
planation of the load calculation, does not
start continuous operation until over 90°F
(32°C) at a setpoint of 76°F (24°C), and so
is oversized even at design conditions.2
ACCA Manual J lists outdoor design
conditions for general localities as well
as a suggested indoor summer tempera-
ture of 75°F (24°C) at 50% or 55% RH.
ASHRAE also lists outdoor design conditions and an indoor
“comfort zone” that represents acceptable indoor conditions
for most people.3 The area of comfort falls roughly around
75°F (24°C) at a similar indoor humidity range. A solution to
over-calculating would be for the industry to start using a tem-
perature difference that would result in a cooling load that
better reflects prevailing outdoor conditions at recommended
indoor conditions. Consult the appropriate outdoor design
condition table, Table 1 in ACCA Manual J, and subtract 75
from the published summer design dry-bulb temperature for
your region. Then, resist the urge to round up.
Windows and glass doors are another area where an excess in
Moisture &
Continuous Fan
Operation
Discussion over the last several
years implicates continuous fan op-
eration in the elevation of indoor air
moisture levels. While I can under-
stand how this might occur, switch-
ing the fan to run only during the
compressor cycle does not replace
proper installation practices and cer-
tainly does not equate to a solution.
All evaporator coils and drain pans
drain if the equipment is installed
and maintained per manufacturer’s
recommendations. If indoor humid-
ity levels are so high in a given struc-
ture that someone is studying them,
why is the equipment cycling off?
With today’s identified need for bet-
ter filtration and ventilation, continu-
ous fan operation is a valuable tool.
Sizing
January 2003
ASHRAE Journal
19
the total structural heat gain can easily exist. Windows and
glass door heat gains are calculated and added to the total
according to their compass orientation. The actual amount of
excess represented by the windows is dependent on the level
of attention to detail exercised by the person performing the
calculation, the quality of the information (compass orienta-
tion, external shading, window performance, etc.) used in per-
forming the calculation and the actual square footage of
windows by their orientation. Other than the potentially in-
flating attention to detail issue, window orientation (in certain
cases) represents the most identifiable excess in the total. How-
ever, the subject is so complex, that an attempt at an explana-
tion (that is what it would be) probably has no place in an
article on the residential heat gain calculation and equipment
application. Having said that, a general overview is offered.
Summer window heat transfer multipliers (HTM) represent
the unit’s heat transfer by conduction plus heat gain resulting
from radiation. The HTM values are based on expected condi-
tions occurring on the warmest day of the year, sometime be-
tween the end of July and the beginning of August. The
radiation portion is added as an average between sometime in
the morning and sometime in the afternoon during midsum-
mer and is affected by the window’s compass orientation.
As suggested by the preceding description, the whole basis
for the residential window heat transfer multipliers is almost
infinitely variable. Two things can be viewed as certain. The
sun can only impact window heat gain in part of a structure for
part of the day. On a day with cloud cover (degrees of cloud
cover exist) or when the sun is below the nominal horizon, all
windows transfer heat at the rate dictated by their respective
transmission coefficients or U values. Heat gain from radia-
tion disappears from the working structural load sometime af-
ter sundown, to an enormously varying degree. This same line
of thought also can be applied to opaque structures and their
equivalent temperature differences.
An honest representation of all of this can be found in the
2001ASHRAE Handbook—Fundamentals, 29.2, bottom of the
left column. “The sum of all space instantaneous heat gains at
any given time does not necessarily (or even frequently) equal
the cooling load for the space at that same time.” This discus-
sion might add, “Some of these heat gains may not occur at all
on any given day.” A human inclination (at least among HVAC
contractors and design engineers) is to react to window heat
gain with a “safe” estimate. It has not been my experience that
incomplete, or complex data results in system capacity defi-
ciencies. It can be assumed that the use of the summer heat
transfer multipliers in Tables 3 and 4 of ACCA Manual J, 7th
Edition, results in an ample total heat gain.
System Selection
The principal obstacle to the design objectives laid down in
the opening paragraph is the system off cycle. A summer con-
ditioning system is in a race with sensible heat and water va-
por. Sensible heat is easily beaten. However, unless water vapor,
latent heat, also is beaten the whole race is lost. The race against
sensible heat can be partially lost without jeopardizing the
acceptable operation of the system. As long as the latent por-
tion of the heat load is dealt with and indoor humidity is main-
tained at the lower comfort ranges, occupants simply may not
notice a slight increase in dry-bulb temperature. However, to
accomplish this, a system must be running. Thanks to manu-
facturers, equipment selection as it applies to all of this has
never been easier.
Much has been written about air-handling units with elec-
tronically, or internally commutated motors that can operate
in what is referred to as an enhanced mode. These units begin
each equipment cycle with several minutes of cold evaporator
coil operation by starting at 30% of full air volume, stepping
up to 80% then to full volume operation over the course of 12
minutes or so. This transfers a percentage of any system’s ca-
pacity into the latent column during the beginning of every
run cycle, enhancing the system’s dehumidification capabili-
ties and lengthening overall run time. The system has the added
advantage of allowing the full volume air delivery to be pre-
set, by way of DIP switches. The performance possibilities are
almost endless for tailoring application specific system ca-
pacity. Enhanced blowers are definitely a part-solution to meet-
ing today’s design objectives. However, so much has been
written about them and their benefits, the discussion will turn
to a related subject and an alternate choice.
I made passing mention of HVAC dogma earlier in this ar-
ticle. The total volume of air required to operate a summer
‘A summer conditioning system is in a race with sen-
sible heat and water vapor. Sensible heat is easily
beaten. However, unless water vapor, latent heat, also
is beaten the whole race is lost.’
20
ASHRAE Journal ashrae.org
January 2003
conditioning system is another part of this. The majority of
contractors and designers will tell you that indoor air volumes
should be in the 400 cfm per ton (53 L/s per kW) range to give
good system performance. This is not wrong. However, the
more informed will add that it depends on the design objec-
tive of the system. A look at published system performance
data gives some insight on how to select a system with long
run times and increased latent capacity.
A major equipment manufacturer’s 2 ton (7 kW) 12 SEER
fixed capacity outdoor condensing unit is performance rated
from 625 to 1,025 cfm (295 to 484 L/s). We will use 76°F
(24°C) dry bulb and 63°F (17°C) wet bulb (just under 50%
RH) as the indoor conditions and 85°F (29°C) as the outdoor
dry bulb temperature for the performance examples. At 800
cfm (378 L/s) the system has a total capacity of 21,210 Btu/h
(6216 W) with a latent capacity of 4,356 Btu/h (1277 W),
about 20% of the total. Operating at a total indoor airflow of
625 cfm (295 L/s), the same system has a total capacity of
20,580 Btu/h (6032 W) (a 3% decrease), with the latent capac-
ity increased to 5,475 Btu/h (1604 W). This is an increase to
about 27% of the total and represents a latent capacity in-
crease of more than 25%. Understand that a system has latent
capacity partly because latent heat exists to be removed.4
As the system removes latent capacity, its total capacity
drops primarily by the amount of latent heat removed from
the space. Moving down the chart to 59°F (15°C) wet-bulb
indoor temperature (approx. 35% RH and still wet coil con-
ditions), the total system capacity at 625 cfm (294 L/s) is
18,816 Btu/h (5515 W) with the latent portion being 1,336
Btu/h (392 W) or about three times the latent capacity of the
same system at 800 cfm (378 L/s).
It is a given that dry coil conditions occur in normal resi-
dential systems at an entering air relative humidity just above
30% as a consequence of the evaporator coil temperature. At
higher air volumes this occurs at slightly higher relative
humidity values. Lower the total airflow and the humidity
control capability of the system increases, as shown in the
example, without a drastic decrease in total capacity. As it
relates to this discussion, a slight decrease in total capacity
is not bad because it represents extended run cycles at all
conditions.
Coincident Benefits
Reduced system airflows provide additional benefits for the
homeowner. All filters give better performance at the reduced
face velocities associated with reduced total system volume.
Improved filtration—however it is achieved—should be part
of today’s design objective.
The second benefit, is that reduced total air volume should
result in reduced system noise levels, which is always a com-
fort consideration. This is certainly not automatic, since a 625
cfm (295 L/s) duct system can be designed at an air velocity of
1,500 fpm (8 m/s) and presents a noise problem. The reduced
system air volumes afford the contractor or designer the op-
portunity to use lower duct velocities without an increase in
installation costs. It is a matter of judgment whether or not the
opportunity is seized.
Throughout the discussion these phrases have been used:
increased run times, long run cycles, extended run cycles. They
have been used largely without explanation because long run
cycles are widely accepted as a major indoor comfort compo-
nent. The comfort–long run cycle association refers to tem-
perature and humidity swing. It’s no secret that the system off
cycle causes temperature and humidity swing. If a system is
correctly sized and runs with a minimum amount of off cycles,
indoor temperature and humidity swing is in turn minimized,
resulting in a high level of comfort. But how can a system that
is designed to run a high percentage of every hour possibly be
economical to operate? A 2 ton (7 kW) system consumes less
power than a 2.5 ton (9 kW) system, roughly 1.5 kW versus 2
kW at our stated conditions. However, simple power consump-
tion is not the answer. The answer is found in the relationship
between oversizing, part-load conditions, the off cycle, and
acceptable comfort and how the thermostat sizes the system.
The system that is being assembled here is set up to run with
a comparatively high percentage of latent capacity. On a de-
sign day it will be running steady state and maybe loosing the
sensible heat race by a couple of degrees. In doing so, it will be
providing maximum dehumidification. The 2.5 ton (9 kW)
system may be cycling off on a design day, but it uses 33%
more power. So, the actual power consumption between the
two systems may be a “wash.” While the 2.5 ton (9 kW) system
will be maintaining acceptable perceived indoor humidity lev-
‘The reduced system air volumes afford the contractor or de-
signer the opportunity to use lower duct velocities without
an increase in installation costs. It is a matter of judgment
whether or not the opportunity is seized.’
Sizing
January 2003
ASHRAE Journal
21
els at outdoor design conditions, it will be maintaining those
at a higher average than the smaller system setup at reduced
total airflow. Sometime either side of sundown, the sensible
heat load drops, the 2.5 ton (9 kW) system’s run times shorten
(it’s total capacity is increasing), and the race with the latent
portion of the load will be lost. Remember, that the water va-
por contained in the outdoor air at 2 p.m. is usually still there
at 9 p.m. Although the indoor temperature will be maintained
at the setpoint (we have assumed 76°F [24°C]), indoor humid-
ity levels will be increasing. The homeowner will be stimu-
lated (“Is it warm in here?”) to start downward adjustments of
the setpoint, artificially increasing the sensible load on the
structure.
This exercise often ends with the set point at or below 70°F
(21°C). It is here that cost of operation enters the equation. Our
properly sized 2-ton (7 kW) system will probably still be ad-
dressing sensible heat load from the afternoon at 9 p.m., hav-
ing run “under-capacity” through the heat of the day. And
while the indoor temperature at 9 p.m. may still be hovering
above 76°F (24°C), because humidity levels have been and are
being maintained in the lower portion of the comfort zone, the
homeowner never thinks to check the thermostat. Admittedly,
proving that the 2-ton (7 kW) system is less costly to operate
over the course of a cooling season is no simple matter. It is a
project for another article by someone with a different set of
skills and a different mind-set. From an application perspec-
tive, the 2.5 ton (9 kW) system does not meet all of the design
objectives, and so is unacceptable.
Some additional thoughts need to be thrown into the mix.
First and foremost, it should not be construed that ACCA
Manual J is being accused here. Besides the fact that this would
constitute a “treasonous act” by a member of Ohio’s HVAC
community, Manual J has served the HVAC industry well and
will continue to do so.
The second thought involves the application of a bit of as-
sembly room sizing logic. Residences generally are assumed
to be 100% occupied. However, if you were forced to guess,
you might prejudice your sizing considerations with the pos-
sibility that a residence will be unoccupied during 70% of the
time that peak summer load conditions occur. Does this mean
that there is a good chance that when and if design conditions
occur, a system may be operating in part-load conditions (no
activity, no door openings, thermostat setpoint at 80°F [27°C])?
Check the numbers of two-income families, and the sales of
programmable thermostats. I think the answer to this question
could be yes.
It should be stressed that without a properly constructed
building envelope, insulation, sealing materials and vapor
barriers correctly installed and placed (basements and crawl
spaces included), all of this talk of proper sizing and latent
capacity becomes mostly talk. The discussion of how we run
the load calculations, capacities at air volumes and applying
the equipment is still valid. However, a “good enough” enve-
lope is not good enough if one of the design objectives is to
reduce summer indoor moisture levels. As stated earlier, excel-
lent work has been done to improve materials and techniques
for reducing heat and moisture transfer, even in existing resi-
dences. There is no reason for not using these capabilities and
materials in new construction, where the additional costs are
insignificant when weighed against the long-term benefits.
Finally, apply all of the foregoing discussion to coastal re-
gions where outdoor design wet-bulb temperatures approach
80°F (27°C) and it isn’t difficult to see why the HVAC industry
has a new four-letter-word.
Conclusion
How does one select a summer conditioning system that
provides good thermal comfort, results in reasonable utility
bills, and addresses high indoor moisture levels? Start by per-
forming the heat gain calculation using a temperature differ-
ence that will not bias the system size in an upward direction.
Be attentive to details. A little detail work usually eliminates
loads of worry. Then, and this might be the hardest part, stick
by the numbers and use them for the equipment selection.
Move your sizing focus away from the outdoor design dry-
bulb temperature. If the calculated load doesn’t fit your expec-
tations (it won’t at first), try to identify possible excesses—they
do exist. The slop identification helps to refocus the equip-
ment selection process and avoids the tendency to round up to
the next larger system. Where feasible, use indoor air-han-
dling equipment with enhanced blower operation capabili-
ties. Otherwise, in more humid regions of the Midwest and
South, set up systems to operate at high latent capacities using
indoor airflows in the 300 to 350 cfm per ton (40 to 47 L/s per
kW) range.
In closing, none of this represents an absolute answer to the
comfort-cost of operation-indoor moisture question. No single
answer exists for the indoor moisture question. All of this is
simply a nonscientific, experience-based approach to summer
conditioning equipment sizing and selection that will result
in overall improved system performance. Residential summer
conditioning equipment application, not an exact science, is
truly an art form.
Notes
1. Bin weather data, Akron, Ohio, based on 2,303 hours annual
average above 65°F (18°C). The assumption being made is that these
temperatures are most likely to occur during cooling season. There are
2,928 hours from June through September. A look at Brownsville,
Texas and West Palm Beach, Fla., indicates that the relationship be-
tween design conditions and the most likely to occur range of summer
conditioning temperatures is similar to the Akron-Canton region of
Ohio.
2. Anyone have any idea why this needs fixing? A General Electric
training publication dated January 1970, lists comfort values that match
current recommendations.
3. 2001 ASHRAE Handbook—Fundamentals, 8.12, Fig. 5.
4. This is at or very near suggested indoor design conditions.
28
ASHRAE Journal ashrae.org
January 2003
mproper humidity control is caused by factors such as the mismatch
between the building latent load and equipment latent capacity, in-
appropriate HVAC equipment operation (i.e., turning the equipment
off when the building is vacant), building defects (i.e., improperly in-
stalled vapor barriers, etc.), or inappropriate building operation (i.e.,
incorrect building pressurization).
moisture-related) and the sensible heat
ratio (SHR; defined as the ratio of the
sensible load to the total load, sensible
plus latent). However, there is a differ-
ence between equipment SHR (occurs at
the evaporator coil) and application SHR
(occurs in the conditioned space).
Equipment SHR is largely a function
of the coil apparatus dew point — the
larger the temperature difference between
the coil temperature and the entering air
wet-bulb temperature, the greater the abil-
ity the coil has to remove excess mois-
ture. How the equipment is designed,
maintained, and controlled governs the
coil temperature.
Application SHR is largely a function
of equipment selection/operation and the
latent loads generated within the build-
ing, infiltrated through the envelope, or
introduced as ventilation requirements.
Part-load operation can affect equipment
SHR, as well as application SHR.
As an example, many commercial
HVAC systems operate with continuous
fan while allowing the compressor to
cycle on and off based on thermostat
setpoints (sensible control). Hence, un-
der part-load conditions, continuous
fan operation with an “off ” compressor
increases the evaporator temperature
and could allow moisture on the coil to
evaporate and be reintroduced into the
building, raising the humidity level in
Increased interest in maintaining
proper indoor humidity levels stems from
the need to achieve appropriate occupant
comfort and acceptable indoor air qual-
ity, while minimizing energy use. The
proper application, selection, sizing, and
operation of the HVAC equipment are the
keys to controlling humidity levels. This
requires that the HVAC system meet sen-
sible and latent loads, not only at the
design conditions (full load), but also
over a broad range of off-design condi-
tions (part loads).
When addressing moisture control, it
is common to discuss sensible loads (i.e.,
temperature-related), latent loads (i.e.,
By Karim Amrane, Ph.D., Member ASHRAE, Glenn C. Hourahan, P.E.,
Member ASHRAE, and Greg Potts
About the Authors
Karim Amrane, Ph.D., is director, public policy
at the Air-Conditioning and Refrigeration Insti-
tute, Arlington, Va.
Glenn C. Hourahan, P.E., is vice president, re-
search and technology for the Air Conditioning
Contractors of America, Arlington, Va.
Greg Potts is a research assistant at the Air-
Conditioning and Refrigeration Institute.

the conditioned space. (For the balance of this article, discus-
sion on SHR applies only to the equipment.)
A study was performed to assess whether the latent capacity
of unitary equipment is a function of efficiency. Another ob-
jective was to determine if the latent capacity of unitary prod-
ucts has declined over the years as efficiencies have increased.
The sensible heat ratio (SHR) of large unitary equipment (cool-
ing capacity between 65,000 and 240,000 Btu/h [19 to 70
kW]) was analyzed as a function of the energy efficiency ratio
(EER). In addition, SHR data from previous studies1,2 on small
unitary equipment (cooling capacity below 65,000 Btu/h [19
kW]) was examined and updated with recent data to analyze
trends with respect to EER and seasonal energy efficiency
ratios (SEER).
Analysis
The study focused on large unitary equipment with cooling
capacities between 65,000 and 240,000 Btu/h (19 to 70 kW).
Detailed data on SHR and EER from reports of the 1996, 1998
and 2001 ARI certification test program was examined. In to-
tal, 104 test reports were examined (20 for 1996, 42 for 1998,
and 42 for 2001). These reports represent more than 50% of all
certification test reports generated during these years, from 12
different manufacturers. Although the certification program
from which the data was gathered is managed by ARI, the tests
were all conducted by an independent testing laboratory that
has been conducting tests on unitary large products for over a
decade and on small unitary products for more than 40 years.
For unitary small products (cooling capacity below 65,000
Btu/h [19 kW]), data on SHR, EER (A and B tests) and SEER
were selected from the 2001 certification program. In total,
207 test reports were examined. This represents approximately
40% of all certification test reports generated that year, from
more than 25 manufacturers.
The SHR and EER for large unitary products were obtained
at the standard ARI rating conditions of 80°F (26.7°C) dry-bulb
and 67°F (19.4°C) wet-bulb coil entering air temperatures and
outdoor conditions of 95°F (35°C) dry-bulb.3 For small unitary
products, the SHR and SEER were examined at two different
ARI rating conditions — the “A” and “B” tests, while the SEER
was calculated using the parameters and procedures listed in
ARI standard 210/240.4 The “A” test is run at 80°F (26.7°C) dry-
bulb and 67°F (19.4°C) wet-bulb coil entering air temperatures,
and 95°F (35°C) dry-bulb outdoor air temperature. The “B” test
is run at the same entering air conditions but at an outdoor dry-
bulb air temperature of 82°F (27.8°C).
EER, SEER, and SHR were measured under steady-state
operation. Also, the data upon which this analysis is based was
taken from tests conducted on several types of unitary air con-
ditioners and heat pumps, including single packaged units,
split systems, and condensing units (see Nomenclature sidebar).
Finally, the data collected and reported in this study are actual
data from an independent testing laboratory and are not manu-
facturers’ claims or ratings.
Results and Discussion
Figures 1a through 1c show the SHR as a function of EER
for large unitary products from the 1996, 1998 and 2001 certi-
fication test reports. EER values vary from as low as 8.1 to as
high as 13.1, while the SHR ranges from 0.66 to a maximum
value of 0.8. As can be seen, there is no particular trend in the
SHR as a function of EER. SHR values seem to be independent
of efficiency, as units with low EERs have SHR values in the
same range as units with high EERs. On average the SHR was
found to be around 0.70 (with a standard deviation on the
order of 3%); about the same for 1996, 1998 and 2001 test
results. A linear regression analysis also was performed on the
data and the R2 values were found to vary from a maximum of
0.1 to as low as 0.0001, clearly indicating that no correlation
exists between the sensible heat ratio and the efficiency.\*
The SHR data was also plotted with respect to EER by equip-
ment type and for all years combined as seen in Figures 2a
Figure 1: SHR as a function of EER for large unitary products from the 1996, 1998 and 2001 certification test reports.
\*The relationship between two independent variables is measured in
the case of a regression analysis by the determination coefficient R2.
The larger that R2 becomes, the stronger the relationship between the
variables is, with a value of 1 indicating a perfect linear fit.

through 2c. The graphs indicate that SHR is independent of
equipment type and has not changed over time. On average,
SHR was found to vary between 0.7 and 0.72 for a wide range
of EER values. Again, no particular correlation between SHR
and EER could be detected as the R2 values from the linear
regression analysis were found to vary from a minimum of
0.04 to a maximum of 0.17.
The latent capacity of small unitary equipment was last
analyzed in 1998.1 The analysis looked at 1994 ARI certifi-
cation test data on SHR, EER and SEER, and compared it to
an earlier study done by ARI in 1986. The major finding of
this investigation was that the SHR of small unitary equip-
ment was not a function of efficiency. To determine if the
relationship between SHR and efficiency had changed since
then, the 2001 SHR data under the “A” and “B” tests were
plotted against EER and SEER as shown in Figures 3a through
3e. The graphs indicate no particular relationship between
the SHR and efficiency. Under “A” test conditions, SHR var-
ied from 0.59 to 0.79 (with an average value of 0.7), for a
broad range of EER and SEER values. The R2 values from the
regression analysis were 0.01 and 0.03 for the EER and SEER
respectively, showing again that no correlation exists between
SHR and efficiency. Similar conclusions are drawn under “B”
test conditions.
To assess if the SHR of small unitary equipment had
changed over the past 30 years, the 2001 data was compared
to the data from 1994, 1986, 1976 and 1971 reported in the
1998 analysis.1 The findings are summarized in Table 1 and
indicate that at “A” test conditions, there has been essen-
tially no change in the SHR over the past 30 years. On the
other hand, during the same period of time, the average EER
of tested units increased by 60% from 6.4 in 1971 to 10.2 in
2001. Similarly, the SHR at “B” test conditions has been
relatively constant (at 0.69) while the average SEER increased
by more than 20% since 1986. In summary, the data confirms
that the SHR is independent of efficiency and has remained
relatively constant over the years.
Conclusions
The analysis of hundreds of independent test reports has re-
vealed that the latent capacity of small and large unitary equip-
ment (under steady-state operating conditions) is independent
Nomenclature
EER: Energy Efficiency Ratio, defined as the ratio of the cooling
capacity in Btu/h to the total power input in watts.
EER-A: Energy Efficiency Ratio at the “A” test conditions.
EER-B: Energy Efficiency Ratio at the “B” test conditions.
SEER: Seasonal Energy Efficiency Ratio, defined as the ratio of
the total cooling of the equipment during its normal usage period
for cooling (in Btu/h), to the total electrical energy input (in Watts)
during the same period.
SHR: Sensible Heat Ratio, defined as the sensible cooling load
divided by the total cooling load (sensible plus latent).
SHR-A: Sensible Heat Ratio at the “A” test conditions.
SHR-B: Sensible Heat Ratio at the “B” test conditions.
HRCU-A-CB: Air-cooled split system heat pump (heating and cool-
ing) with indoor fan.
HSP-A: Air-cooled single-package heat pump (cooling and heat-
ing).
RC-A: Air-cooled air conditioner with remote condenser.
RCU-A-C: Air-cooled condensing unit, coil alone, without blower.
RCU-A-CB: Air-cooled condensing unit, coil with blower.
SP-A: Air-cooler, single package air conditioner.
SPY-A: Air-cooler, year-round single package air conditioner.
SP-W: Water-cooled, single package air conditioner.

ASHRAE Journal
29
of efficiency and equipment types. The data indicates that on
average, the sensible heat ratio (SHR) is on the order of 0.7
(70%) at entering coil air temperature conditions of 80°F (26.7°C)
dry-bulb and 67°F (19.4°C) wet-bulb. In addition, a look at equip-
ment SHR data over many years indicates that the latent capac-
ity of unitary products has remained relatively constant. For
small unitary products, SHR has remained at a constant level
over the past 30 years while efficiencies have increased by 60%.
These improvements in efficiencies are mostly due to better
compressors, motors, and heat exchanger surface areas.
Proper selection and sizing of HVAC equipment is essential
to control humidity levels in buildings. This entails conduct-
ing appropriate design analyses to ensure that the HVAC sys-
tem meets both sensible and latent loads at full and part-load
conditions. Proper sizing and selection is particularly impor-
tant when outdoor air ventilation is required and/or if the HVAC
system operates with continuous fan while the compressor
cycles. Additionally, improper space pressurization can in-
crease the infiltration of moist outdoor air to the building,
thereby overcoming the capability of mechanical equipment
to control excess moisture.
Other factors also exist that could contribute to excessive
humidity levels. These factors include high thermostat set
points during unoccupied periods, improper system operation,
occupancy and ventilation requirements. These factors need
to be considered and addressed appropriately to better control
humidity concerns within buildings.
