50
ASHRAE Journal
March 2003
between the source and the load. The results of actual testing
in a 12 in. (305 mm) raised floor demonstrates that a perforated
tile placed within 6 to 8 ft (1.8 to 2.4 m) of a CRAH, provides
little benefit. Static pressure this close to the CRAH is mini-
mal and supply air would be equally deficient (Figure 4b). To
correct the situation, designers should position perforated tiles
over still-air areas under the floor. Since air slows down as it
spreads out, this means placing the perforated tile as far from
the CRAH as possible (Figure 4c). Relocating floor openings
to low velocity (high static pressure) areas improves cooling
distribution. This condition is exacerbated in shallow floors
(12 in. to 18 in. [305 mm to 457 mm]) where a designer adds
CRAHs in response to increased computer load. The increased
airflow, without a subsequent increase in the cross-sectional
area of the raised floor plenum, increases velocity pressure and
decreases static pressure. The low static pressure over the high
velocity plumes in the floor actually reduces cooling capacity
to critical load.
As data processing demands increase, densely packed hard-
ware plans allow less opportunity to add tiles, or relocate com-
puters over low velocity areas. Adding CRAHs will not
necessarily improve cooling. Increased CRAH output trans-
lates to increased air velocity and isolated plumes. Again static
pressure beneath the floor, which is already critically low, de-
teriorates and air circulation suffers. Boundary layer theory
can help designers to understand the phenomenon. High ve-
locity airflow below the floor creates boundary layers (shear
boundaries) between CRAH supply air plumes. In addition to
a velocity boundary layer, such flows develop a thermal bound-
ary layer and its existence plays an important part in the pro-
cess of heat transfer between the two fluid flows.
Cascading Static Regain
Raised floor cooling, even under current limitations imposed
by faulty design, remains the most efficient, flexible, cost ef-
fective, and least infrastructure-intensive method of cooling
computer room airspace. Air-cooled systems can be effective.
However, the typical approach is to treat the situation as a
thermodynamics problem by attempting to trade Btus of heat
from one area to another. This is actually a fluid mechanics
problem. To convey the cooling, fluid dynamics (see sidebar
“Understanding Fluid Dynamics”) must be properly modeled.
One approach to this is a data center design process known
as cascading static regain (CSR). The layout of inward facing
constant volume CRAHs (side-to-side) is replaced with rows
of equipment aligned front to back. Instead of creating shear
boundaries along the edges of the underfloor plumes (as with
the inward facing arrangement), CSR uses the turbulent flow
from downstream CRAHs to blend with the plume from an
upstream CRAH. Static pressure actually increases as the cas-
cading CRAH plumes are distributed along the length of the
computer room.
A CSR design meets four requirements critical to any cool-
Advertisement in the print edition formerly in this space.
Cooling
ing system. First, it can predict the distribution of airflow and
static pressure in a raised floor environment. Second, it miti-
gates the degradation of static pressure and allows computer
hardware to be placed over high velocity areas and still main-
tain adequate static pressure. Third, because of the arrange-
ment of CRAHs, underfloor air plumes are allowed to mix,
thereby improving access to redundant capacity. Finally, all of
this is accomplished without increasing cooling output or the
number of floor space wasting CRAHs.
Cascading Static Regain in Action
A large Midwest data processing company had a legacy air-
distribution design that was degrading the static pressure be-
neath the raised floor. (Remember, that static pressure is necessary
to allow airflow to percolate up through raised floor systems.)
For 16 years, areas of the 50,000 ft2 (4650 m2) data center had
insufficient cooling due to low static pressure. Additionally,
some areas would become warm inexplicably, and then mysteri-
ously return to provide full cooling. Testing determined that
plumes were isolated from each other due to thermal bound-
By Donald Wroblewski, Ph.D., P.E.
When two regions of air at different velocities are side by
side, there is an area between the two streams (called a
shear layer) where the velocity goes from the higher to the
lower velocity. The shear layer is a boundary that prevents
the plumes from mixing. These shear layers define the
boundaries of all the plumes under a raised floor.
Whenever a temperature differential exists, the natural pro-
cess is for heat transfer to occur. As you begin to transfer heat
between two layers, you get an intermediate layer of air that is
at a temperature between the two that acts as an area of
thermal resistance. This thermal boundary layer, next to the
shear boundary, grows thicker with time.
To understand the impact this thermal boundary layer has
on a raised floor environment, you have to understand ther-
mal resistance. The rate of heat flow depends on the tem-
perature difference, the area across which you are transfer-
ring heat, and a constant of thermal conductivity for the ma-
terial. Air is a very good thermal resistor, and as the layer’s
thickness increases, so does the resistance.
For example: Testing performed in a center operated by a
global Internet service provider demonstrated that opposing
and adjacent plumes were almost perfectly isolated. A CRAH
was allowed to overload with 82°F (28°C) air under the floor.
Adjacent and opposing plumes remained at a steady state of
58°F to 62°F (14°C to 17°C). Redundant capacity from adja-
cent plumes was isolated from the overloaded air conditioner
by the shear boundaries formed between the CRAH plumes.
To encourage heat transfer, you must break up the thermal
boundary layer between the plumes with turbulent eddies.
CSR creates such turbulent eddies. To describe why this
Understanding Fluid Dynamics
works, you would need several very complex differential
equations. But if we boil it down, a simple formula tells us
how a shear layer becomes a thermal boundary.
Donald Wroblewski, Ph.D., P.E., is an associate professor of
aerospace and mechanical engineering at Boston University.
aries. Also, a chilled water distribution problem caused low flow
to certain units. When the chilled water flow was low, those units
had warm supply air plumes that were isolated from adjacent
cold plumes. Since the operators and designers of the data cen-
ter were unaware of the fluid dynamic conditions that were cre-
ating the problem, they were forced to set up propeller-type
pedestal fans on top of the raised floor to help with cooling
distribution.
Initial benchmarking drawings for the facility (Figure 5a)
showed isolated pockets of low static pressure (red, yellow,
pink) underneath the raised floor. Since underfloor supply air
plumes did not mix, redundant capacity could not be accessed
for critical process loads.
It would appear to a designer that the safest place to position
a critical computer would be next to a CRAH. As previously
explained, fluid dynamic models show that this is incorrect.
Until velocity dissipates, cooling is limited by static pressure
that is eroded by underfloor velocity. Furthermore, designers
had placed computers close to CRAHs and over cramped
underfloor spaces. Both are high air velocity areas, and static
52
ASHRAE Journal ashrae.org
March 2003
pressure suffers in both (Figure 6).
Raised floors need to be designed such that even distribu-
tion of redundant static pressure allows the installation of critical
electronic loads anywhere it is required. The CRAH placement
on the floor was redesigned to allow the plumes to mix and
enhance the distribution of cooling capacity and static pres-
Figure 5: Before and after drawings of static pressure distribution. Drawing B shows the improvement after using CSR.
5A
5B
Advertisement in the print edition formerly in this space.
Cooling
March 2003
ASHRAE Journal
53
sure. The remedy to this problem required a renovation with
these design goals:
• The entire renovation had to occur without any disruption
to critical processes.
• Existing air-conditioning equipment was to be relocated
only. No additional cooling capac-
ity or airflow was added. This pro-
vided a reasonable test of the
benefits of CSR.
• The existing hardware configu-
ration was to remain constant
throughout the CSR renovation to
validate the benchmarking exer-
cise. Perforated tiles and cable cuts
were retained to keep their impact
on static pressure constant.
Improvements were seen after re-
positioning CRAHs in accordance with CSR design criteria.
The plumes mix and higher levels of static pressure are avail-
able to support cooling process loads. Residual static pressure
from beginning stages of CSR boosts lower static pressure areas
so those high static pressure regions cover large areas of the
floor. Additionally, CRAH turbulence mixes plume shear bound-
aries, thus allowing cool air to be distributed over wider areas of
the data center.
The designers completed commissioning and static pres-
sure mapping at the facility before and after the remediation
project. Maximum, minimum and average static pressure read-
ings after executing CSR were all higher than the original
readings.
CSR improved the static pressure
in areas that already had an accept-
able level, but the most dramatic
improvement was in the areas with
the lowest readings, which im-
proved by more than 200%.
Minimum and maximum values
reflect only a lowest and highest
single reading and may not be re-
flective of the floor in general. More
significant is the average value,
which increased by 26% throughout, which more accurately
reflects the benefit of CSR over the entire raised floor area, in
the reconfigured data center.

About the Author
Steven T. Taylor, P.E., is a principal at Taylor
Engineering in Alameda, Calif. He is a member of
TCs 4.3 and 1.4 and former chair of SSPC 62.1.
isleading and sometimes incorrect statements regarding how to
select and pipe expansion tanks can be found in design manuals
and manufacturers’ installation guides. Some of the more common
claims follow. Claim: Expansion tanks must be connected to the sys-
tem near the suction of the pump. Fact: Closed expansion tanks (those
not vented to the atmosphere) may be connected anywhere in the
system provided their precharge (initial) pressure and size are cor-
rectly selected. However, maximum pressure ratings of some system
components may limit expansion tank connection location.
est point in the system on the return pip-
ing to the pump. But the “best” location
may not be the one that results in the
smallest tank size. It may be where the
tank may be most conveniently located,
such as in a mechanical room where space
is available and the tank is readily ac-
cessible for service. The low-pressure
point may, for instance, be located over a
hotel guest room that is not a convenient
location for an expansion tank.
Claim: The connection point of the
expansion tank is the “point of no pres-
sure change,” i.e., the pressure at the ex-
pansion tank remains constant.
Fact: This claim has resulted in unnec-
essary confusion and concern among
operating engineers when they find sys-
tem operating pressures varying widely.
The pressure at the expansion tank will
not change when pumps are started and
stopped (other than a brief pulse), but
the pressure will change when the tem-
perature of the fluid in the system
changes, causing the water volume to
expand or contract. The amount of pres-
sure change is a function of the size of
the tank and the change in fluid tempera-
ture. As shown in the examples below,
the designer can select a tank to operate
over a wide range of pressures, e.g., 10 to
1 or more.
To better understand these facts and to
properly select expansion tanks, we must
start with the fundamentals.
Purpose
Expansion tanks are provided in
closed hydronic systems to:
By Steven T. Taylor, P.E., Member ASHRAE
Claim: The best connection point for
expansion tanks is near the suction of
the pump.
Fact: If “best” means the point that will
result in the smallest (lowest cost) expan-
sion tank, the best location is at the point
in the system that has the lowest gauge
pressure when the pump is on. For a sys-
tem that is distributed horizontally (e.g.,
a one-story building), the low-pressure
point will be at the pump suction. How-
ever for a system that is distributed verti-
cally (e.g., a multi-story building), the
low-pressure point will be near the high-
Practical Guide
M
The following article was published in ASHRAE Journal, March 2003. © Copyright 2003 American Society of Heating, Refrigerating and Air-
Conditioning Engineers, Inc. It is presented for educational purposes only. This article may not be copied and/or distributed electronically or in

March 2003
ASHRAE Journal
25
Hydronic
• Accept changes in system water volume as water density
changes with temperature to keep system pressures below equip-
ment and piping system component pressure rating limits.
• Maintain a positive gauge pressure in all parts of the sys-
tem in order to prevent air from leaking into the system.
• Maintain sufficient pressures in all parts of the system in
order to prevent boiling, including cavitation at control valves
and similar constrictions.
• Maintain required net positive suction head (NPSHr) at
the suction of pumps.
The latter two points generally apply only to high tempera-
ture hot water systems. For most HVAC applications, only the
first two points need be considered.
Types
There are basically two types of expansion tanks:
• Tank type or “plain steel” tanks (water in contact with air),
which may be atmospheric (vented to atmosphere) or pressur-
ized, and
• Diaphragm or bladder-type tanks (air and water separated by
a flexible diaphragm or bladder, typically made of heavy-duty
butyl rubber). The bladders in bladder-type tanks generally are
field replaceable should they fail while failure of a diaphragm
tank would require complete replacement of the tank.
Both types of expansion tanks work by allowing water to
compress a chamber of air as the water expands with increas-
ing temperature. When the system is cold and the water in the
tank is at the minimum level (which may be no water at all),
the tank pressure is at its initial or precharge pressure Pi. As the
water in the system expands upon a rise in temperature, water
flows into the tank and the air pocket is compressed, increas-
ing both the air and water system pressure.
When the system is at its highest temperature and the tank
water volume is at its design capacity, the resulting air and water
system pressure will be equal to or less than the design maxi-
mum pressure Pmax. Both Pi and Pmax are predetermined by the
designer as part of the tank selection process outlined below.
Initial Pressure
The tank initial or precharge gauge pressure Pi must be
greater than or equal to the larger of the following:
A. The minimum pressure required to prevent boiling and
to maintain a positive gauge pressure at any point in the
system.
This pressure can be determined as follows:
1. Find the low pressure point (LPP) in the system when the
pump is on. To do this, start at the highest point in elevation on
the return side closest to the pump suction (Point A in Figure
1). Calculate the net pressure drop from that point to the pump
suction with friction and dynamic head losses as negative and
static head increase due to elevation as positive. The point
with the lowest net pressure is the LPP. The static pressure
increase as you move down in elevation from the high point
increases 1 foot of head for every foot of elevation drop (3 kPa
for every 0.3 m). In normal practice, the frictional pressure
drop rate will be almost two orders of magnitude smaller, so the
increase in pressure due to a reduction in elevation is always a
much larger factor than the pressure decrease due to friction.
Hence, the LPP will almost always be the highest point of the
return line just after it drops down to the pump.
2. Determine Pmin, the minimum pressurization required at
the LPP to maintain a positive gauge pressure (to prevent air
from leaking into the system when a vent is opened) and to
prevent boiling. A commonly recommended minimum pres-
surization is 4 psig (28 kPa) plus 25% of the saturation vapor
pressure when this pressure exceeds atmospheric pressure (Fig-
ure 2). For chilled water, condenser water, and typical hot wa-
ter (<200°F [93°C]) systems, the recommended minimum
pressure is 4 psi (28 kPa).
For high temperature hot water, the minimum pressure will
need to be higher as shown in Figure 2. For hot water systems
that have high pressure-drop control valves located near the
LPP, the minimum pressure may need to be even higher to
prevent cavitation downstream of the valve.1
3. Locate the tank position. The tank will be the smallest
and least expensive if located near the LPP. However, space or
other considerations (e.g., convenient makeup water source,
which should be connected near the same point in the system)
may make another location more desirable.
4. Calculate the static pressure rise

The tank precharge pressure Pi is often selected at a mini-
mum of 12 psig (83 kPa), which is the industry standard
precharge for diaphragm and bladder tanks when no other
value is specified.
Tanks typically are precharged to the specified pressure in
the factory. However, the precharge pressure should be field
verified and adjusted if necessary. First, turn off any heat-pro-
ducing equipment in the system, then close the tank isolation
valve so it is isolated from the system. Fully drain the tank,
then check and adjust the tank air pressure to the desired Pi
setpoint using an air compressor. Before opening the tank iso-
lation valve, the system must be near its lowest temperature (as
determined under Selection later). Connecting the tank to a
system that is warmer than the minimum temperature will re-
sult in pressures below Pi when the system temperature drops
and water volume decreases.
B. The minimum pressure required to maintain the available
net positive suction head (NPSHa ) at the pump suction above the
pump’s minimum required net positive suction head (NPSHr ):
This criterion is only a factor in determining Pi for high tem-
perature (>200°F [93°C]) hot water systems where the pump is a
long distance (hydraulically) from the expansion tank or lo-
cated well above the tank. In almost all other applications, the
minimum pressurization resulting from Step A will result in pump
suction pressures well above the NPSHr . Hence, for the large
majority of common HVAC applications, the calculations in
Step B may be skipped. This step is listed here for completeness.
The precharge pressure required to maintain NPSHr is deter-
mined as follows:
1. Find the required pump net positive suction head (NPSHr)
from the manufacturer’s pump curve or selection software.

Maximum Pressure
Tank maximum pressure Pmax is determined as follows:
1. Determine the maximum allowable system pressure
Pma and critical pressure point, CPP. The CPP is the “weakest

27
link” in the system. It is a function of the pressure ratings at
the maximum expected operating temperature of components
and equipment (obtained from the manufacturer) and their
location in the system in elevation and relative to the pump.
To find the CPP, make a list of the components and equip-
ment with the lowest pressure ratings (often boilers and other
pressure vessels) and calculate the difference between their
rating and their vertical elevation, in consistent units
(e.g., convert the psig pressure ratings into units of head (feet
of water) and subtract the elevation of the component).
The component with the smallest difference is the “weakest
link.” Its location on discharge side of the pump is the CPP
and the maximum pressure Pma is the rating pressure of the
equipment.
2. Locate the pressure relief valve. Typically, the best loca-
tion is near the CPP and the component the relief valve is
intended to protect, but a common location is near the expan-
sion tank connection to the system, on the system side of the
tank isolation valve.

Tank Selection
Typically, expansion tanks are selected using manufactur-
ers’ software or selection charts based on the following data:
• The minimum temperature the system will see, Tc . For
heating systems, this would generally be the initial fill tem-
perature, e.g., 50°F (10°C). For cooling systems, this would
be the design chilled water temperature, e.g., 40°F (4°C).
• The maximum temperature the system will see, Th. For heat-
ing systems, this would be the design hot water temperature,
e.g., 180°F (82°C). For cooling systems, this would generally
be the temperature the system may rise to when it is off, e.g.,
80°F (27°C) or so depending on the location of piping (in-
doors or outdoors).
• The total volume of water in the system, Vs , including all
piping and vessels.
• The precharge pressure Pi and the maximum pressure Pmax
determined previously.
Tank volume also may be selected from fundamental equa-
tions such as Equations 5 and 6,2 which apply to
bladder/diaphragm type tanks:

Equation 5 ensures that the acceptance volume Va exceeds
the expanded water volume Ve to avoid damage to the blad-
der or diaphragm when the system is at its highest tempera-
ture and pressure. Equation 6 ensures that the tank volume Vt
is sufficient for both the expanded water Ve and the air cush-
ion necessary to maintain pressures in the tank between Pi
and Pmax .
Equation 6 conservatively ignores the expansion of the
system piping since the resulting increase in volume is rela-
tively small and the calculation is complicated in systems
with various piping materials, each with different coefficients
of expansion.
Specific volume at various temperatures is shown in Table 1.
Examples
Example 1: Chilled Water System
Assume the system is a chilled water system with a design
chilled water temperature of 40°F and system volume of 1,000
gallons (3785 L). The layout is as shown in Figure 1 with the
pump at the bottom of a multistory building. Pump head is 80
ft (240 kPa).
First calculate the initial precharge pressure Pi . Since the
system is chilled water, there is no concern about net positive
suction head, so only Step A needs to be followed:
1. The LPP in the system is the highest point on the return
line just as it drops down to the pump (Point A in Figure 1).
2. Pmin is 4 psig (28 kPa) as shown in Figure 2.
3. The tank will be the smallest and least expensive if lo-
cated near the LPP. However, for this example, assume the tank
is located near Point B at the pump suction, which is often the
most convenient location since space is usually available.

As in Example 1, the expansion tank must have an accep-
tance volume greater than 3.75 gpm (0.24 L/s) (the volume
of expanded water is unchanged), but the total volume re-
quired falls to 5.4 gallons (20 L). This demonstrates that a
tank located at the LPP in a multi-story system is smaller and
therefore less expensive than a tank located at the pump suc-
tion (Example 1).
Example 3: High Temperature Hot Water System
Assume the system is a high temperature hot water system
with a design hot water temperature of 300°F (149°C) and
system volume of 1,000 gallons (3785 L). The pumps’ required
NPSHr is 5 ft (15 kPa) and its head is 50 ft (150 kPa). The
layout is horizontal as shown in Figure 3.
First calculate the initial precharge pressure Pi , which will be
the larger of that required to prevent boiling and that required to
maintain adequate net positive suction head at the pump. The
pressure required to prevent boiling is determined as:
1. The LPP in the system is the highest point on the return
line just after it drops down to the pump (Point A in Figure 3).
2. Pmin is recommended to be 70 psig (482 kPa) as shown in
Figure 2. If there are control valves located near the LPP, the
minimum pressure may need to be higher.1
3. For this example, assume the tank is located near Point B
at the pump suction.

As a home exercise, redo the above calcu-
lations with the tank at the pump discharge,
Point C. You will find that the precharge pres-
sure increases while all other variables re-
main the same, resulting in a somewhat larger
tank. Nevertheless, the tank will still meet
all of its intended functions so this is a per-
fectly acceptable, if unusual, location.

About the Author
Eric Utterson, P.E., is a principal with McClure Engineering Associates of
Denver.
lthough many articles have discussed proper control valve and damper selection for linear response,
the attention has focused on selecting control components to reduce the inherent nonlinear behavior
rather than using direct digital controls (DDC) for linearizing the behavior. This article addresses a method
for achieving predictable linear response for supply duct pressure control, economizer damper control
and steam heat exchanger leaving water temperature control using PID (proportional plus integral plus
derivative) control loops. Using this technique normalizes PID loops, which prevents the need for tuning
many common HVAC PID loops.
In the simplest form, the output of a PID loop is described as:
Pgain
PIDOutput =
(1)


dt

dt

Error
1
where
Error = PID Input – PID Setpoint (for direct acting control
scenarios)
Pgain = Proportional Gain Term
Itime = Integral Time
Dtime = Derivative Time
Each controls manufacturer implements Equation 1 differ-
ently. The terms Pgain, Itime and Dtime are mathematically
related to the terms used by many manufacturers. Other names
for PID terms include throttling range, proportional band, re-
peats per minute, integral gain and derivative gain.
The key advantage of using PID control versus proportional-
only control is the elimination of steady-state error. Propor-
tional control works by adjusting the output proportionally to
the error between the input and setpoint. This method exactly
achieves setpoint under steady-state conditions in only a few
circumstances. The addition of the integral component (a sum
of errors) to proportional control allows tight control of setpoint
at steady-state conditions.
For HVAC applications, the response time of the PID control
algorithm is dependent on the amount of response that occurs
because of a control action and the transient (time) response
between a control action and the response. These components
are best demonstrated in an open loop test as shown in Figure
1, which illustrates the step response of supply duct pressure
versus time for a step input in fan speed.
For the conditions that existed during this test, supply duct
pressure increased from 0.4 in. w.c. to 1 in. w.c. (99 to 249 Pa)
for a fan speed increase from 30% to 50%. The amount of time
required to achieve the full response of the input is approxi-
mately 25 seconds (note that the pressure sensor reading repre-
sents a digitally filtered input). Many techniques exist for
calculating (tuning) Pgain, Itime and Dtime from this informa-
tion.1 For most HVAC control loops, the derivative component
of the equation should be eliminated.
By Eric Utterson, P.E., Member ASHRAE
A

41
Controls
Application of the PID loop (and calculated Pgain, Itime
and Dtime) in this manner assumes that a rise in supply duct
pressure of 0.6 in. (149 Pa) results from a fan speed increase of
20%. Likewise, an increase of 40% in speed would result in a
pressure increase of 1.2 in. (299 Pa). A review of simple fan
laws reveals that supply duct pressure rise for a given fan speed
increase is not directly proportional.
Non-Linear Supply Duct Pressure Control. For the sup-
ply duct pressure control scenario, the traditional method of
control is to adjust fan speed to maintain the supply duct
pressure setpoint (Figure 2). Fan laws state that supply duct
pressure is related to more than supply fan speed increase
(which is actually only a resulting system response to the
change in variable system components). Supply duct pres-
sure also is related to supply airflow rate, supply duct pres-
sure losses and supply fan speed.
Suppose the open loop test described in Figure 1 was per-
formed when all of the VAV box control dampers are fully opened.
From this information, it is possible to calculate the supply duct
pressure loss coefficient. With all VAV box control dampers fully
open, what happens when fan speed is increased from 50% to
80%? Using the earlier example, the PID terms were calculated
using the assumption that a 30% change in fan speed will result
in a supply duct pressure increase of 0.9 in. w.c. (224 Pa). How-
ever, fan laws reveal that supply duct pressure will increase to
2.6 in. w.c.(647 Pa) (an increase of 1.6 in. w.c. [398 Pa] compared
to the predicted 0.9 in. w.c. [224 Pa]).
Next, suppose that all of the VAV box control dampers are
throttled such that the supply duct pressure loss coefficient is
increased. Now, a supply fan speed of 30% may produce a
supply duct static pressure of 0.9 in. w.c. (224 Pa). A fan speed
increase from 30% to 50% results in supply duct pressure of
2.5 in. w.c. (622 Pa) (an increase of 1.6 in. w.c. [398 Pa]) A fan
speed increase from 50% to 100% causes a supply duct pres-
sure increase to 10 in. w.c. (2488 Pa) (an increase of 7.5 in. w.c.
[1866 Pa]) This example shows that depending on several fac-
tors, supply duct pressure increases as little as 0.03 in. w.c. (7.5
Pa) per 1% fan speed increase and as much as 0.15 in. w.c. (37
Pa) increase per 1% fan speed increase.
Non-Linear Economizer Damper Control. The traditional
method of controlling discharge air temperature with the
economizer dampers is to modulate the outside air damper and
return air damper in unison to meet the discharge air tempera-
ture setpoint. While this relationship at first appears linear,
closer examination reveals that outside air temperature (OAT)
and return air temperature (RAT) have a dramatic effect on the
linearity of this control process.
Suppose a PID loop is tuned when the outside air tempera-
ture is 50°F (10°C) and return air temperature is 70°F (21°C).
When the economizer damper is 100% open, discharge air
temperature will be 50°F (10°C) (not including the effects of
fan heat gain). When the economizer damper is 0% open, dis-
charge air temperature will be 70°F (21°C). Discharge air tem-
Linearization Technique
Developing linear PID input and setpoint equations
for a particular process requires no more knowledge
than was originally used to select system equipment.
Given the following example, one can apply basic engi-
neering approximations to select the appropriate con-
trol input equations.
Suppose you are controlling condenser water tempera-
ture leaving a cooling tower by adjusting the cooling tower
fan speed. The cooling tower is connected to three chillers
and multiple computer room air-conditioning systems. The
amount of water flowing over the cooling tower is not
known. At this point in time, the condenser water tempera-
ture entering the cooling tower hot basin is 90°F (32°C).
The cooling tower leaving water temperature is 82.5°F
(28°C). The outside air temperature/humidity is 85°F (29°C),
50% RH. The leaving water temperature setpoint is 80°F
(27°C). The cooling tower fan speed is 30 Hz.
For a typical PID control loop, the only information that
is used is cooling tower leaving water temperature and
the associated setpoint. How well could you control this
process knowing only these two small pieces of infor-
mation? Would you increase fan speed by 0.6 Hz, 6 Hz
or 60 Hz? Information that is highly relevant in determin-
ing the proper control response is simply ignored by the
typical PID control loop.
A more intelligent way to guess the control response
would be to evaluate all the information given. We know
that fan speed is directly (not necessarily linear how-
ever) related to the amount of heat that is transferred
from the condenser water to outside air. We also know
that at the current fan speed, the cooling tower is trans-
ferring 75% of the required heat [%Q = (EWT – LWT) /
(EWT – LWTsp)]. Knowing these two pieces of informa-
tion, we can approximate the required fan speed by di-
viding the current fan speed by the fraction of required
heat: 30 Hz/75% = 40 Hz.
The combined equation would become the PID
setpoint equation:
(

(
)

PID Input = Fan Speed
Will 40 Hz produce exactly 80°F (27°C) condenser
water in the above example? Most likely not. There ex-
ists a more complex engineering approach using out-
side air conditions that produces better results. However,
coupled with the flexibility of the PID control loop, the
method outlined previously produces far better results
than the traditional PID control loop during periods of
high and low ambient wet-bulb temperatures.
42
ASHRAE Journal ashrae.org
March 2003
perature changes 0.2°F (0.11°C) per 1% economizer travel.
Next, suppose outside air temperature is 0°F (–18°C) and
return air temperature is 75°F (24°C). In this new scenario,
discharge air temperature can vary from 0°F to 75°F (–18°C to
24°C) (depending on economizer damper position). Discharge
air temperature changes 0.75°F (0.4°C) per 1% economizer
travel. In other words, when outside air temperature is 0°F
(–18°C), the controlled discharge temperature is more sensi-
tive to economizer damper position than when outside air tem-
perature is 50°F (10°C). If the PID terms are tuned using the
first case, control will be poor at times of lower outside air
temperature or higher return air temperature. The traditional
PID control loop does not account for this basic non-linearity.
Non-Linear Steam Heat Exchanger Leaving Water Tem-
perature Control. Leaving water temperature control of a steam
heat exchanger typically is controlled by modulating the steam
valve to maintain the leaving water temperature setpoint. For
variable flow heating systems, such as domestic hot water or
heating water control, the main source of non-linearity is the
unknown mass flow rate of water.
Consider the case when a steam heat exchanger has the capac-
ity to heat 100 gpm (6 L/s) of water from 100°F to 120°F (38°C
to 49°C). Suppose the PID loop is tuned when 100 gpm (6 L/s) is
flowing and the entering water temperature is 100°F (38°C). The
leaving water temperature can be as low as 100°F (38°C) and as
high at 120°F (49°C) when the steam valve is modulated from
fully closed to fully open. The traditional control system will be
tuned such that the leaving water temperature changes 0.2°F
(0.11°C) per 1% change in steam flow rate.
Next, suppose that the water flow rate is reduced to 10 gpm
(0.6 L/s) and entering water temperature is 100°F (38°C). For
this operation point, only 10% of the maximum steam flow
rate is required to raise the leaving water temperature to 120°F
(49°C). In other words, leaving water temperature changes 2°F
(1.1°C) per 1% change in steam flow rate. If the PID loop is
tuned when water flow rate is near the design quantity of 100
gpm (6 L/s), the control loop will have a tendency to hunt and
overshoot at lower water flow rates. For Figure 4, the optimal
PID terms for both sets of operating points (100 gpm vs. 10
gpm [6 vs. 0.6 L/s]) would vary by a factor of 10.
Linearization of the PID Input and Setpoint
Example 1: Linear Supply Duct Pressure Control. The
goal of the linearization process is to create PID input and
setpoint equations that describe the controlled process and
compensate for all known varying inputs. For supply duct pres-
sure control, the final linearized equation must apply for any
given supply airflow rate, supply duct pressure loss coeffi-
cient and supply fan speed. Equations 2–4 apply.

combined into Equation 5:

Using the PID input and setpoint equations listed earlier
and by performing a simple open loop test (Figure 1), Pgain,
Itime and Dtime can be determined. Moreover, for similar in-
stallations (such as return duct pressure control and hydronic
system pressure control), the same PID Input and setpoint equa-
tions and corresponding PID coefficients can be used without
any additional loop tuning. These equations only hold when
0% fan speed equals 0 Hz on a variable frequency drive (VFD).
Depending on the VFD manufacturer, a simple mathematical
correction is required to properly correlate fan speed com-
mand and actual fan speed.
Example 2: Economizer Damper Control. Similar to the
supply duct pressure control linearization method described
earlier, the goal of the economizer damper linearization pro-
cess is to calculate a PID input equation and PID setpoint
equation so that each compensates for the known non-
linearities. Equations 7 and 8 characterize economizer dis-
charge air temperature control.
CFMOA × OAT + CFMRA × RAT = CFMSA × MAT
(7)
CFMSA = CFMOA + CFMRA
(8)
where
CFMOA = Outside Airflow Rate
CFMRA = Return Airflow Rate
CFMSA = Supply Airflow Rate
OAT = Outside Air Temperature
RAT = Return Air Temperature
MAT = Mixed Air Temperature

The overall flexibility of the PID control loop algorithm
compensates for the remaining non-linearities including fan
heat and non-linear economizer damper operation.
Example 3: Linear Steam Heat Exchanger Leaving Wa-
ter Temperature Control. Linearization of the steam heat ex-
changer system requires measurement of either water flow rate
or entering water temperature. Because obtaining the water
temperature is less expensive and more reliable, the following
linearization method addresses the case where the entering
water temperature is known. Equations 14–17 are the funda-
mental equations that describe the system.

Depending on the type of control valve selected for this ap-
plication, further correction will be required through the DDC
to correlate the valve position to %Q. Many control valve manu-
facturers publish enough Cv data to develop correlation through
a mathematical regression technique. In general, however, %Q
can be approximated to equal valve position (%Q = valve com-
mand) if a valve with a linear flow characteristic is used.
Experimental Results
A simple way to demonstrate the performance improvement
associated with the linearization technique is to conduct a
comparison test between a hydronic system where a variable
speed pump is used to control differential pressure and an air
system where a variable speed fan is used to control duct pres-
sure. From a theoretical standpoint, both systems control simi-
larly. Furthermore, the linearization technique yields the same
PID input and setpoint equation for both systems. For these
reasons, the PID terms used on one system should work the
same on the other system.
Despite the similarities between the two systems, when us-
ing normal (non-linearized) PID control, the PID terms must be

Controls
March 2003
ASHRAE Journal
45
modified to obtain optimal results. It is not uncommon to find
instances where a loop-tuning technician incorrectly attempts
to use the same Pgain and Itime for both types of systems.
To demonstrate the power of linearization in PID loop con-
trol, an open loop test was conducted on a pumping system. The
results of this open loop test were used to determine the Pgain
and Itime for normal (non-linearized) PID control (without the
benefit of linearization) and for the linearized PID control. These
PID terms are listed along with setpoint change response results
in Figure 5, which demonstrates that when operating at the
tuned condition, both methods produce the same result.
Next, the same PID terms were installed on a supply fan
system. Figure 6 displays the performance of the supply fan
system for normal (non-linearized) PID loop control and for
the linearized method. From Figure 6, it is obvious that the
normal (non-linearized) PID terms used to control the pump-
ing system are not optimal for the supply fan. Control is slug-
gish and slow. However, when the linearized technique is used,
the results are nearly identical to the pumping system.
Conclusions
Most HVAC control processes can benefit from linearization.
This technique can be used for building pressure control, air-
flow damper control, heating and cooling coil control, second-
ary bypass valve control, humidity control, cooling tower fan
speed control and space temperature control. Most of these tech-
niques can be implemented using sensors that are specified for
diagnostic purposes.
All of the PID setpoint and input equations are dimension-
less quantities. Because of this, the PID terms can be trans-
ferred from one system to another. As engineers and PID loop
tuning technicians develop an understanding of when to ap-
ply the different PID input and setpoint equations, a list of
known good PID terms can be developed that can be trans-
ferred from building to building.
The ultimate goal of using this technique for control setup
is to reduce the need for costly PID loop tuning and to elimi-
nate the need for seasonal adjustments to PID terms (which,
though necessary, almost never happen in the real world). This
benefits the building owner in terms of better control and re-
duces the temperature control contractor’s PID loop tuning
time. In a larger sense, the use of this technique would permit
our industry to realize the full benefits of the PID loop tech-
nology available in DDC systems without the unrealistic de-
mands of proper loop tuning that are, in general, not being
achieved today.
References
1. St. Clair, D.W. 1995. Controller Tuning and Control Loop Perfor-
mance, 2nd Edition.

About the Author
Gerhard W. Knutson, Ph.D., CIH, is presi-
dent of Knutson Ventilation, Edina, Minn. He is
chairman voting designer of Standards Project
Committee 110-1995R, Method of Testing of
Laboratory Fume Hoods and is a member of TCs
5.4, 5.8, and 9.10.
he design of an odor control system must address both health stan-
dards or guidelines, and odor thresholds. The odor threshold “re-
fers to the theoretical minimum concentration of odorant stimulus
necessary for detection in some specified percentage of the popula-
tion, usually the mean. Threshold values are not fixed physiological
facts or physical constants but are a statistical point of representing the
best estimate values from a group of individual respondents.”1
The problem of odor perception is in-
tensified because detection levels vary
significantly among people, following a
log normal distribution. Odor control
usually addresses the “average” person.
Reasonable controls may still result in
complaints from individuals that have a
particularly acute sense of smell.
Health Concerns
Nearly every chemical has toxicologi-
cal properties. Many chemicals have an
OSHA standard (permissible exposure
limit or PEL2). Other sources of guid-
ance for acceptable exposure levels are
tion), Table 1 tabulates the PEL and odor
threshold of several common chemicals.
Controlling Odors
The first steps in controlling odors are
odor evaluation and establishing a de-
sign goal. The odor sources are deter-
mined by observation, evaluation of
industrial hygiene air samples, and lis-
tening to operator complaints. Although
operator comments, or occupant odor
complaints, are essential to evaluation,
two factors need to be weighed.
First, sensitivity to odors diminishes
over time. This phenomenon, called ol-
factory fatigue, means that the odor can
be sensed when first arriving at the facil-
ity. However, after a short time the nose
stops signaling the brain that it smells
something. Sometimes a worker stand-
By Gerhard W. Knutson, Ph.D., CIH, Member ASHRAE
T
threshold limit values (TLVs) estab-
lished by the American Conference of
Governmental Industrial Hygenists
(ACGIH).3 When no guideline has been
established, the designer should consult
an industrial hygienist or toxicologist
to conduct a hazard analysis and estab-
lish an occupation exposure limit (OEL)
for the chemical of concern.
Since the driving factor on some ex-
haust ventilation systems is odor, it is
important to note that odor and health
consequences are not correlated. Using
the PEL as an index of the potential tox-
icity of the chemical (an oversimplifica-
The following article was published in ASHRAE Journal, April 2003. © Copyright 2003 American Society of Heating, Refrigerating and Air-
Conditioning Engineers, Inc. It is presented for educational purposes only. This article may not be copied and/or distributed electronically or in
paper form without permission of ASHRAE.
April 2003
ASHRAE Journal
3 7
Odor
ing over the barrel of solvent does not notice the strong odor,
while a secretary in an adjoining office complains about a
fleeting odor.
Second, odors are subjective. A foundry man working in the
core room does not complain about the diethylamine (musty
fish) odor because it represents employment. On the other hand,
the hardware store next to the bakery could complain about
the (pleasant) odor of fresh bread.
As discussed earlier, design goals are dependent on health
levels (PEL, TLV, OEL, or other) and the odor threshold. The
design goal must be below the PEL, but may be above the
odor threshold.
Once the sources of odor have been identified, and a design
goal established, there are two basic approaches to controlling
odor: local exhaust ventilation (LEV) and general or dilution
ventilation. The two approaches are not exclusive and many
successful exhaust systems use a combination of the two.
Local Exhaust Ventilation
LEV uses a hood positioned near the operation along with
air movement to capture the contaminant (odorous compound)
as it is generated. The most critical aspect of an LEV system is
the hood. Hoods are frequently categorized as enclosing hoods,
capture hoods, and receiving hoods. In all cases, the first step
is to determine the capture velocity required to control the
airborne contaminate.
Capture Velocity
The “art” in hood design is to match the operation with the
proper hood. The initial phase consists of watching the opera-
tion to determine the tasks that must be accomplished and the
accessibility requirements for the hood. Building a mockup
hood can allow the operator to understand the constraints of
the hood. This goes a long way toward developing a hood that
will be acceptable.
It is important to discuss the hood and its operation with
maintenance personnel. An enclosing hood restricts access for
both the operator and maintenance personnel, and the designer
must understand the constraints. Failure to do so often will
result in a “night-shift” modification of the hood by a cutting
torch or by a forklift “inadvertently” hitting the hood.
Once the hood opening has been determined, a capture ve-
locity must be established. The capture velocity is the speed of
air moving toward the hood sufficient to capture and draw the
contaminant toward the hood. The capture velocity must be
sufficiently high to remove the gasses and particles of health
concern (the breathable particles are typically less than 50
micrometers in diameter). To overcome the settling velocity or
the diffusion rate, a capture velocity of 50 fpm (0.25 m/s) is
often sufficient. However, higher capture velocities are required
to overcome other factors. These factors include:
• Room air currents,
• Operators motion,
• Activities in the area,
• Toxicity or odor threshold,
• Momentum or airflow at generation, and
• Heat sources.
Although there are no golden numbers for the capture ve-
locity, many designers start with 50 to 100 fpm (0.25 to 0.5
m/s) and increase the capture velocity because of the previ-

Table 2: Range of capture velocities.4
Comments
1. The odor range reflects literature reporting odor panel results that met 13 spe-
cific criteria. The threshold is the geometric mean of the acceptable data.1
2. The odor threshold is independent of potential health aspects.
a. Some toxic chemicals do not have adequate warning characteristics.
b. Some chemicals have very strong odors at levels much below the stan-
dards and guidelines.
c. Some chemicals have a PEL within the range of the acceptable odor
thresholds.
3. The odor threshold is not a tight value but may have a range of uncertainty. This
is especially true as one observes the range of acceptable odor thresholds.
4. In odor control, the designer must carefully examine the health and odor char-
acteristics of the chemical.
ously mentioned factors. Energy conservation often requires
the designer to control the adverse factors rather than increase
the ventilation rate.
Most recommendations for capture velocities and hood
design (e.g., Industrial Ventilation: A Manual of Recom-
mended Practices,4 or the “Vent Manual,” which lists capture
velocities as shown in Table 2) are for rather “ordinary” haz-
ardous materials with a PEL range of 50 to 500 ppm. All other
factors being equal, chemicals with lower PELs require a
greater degree of capture by the hood. This can result in us-
ing the upper range of capture velocities from Table 2 for
chemicals with low PELs.
Using a capture velocity, V, and the area over which the cap-
ture velocity must work, A, the volumetric flow, Q, is given by
the continuity equation Q = VA. Figure 1 shows four simple
examples. In each case, the geometric figure is idealized. The
end of a duct is considered a point sink, the slots are treated as
a line, and frictional resistance and the momentum of air are
neglected. The formulas approximate the actual flow and serve
as illustrative examples.
In the first case, a plane duct (sometimes called an elephant
trunk) is simulated. Air flows toward the opening much as mat-
ter falls into a black hole. Under ideal conditions, air of equal
velocity will form concentric spheres. If the capture velocity is
100 fpm (0.5 m/s) and the distance is 2 ft (0.6 m), the required
volumetric flow can be calculated by determining the area. In
this simple case, the area is the surface of a sphere. (In the real
world, the sphere would be distorted by the duct.) The area of a
sphere of radius R = 2 ft (0.6 m), is given by:

Similar examples can be determined from the half sphere (Q
= 0.5 × 4 π R2 × V), the face opening (Q = L × W × V, using the
face velocity as the capture velocity), the long slot (Q = 0.5 ×
2π R × L × V), and other similar geometric figures. Since the
formulas are derived from idealized figures, they should be
modified for end effects.
Hoods that are more realistic provide a greater challenge to
determine the equal velocity surfaces. The “Vent Manual”
provides some guidance by presenting several sketches (VP
prints) of exhaust hoods including suggested volumetric flow
rates criteria. Other suggestions are found in most industrial
ventilation textbooks. The designer must often extrapolate
from such sketches to determine the volumetric flow at a
particular hood.
Enclosing Hoods
The best method of controlling any airborne contaminant is
containment and isolation of the source. An enclosing hood
Table 1: PEL and odor threshold of common chemicals.
surrounds the generating equipment or the source of odor. A
small amount of ventilation exhausts the enclosure and pre-
vents the offending odors (or contaminants) from escaping.
The design parameter is to provide an inward airflow of 50 to
Odor
April 2003
ASHRAE Journal
3 9
150 fpm (0.25 to 0.75 m/s) through all openings. The lower
velocity is used for small openings like cracks and the larger
value is used for access openings. The volumetric flow will
depend on the openings in the enclosure. For a laboratory
hood, which is an enclosing hood, the opening is essentially
the face opening of the hood. However, the opening also in-
cludes the area under the airfoil and the space between the
hood header panel and the sash.
Figure 2 shows an enclosing hood at a “put and take” opera-
tion. Solids and liquids are measured and added to a container
for further processing. The critical values in determining the
ventilation rate for an enclosing hood are the open area and
the average “face” velocity. For energy conservation, the open-
ing is maintained as small as possible. In Figure 2, the upper
portion of the opening has a clear plastic panel to allow the
operator to view the process while reducing the size of the
opening. The front panel can be hinged to open during setup
and close during normal operations.
Capture Hoods
A capture hood draws air into the hood. The operation is
outside the hood itself. The hoods in Figure 1 are capture
hoods. Hoods that are more realistic have contours determined
by the shape of the capture hood. Complex hoods produce
complex contours. To design a capture hood, the shape of the
hood must be determined and the contours estimated. All work
must be conducted within the contour with the selected cap-
ture velocity.
Understanding the equal velocity contours allows the de-
signer to predict the hood performance and determine the re-
quired volumetric flow rate. Once the contour and capture
velocities are determined, the required exhaust volume is the
product of the two.
A similar approach can be used for a variety of hood shapes.

In industry, many types of capture hoods are used. Figure 4
shows a capture hood at the charge port of a reactor or vessel.
Other examples include, lateral exhaust hoods on plating tanks,
hopper or equipment charge hoods, bag filling, packing sta-
tions, drum loading, to mention a few.
Receiving Hoods
The most used (and most misunderstood) hood type is a
receiving hood. When the operation generates a contaminant
that has some momentum, the hood can be designed to receive
the induced airflow. For example, a kiln generates heat, odor,
4 0
ASHRAE Journal ashrae.org
April 2003
and contaminants, depending on the glazes and composition
of the clay. The hot air rises and can be captured by an over-
head canopy. Figure 5 shows a receiving hood (a close-fitting
canopy hood) above a kiln.
Because of the simplicity of the canopy hood, it tends to be
overused. Photograph 1 shows a canopy hood used above two
autoclaves. As the organic material in the autoclave is heated,
some very foul odors are generated. When the door to the
autoclave is opened, the foul odors escape. The overhead
canopy is intended to capture the warm gas released when the
autoclave door is opened. In the photograph, the hood is so
high above the door that it cannot be effective. Room air cur-
rents will disturb the rising air and make the hood ineffective.
In addition, as the odorous air escapes from the open door, it
expands as it rises. This expansion reduces the effectiveness of
the hood. The hood would have been much more effective if
the hood were designed around the door and lowered to just an
inch or two above the door. Administrative practices, cracking
the door and letting it vent, can also improve performance.
Three problems frequently encountered with canopy hoods
are:
1) Often, a canopy hoods acts as a capture hood rather than
a receiving hood. Without some driving force, the hood must
provide the momentum to move the contaminated air.
2) The volumetric flow is often too low. Figure 64 shows the
design parameters for a canopy hood. The required exhaust
rate is given by
Q = 1.4PHV
Where
P is the perimeter of the tank or process
H is the height above the tank or process
V is the capture velocity
For relatively small systems, the canopy hood requires sig-
nificant volumetric flow. For example, a freestanding work-
bench 3 ft (1 m) on a side with a canopy 3 ft (1 m) above the
bench, would require a canopy about 65 in. (1.8 m) on a side.
For a capture velocity of 100 fpm (0.5 m/s), the system would

The required volumetric flow is much higher than the typi-

3) Frequently, the canopy is raised above the operation to
keep it out of the way. When this happens, the operator can
easily be placed between the operation and the hood. The
ventilation could then draw the contaminants through the
operator’s breathing zone, thereby increasing, rather than de-
creasing, the potential exposure.
General Ventilation
As an alternative to local exhaust ventilation, general venti-
lation provides control of odors and contaminates by diluting
the offending chemical contamination to acceptable levels.
The design includes both supply and exhaust ventilation. In
many cases, odor control by general or dilution ventilation is
not a reasonable approach. As shown in Table 1, many chemi-

cals have very low odor thresholds. Consequently, the dilu-
tion volume necessary to provide adequate control often is
very large. From an energy perspective, general ventilation is
not effective.
In general ventilation, the designer works with both the
exhaust and supply system. Depending on the geometry of
the workroom, the design can use a turbulent approach with
significant mixing. Alternatively, the exhaust can be on one
side and the supply on the other, providing a plug flow that
sweeps the contaminated air across the room to the exhaust
fixtures.
Although general or dilution ventilation is not often used
to control odors, it can be used in conjunction with the ex-
haust ventilation system. Consider a sign painting opera-
tion. Local exhaust is used at the press and the drying rack.
However, the signs are moved outside the control of the local
exhaust ventilation. Since the exhaust air will require re-
placement, by designing the supply air (replacement air) to
introduce the air (such that it provides dilution ventilation
without challenging the local exhaust hoods), the local ex-
haust functions as the exhaust hoods for a dilution ventila-
tion system.
Air Emissions
Exhausting an odorous chemical from a manufacturing plant
causes the emission of the chemical to the atmosphere. As with
all exhaust systems, the designer must be aware of potential
emissions and constraints placed on ventilation systems by
federal and local environmental protection agencies. In addi-
tion, the location and design of the exhaust stack is critical to
reduce the potential of reentry of exhaust air.5
In addition, the odorous chemicals, at levels well below the
regulated quantities, may produce a neighborhood nuisance.
Consequently, the design often must consider reduction of
odors at the potential receptors. Two general approaches to
reduce the effect of the emissions are tall stacks for dispersion
and air cleaning devices for reducing the quantity of the emis-
sions.
Tall stacks do not reduce the quantity of the emission. They
provide a method of dispersion and dilution of the odorous air.
With adequate dilution of the odorant, the neighborhood nui-
sance can be minimized. In many cases, the required dilution
is difficult to achieve, even with tall stacks.
Alternatively, emission reduction can be achieved by air
cleaning devices. Because of the variable nature of odorants,
many different control devices are possible. The odorant can
be removed from the gas stream by adsorption (for example
activated carbon), absorption (scrubbers), condensation,
chemical reaction, oxidation, or even biological destruction
in biofilters.
References
1. American Industrial Hygiene Association (AIHA). 1989. Odor
Thresholds for Chemicals with Established Occupational Health Stan-
dards.
2. Occupational Safety and Health Administration (OSHA). 29 CFR,
Part 1910, Subpart Z (Table Z-1, Z-2, Z-3, and several sections for
specific compounds).
3. American Conference of Governmental Industrial Hygienists
(ACGIH). 2002. 2002 Threshold Limit Values for Chemical Substances
and Physical Agents.
4. American Conference of Governmental Industrial Hygienists
(ACGIH). 2001. Industrial Ventilation: A Manual of Recommended
Practices, 24th Edition.
5. 2001 ASHRAE Handbook—Fundamentals, chapter 16, Airflow
Around Buildings.

tion: A Manual of Recommended Practice, 24th Edition.

ASHRAE Journal ashrae.org

About the Authors
Warren L. Beeton is a vice president of refrig-
eration engineering and Hung M. Pham is a se-
nior lead engineer with Copeland, Sidney, Ohio.
The economized scroll compressor
cycle is similar to a two-stage cycle with
interstage cooling, but is accomplished
with a single scroll compressor as shown
in Figure 1a. The high-stage of compres-
sion is accomplished by extracting a
portion of the condenser liquid and ex-
panding it through a thermostatic expan-
sion valve (TXVi) into a counterflow
brazed-plate heat exchanger acting as a
subcooler. The superheated vapor is then
injected into an intermediate vapor in-
jection port in the scroll compressor as
shown in Figure 1b. The additional
subcooling increases the evaporator ca-
pacity by reducing its inlet enthalpy,
thereby increasing refrigeration effect
and decreasing mass and volume flow.
This capacity gain can be improved
by decreasing the injection pressure,
thereby increasing the second-stage in-
jection mass flow. The lowest injection
pressure achievable is governed by the
pressure drop in the injection passage
and the scroll porting location.
It is well known that the efficiency of
a two-stage cycle is higher than the con-
ventional single-stage delivering the
same capacity because the added capac-
ity from subcooling is achieved with less
power. This is because incremental va-
por created in the subcooling process is
compressed only from the higher
interstage pressure rather than from the
lower suction pressure. The interstage
cooling also decreases the first-stage suc-
tion pocket and increases the mass flow.
By integrating vapor injection into one
scroll compressor, the isentropic effi-
ciency is comparable for both stages, pro-
viding high efficiency without requiring
dedicated low-stage compressor optimi-
zation. The compressor designer has a
choice of injection location. A lower pres-
sure location gives more capacity gain
while a higher pressure location provides
more efficiency gain.
Since the added capacity achieved by
enhanced subcooling provides higher
enthalpy gain across the evaporator, the
compressor displacement required can be
reduced by the percentage enthalpy gain
for the same evaporator capacity. This
By Warren L. Beeton, Member ASHRAE, and Hung M. Pham, Associate Member ASHRAE
apor injection has been available on large commercial screw and
multistage centrifugal compressors, but not on small hermetic com-
pressors. The recent introduction of vapor-injected scroll compressors
allows the economized refrigeration cycle to be applied with individual
small compressors, and achieve the benefits of increased capacity and
efficiency. This article describes the economized vapor-injection (EVI)
scroll compressor concept, its key components, and the associated per-
formance and application aspects for commercial refrigeration systems.
V
The following article was published in ASHRAE Journal, April 2003. © Copyright 2003 American Society of Heating, Refrigerating and Air-
Conditioning Engineers, Inc. It is presented for educational purposes only. This article may not be copied and/or distributed electronically or in
paper form without permission of ASHRAE.

ASHRAE Journal
23
Compressors
reduction in displacement can provide a means for
capacity modulation at lower loads by turning off
the vapor injection circuit using a liquid-line or
vapor-line solenoid. This performance gain is more
significant than the passive liquid-suction heat
exchanger. It increases with pressure ratio, and is generally
better with the newer HFCs than HCFC-22 due to the more
favorable properties of the HFCs. These are the primary rea-
sons why the economized vapor-injected scroll compressor
cycle is especially suited for commercial refrigeration.
Subcooler Sizing Parameters
In addition to the vapor-injected scroll compressor, another
key component is the economizer heat exchanger (HX). Fig-
ure 2 depicts the parameters for sizing the HX for optimum
cycle performance and cost. This HX can be viewed simply as
the evaporator of the second-stage cycle, and is generally bet-
ter configured as counterflow instead of parallel flow. The key
parameter is its saturated injection temperature (SIT), which is
the saturated evaporator refrigerant temperature at its outlet
interstage pressure. The SIT is a function of the evaporator and
condenser pressures, and the mid-stage injection ratio as de-
fined by the compressor designer.
Since the HX pressure drop for most brazed-plate HX is very
low, the interstage pressure entering the scroll compressor va-
por injection port depends mainly on the pressure drop be-
tween the HX and the compressor injection port location. For
good performance, this vapor injection line pressure drop should
be minimized, preferably to less than 3°F (1.6°C) SIT-equiva-
lent. It is important that the diameter and length of this vapor
injection line (plus any other flow controls such as solenoid or
pressure regulator) should be sized for vapor not liquid.
In practice, the capacity gain calculation is somewhat a func-
tion of the HX size, so it is important to define the sizing
criteria. A simple approach to sizing the HX is to consider the
temperature difference (TD) between its liquid out tempera-
ture (LOT) and expanded vapor in temperature (VIT). The
smaller the TD, the larger the HX and vice versa. Thus, LOT is
derived based on an assumed TD and an experimentally de-
rived SIT. Then, for a given liquid inlet temperature (LIT),
which is basically the condenser outlet temperature, the
subcooling (SChx) across the HX can be determined as SChx =
LIT–LOT.
The last parameter is the HX vapor out temperature (VOT),
which is governed by the superheat (SH) control of the TXVi.
The HX capacity is determined as Qhx = Me\*(Hlit–Hlot),
which is the product of the evaporator mass flow and the
enthalpy difference across the HX liquid side. The injection
mass flow (Mi) can then be determined from the energy bal-
ance equation for the HX as shown in Figure 2a. A simplified
approach is to use the 10°F (5.5°C) design guideline as de-
scribed later.
A 10°F (5.5°C) TD is a good balance between HX perfor-
mance and cost, typically in the range of 90%+ HX effective-
ness. Higher TD results in underperformance. Lower TD requires
a larger area or more plates resulting in higher cost. Oversizing
at added cost is at the designer’s discretion depending on the
application need.
A 10°F (5.5°C) superheat (SH) is generally a good balance
to ensure stable TXVi control. Excessive SH means “starved”
HX performance. A flooded evaporator would provide “wet”
injection into the second-stage and increase power signifi-
cantly. Although it is known that true 0° SH is optimum, this is
difficult to achieve in practice without TXV hunting. The TXVi
should be sized to deliver Qhx. The system designer might be
able to size a fixed-expansion orifice to achieve 0° SH, but not
for all conditions. The ideal flow control is an electronic ex-

of a two-stage cycle is higher than
the conventional single-stage
delivering the same capacity.’

April 2003
pansion valve (EXV), but its added costs and controls/sensors
cannot always be justified.
A 10°F (5.5°C) condenser subcooling (SCcd) is generally a
reasonable minimum to ensure pure liquid entering the TXVi.
Flash gas entering the TXVi reduces the injection mass flow
because the HX would be “starved” of refrigerant. Higher SCcd
is not necessary since it would reduce the injection mass flow
(Mi) for the same HX. An example of varying the SC entering
the TXVi is to connect it to the LOT (downstream extraction)
instead of LIT (upstream extraction) as shown in Figure 2b.
The difference is that the HX liquid-side mass flow is now the
sum of the evaporator and injection flow, and the TXV inlet is
seeing the lower LOT. However, the energy balance equation is
still the same, thus the two plumbing configurations are ex-
pected to be comparable in terms of overall performance.
Using this 10°F (5.5°C) guideline for TD, SCcd, and SH, the
only key parameter remaining is the SIT, which is determined
experimentally as a function of Tevap and Tcond for a given
family of hardware designs and application approaches.
Performance
Figure 3a shows an example of % capacity and % EER gains
respectively for a R-404A scroll compressor versus a conven-
tional single-stage compressor at standard ARI low-tempera-
ture (–25°F/105°F) and medium-temperature (20°F/120°F)
compressor calorimeter tests. As can be seen, the gain is most
significant at low-temperatures (+41% capacity and +20% EER)
where the baseline single-stage compressor requires liquid in-
jection for acceptable discharge temperature. Even at the me-
dium-temperature condition, the gain is still significant (+27%
capacity and +10% EER) and attractive since most medium-
temperature applications today do not have subcooling capa-
bility without adding a compressor.
One important aspect is that these percentage gains are at the
standard ARI compressor rating condition of 65°F suction re-
turn gas (RG). This gain in percentage term is significantly higher
in actual systems since the base evaporator ∆h is smaller with
the evaporator outlet return gas being typically about 10°F su-
perheat instead of the constant 65°F RG as shown in Figure 3b.

25
On the opposite side, this percentage gain would be less for
systems with condenser subcooling higher than the standard
rating of 0°F since the base evaporator ∆h is larger. However,
for most refrigeration applications, the natural subcooling from
the condenser is typically fairly low with the use of a receiver.
In addition to performance gain, there are three other benefits.
First, the compressor-operating envelope is improved since
its discharge temperature is reduced from vapor injection, thus
replacing liquid injection for low-temperature applications
with much better efficiency. Figure 4 shows the envelope of
vapor-injected scroll compressors compared to standard scroll
compressors.
Second, it offers a means to increase or decrease compressor
capacity (modulation) with load as illustrated in Figure 5.
Modulation can be achieved by using the capacity gain at the
high-ambient full-load design
point, then turning it off on-de-
mand via a solenoid upstream of
the TXVi or at the compressor in-
jection port. In practice, since the
capacity gain decreases with
lower condensing temperature, it
provides a natural means of ca-
pacity reduction at part-load
lower-ambient conditions. Re-
ducing capacity at part-load im-
proves system efficiency versus a conventional system sized
for the same full-load capacity due to reduced operating pres-
sure ratio.
Since the vapor-injected scroll compressor is capable of
operating at lower condensing temperatures than typical re-
ciprocating compressors, this advantage can be exploited by
turning off vapor injection capacity at a condensing tempera-
ture as low as 40°F (4.4°C). Since the majority of the annual
hours as performed in a typical bin analysis is closer to the
minimum condensing temperature, vapor injection modula-
tion provides an efficiency gain on an annual basis.
Third, suction line pressure drop would be reduced com-
pared to a conventional cycle delivering the same capacity
since the suction flow is reduced with the higher enthalpy
across the evaporator. The suction line pressure drop reduction
can be significant since it is proportional to the square of the
flow. For example, a 40% reduction in evaporator flow would
reduce suction line pressure drop by 64%.
Impact of HFC Refrigerants
Since the economized scroll compressor cycle is more ben-
eficial with HFC refrigerants, it might be of interest to review
these HFC options. R-404A has been recognized as the zero
ozone-depleting potential HFC replacement for R-502, espe-
cially for low temperatures. However, it has a fairly high global
warming potential (GWP) of 3,260 and is about 14% less effi-
cient than R-22 in medium temperature applications. R-134a
could be a more efficient choice for medium temperatures
where its theoretical EER is
higher. However, its lower theo-
retical capacity requires about
65% and 100% larger compres-
sor displacement respectively at
medium temperatures and low
temperatures, resulting in higher
compressor cost. R-407C and R-
417A could be considered, but
their heat transfer properties are
worse than R-22, and the high
9°F (5°C) temperature glide poses higher pressure ratio and
service concerns. R-410A has been recognized as the leading
HFC for air conditioners and heat pumps due to its superior
heat transfer and pressure drop properties, and its GWP of 1,730
is 47% less than R-404A. The initial R-410A scroll experience
in the residential markets has been excellent as demonstrated
by better compressor reliability and sound.
From a GWP and efficiency standpoint, it seems R-410A
deserves further investigation. It is expected to closely match
R-22 system performance and appears to be a better choice
than R-404A, especially in light of the previously mentioned
benefits with the vapor-injected scroll compressor. Using R-
410A also would leverage residential market and service ex-

perience. Some advantages and disadvantages of R-410A vs.
R-404A are listed in Figure 6. Since the theoretical cycle EER
of these two HFCs is comparable, it is expected that the com-
pressor EER and economized performance could be designed
to be comparable thus leaving R-410A with the other system
benefits, namely heat transfer and pressure drop. The use of
vapor injection would solve the higher discharge temperature
disadvantage. Lastly, concerning the higher pressure, the rela-
tively lower Tcond together with the use of closed-coupled
system design may facilitate the redesign.
Applications
This section illustrates how the vapor-injected scroll com-
pressors can be applied for commercial refrigeration applica-
tions. Figure 7 shows a schematic of a typical multi-compressor
rack application in a supermarket. The disadvantages are (1)
the medium-temperature rack does not have any subcooling,
and (2) the low-temperature rack mechanical subcooling re-
quires a separate compressor from the medium-temperature

rack. In addition, the low-temperature compressors may re-
quire liquid injection.
Figure 8 shows a schematic of this application with vapor-
injected scroll compressors. Now both racks have independent
mechanical subcooling, and the low-temperature compressors
would not require any liquid injection. The capacity gain from
vapor injection would reduce the number of scroll compres-
sors required. In addition, the vapor injection can be modu-
lated via individual solenoids at each scroll injection port. It
is more cost-effective and easier to plumb with a single HX
sized to accommodate all compressors instead of individual
HXs.
By far, the most significant benefit would occur in single-
compressor applications where subcooling and modulation
do not exist today. Figure 9 shows the schematic for a single-
compressor system with either single-evaporator (self-con-
tained) or multi-evaporators (distributed). Since most
single-compressor systems today use pump-down cycling con-
trol due to lack of modulation, the benefits are significant and

Summary
Vapor injection offers a versatile means for extending ca-
pacity and efficiency and improving the operating envelope
for scroll compressors in commercial refrigeration applications.
The performance gains are significant and both system and
compressor design dependent. Vapor-injected scroll applica-
tions are system-oriented, which requires a good understand-
ing of system design and applications. It offers several new
potential applications such as supermarkets, marine contain-
ers, chillers, walk-ins, and closed-coupled systems and favor-
able performance with HFC refrigerants.

haute temperature.” Ecole Polytechnique Federale De Lausanne, Final
Report, June.
3. Diab, A., R. Tariq and J. Gephart. 1990. “Compressor technolo-
gies for low temperature applications of R-22.” Revue Int. Froid 14(1).
4. Andrews J.W., T.A. Butcher, and W.G. Wilhelm. 1989. “Test re-
sults on a supercharged compressor for commercial refrigeration.”
ASHRAE Transactions 95(2).
5. Domanski, P.A. 1995. “Theoretical evaluation of the vapor com-
pression cycle with a liquid-line/suction-line heat exchanger, econo-
mizer, and ejector.” NIST, Report 5606.

About the Author
Neil Moiseev is an associate principal with Cerami
& Associates, Inc., New York. He is a member of
TC 2.6, Sound and Vibration Control.
By Neil Moiseev, Member ASHRAE
or outdoor HVAC systems, noise generated by equipment such as
fans, air-cooled chillers, dry-coolers, pumps, and cooling towers
needs to be predicted at a receiver location. The factors that play a
role in predicting the ultimate sound level heard at a site from HVAC
equipment include application of manufacturers’ sound data, location
effects, source noise control, and outdoor sound propagation.
Manufacturers' Sound Data
Published Sound Data
Published Sound Data
Published Sound Data
Published Sound Data
Published Sound Data
The manufacturers’ data must be ob-
tained according to a published standard.
Such standards include, but are not lim-
ited to, those listed in Table 1.
Sound data can be given in terms of
sound power, which is the total sound
energy output of a source. This is analo-
gous to the total heat energy output of a
source. Sound data can also be given in
terms of sound pressure level, which is
the sound level at a specific distance
from a source. This is like the tempera-
ture in a room from a heat source.
The data can also be presented in terms
of sones. A sone is a psycho-acoustical
sound measurement that is not helpful
in an engineering evaluation because no
spectral data are presented. Sones are
helpful when comparing two or more
pieces of equipment.
An excellent explanation of manufac-
turers’ sound data and its correct usage is
found in Application of Manufacturers’
Sound Data.6
Potential Pitfalls
Potential Pitfalls
Potential Pitfalls
Potential Pitfalls
Potential Pitfalls
Many potential pitfalls need to be con-
sidered when using manufacturers’ data:
• Uncertainty of the data. Due to the
nature of sound and limitations of mea-
surement methods, a degree of uncer-
tainty exists in any sound data. Table 2
presents the allowable tolerances for Air
Movement and Control Association In-
ternational (AMCA) and Air-Condition-
ing and Refrigeration Institute (ARI) test
data. These tolerances represent is the
potential difference between the reported
data and the actual sound for a piece of
equipment. In practice, actual sound data
tend toward the upper limits.
• New equipment vs. used. The tested
equipment is likely in perfect working
order and in the middle range of manu-
facturing tolerances. The sound from
such a unit is likely less than that for a
unit that has been in the field for some
time or that varies substantially from
specifications.
F
