Noise
• Quality control (manufacturing tolerances). Differing
quality control protocols exist among HVAC equipment manu-
facturers:
• Reported data may have been obtained through mathemati-
cal estimations based on idealistic assumptions (and/or simi-
lar tested equipment) and then scaled for size, speed, pressure,
load, etc. Measured data at specific operating points of the
equipment are more reliable.
• Manufacturers have difficulty keeping sound data cur-
rent because of changes in equipment design, production
methods, parts suppliers, and test standards. Published data
may be from prototype units that differ substantially from
production models.
• Data may be reported only as a single number without
information about the sound spectrum, i.e., sound levels in
octave bands. Frequency information may not address lower
frequencies, e.g., data may be reported in 125 to 2,000 Hz, or
tonal components, such as blade pass frequency, are not given.
The directionality of a source can be significant. Cooling tow-
ers, chillers, and exhaust fans all have different sound levels
depending on which side of the equipment the data are taken.
Location Effects
Sound from a source depends on the location where it oper-
ates. Manufacturers’ data are taken in a reverberation chamber
for sound power, or in an open field, where the only reflecting
plane is the ground. In reality, the
equipment may be located near a
wall or in a corner near two walls,
which changes the resulting sound
levels. The most accurate way to
account for the presence of a wall is
to represent a wall surface by an image source located behind
the wall at the same distance as the actual source will be in
front of the wall. The simple way to model a wall is to add 3 dB
to the source sound data for each wall present.
Source Noise Control
Source noise control consists of several different measures:
• Inlet or exhaust silencers (sound traps);
• Enclosures around the equipment;
• Sound barrier wraps of equipment components; and
• Barriers around the equipment.
These measures are tools in the challenge of reducing the
noise from existing installations. Barriers are included as part
of source noise control since the designer has control over the
source location, but not usually at the receptor.
Silencers
Silencers
Silencers
Silencers
Silencers
Three different source components need to be taken into
account when predicting sound levels at a receiver: the ex-
haust system noise, the inlet system noise, and the sound radi-
ated by the equipment body or casing. Silencers are used to
reduce exhaust and inlet noise.
Silencers can consist of sheet metal boxes or pipes with
interior perforated baffles packed with a sound-absorbing me-
dium. Some duct silencers depend on non-fibrous means of
sound attenuation, such as resonators and discontinuities in
the flow stream. Other silencers consist of spiral fiberglass
baffles. Some simple silencers consist of sintered metals that
reduce discharge noise by reducing the air velocities from
exhausts, such as from an air jet.
The attenuation from these silencers is applied to the ex-
haust or inlet noise of the source before any other attenuation
is taken into account. The silencer reduction does not apply to
noise radiated from the equipment’s body.
Two considerations when using a silencer are pressure loss
imposed on the system and self-generated noise of flow through
the silencer. In general, if the pressure loss is kept to less than
0.25 in. w.c. (62 Pa), there will be no self-generated noise issue.
Enclosures
Enclosures
Enclosures
Enclosures
Enclosures
To control noise radiated by the source’s body or casing, an
enclosure is built around the equipment. Openings for air intake
and exhaust must be provided. The enclosure may be constructed
Cooling towers
with sound barrier
wall and external
noise control.
3 0
ASHRAE Journal ashrae.org
April 2003
of 4 in. (102 mm) thick double-wall acoustical panels or may
be simple all-weather wood structures. Enclosure attenuation
applies only to the radiated noise, and not to the inlet and ex-
haust openings, which must be treated separately.
Sound Barrier Wraps
For some equipment, such as air-cooled condensers, there are
two noise sources: compressors and cooling fans. Sometimes
the problem is limited to pure tones generated by compressors.
In this case, a sound barrier wrap of loaded vinyl and fiberglass
or plastic foam composite material around the compressor can
be specified. Reductions of 6 to 10 dB can be achieved, but
only for the compressor component of a unit’s sound.
Barriers
At times a barrier can be the most cost-effective means of
reducing noise from a source, such as with air-cooled condens-
ers or cooling towers. Sometimes a barrier is used as an adjunct
to other source noise control measures, such as silencers.
Barriers are solid objects, usually walls, placed between the
source and potential receivers of the source’s noise. Figure 1
shows a typical barrier with a source and receiver. Barrier at-
tenuation is a function of the path difference (D) between the
direct path with no barrier (C) and the indirect path over the
barrier (A+B). When the receiver is in the area where no direct
sound reaches, then the receiver is in the shadow zone and a
minimum of 3 dB loss is achieved.
Based on diffraction theory, the approximate barrier attenu-
ation is calculated from the following equation:

As a general rule, the closer the source is to the barrier, the
greater the reduction. The barrier needs to break the line of
sight by a minimum of about 2 ft (0.6 m) to be effective. About
20 to 24 dB of attenuation is the maximum that can be nor-
mally obtained.
Outdoor Sound Propagation
Sound Radiation
The first issue in sound propagation is the different types of
sound radiators, which are point, line and plane sources. An
example of a point source is a small fan. Point source sound
radiates in three dimensions, in a sphere away from the source
as pictured in Figure 2. This results in 6 dB change per dou-
bling of distance. Every time the distance from a source
doubles, the sound level is 6 dB less. Sound loss from a point

source is proportional to the square of the distance. It is calcu-
lated as 20 times logarithm (distance to receiver over 20 times

The equation for a small non-directional point source placed

Several units in a row is a line source. Examples are lines of
cooling towers and roof exhaust fans, or thin louvers in a build-
ing face. Sound radiates from a line source in two dimensions
as a cylinder (Figure 3). The sound drops at 3 dB per doubling
of distance and is calculated as 10 times logarithm (distance to
receiver over distance from source). When the distance from
the source becomes greater than the length of the source, then
the point source equation should be used. A detailed discus-
sion of line source attenuation is found in Noise and Vibration
Control.8
A plane is any large surface, such as a cooling tower inlet, a
large louver wall, or a field of dry coolers. Figure 4 shows a
cooling tower. In the near field of a plane source, no attenua-
tion occurs with distance. When far enough away (about twice
the source dimensions), some attenuation occurs depending
on the frequency. This is difficult to predict and it is better not
to take into account, unless specific data are available from
the manufacturer. In the far field of a plane source (about four
or more times the average source dimensions), the 20 times
logarithm of the ratio of distances equation holds true.
Figure 5 shows a plane source that is 20 ft2 (1.9 m3) in size
and a person who is 5 ft (1.5 m) away. Another person is 50 ft
(15 m) away. Does the sound loss from one to the other follow

the 6 dB per doubling of distance and amount to 20 dB loss?
The answer is no. From the nearest person to the furthest
person transition from the near field to the far field occurs.
Depending on frequency it can be a 5 to 12 dB loss from 5 ft to
50 ft (1.5 m to 15 m). Unless you have manufacturers’ data at 5
ft and 50 ft (1.5 m and 15 m), this attenuation cannot be ac-
counted for accurately.
In the figure, another person is 100 ft (31 m) away. For him,
the sound level is 6 dB less than for the person at 50 ft (15 m)
away. Thus, to use the 6 dB per doubling of distance sound
attenuation rule, one must start with a sound level in the far
field or a minimum distance of twice the size of the source.
Landscaping: Tree or Vegetation Attenuation
Consider a situation where a noise source exists so someone
suggests planting a row of trees to attenuate the noise. This
will not help reduce the sound but may help to hide the source
from view. To significantly reduce the noise, at least a 66 ft (20
m) swath of trees would be needed
to block that line of sight. The In-
ternational Standards Organization
(ISO) publishes a standard,7 which
provides attenuation values (Table
3). These values only apply when
the foliage is very dense, i.e., it can-
not be seen through.
For less than 66 ft (20 m) there is
no significant attenuation. Beyond
660 ft (200 m) the tree attenuation
is affected by refraction effects of
the atmosphere — sound goes over
the trees.
Atmospheric Effects
Four important atmospheric effects are air absorption; wind
gradient (such as speed and turbulence), temperature gradients
(inversions and lapses), and non-uniform air temperature effects.
Air Absorption
Sound propagates (travels) by vibration of air molecules.
Air absorption of sound is a result of vibration of primarily
water vapor molecular bonds. Air absorption decreases with
increasing temperature and increasing humidity. It is greater at
higher frequencies than at low frequencies. But air absorption
only becomes important at frequencies above 2,000 Hz and
distances beyond 2,000 ft (0.6 km).
It is said that sound carries further on foggy or rainy days. To
some extent, that’s true. Foggy or rainy days are cooler with
higher humidity and, therefore, less air absorption. Actually,
sound appears to travel further on these days, generally be-
cause of lower background noise levels as a result of less hu-
man activity and natural occurrences such as wind. Therefore,
any generated sound tends to stands out.
Table 4 presents air absorption coefficients that are calcu-
lated using formulas from ISO 9613-28.8. The table is arranged
by octave frequency bands and gives the attenuation coeffi-
cients in dB per kilometer. The use of kilometers in the chart is
an indication that air absorption is not important for short
distances. The first part of the chart shows the effect of increas-
ing temperature and steady humidity. The degree of absorp-
tion goes up with frequency but drops with higher temperatures.
The second part of the chart shows the air absorption with
constant temperatures and increasing humidity. Again, attenua-
tion increases with frequency, but the attenuation is lower with
higher humidity. The last line shows the calculation for 2,000 ft
(0.6 km) at 59°F (15°C) and 50% RH. Changes less than a couple
of dB are not significant.
Wind
Wind can play a role in sound propagation. Sound is higher
downwind of a source than upwind. Figure 6 illustrates the
effect downwind of a source. It
shows a small factory or labora-
tory with exhaust fans on the
roof and a person that will be
affected by the noise. The curve
to the right illustrates the wind
velocity profile, which is al-
ways higher at roof level and
lower at the ground.
The sound speed is faster at
roof level than at ground level
since the wind speed adds to
the sound speed. As a result of
the increased speed, the sound
bends down toward the ground,
focusing on the person. With steady winds of 20 mph (32 km/
h) there can be increases of up to 10 dB. Areas where this
occurs frequently are coastline areas, plains, and at the end of
valleys. For example, when working on a project in Pennsyl-
vania, our design team noticed strong winds coming up the
valley when conducting the ambient noise surveys. Natu-
rally, residences were downwind of the site. By taking into
account the wind effect, the team was able to design the proper
noise control measures and residents did not complain about
noise levels.
In the case of a source downwind of the receiver, the source
noise is attenuated to the same degree. Under this upwind con-
dition the sound waves are bent upward, away from receivers.
Temperature Inversions
Normally temperature falls with increasing altitude (tem-
perature lapse). A temperature inversion occurs when at upper
levels the opposite happens and temperature rises with alti-
tude. At those times a pocket of warmer air can hover over a
location. This discontinuity in temperature bends sound back
Figure 6: Sound is higher downwind of a source.
Wind Effects
Sound Speed
+ Wind Speed = x
Sound Speed + Wind Speed = x
3 4
ASHRAE Journal ashrae.org
April 2003
to the ground, reinforcing the direct sound. This has a greater
effect on locations more distant from the sound source than in
the immediate vicinity. Under clear sky conditions a tempera-
ture lapse occurs during the daytime with the sound waves
being bent upwards and reducing
the sound levels. In the evening
and night, the sound waves bend
downward with the temperature
inversion, thereby increasing the
sound levels. A 10 dB difference
can easily occur between daytime
and nighttime conditions.
Figure 7 shows a factory and a
listener. The arrow to the right
shows the temperature gradient
with an inversion. The other arrows
show how sound is refracted down-
ward by the inversion and can in-
crease sound for the listener.
Temperature inversions can
happen anywhere but are more
frequent in deserts and along the
coast. Inversions occur on clear,
windless nights when radiation
cooling causes the ground to cool
faster than the air above.
A temperature inversion can
explain odd effects. Our design
team recently encountered an ex-
ample at a corporate campus in
central New Jersey. A building in the center of the campus has
noisy rooftop fans. At the site’s property lines, these fans are
not audible. However, on calm, clear nights, there have been
complaints about noise from the site from neighbors 0.5 miles
to 1 mile (0.8 km to 1.6 km) away. This is most likely due to a
temperature inversion.
Non-Uniform Air Temperature Effects
Temperature effects are caused by the non-uniform nature of
air temperature between any source and receiver. Air in one
place will be a degree or two higher, or lower, than the adjacent
space. This increases sound absorption and reflection effects.
It is not predictable but it’s always in the designer’s favor.
Ground Effects
Ground effects are simply absorption and scattering of sound
by the ground surface. Hard surfaces like parking lots and
bodies of water have little sound absorption, which is one
reason why sound is said to carry over water. (The other reason
is that it’s generally quieter on the water than it is on shore.)
Ground effects have a greater impact on sound from short
sources than from tall sources because there is much more
interaction between the sound waves and the ground. This is
illustrated in Figure 8, which shows a building with an air-
cooled condenser at the ground level and someone in a grassy
area. The sound from the source strikes the grassy area and is
partly absorbed.
For a tall source with the con-
denser on the roof, the sound will
travel directly to the receiver with
little interaction with the ground.
No sound absorption takes place.
A series of graphs in ISO 9613-
Part 27 give the attenuation for
different height sources at differ-
ent frequencies for various dis-
tances.
Summary
Predicting the noise of HVAC
equipment in outdoor locations
must apply manufacturers’ sound
levels properly and take into ac-
count the location of the equip-
ment and source noise control
measures. Distance attenuation
must be considered when the
equipment are point, line, or plane
sources. Air absorption and
ground can affect sound propaga-
tion. Finally, anomalous sound
levels can result from strong, steady
winds and temperature inversions.
With some experience and the use of the reference materi-
als discussed here, the impact of outdoor HVAC equipment
can be predicted.

About the Author
William Ryan, Ph.D., P.E., is senior researcher at
the Energy Resources Center/Midwest CHP Ap-
plications Center, University of Illinois at Chicago.
t the beginning of 2002, new technologies for gas cooling and
cogeneration systems were emerged from the laboratory and
the minds of designers. Today, some of these systems have been
installed and are being tested, including residential, light commercial,
and central chiller plant systems. This article discusses why there is
so much interest in these systems and some of the demonstrations
that are currently underway.
are designed with two independent
single-zone systems, often one upstairs
and another downstairs. However, by the
time the home has increased to 4,000 ft2
(372 m2), four or more zones may be re-
quired. At this point, the conventional
design becomes complex and expensive
with four or more independent condens-
ing units decorating each wall of the
home and operating noisily. Clearly,
some other system that addresses the
needs of these large homes is required.
Commercial HVAC systems provide
some design and equipment direction.
Novel residential systems that have come
to market for larger homes include: air
systems with power zoned air valves
(similar to VAV systems), multi-zone, mul-
tiple evaporator refrigerant-based fan
coil systems, and electric chilled water
systems with multiple fan coils. All of
these systems are more expensive than
single-zone systems, but provide greater
comfort. Zoned hydronic heating sys-
tems, whether conventional or in-floor
hydronic, provide multi-zone heating
comfort but cannot handle cooling.
Gas Air Conditioners
Small gas air conditioners, produced
in large numbers in the 1960s, operated
on an ammonia water absorption cycle.
By code, ammonia is not allowed within
residences in any quantity. Therefore,
these systems have always been outdoor
air-cooled chillers that delivered chilled
water to indoor cooling coils.
Such systems are at a distinct first-cost
disadvantage relative to a simple direct
expansion system for small 3-ton (10.5
kWc) single-zone homes. Although these
gas air-conditioning systems often were
cheaper to operate, the savings were gen-
erally not enough to justify the added
first cost and gas air conditioning fell
from common usage.
The revival of interest in gas air con-
ditioning is, in part, due to the benefits
Large Residential Applications
Since 1970, the average new home size
has grown from less than 1,600 ft2 (149
m2) to more than 2,200 ft2 (204 m2). More
than 10% of all new single-family de-
tached homes built today are more than
4,000 ft2 (372 m2).
These larger homes are at the high end
of the luxury market, where “high com-
fort” heating and cooling options are re-
quired. In addition, these large homes
face some of the same HVAC design is-
sues as small commercial buildings.
Traditionally, homes have been treated
as a single-zone system with heating and
cooling operating on one thermostat.
When a large home is designed in this
way, uneven temperature distribution
can become extreme. For this reason,
many homes above 2,000 ft2 (186 m2)

19
Gas Cooling
of chilled water. By providing cooling
with chilled water, the systems can be
zoned for the high-end residential mar-
ket (Figure 1). Adding a heating boiler
can provide a fully zonable hydronic
heating and cooling solution. In many
cases, this hydronic approach is close to
the first cost of “high-end” zoned elec-
tric systems, while still maintaining op-
erating cost advantages (see sidebar, “A
California Test Case”).
Another Approach to Gas Cooling
Taking the technology to a more ad-
vanced stage, a field demonstration in
Ohio is operating a “microcogeneration”
system, teaming a microturbine with a
gas air conditioner converted to run on
the microturbine’s exhaust (Figures 2, 3
and 4).
One novel aspect of this particular
chiller is that it can be driven by either
the exhaust gas or by direct gas firing, or
a combination of the two. This provides
uninterrupted cooling even if the
microturbine exhaust is not available.
This is an important feature. If the gen-
erator is down for maintenance, the elec-
tric load switches to the utility without
interfering with building operation.
However, owners expect continuous cool-
ing operation. Providing two ways to fire
the cooling system increases reliability.
Commercial Cooling
Systems for larger light commercial
applications have been installed in
A California Test Case
located outdoors, and the system is con-
trolled to provide 55°F (13°C)chilled water
return water temperature, and 45°F (7°C)
supply water to the building. The 45°F (7°C)
water provides coil temperatures signifi-
cantly below those in most residential sys-
tems, leading to improved dehumidification.
The chilled water used in these systems
is a combination of water and glycol that is
left in the system at all times, providing
freeze protection. Such systems have been
run in much colder climates such as Chi-
cago or Boston.
The ammonia-water absorption units used
in this application have no crystallization is-
sues. These particular chillers are designed
for minimal capacity deterioration at higher
temperatures, with only a 6% decrease in
listed capacity at 105°F (41°C) and 10% at
115°F (46°C), albeit with an increase in chilled
water supply temperature. This is less of a
capacity reduction at high temperatures
than standard electric units, and should al-
low less oversizing in hot desert climates, a
concern in much of California.
One interesting feature of the installation
was that the home was completed before
the air conditioning was installed. The chilled
water connections from the outdoor units to
the copper piping system in the attic were
done with a flexible plastic piping. This al-
lowed the chilled water lines to be “snaked”
through existing walls without demolition.
Although this particular application is in
an unusually large home, such showcase
applications may pave the way for appli-
cations of this system in the 3.2 million homes
in the United States that are more than
4,000 ft2 (372 m2).
A demonstration of a high-end zoned gas
cooling system is being run in California.
Data monitoring is under sponsorship from
the Gas Technology Institute Cooling Solu-
tions Collaborative Research Program. Its
interest is in the potential for gas cooling to
reduce peak summer electric demand on
generating and transmission systems.
The California residence is a 6,000 ft2
(557 m2), two-story home that has been
split into four major zones. Separate zones
were installed for 1) upstairs bedrooms, 2)
a family room-kitchen area, 3) an office area,
and 4) the bathroom areas. Each zone is
controlled by a different air handler located
in the attic space.
The air handlers are equipped with sepa-
rate heating and cooling coils. Hot water is
supplied by small water heating packages
with hydronic heating circulating pumps,
each located at the air handler. The chilled
water system is supplied by two gas-fired
absorption air conditioners located together
outside the home.
Each air handler is controlled by the zone
thermostat and set up with three-way valves
on a bypass system. Controls lock the en-
tire system into either heating or cooling.
Three of the four zones are standard air-
flow systems. The fourth system in the of-
fice area is a high-pressure air system with
compact ductwork.
Four separate bathroom areas are each
controlled by separate small fan/coil units,
bringing the total air handlers to seven.
Outdoors, two 5-ton (17.5 kWc) gas air-
conditioning units are linked to act as one 10-
ton (33 kWc) chiller. At low load, only one of
the air conditioners serves the entire chilled
water system, while the other unit assists at
higher loads.
Making the system even more respon-
sive are the chiller’s air-cooled condenser
cooling fans. Each unit has two fans, both
capable of two-speed operation. Controlled
by condenser pressure, the fans are cycled
on and the speed controlled in sequence,
allowing each condenser to have four dif-
ferent possible airflows.
Pumps for the chilled water system are
California home is split into 4 zones.
‘Traditionally, homes have
been treated as a single-
zone system.... When a
large home is designed in
this way, uneven tempera-
ture distribution can be-
come extreme.’
20
ASHRAE Journal ashrae.org
April 2003
Mexico, New York, and Boston.
These systems deliver chilled wa-
ter from multiple chillers all oper-
ating in sequence to meet varying
building loads.
Larger Applications
A larger California cooling
plant is taking an entirely differ-
ent approach to gas cooling. Cali-
fornia has some of the highest
electric prices in the country and
is still facing a tight electric sup-
ply situation during hot weather.
Gas chillers remove the chiller
load from the electric grid. Elimi-
nating both the cooling system and
the attendant accessories from the
electric grid, and tying these sys-
tems together for greater efficiency
improves the situation further. This
is just what has been done in one
smaller central plant in California.
The system is shown in Figure 5.
Inside the cooling plant, a
microturbine generates just
enough electricity to operate the
plant’s chilled water pump, chiller
solution pump and controls, and
cooling tower pump and fan. The
exhaust heat from the microturbine
passes to a double effect direct
fired absorption chiller.
This chiller, unlike units dis-
cussed previously, is of the more typical lithium bromide solu-
tion water-cooled variety. The unit used in this plant is a
customized version of a direct-fired double effect absorber.
The modifications allow the chiller to, once again, use a com-
bination of microturbine exhaust heat and direct gas firing.
However, this is a much larger capacity unit at 300 tons (1,050
kWc). The chiller is also a “chiller/heater” system, producing
hot water when cooling is not needed. This makes the plant a
self-contained, self-powered heating and cooling package.
The exhaust from the 60 kW microturbine is in the 550°F
(288°F) range and carries enough unburned oxygen to serve as
preheated combustion air for the chiller.
This layout resembles a cogeneration system. However, un-
like most cogeneration schemes, the electric load and the ther-
mal load are coincident as both follow the cooling load. As the
microturbine exhaust heat is well below the overall heat re-
quirements for this chiller, and the operation of the turbine and
chiller are coincident, the useful heat recovery can be well
above that seen in most cogeneration arrangements.
Looking at this system from the outside, this plant is a self-
contained gas cooling “system.” Gas goes in — chilled water
comes out. The system is installed as a freestanding central
plant, feeding chilled water to other buildings.
The high-efficiency and self-contained nature of this sys-
tem may be attractive for third-party owner or leasing arrange-
ments. The system can also be viewed as a cogeneration option
that requires none of the complexities or expense of grid inter-
connection.
The chiller/microturbine plant began operation late Novem-
ber 2001 (Figure 6). In this particular application, the
microturbine can also send power to other buildings when
cooling is not required, allowing the microturbine to operate
full time. After some initial compatibility problems with other
controls systems at the site were settled, the turbine reached
5,400 hours of operation last July without incident.
The chiller, which operates only as the cooling load occurs,

21
operated for fewer hours. It has experienced no mechanical
problems though controls required some fine adjustment in
midsummer.
The task of defining an overall efficiency for this plant is
unusual — the outputs can be both cooling and power. How-
ever, to focus on the cooling operation, the waste heat intro-
duced even by this comparatively small gas turbine contributed
significantly enough to allow the gas only COP of the direct
fired absorber to frequently exceed 1.2 in actual run-of-plant
operation.
Conclusions
New gas cooling technologies and system arrangements are
finding their way into new applications such as high comfort
residential systems, small cogeneration systems, and even us-
ing a “cogeneration approach” to produce a higher efficiency
central cooling plant. The common message is the need to
take new technologies to the market in novel cooling system
designs that can truly capture their benefits.
In the long run, these systems demonstrate new thinking for
thermally activated cooling, ready for a future where the most
desirable heat source may be a gas burner, the generator in a
cogeneration system, or solar.
Will these systems find wider application? There is no way
to know which direction the market will take, but in changing
times the greatest risk is the risk not taken. With changes con-
tinuing in the energy industry, believing that the current, of-
ten “cookie cutter” approach, to HVAC design can continue in
the future may be the biggest risk of all.

About the Authors
Robert Besant, P.Eng., is professor emeritus
and Carey Simonson, Ph.D., P.Eng., is assis-
tant professor at the University of Saskatchewan’s
Department of Mechanical Engineering.
hen is it cost effective to include air-to-air energy exchange?
Most HVAC engineers know that air-to-air devices for heat and
moisture exchange (e.g., heat exchangers and energy wheels) can be
used to significantly reduce utility cooling and heating loads and costs.
However, engineers often are not sure how the devices function and
how they can be assembled and sized for an HVAC system for maxi-
mum savings. In addition, what energy savings can be expected and
will these devices increase or decrease the total equipment cost?
to-air heat and moisture exchange be-
tween supply and exhaust air. The article
discussed how to integrate one air-to-air
exchanger (e.g., heat exchanger or en-
ergy wheel) into an HVAC system, main-
tain part-load control, do a net annual
total cost analysis and achieve a maxi-
mum benefit. This article is an extension
to this discussion. It includes all air-to-
air devices, which economically exploit
the waste energy sources in a building
into an HVAC system that may contain
one or more air-to-air exchangers as well
as conventional components for ventila-
tion and comfort control.
This type of design is well-known in
chemical process design as process en-
ergy integration for minimum utility
costs.2,3 It is achieved by exploiting
waste thermal or sensible energy from
one part of a process for use in another
part using heat exchangers placed and
sized to minimize utility costs. These
optimal designs identify heat exchang-
ers near the critical “pinch point” in the
process that maximizes savings. In in-
dustrial applications, the cost savings
due to energy reductions from reclaim-
ing waste hot and cold fluid streams in
optimal or near optimal heat exchanger
By Robert Besant, P.Eng., Fellow ASHRAE, and Carey Simonson, Ph.D., P.Eng., Associate Member ASHRAE
These are not simple questions because
many factors and constraints need to be
considered in the design of HVAC sys-
tems, and because the answers imply
more than a system that just functions
reliably to meet the peak summer and
winter loads. An HVAC system designed
for maximum benefit clearly implies us-
ing waste energy, trade-offs, high-perfor-
mance devices, and optimization.
Besant and Simonson1 discussed air-
to-air devices and topics related to air-

designs often exceeds 30% of the original energy used.
Supply air processing in HVAC systems for ventilation and
comfort often has fewer components than chemical processes.
However, it is more complex than chemical process design
since both air temperature and humidity must be controlled to
maintain thermal comfort and the HVAC system must function
well over a range of operating conditions.
Ventilation air is required for all indoor spaces that are likely
to be occupied.4 Some recent studies show strong linkages
among indoor air quality (IAQ), temperature, humidity and
occupant productivity.5,6 These studies imply that only meet-
ing the minimum ventilation requirements may not be in the
best interest of the building owner/operator, whose annual pay-
roll cost may be 20 times greater than the HVAC costs. Using
various air-to-air exchangers, it is now possible to maintain
good IAQ and comfort conditions while lowering the HVAC
system utility costs and the capital costs compared to conven-
tional HVAC design.
This article reviews various air-to-air ex-
changers and shows the best way to integrate
them into an HVAC system.
Heat Exchangers: Plates, Pipes, Wheels
and Runarounds
Heat exchangers transfer sensible energy
due to a temperature difference across the
exchanger. Air-to-air heat exchangers have
been used in HVAC applications for more than
100 years. Several types of air-to-air heat ex-
changers commonly are used for HVAC ven-
tilation applications: cross flow plates, heat
pipes, regenerative rotary heat wheels and aqueous glycol coil-
coupled runaround coils. Physically these low-cost devices,
listed in Table 1, are characterized by large heat exchange
surface areas per unit volume (i.e., 100 to 4000 m2/m3 [30 to
1,200 ft2/ft3]) and are manufactured using low-cost materials
that do not react with exhaust air or water vapor condensation.
Several constant or nearly constant factors are used to char-
acterize their performance for heat rate, pressure drop, exhaust
air transfer into the supply air, and supply air leakage lost. The
heat rate is characterized by the sensible effectiveness, εs
7,
which is defined at steady state as the ratio of heat rates:

The value of εs , which must lie between zero and one (0 ≤ εs
≤ 1.0) increases with the exchanger surface area and decreases
with the face velocity. For HVAC applications with balanced
supply and exhaust airflows, values above 0.85 may not be
economically justified. Table 1 shows typical ranges of εs for
each type of heat exchanger. For a given air-to-air heat ex-
changer, εs is determined in an independent testing laboratory
according to a specified standard.7
The pressure drop across an air-to-air heat exchanger does
not need to be the same for the supply and
exhaust airstreams. It needs to be specified
for both airstreams for each face velocity and
inlet temperature. Typical values are shown
for each type of heat exchanger in Table 1.
These values should be determined in an in-
dependent laboratory.
The exhaust air transfer to the supply air is
characterized by the exhaust air transfer ratio
(EATR), which represents the fraction of ex-
haust air that is transferred (recirculated) into
the supply air through the exchanger. A
smaller EATR is better than a larger one. How-
ever a moderate EATR may be acceptable in
many HVAC applications where the air supplied to a space is a
mixture of outdoor and recirculated air, though this may re-
quire that the ventilation air volume be increased to provide
the desired ventilation effect. The EATR essentially increases
the fraction of recirculated air supplied to the building space.
Therefore, in cleanrooms of hospitals and research labs, an
EATR above 0% may be unacceptable. For balanced mass flows,
it is defined as:

(Figure 1). It is best measured in an independent laboratory set
up to do testing according to the accepted standard.7 Typical
values of EATR are listed in Table 1.
The outside air correction factor (OACF) for the supply air
accounts for any leakage from the supply air duct at the air-to-
air device. For balanced mass flows, it is defined as:

4 4
ASHRAE Journal ashrae.org
April 2003
OACF is greater than 1, air is transferred from the supply inlet
side to the exhaust outlet side and the supply of outdoor air
must be increased accordingly to provide adequate outdoor
ventilation to the building space.
Condensation and/or frosting can occur in any air-to-air
energy exchanger under cold weather operating conditions
though different technologies can be more or less resistant.8 If
condensation may occur under system design conditions, a
condensation drain should be provided. If frosting would un-
acceptably compromise ventilation, or if equipment would be
damaged by frost, a frost control scheme should be provided.
Frost control can be achieved using several different methods
including supply air bypass or throttling, preheating the sup-
ply or exhaust air inlet flow, and speed control on rotary wheels.
The manufacturer should address the issue of frost control for
units sold for use in cold climates.
Regenerative Moisture Exchange
Desiccant-coated regenerative wheels for dehumidifying
moist air were patented first in 19749 and have been exten-
sively used in HVAC applications where waste heat can be
used for regeneration. Waste hot air, exhausted at temperatures
near or above the boiling temperature of water, is usually well-
suited for regenerating a rotary dehumidifying wheel.10 A va-
riety of desiccant coatings can be used, with each having
inherent advantages and disadvantages. Two common desic-
cants are 4A molecular sieve and silica gel.
In warm, humid weather, the first emphasis is put on water
vapor adsorption from the building supply air and desorption
or rejection of this water to the outside in the hot regeneration
exhaust airstream. A small purge section may be used to limit
any heat transfer into the supply airstream from the hot air-
stream. These desiccant-coated regenerative wheels adsorb
water vapor rather slowly. They are rotated at speeds usually
less than 1 rpm, and they require a large exchanger surface area
per unit mass flow rate of supply air.10
The hotter the regenerative air, the smaller the surface area
needed for regeneration, so it often occupies a smaller fraction
of the whole wheel face.
The coefficient of performance (COP) often is used to repre-
sent the performance of regenerative desiccant-coated dehu-
midifiers. COP is a ratio of the latent energy removed from the
supply airstream to the energy supplied to heat the exhaust
airstream. Typical values range from 0.4 to 1 as shown in Table
2. In addition, it is possible to define a performance factor in a
form similar to heat exchangers. The moisture transfer effec-
tiveness, εm, of regenerative dehumidifier wheels is given by

where ∆Ws = W1 – W2 = decrease in humidity ratio in the
supply airflow stream and ∆Wmax = W1 – W3 = maximum hu-
midity ratio difference in the exchanger (Figure 1). Since a
large temperature difference exists between airstreams, mois-
ture transfer effectiveness can exceed 100%, but significant
energy is required to achieve this as evidenced by the low
COP.
The other performance factors for heat exchangers (i.e., pres-
sure drop, EATR and OACF) should also be used to fully char-
acterize these exchangers. Table 2 presents typical values for
these factors.
Desiccants are known to have a strong affinity for many
different gases and vapors in addition to water vapor.11 They
can be used in regenerative wheels for stripping some undesir-
able supply airborne contaminants such as volatile organic
compounds and perhaps some toxic gases and vapors. Select-
ing materials used in the desiccant coating becomes more com-
plex because the effectiveness for the removal of each gas or
vapor chemical depends on the type of desiccant and regen-
eration conditions. For some applications, more than one type
of desiccant may be used for the regenerative wheel coating,
so that it works well for both water vapor and some other gases.
If this is done, a unique effectiveness (εci similar to Equation
5) should be defined for each airborne chemical concentra-
tion, ci , which is to be removed from the supply air. By anal-
ogy it can be written as:

ASHRAE Journal

Heat and Moisture Exchange
A single rotary wheel exchanger that trans-
ferred both heat and moisture between the sup-
ply and exhaust airstreams was first patented
in 1978.12 Since the prime focus of HVAC sys-
tems is the control of indoor temperature and
humidity while providing adequate ventila-
tion, such a regenerative wheel could cool and
dehumidify the supply air in summer and heat
and humidify air in winter using exhaust air as
the sink or source for heat and moisture. The
potential savings for cooling and dehumidify-
ing the supply air in summer using a total en-
ergy wheel is often sufficiently large to reduce
not only the utility costs but also the capacity
required for the chiller and boiler.
The rotational speed of most desiccant-
coated energy wheels ranges from 20 to 60 rpm,
which is necessary to obtain a good heat trans-
fer effectiveness (i.e., 60 < εs < 85%). There-
fore, the desiccant coatings must be able to
absorb moisture nearly 100 times faster than
for similar desiccant dehumidifying wheels
that operate near their optimal speed of less
than 1 rpm.
Table 2 includes the performance factors for
energy wheels. These include all five for heat
exchangers and two more for latent energy or
moisture and total energy or enthalpy effec-
tiveness, giving a total of seven factors (i.e.,
three effectivenesses, two pressure drops, EATR,
and OACF). A further complicating consideration is that the
three effectivenesses will, in general, each have a different
value. These values will differ somewhat with each operating
condition. To avoid confusion, ARI Standard 106013 specifies
one operating condition for summer testing and another for
winter. These operating conditions are used for testing all cer-
tified exchangers.
For testing energy wheels, use the revised ANSI/ASHRAE
Standard 84-1991, Method of Testing Air-to-Air Heat Exchang-
ers and the ARI certification test conditions to minimize the
errors that can easily occur.14 For comparison with rotary de-
humidifiers, a recovered efficiency ratio (RER) is presented in
Table 2 for energy wheels. The recovered efficiency ratio (RER)
is defined in a similar manner to the energy efficiency ratio
(EER) as used for chillers — that is, RER equals the recovered
energy rate for the exchanger at ARI test conditions13 divided
by the sum of the total electrical power input for the fans,
auxiliary motors and heaters. Values of RER range from 40 to
100 for typical energy wheels.
Plate exchangers that can transfer water vapor as well as heat
have been available for many years. Two different types of
permeable exchange plates are known. The first type, which is
made from treated paper, allows water to pass through the sur-
face as a gas or liquid due to the partial vapor pressure differ-
ences in the supply and exhaust airstreams. It usually has a
moderate latent effectiveness (e.g., 30% to 40%). The second
type uses hydrophilic chemicals within a very thin permeable
plastic sheet to achieve a higher latent effectiveness. Ranges
of these performance factors are shown in Table 2.
System Integration
In the past, conventional HVAC systems usually contained
no air-to-air exchangers. All of the supply air heating, cooling
and moisture removal (or addition) was achieved using utility
energy. Today, well-designed HVAC systems often integrate
one or more air-to-air exchangers into processing the supply
air. Examples of such systems are shown in Figure 2 using
energy (e) and heat (h) exchangers and Figure 3 using a heat
(h) exchanger and a rotary desiccant dehumidifier for mois-
ture (m) exchange. In Figure 2 the only useful source of waste
airflow and energy is assumed to be the space return and ex-
haust air while Figure 3 implies an external hot waste air is

also available to regenerate the dehumidifier. Other sources of

tem and for any operating condition,
not all of these components will be
used. Some components may not be
necessary at all because they will not
be used at any time or comfort condi-
tions in the space can be met by other
means (e.g., direct space heat).
The HVAC system design process is
sequential and iterative starting with
the building requirements and the sum-
mer and winter design conditions for a
particular location. This design se-
quence is shown in the sidebar, “HVAC
System Design Process,” and it includes
the design objectives of minimum life-
cycle and first costs. The tradeoff or
weighting between the life-cycle and
first costs is a decision that should be
made by the building owner.
Example
The system design process is best il-
lustrated using a simple example. For a
particular new building in a specified
location, we will estimate the first de-
sign iteration, size the air-to-air ex-
changers, calculate the expected
savings in cooling and heating equipment capacity, and cal-
culate the expected annual energy savings as outlined in the
first three steps of Table 3. Costing of all the installed compo-
nents and computing the optimal design for the weighted mini-
mum first cost and life-cycle cost are not included due to the
need to use more data.

(c) Determination of properties in winter and summer: the
remaining temperatures and humidity ratios at each station in
Figure 2 are determined using energy balance equations. The
results for the winter and summer design conditions are in

but remain in the range 15 ≤ t6 ≤ 23°C (59 ≤ t6 ≤ 73°F). It is also
1. List all building requirements for the HVAC system and
constraints including each utility cost. Design a process for the
supply air that fully exploits the waste energy flows from the
exhaust air and other flow sources.
2. Estimate the properties at each station and the peak heat
and moisture exchange energy rates benefit at the summer and
winter design conditions using typical performance factors for
the air-to-air exchangers as in Tables 1 and 2. From these results,
estimate the remaining utility peak energy rates.
3. Estimate the utility annual energy loads and costs using an
estimate of part load and operating condition time duration.
4. Estimate the first or installed cost of all components from
known data.
5. Iterate on Steps 2, 3 and 4 using the above estimates and
manufacturer’s quoted prices, certified equipment performance
data, utility energy rate data, and perhaps simulation studies to
meet the design objectives of a weighted minimum life-cycle and
first costs.

ASHRAE Journal ashrae.org

assumed that the fan power input has a negligible effect on the
temperature of the air.
(d) Determination of energy rates in winter and summer.
From the equations in (a) we get the winter and summer peak
heating and cooling rates shown in Table 3.
Table 3 shows significant reductions in the required chiller
and boiler capacities when air-to-air energy exchangers are
installed. The saving in boiler capacity due to the air-to-air
exchangers exceeds the required boiler capacity to heat the
ventilation air at the design conditions. This means that at
both the winter and summer design conditions no heat is re-
quired to heat the air before it is supplied to the building space
at 16°C (61°F) (winter) and 15°C (59°F) (summer). Thus, the
heating coil can be eliminated from the air handling system.
(Still heat is needed to satisfy the space-heating load.) The
heat exchanger (h) need have been only 30% effective to in-
crease the temperature at Station 5 to the required supply air
inlet temperature of 15°C (59°F) in summer. Similarly, only a
55% effectiveness is required in the winter. Therefore, this heat
exchanger (h) must be controlled for part load during the en-
tire summer operational time and part of the winter.
The humidity ratio, W12 = 1.3 g/kg in winter, likely will lead
to slight frosting problems because the saturation humidity
ratio is 1.2 g/kg at t12 = –13°C (8.6°F). The energy wheel would
not likely have any operational problems at this condition
unless the outside air were supersaturated, in which case a
defrost strategy would be required.
(e) The remaining auxiliary heating and cooling for the
design conditions are implied in Table 3. The last column gives
the reduction in installed capacities for the design example. In
winter the boiler capacity can be reduced by 44% compared to
the base case with no air-to-air exchangers. In summer the
chiller capacity can be reduced by 52%.

Besant and Simonson1 show how this can be readily done
once the typical year outside air temperatures are rearranged
so that t1 increases monotonically from its lowest to its highest
value as a function of time duration.
Table 4 presents the results of such calculations for both
temperature and enthalpy for each of the ventilation air, the
space heating and cooling
needs and the totals. It shows
that the total heating energy
can be reduced by 56% in win-
ter and 100% in summer for a
total reduction of yearly heat-
ing energy of 64%. The total
chiller cooling energy can be
reduced by 31%. Table 4
shows, similar to Table 3, that
the air-to-air exchangers are able to eliminate the need to heat
ventilation air in the winter and reheat the ventilation air in
the summer. Therefore, the energy savings for ventilation air
heating are equal to the energy required to condition ventila-
tion air throughout the year in this example where the supply
temperature is 16°C (61°F) in the winter and 15°C (59°F) in
the summer. Since the supply air is always brought into the
space at temperatures above 16°C (61°F) during the heating
season, the baseboard space heating will drop compared to the
case of 16°C (61°F) space supply air temperature such that the
total thermal energy input will be fixed for any outdoor air
temperature. In this manner, supply air heat gained while the
outdoor temperature is less than 10°C (50°F) reduces the total
boiler space heat load provided the supply air remains less
than 23°C (73°F). Table 4 includes these savings.
The net extra fan power required with total fan efficiencies
of 50% as a result of introducing exchangers (e) and (h) and
eliminating the auxiliary heater (H) are about 180 W for the
supply air motor and 540 W for the exhaust. Without part load
by-pass for the exchangers, this extra power is essentially con-
stant for all the operating hours, giving an annual energy use
of about 6,300 kWh for the specified flow rates and assuming
a pressure drop of 200 Pa (0.8 in. w.g.) for each airstream across
each exchanger. The auxiliary power to rotate the energy and
heat wheels would be about 200 W and over the year the an-
nual energy use would be 1,750 kWh. The ratios of all the
energy saved over the year di-
vided by the extra energy in-
put for the fans and auxiliary
motors gives a seasonal recov-
ery efficiency ratio (SRER) of
18.8.
These significant reductions
in energy use can be related to
annual operating cost savings
using typical energy cost data.
If we assume net total heating energy costs provided to the
building are $0.05/kWh and the cooling energy costs are
$0.15/kWh, then the total annual energy costs will be $8,380
and $2,980 for the boiler without and with the air-to-air ex-
changers. For the chiller these corresponding costs will be
$21,300 and $14,800.
It can be observed from these results that:
(1)The energy gained by allowing the supply air tempera-
ture to increase from 15°C to 23°C (59°F to 73°F) as it is recov-
ered from the exhaust air is significant (i.e., 20% of the total
recovered energy).
(2)The energy saved for cooling would be only 31% of the
total energy used for cooling without air-to-air recovery in

spite of the fact that the cooling capacity reduction is ex-
pected to be 52% (Table 3). This result, which is a conse-
quence of the fact that, for cooling energy recovery, the
outside air enthalpy must be higher than the inside air en-
thalpy, means that a chiller, used in conjunction with air-to-
air recovery, would operate over a much narrower range of
operating conditions and would likely have a higher average
COP or SEER.
Discussion
In the example of the calculation of air-to-air energy recov-
ery in the HVAC system shown in Figure 2, one could ask
many “what ifs.” What if the heat exchanger was not included?
What if the system was 10 times bigger? What if the effective-
ness of the exchangers was 10% lower? What if there was a
failure of one of the exchangers? The answers to these ques-
tions are implied in this article and the example.
The air-to-air heat exchanger plays an important role for
both winter and summer operation just as the energy exchanger
does. Systems of any size are implied in this example, but
small systems may have a long payback period.
If either air-to-air exchanger has a significantly lower effec-
tiveness than what was specified in the design, the system will
not perform as expected and the peak design loads will not be
met. To avoid such equipment problems, the designer should
specify that only certified equipment should be used. If an air-
to-air exchanger fails it will be akin to the boiler or chiller
failing — it must be repaired or replaced if ventilation is to be
maintained, especially if the outside air conditions are near
the peak winter or summer conditions.
In the above example, the least installed first cost will most
likely include the energy (e) and heat (h) exchanger as shown
in Figure 2 and exclude the heating coil (H). Therefore, the
answer to the first question of the article (“When is it cost
effective to include air-to-air exchange?”) is “always” for
systems of equal or larger size than this example system in
Chicago, provided the air-to-air exchangers, like the boilers
and chillers, perform at least as well as specified.
Buildings in other climates and smaller capacity systems
may require a more detailed cost analysis. Total life-cycle
cost optimization for this system may allow the HVAC de-
signer to increase slightly the air-to-air exchanger effective-
ness and/or the outside airflow rate (
1
m& ) to further increase
the annual savings and/or the indoor air quality.
Conclusions
Several types of air-to-air heat and energy exchangers are
available for HVAC applications. The performance factors for
these devices are documented and can be accurately deter-
mined following the recently revised ANSI/ASHRAE Standard
84-1991 for exhaust-to-supply air energy exchange. Further,
manufacturers’ claims of exhaust-to-supply air exchange com-
ponent performance can be verified through ARI’s Standard
1060 Certification Program.13
HVAC systems with air-to-air exchangers need to be carefully
designed for the location of each device in the system, the win-
ter and summer design performance of each device, and the total
annual energy saved by the HVAC system. When this is done,
the example system in Chicago shows large reductions in the
required capacity of the boiler (44%) and chiller (52%) and the
elimination of any auxiliary heating in the supply air duct. The
resulting annual energy savings of 64% for the boiler and 31%
for the chiller lead to significant operating cost savings.

System & Component Diagnostics
By Detlef Westphalen, Ph.D., Member ASHRAE, Kurt W. Roth, Associate Member ASHRAE,
and James Brodrick, Ph.D., Member ASHRAE
agnostics depends on the frequency and severity of major fault
modes in typical equipment and the extent to which the diag-
nostic systems would eliminate or reduce operation in fault
modes. At present, accurate information allowing such an esti-
mate is not publicly available.
A handful of engineers and contractors interviewed about
diagnostics suggest that the energy “wasted” by inefficient op-
eration likely equals at least 20% to 30% of HVAC energy con-
sumption. The principal of a company that performs energy
conservation studies and designs energy improvements for com-
mercial buildings said that improper equipment operation, poor
installation, etc., are responsible for more than 20% of HVAC
energy consumption. In his experience, low-cost measures to
make equipment and systems operate properly — rather than
capital-intensive improvements — account for most of the en-
ergy savings that he has identified during his 20-year career.1
In particular, diagnostics offer substantial energy savings
potential for packaged rooftop units, which appear to have
high fault rates. One engineer suggested that more than 50%
of packaged rooftop units are not operating properly,2 while a
recent study found that most economizers do not function
properly.3 Another (two-year) study of the energy and demand
impacts of maintenance on packaged rooftop equipment4 found
that problems due to improper system installation did have a
significant negative impact, while maintenance-related items,
e.g., improper refrigerant charge and air filter change frequency,
had little energy impact. Table 2 summarizes the range of en-
ergy impact that could be attributed to different fault modes
for unitary air conditioners.5
On a whole-building scale, Claridge et al.6 measured sav-
ings of 14% to 33% for several medical office buildings with
a simple payback period (SPP) averaging about a year using
whole building diagnostics (WBD).
Market Factors
A range of implementation scenarios can be conceived for
system diagnostics for HVAC equipment, but the most promis-
ing approaches from the standpoints of successful operation
and marketplace acceptance remain unclear. The lowest cost
approach is to integrate diagnostic capability into a system’s
electronic controls, a standard practice for some HVAC equip-
ment (e.g., centrifugal chillers).
Even if diagnostics capability is integrated with equip-
ment controllers, it requires greater sophistication to address
HVAC system operational faults, since systems incorporate a
range of equipment types. Applying diagnostics to equip-
This is the second article covering one of several new en-
ergy-saving technologies evaluated in a recent U.S. Depart-
ment of Energy report. The complete report is at
www.eren.doe.gov/buildings/documents.
ystem diagnostics can be used to automatically identify
operation failures of HVAC equipment and systems. If
such systems can identify inefficient system performance
and alert building operators, the systems can be fixed sooner,
thus reducing the time of operating inefficiently or in failure
modes, thus saving energy.
A range of diagnostic systems have been proposed, re-
searched, developed, and/or commercialized for detection of
faults in commercial HVAC equipment and systems. The com-
mon thread in all of these systems is monitoring of equipment
to determine whether it is operating properly or needs service.
Some examples include:
1. Electronic controllers programmed for maximum and mini-
mum values of key control parameters, with notification of
alarm conditions.
2. Algorithms integrated into electronic controllers, or add-on
systems incorporating sensors and electronic processors, that col-
lect operating data and analyze equipment operating parameters.
3. Facilities connected to building energy management sys-
tems (BEMS) that monitor key operating parameters of major
equipment to ensure proper equipment operation. Variations
include easily viewable graphic displays, plotting of data
trends, comparison of actual and modeled building operation.
4. Computer programs that actively analyze building operat-
ing data from a BEMS to spot possible equipment malfunctions.
5. Enhanced communications interfaces to improve access
to data, including: BACnet® and other approaches to
interoperability of building equipment controls, private net-
works linking buildings to central management locations, and
wireless communications.
Table 1 offers some illustrative examples of the consider-
able work that has gone into developing HVAC diagnostics,
but is by no means exhaustive.
Energy Savings Potential
Although the literature reports few good estimates of energy
savings resulting from the use of automatic diagnostics, ex-
tensive anecdotal evidence exists regarding the number of
improperly operating HVAC systems. Similarly, there are sev-
eral specific examples of diagnostics applied to simulated
equipment failures. The energy savings potential of HVAC di-

·CHW Flow Reduction

Recognition, Expert

Conditioner
·Refrigerant Leak

Parameters14
HVAC,

etc.

Whole Building
Diagnostics6, 15, 16
ARMAX: Autoregressive moving average with exogenour input; ARX: Auto-
regressive with exogenous input; CHW: chilled water; CW: condenser water
ment without electronic control requires add-on systems con-
sisting of sensors, communications interfaces, and micropro-
cessors, thereby increasing costs.
It is difficult to quantify the benefit of automated system
diagnostics, as the rate and level of faults in actual equipment
varies significantly from one unit to another, creating a broad
range of SPPs. TIAX developed two estimates for the installa-
tion cost and economics of system diagnostics for a rooftop
unit serving a fast food restaurant. It found that the SPP ranged
from under a year to three years, with more complex systems
having a longer SPP.5
Several non-economic market barriers have impeded the adop-
tion of system diagnostics in the HVAC industry. In general, the
use of electronic controls is increasing but not yet dominant for
many equipment types. Hence, using all but the simplest diag-
nostic approaches requires separate systems, increasing system
cost and complexity. In addition, many stakeholders do not
recognize a need for automated diagnostics.

40
ASHRAE Journal ashrae.org
May 2003
he news is full of headlines such as “School to Close Again due to
Mold,”1 “Mold Forces NJ School to Close for Rest of Classes,”2
“Washoe Widens Probe on School Mold,”3 or “Moldy Madison School
Will Remain Closed Thursday.”4 Often, the mechanical systems or the
envelope is blamed for moldy conditions. However, the school’s op-
eration and maintenance plays a significant role in achieving and main-
taining the indoor environment. Effective operation and maintenance
starts at project inception and not at facility completion.
Key Design Issues
So, what is the solution? The simple
answer is that designers need to evaluate
their designs specifically for achieving
owner’s needs and provide documenta-
tion on their basis of design for the me-
chanical systems relative to the IAQ. The
longer answer is based on lessons learned
during the past 30 years with direct in-
volvement and responsibility for both
troubled and prosperous school systems.
The eight key design issues are:
1. Identify the owner,
2. Listen to the owner,
3. Understand O&M limitations (from
a financial and a staff capability stand-
point),
4. Avoid first-day syndrome,
5. Detail access for maintainability
and replacement lifespan,
6. Spend more time on controls,
7. Provide basis of design narratives,
and
8. Be aware of effect O&M has on stu-
dents.
Identify the Owner
Typically, the architect and engineer
have a contractual relationship with the
school district through a single contract-
ing or technical contact. This person of-
ten is considered the owner as they have
the authority to make decisions on be-
half of the school district and approve
By Robert Flach and Chad B. Dorgan, Ph.D., P.E., Member ASHRAE
To avoid association with a juicy
headline or lead news story, several key
issues must be covered during planning
and design phases for a successful
school operations and maintenance pro-
gram. Further, to better understand the
true impact of poor indoor air quality
(IAQ) in schools, information on the ef-
fect of IAQ on students is presented. Fi-
nally, the ever-changing needs of
schools are addressed through a discus-
sion of ongoing documentation and
training.
T
About the Authors
Robert Flach is chief financial officer for the
Scottsdale School District in Scottsdale, Ariz.
Chad B. Dorgan, Ph.D., P.E., is director, facili-
ties science and technology, for the Farnsworth
Group, Madison, Wis.
The following article was published in ASHRAE Journal, May 2003. © Copyright 2003 American
Society of Heating, Refrigerating and Air-Conditioning Engineers, Inc. It is presented for educa-
tional purposes only. This article may not be copied and/or distributed electronically or in paper
form without permission of ASHRAE.
May 2003
ASHRAE Journal
41
payment for services. However, who is the “owner” of a school?
Consider the principal, vice-principal, administrative staff,
teachers, parents, community groups, neighbors, operation and
maintenance personnel, and the taxpayers?
For a successful project, input from all of these entities as the
owner is required. Therefore, the first step of a school project is
to identify representatives of the owner. Owner-selected repre-
sentatives need to be able to properly and clearly communicate
their needs to the design team and the other representatives, as
well as convey the group’s decisions back to their individual
organizations. Representatives need to be able to make bind-
ing decisions and team compromises throughout the planning,
design, and construction phases. If the representative cannot
make decisions, then it is strongly encouraged to replace the
representative with someone who can work with the team.
Listen to the Owner
Do you really listen to the owner? When they say they
want good indoor air quality, do you immediately think about
ANSI/ASHRAE Standard 62-2001, Ventilation for Accept-
able Indoor Air Quality? As indoor air quality and construc-
tion are complex concepts to most lay people (school boards,
parent and teacher organizations, etc.), it is critical that the
owner express success criteria in terms that can be under-
stood by all. In the recent public review version of proposed
ASHRAE Guideline 0, The Commissioning Process, a method
called the nominal group technique5 helps the owner’s repre-
sentatives brainstorm needs and prioritize each issue quickly
and concisely.
The nominal group technique has five steps.
1. Develop questions. Questions should be broad in scope
to obtain a variety of viewpoints and encourage interaction.
Instead of asking, “What do you do in the school?” It is better
to ask, “What is required for a functional school?” The second
question focuses the attendees on the school as a whole and
helps them to look beyond their individual areas. Other poten-
tial questions are:
• What conditions are important to your comfort in an ideal
school?
• What activities generate pollutants in this facility?
• What must be accomplished for a successful project?
• What problems with previous projects should be avoided?
• How do we make this project more sustainable?
2. Present a question with five minutes quiet response.
The quiet response time enables attendees to quickly purge
their minds of their concerns. Any discussion at this period
will skew the results.
3. Record responses. Each person is asked to provide one
response with no discussion. Responses are recorded on a flip
chart in sequence until all responses are received. The no dis-
cussion rule holds down the Type A personalities (they cannot
control the meeting) and brings up the Type B personalities
(they are heard from).
4. Discuss responses. Each recorded response is reviewed
to clarify what was meant by the response. It is also possible to
combine similar responses at this time.
5. Individuals rank preferences. The last step is to have
each attendee provide a top five list of all the responses. Col-
lecting and collating these rankings quickly provides an un-
derstanding of the group’s needs, directions, and success criteria
for the project.
Understand O&M Capabilities
Budgetary limitations, the large number of schools, and lim-
ited local resources can restrict the operations and mainte-
nance staff’s ability to provide timely response and preventive
maintenance services. Understanding how the operations and
maintenance staff works, its capabilities, and technology skill
level will aid in designing a facility within the capability of
available resources.
The essential issues relative to the operations and mainte-
nance staff are:
• Will operations and maintenance be handled in-house or
by a service contract? A different approach is typically re-
quired for systems where a service contract is being used for
operations and maintenance. The primary reason for this is a
different person could show up at each visit and the period
between service visits is often longer. Therefore, the designs
42
ASHRAE Journal ashrae.org
May 2003
for service contracts need to be more robust and have consis-
tency from one system/building to the other. Further, how op-
erations and maintenance is accomplished may change
throughout the life of the facility.
• What is their knowledge level? In-house staff’s current
knowledge level can be determined through interviews and
interaction. For service contracts, an assumption of the worker’s
skill levels will need to be made and included in the service
contract agreement. Lower knowledge levels require more ro-
bust systems with a simple interface. While it is always impor-
tant to have a robust and simple-to-use system, this is required
for less knowledgeable staffs.
• What training will be provided? If the system being de-
signed is beyond the operations and maintenance staff’s cur-
rent abilities, then training is required. The training should
focus on the operation of the school as a whole and how to
troubleshoot potential problems.
• Understand the reason behind directives/design standards.
School districts typically have a list of directives/design stan-
dards they give to the design team indicating which manufac-
turers to use or what type of material to use. It is essential that
the reason behind the directive is understood so the design
team can provide the best solutions for the intent of the direc-
tives. For example, when the operations and maintenance staff
say they only wanted air-cooled condensers they really meant
that they wanted to keep the different types of systems to a
minimum throughout the school district for simplicity of op-
eration, training, and parts storage.
Avoid First Day Syndrome
First day syndrome occurs when the entire focus of the de-
sign team, contractors and the owner becomes the first day of
operation/occupancy. By focusing on the first day, long-term
requirements (30+ years) of a typical school building are for-
gotten and opportunities to significantly reduce operating costs
and improve maintainability are lost.
Therefore, an ongoing focus is needed on the life cycle
cost of a facility. For example, in a recent renovation project
the design team was so focused on how to replace and im-
prove the mechanical systems to meet code they forgot to
look at opportunities to improve the entire facility. By chang-
ing the focus, the new design replaced exterior walls, which
reduced the cooling load, thus eliminating approximately
80 perimeter fan coil units (load reduction from better
envelope resulted in smaller mechanical systems and elimi-
nated need for perimeter chilled water fan coils). This re-
sulted in reducing the operating costs by 40% and
maintenance costs by 50%.
Access for Maintenance and Replacement Lifespan
Space is at a premium in any school. The goal is often to
maximize classroom and learning space and minimize the
(perceived) non-usable space (mechanical rooms, electrical
rooms, etc.). In the short-term this approach appears to be the
best solution for a limited budget, but it severely hampers
the ability of the operations and maintenance staff to main-
tain the systems.
To minimize the potential for designing non-maintainable
systems, it is recommended that a clear maintenance space for
all equipment is indicated on the drawings. This enables for
quick review of the design documents and identification of
issues when changes do not affect the project’s cost or sched-

May 2003
ASHRAE Journal
43
ule. By showing the maintenance space, safety and ergonomic
concerns can be addressed and resolved quickly.
Further, it is important to understand when the various com-
ponents in a school will require replacement. If the replace-
ment period is less than 30 years, then provisions need to be
made to be able to easily remove and replace the component.
The primary reason for this is that major renovations on schools
are typically near the 30-year mark. Any replacements before
this will require working in an occupied school.
Spend More Time on Controls
Spend More Time on Controls
Spend More Time on Controls
Spend More Time on Controls
Spend More Time on Controls
The use of incomprehensible sequences of operation and
the lack of sensor and component calibration results in many
failed systems and problem schools. While the mechanical
engineer is the one with the best understanding of their in-
tentions for operating the
school systems, they rarely
provide anything more
than just a simple se-
quence of control and a
points listing. It is essen-
tial that the mechanical en-
gineer be paid to, and
takes on the responsibility
to, develop detailed logic
diagrams that specifically
address how the control
system is to operate. These
logic diagrams include all
hardware points as well as
the software logic of con-
trol from input to output. Figure 1 shows a sample logic
diagram for a duct-mounted humidifier.6
Further, it is critical that the operations and maintenance
staff be involved in the development and review of the logic
diagrams to provide their input and knowledge of what does
and does not work for the school district.
Provide Basis of Design Narratives
Provide Basis of Design Narratives
Provide Basis of Design Narratives
Provide Basis of Design Narratives
Provide Basis of Design Narratives
Although few lay people can understand architectural and
engineering drawings, design professionals use these draw-
ings to explain to the owner how the success criteria were
achieved. Therefore, instead of receiving well-focused review
comments, many comments only address what the owner’s rep-
resentatives could or could not understand.
To enable the owner to better understand the project and
how the design professionals achieved their success criteria,
it is essential that the design professionals clearly document
their basis of design in a narrative format. This includes brief
descriptions of the evaluated options and why the final op-
tion was selected. By having this information documented
in an easy to understand format, the owner can see what was
done, why it was done, and how the school will be successful
upon occupancy.
O&M’s Effect on Students
Why do we care? In looking at the guidance provided here,
many may not see the value of it. You may not be paid for the
level of detail required, however, you should be concerned
about the impact your design has on school children. Asthma
is at record levels in schools and doubling every three years.6
Additionally, more people are hypersensitive to odors and have
allergic reactions ranging from severe to mild. It seems that
there is always a news story of another closed school with
mold and other indoor air quality issues.
Past studies7 showed a link between indoor air quality and
student performance. A recent
ASHRAE project8 showed a
link between the level of op-
eration and maintenance per-
formed on an office building
and its perceived indoor air
quality (Figure 2).
Forget the studies and what
experts are saying. Think
about how you react to learn-
ing when you are hot, sick, or
tired. What is the impact of
poor indoor air quality on the
competitiveness of the United
States to other children around
the world? How much more
could our children learn, and enjoy learning, if we would
maintain good indoor air quality?
Unfortunately, no easy answers exist. However, even a 5%
increase in learning ability during a 12-year period has im-
mense value to the individual learning. While in most cases
there is only a grade level increase (e.g., from a C to a B) each
year, this results in an 80% cumulative increase over the el-
ementary education period.
Ongoing Documentation and Training
The final area that is often neglected during design is
creating ongoing documentation and training to achieve
long-term optimization of the school. When you think about
it, what is a contractor good at? Their skills and knowledge
lies in the construction and startup of the school. However,
we are asking these same people to develop documentation
and provide training for the operation of our school for the
next 30 years.
Better documentation requires a change from a contractor
placing generic cut sheets into a binder, to having a person
60

45

25
20

0

Individual Buildings
Regression
O&M Level
Figure 2: IAQ and O&M level relationship.
IAQ Degradation
44
ASHRAE Journal
May 2003
who specializes in the development of operations and mainte-
nance documentation. Ideally, these documents should be in
an electronic format that can be accessed on the school’s intranet
or on the Internet.
For the training and documentation, it is critical to get
input from the operations and maintenance staff on what has
and has not worked in the past. From this, the documentation’s
format and level of detail is defined as well as the number,
level, and type of training (e.g., classroom, hands-on, system
focus, etc.). Because individuals have different learning
modes, some will need hands-on training while others do
well with written material.
Often, the manufacturer is the only source of quality docu-
mentation and experienced trainers. Therefore, require the train-
ing to be done by the experienced manufacturer’s trainer.
However, for any training program to be successful, it is impor-
tant to test the knowledge of the school district’s staff. If the
trainees fail the evaluation, then it needs be determined if the
issue was the training session (materials or instructor) or the
trainee (fell asleep). This provides accountability for both the
trainer (contractors and manufacturers) as well as the trainee
(management involvement).
The O&M documentation must be used in the training ses-
sions and the training sessions need to be recorded. All train-
ing should be digitally recorded, edited for content, and
integrated into the electronic O&M documentation. This en-
ables quick access to the training when troubleshooting a prob-
lem and training of new staff. This assumes the O&M staff has
computer access. If they do not have direct access, provide
access through the school’s computer labs.
Conclusion
Design professionals must make the effort (and be reimbursed
for that effort) to design systems for schools that maintain the
comfort and health of students and teachers in the classroom.
This requires diligence, knowledge, focus, and follow-through.
Electronic documentation and ongoing training can trans-
form how schools are operated. The key is accessible and easy
to use documentation and training that the staff can use daily
to make the best of limited resources.
