ASHRAE Journal ashrae.org
July 2003
By Geoff McDonell, P.Eng.
U
About the Author
Geoff McDonell, P.Eng., is a project manager
for Omicron Consulting Group in Vancouver, BC,
Canada.
nderfloor air distribution (UFAD) and displacement ventilation (DV)
often are considered synonyms for describing an air distribution
system that uses a raised access floor plenum. However, just because
the supply air is delivered into a room or space from an access floor
does not necessarily mean it is a DV system. To apply these air delivery
systems properly, building design professionals must understand the
difference between the two systems.
and operating characteristics of these
systems are well understood by design-
ers of building systems.3
UFAD Systems
UFAD systems in North America are
generally conventional “all-air” HVAC
systems. They use supply air for space
temperature control and ventilation,
and a raised access floor as the air de-
livery path. The access floor solves two
immediate problems: adequate access
and routing for computer, communica-
tions and utilities for high churn rate
offices or other occupancies; and a con-
venient, low-cost pathway for a constant
volume or variable air volume (VAV) air-
distribution system (or fan-coils,
Confusion occurs at the area of over-
lap between the two systems. A UFAD
system designed to create a stratified
condition in a space works in a similar
fashion as a DV system in terms of over-
all air movement. However, it does not
necessarily work in the same way for
space comfort control and local air de-
livery. This article presents an overview
of each system and then draws a clear
line between the “labels” of these two
types of air systems (in terms of how they
work in different applications for com-
mercial buildings).
The goals of both systems are to re-
duce building energy use and increase
indoor air quality. They can achieve these
goals provided that the design criteria
The following article was published in ASHRAE Journal, July 2003. © Copyright 2003 American Society of Heating, Refrigerating and Air-
Conditioning Engineers, Inc. It is presented for educational purposes only. This article may not be copied and/or distributed electronically or
in paper form without permission of ASHRAE.
July 2003
ASHRAE Journal
19
underfloor fan-powered mixing boxes, etc.). These systems
typically are based on traditional mixed air systems that blend
return (space) air and fresh air and deliver it to the space for
local temperature control and ventilation as a combined func-
tion. Some other installation variations use a dedicated out-
door air system (DOAS) to supply air to underfloor
fan-powered mixing terminals, four pipe fan-coils or other
underfloor air terminal devices.
Typical North American applications of underfloor air sys-
tems use air as the primary space temperature control means,
with the air being supplied via the raised floor (Figure 1).
With these systems, it is common to use high velocity induc-
tion “swirl” floor diffusers to blend and mix the conditioned
air in the space at the occupant level. Characteristics of these
systems are:6,7
• Conditioned air is used as a combined space temperature
control device, as well as the fresh air/ventilation delivery
system;
• High velocity floor air out-
lets are usually designed to
maximize air mixing within the
occupied zone of the space,
with thermal plumes and strati-
fication occurring as a minor
operational characteristic in
high spaces;
• High airflow volumes are
used relative to displacement
ventilation systems;
• Air is the primary space tem-
perature control means;
• Supply air temperatures of
±64°F (±18°C) or lower depend-
ing on load and occupancy requirements, but seldom lower
than 60°F (15.5°C) due to cold drafts at low levels;
• Some amount of space air (return air) is blended with out-
door air and recirculated depending on the availability of out-
door air for “free cooling;” and
• Typically, a constant volume system in interior zones with
VAV systems is used in perimeter zones (or local four-pipe fan-
coils or other terminal devices are used at perimeter zones).
The advantages of a UFAD system are:
• Due to the higher than conventional supply air tempera-
ture being used (63°F to 65°F vs. 55°F [17°C to 18°C vs.
13°C]), the amount of time during the year when “free cool-
ing” using outdoor air is extended, which saves mechanical
cooling energy;
• For a concrete structure, pre-cooling the floor slab at night
with cool nighttime outdoor air provides a small degree of
thermal storage and supply air cooling during the day, saving
mechanical cooling plant energy (provided that the fan en-
ergy used is less than energy needed to provide mechanical
cooling [ton-hours] for the anticipated loads);
• The air supply system is normally constant volume at low
pressure for low fan energy requirements where four-pipe pe-
rimeter fan-coils are used for zone control (alternatively, VAV
systems can also used for zone control, but an increase in
duct distribution costs and fan power would be required for
VAV systems);
• Supply air distribution ductwork can be minimized, saving
capital costs;
• Supply air controls in the occupied space are minimized,
saving capital costs (provided adjustable floor registers are
used);
• Optimal local air supply control can be achieved by easily
repositioning floor diffusers to suit the floor plan and worksta-
tion layouts;
• Optimal comfort condi-
tions can be achieved in the
occupied zone volume of the
space due to local air mixing
and a good vertical tempera-
ture gradient in the space; and
• The system can be used for
a limited amount of space
heating due to the mixing ef-
fect of the high velocity swirl
diffusers.
UFAD systems used in
spaces with high floor-to-ceil-
ing heights also result in a
stratified layer of warm, stale,
polluted air that is drawn off by the high-level exhaust/return
grilles (not unlike a low velocity DV system characteristic).
With typical North American applications of UFAD systems,
the thermal plumes generated by people and equipment are a
minor, secondary characteristic of the system, with the pri-
mary air motion in the occupied zone created by the high
velocity floor diffusers.
Displacement Ventilation Systems
Modern DV systems supply very low velocity ventilation
air (100% outdoor air) into the space at low levels — either via
a raised floor system or baseboard/sill level air outlets. Room
air typically is extracted at a high level via a ceiling plenum or
duct, and exhausted outside. These types of air systems are
intended to serve primarily the “ventilation function,” but they
can be used for a minor degree of space temperature control for
Figure 1: UFAD features air movement from a combina-
tion of mixing diffusers and bouyant plumes.
Stratification Layer
Bouyant Air From
Warm Objects
20
ASHRAE Journal ashrae.org
July 2003
cooling only. DV systems cannot be used for space heating, as
the warm air supply would short circuit to the upper parts of
the space. Primary air movement in the space is achieved by
natural buoyancy from warm objects, equipment, and people,
with a stratified layer of warm, polluted air being drawn off at
upper levels of the occupied space.
These types of systems are also known as “source flow”
from the German term “quelluftung.”1 Early applications, and
many current applications of DV systems use the supplied air
as a space temperature control function, but only in very low
cooling load applications in occupied buildings. Industrial
applications, where cool drafts at floor level are not as criti-
cal, and human occupancy is low, allow for a greater degree
of space temperature control by the DV air supply. The de-
sign intent of a DV system is to ensure that the cleanest,
freshest ventilation air is available to the room occupant at
all times, while indoor air pollutants are drawn off at a higher
level in the stratified upper air zone.6
Current DV systems used in many European office-building
projects are used as DOAS while
the space sensible cooling loads
are handled by radiant ceiling
systems. The DOAS DV systems
show the best results for space cli-
mate control and air quality, as
well as low energy use.2,4
The concept is that a continual
supply of “fresh air” is supplied
to the room (Figure 2), constantly
filling the room, and room air is
displaced out constantly at the
highest portion of the space, pro-
viding a continual “pool” of
fresh air in the occupied zone.
The supply air temperature is
normally slightly less than the desired room setpoint tempera-
ture, so the low velocity air “spills” evenly across the lower
level of the room. The human body warms the local air and
induces a buoyant movement of air past the breathing zone of
the person at all times. Thus, “fresh air” constantly moves past
the occupant’s breathing zone.
The key issue is that a DV system, as applied in European
applications, generally is not intended or specifically designed
to provide any degree of space temperature control. What little
cooling effect it can supply is a “bonus” depending on the
requirements of the space cooling loads. Used as a room tem-
perature control system, DV systems using lower temperature
air can be self-regulating (an object that is warmer relative to
the supply air temperature causes more air to rise by its buoy-
ant plume, thus drawing more cool air past it). In an industrial
environment, this can be a useful design feature. However, in a
typical human-occupied environment, the limited cooling ca-
pability due to the lower limit of air temperature at the floor
level and the air outlet velocity can be a constraint.
Characteristics of a DV system are:2,3,5
• Ventilation function only (normally consists of 100% out-
door air with no recirculation of space/room air);
• Air is normally supplied at very near room temperature,
typically about 2°F to 3°F (1°C to 1.6°C) below the desired
room temperature;
• System is designed to minimize the mixing of air in the
occupied zone of the space, and thermal plumes are the domi-
nant air motion characteristic;
• Very low air supply velocities at supply air terminals (typi-
cally less than 40 fpm [0.2 m/s]);
• Significantly lower airflow volumes compared to UFAD
systems;
• Air can be supplied through a raised floor system or via
perimeter or other low level sill-type supply outlets;
• Typically a very low pressure, constant volume system in
all zones of the building; and
• Usually some other means is
used for space temperature con-
trol (ideally radiant heating and
radiant cooling systems).
The advantages of a DV sys-
tem are:
• Supply air normally is not
used for space temperature con-
trol. Therefore, air system vol-
umes and infrastructure is
minimized, saving capital cost
and air moving energy;
• Ductwork is minimized if an
UFAD system is used;
• Since it normally supplies
100% outdoor air, the total air
supply rates need only be a maximum of 50 cfm (24 L/s) per
person (as a rule of thumb);
• The air is exhausted constantly. Therefore, heat recovery
ventilators are easy to justify to minimize both air heating
energy and cooling energy requirements;
• Good local air supply control can be achieved by easily
repositioning floor diffusers to suit the floor plan layout and
workstation layouts with an underfloor DV system arrangement.
• Good indoor air quality conditions can be achieved in the
occupied zone of the space.
• Supply air controls in the occupied space are minimized,
saving capital costs.
While UFAD and DV systems share some apparent similari-
ties, their application and method of operation are different.
Where DV systems have been designed and installed in North
American commercial (non-industrial) spaces, they have been
used as a primary space temperature control system. Although
Stratification Layer
Figure 2: Displacement ventilation system features air
movement primarily from bouyant plumes in the space.
Bouyant Plumes Are Sole
Source of Primary Air
Movement in the Space
Sidewall or Floor
Air Outlets
Exhaust Air
Supply Air
July 2003
ASHRAE Journal
21
a DV system could provide some minimal space temperature
control, it is not the best application for this system.3
A key issue with any kind of UFAD system is housekeeping
and cleanliness of the underfloor plenum. In a typical North
American UFAD system, where high velocity air supply points
are used, dust and particles can be entrained from the floor ple-
num into the airflow, and distributed into the occupied zone in
poorly maintained systems. In an underfloor DV system, the air
velocities are low, thus reducing the risk of underfloor debris
being entrained in the supply air stream. The author worked in
an office space that used a typical UFAD system. Every morning
after the air handlers started, dust had to be swept off the desk.
Because of dust allergies, this became an unpleasant work envi-
ronment. If the housekeeping of the floor plenum and the floor
bucket at the diffuser is of a high standard, this is less of an issue.
In North America, the basic HVAC design criteria are based on
“air temperature” as the main
space comfort design param-
eter. In fact, many studies have
shown that human comfort is
made up of 40% to 50% radia-
tion, 30% to 40% convection,
and around 10% to 20% evapo-
ration/perspiration for most in-
door environments.8 The
typical “all-air” HVAC systems
account for only the convec-
tion and perspiration (humid-
ity) aspects, while not
accounting for a significant
portion of the human comfort
factor — radiation. The term
“resultant temperature” needs
to be understood, as recognized
by the Occupational Safety and
Health Administration (OSHA) in its “wet bulb globe tempera-
ture” figures used for workers environmental stress analyses. In
a radiant cooling environment, a “resultant space temperature”
of 72°F (22°C) can be met easily, even in an ambient air tem-
perature condition of 78°F (26°C) (Figure 3).
The 1997 ASHRAE Handbook—Fundamentals provides less
than a half page of descriptive design information for DV sys-
tems.7 In fact, DV systems are tied closely to natural ventilation
schemes that can become very involved, requiring computa-
tional fluid dynamics software to calculate system performance
with any reasonable degree of accuracy. The ASHRAE outline
of displacement ventilation still contains cautions about its “lim-
ited cooling capacity” and air “supply temperature limitations
for room temperature control.” Again, the effective purpose and
function of displacement ventilation has been misunderstood
and is still viewed by ASHRAE as a “space temperature control
element,” rather than a DOAS system.
In Europe, DV systems have been studied and applied as a
means of supplying ventilation (fresh) air to the occupied space
in a most effective low energy manner, while other methods are
used for primary space temperature/comfort control, typically
hydronic radiant cooling and heating systems. A radiant tem-
perature control system coupled with a DV system provides su-
perior comfort and indoor air quality conditions, as well as saves
energy. Comfort factors are satisfied by the resultant tempera-
ture, and the delivery of clean, fresh air to the breathing zone is
most effective. Indoor air quality is maintained at a high level
since all of the room air is constantly purged (displaced), carry-
ing with it any indoor air pollutants (Figure 3). Because these
systems are common in Europe, many studies have been done
and existing installations have long track records of successful
operation. These types of systems are not considered “cutting
edge” approaches. The key point is that radiant temperature
control and DV systems use
substantially less energy than
conventional “all air” systems,
while maintaining superior in-
door comfort and indoor air
quality. These systems are
considered mainstream in Eu-
rope in terms of design appli-
cations for integrated building
HVAC systems.
When a separate space sen-
sible temperature control sys-
tem is used, the air system for
that space can be reduced to
a DOAS consisting of 100%
filtered and treated outdoor
air and 100% exhaust from
indoor pollutant sources.
Space sensible (resultant)
temperature can be maintained easily by hydronic radiant heat-
ing/cooling systems in occupancies that have cooling loads
up to 100 W/m2 (32 Btu/h per ft2).2,4 Water can carry 3,000
times more energy per unit mass compared to air, and in most
“all-air” HVAC systems, fan energy can consume up to 60% to
70% of the HVAC system energy. Therefore, a hydronic tem-
perature control system using radiant systems is more energy
efficient. Based on the track record of the radiant slab systems,
coupled with DV systems as used in Europe, the most energy
efficient building climate control systems are based on a radi-
ant concrete slab conditioning system, where the huge ther-
mal mass of the concrete creates extremely stable indoor
climates. Radiant cooling also can be applied in humid cli-
mates if the DOAS system uses some kind of dehumidification
system, and the building envelope is designed and controlled
properly. The lower limit of any radiant cooling system is the
indoor relative humidity and dew-point temperature.
Figure 3: Example of an all-air HVAC system.

Thermo – Active Slab

Floor to

Light Achieved

to

Supply Air-Ventilation Only
22
ASHRAE Journal ashrae.org
July 2003
The design and construction costs for combined radiant cool-
ing and DV systems in North America can be cost effective.
Radiant slab building systems show the best capital cost advan-
tages, with total mechanical system costs (plumbing, fire pro-
tection and HVAC systems) in the $9.50 to $10 per ft2 ($102 to
$108 per m2) range, compared to conventional VAV or four-pipe
fan-coils HVAC system costs of $14.50 per ft2 ($156 per m2),
using local costs in western Canada. While few of these build-
ings operate in North America, the statistical base is growing.9
Based on European data the author has compiled, and local
data based on current projects in design and in construction
using radiant slab systems in western Canada, the overall capi-
tal costs for these types of buildings are comparable to con-
ventional building systems approaches. Two operating
examples are located in western Canada. The ICT engineering
building at the University of Calgary uses a radiant slab for
100% of the interior zone space temperature control in a high-
rise classroom building; and the newly opened Gleneagles
Community Center in West Vancouver uses a typical Euro-
pean “constant temperature building” radiant slab system with
displacement ventilation systems throughout the project. The
costs for these buildings were the same as a conventional build-
ing HVAC system approach, with the Gleneagles Community
Center incurring a premium due to the geothermal heat pump
field installed as part of the system.
In addition, some suspended radiant panel projects use the
panels as both heating and cooling space temperature control
devices with variations on the air supply systems. An example
of this is the four-year-old Suncor Accommodation Complex
in Fort McMurray, AB, Canada. The 1,500 room dormitory
covers approximately 120,000 ft2 (11 148 m2) using suspended
radiant heating and cooling panels in each room. Using the
radiant panel system saved electrical and mechanical capital
costs, and resulted in ongoing energy savings.9
The big advantage of a radiant slab system building is en-
ergy efficiency. Typical energy consumption for radiant slab
and displacement ventilation HVAC systems is 60% to 70%
less than conventional four-pipe fan coil or VAV HVAC sys-
tems due to the use of the building structure’s thermal mass.
Radiant cooling temperature systems for most office build-
ings only need to operate at a radiant surface temperature of
no lower than 64°F (17.5°C) to provide up to 100 W/m2 (32
Btu/h per ft2), thus making them a good match for geothermal
systems (where the average ground temperature in areas of
North America is compatible with the building radiant system’s
requirements). These radiant cooling surface temperatures are
also well above any extreme indoor humidity and dew-point
conditions that might occur.2
Building systems designers and engineers need to think
about a “building physics” approach and realize that air tem-
perature alone is not the total goal of a building interior cli-
mate control system. UFAD systems certainly have their place,
and work extremely well in an energy efficient manner when
the ventilation function is combined with the space tempera-
ture control function. This can be an effective approach in
higher cooling load applications where the limitations of
DV/radiant systems mean these systems cannot be used.
References
1. Fitzner, K. 1987. “Source flow.” Forschungsverinigung fur Luft
und Trocknungstechnik.
2. Fitzner, K. “Mixed flow, displacement flow and cooled ceilings,
results of laboratory tests and practical installations, limitations and
range of applications.” Hermann Rietschel Institute of Heating and Air
Conditioning, Technical University, Berlin.
3. Yuan, X, C. Quingyan and L. Glicksman. 1998. “A critical review
of displacement ventilation.” ASHRAE Transactions 104(1).
4. Loveday, D., et al. 1998. “Designing for thermal comfort in com-
bined chilled ceiling/displacement ventilation environments.” ASHRAE
Transactions 104(1).
5. Rees, S. and P. Haves. “A model of a displacement ventilation
system suitable for system simulation.” Loughborough University of
Technology, Loughborough, U.K.
6. Vanderweil Engineers, Frank O. Gehry & Associates Architects,
and McKay, Conant, Brook Inc Acoustical Engineers. 1999. “The Stata
center, Massachusetts Institute of Technology, HVAC systems analysis.”
7. 1997 ASHRAE Handbook—Fundamentals, Section 31.5, Space
Air Diffusion.
8. 1997 ASHRAE Handbook—Fundamentals, Chapter 8, Thermal
Comfort.
9. Personal correspondence, Tender results sheets, and quantity sur-
veyor costing estimates for radiant slab/displacement ventilation build-
ing designs currently in Construction.

ASHRAE Journal
91
Demand Control Ventilation
By Kurt W. Roth, Ph.D., Associate Member ASHRAE, John Dieckmann, P.E., Member ASHRAE,
and James Brodrick, Ph.D., Member ASHRAE
T
This is the fifth article covering new energy-saving technolo-
gies evaluated in a recent U.S. Department of Energy report
available at www.eren.doe.gov/buildings/documents.
he 2001 version of ANSI/ASHRAE Standard 62, Ventila-
tion for Acceptable Indoor Air Quality, offers two op-
tions for maintaining adequate ventilation: the ventila-
tion rate procedure and the indoor air quality (IAQ) procedure.
Building codes throughout the United States are in the pro-
cess of adopting the standard.
The ventilation rate procedure uses the traditional prescrip-
tive method, i.e., a minimum quantity of cfm per person based
on maximum occupancy. In contrast, the IAQ procedure allows
designers to vary the outdoor ventilation rate (from 0% to
100% of the design OA) if the carbon dioxide (CO2) level
remains below a recommended level, i.e., 700 ppm greater than
outdoor CO2
levels. In this case, CO2 levels serve as a proxy for
building occupancy and the rate of human-generated indoor
pollutants, e.g., odors.
The addition of the IAQ procedure to the standard allows for
demand control ventilation (DCV) in buildings. In the same
way that thermostats regulate the amount of cooling or heat-
ing supplied to a building space, CO2 sensors measure and
regulate the amount of OA supplied to the space for buildings
that use a DCV strategy.
Since its creation, the IAQ procedure has sparked contro-
versy. Although CO2 levels tend to correlate well with human
occupancy and human-generated pollutants, they do not re-
flect the buildup of pollutants not related to occupancy, such
as fumes from copiers and printers; out-gassing from building
materials, carpets and furniture; and vapors from cleaning sup-
plies. Whereas the ventilation rate procedure “is deemed to
provide acceptable indoor air quality, ipso facto,” the IAQ pro-
cedure “provides a direct solution by restricting the concen-
tration of all known contaminants of concern to some speci-
fied acceptable levels.”1
Consequently, building operators using the IAQ procedure
need to ensure that all potential contaminants remain at safe
levels. An addendum to Standard 62 (62n) is under review that
specifies ventilation rates as a function of both floor space and
occupancy to address nonhuman indoor air pollutant sources.
Energy Savings Potential
If a DCV strategy calls for less outdoor air over the course of
the heating and cooling seasons than does a prescriptive ven-
tilation strategy, then the annual energy required to heat or
cool the OA decreases. In addition, lower OA requirements
decrease the fan energy expended to introduce and expel the
air from the building. It is widely believed that actual occu-
pancy levels in U.S. buildings are significantly lower than the
design occupancy levels that conventional ventilation sys-
tems are set to handle. Field experience indicates that actual
occupancy levels are at least 25% to 30% lower and perhaps as
much as 60% to 75% lower in some buildings than design
levels.2,3 The ultimate energy savings in a given application
depend on the actual (vs. design) occupancy level patterns, as
well as building type and climate.2,4,5 Available data suggest
that DCV reduces ventilation, heating, and cooling loads by
10% to 30%.4 Buildings and spaces with large swings in occu-
pancy, e.g., movie theatres and conference rooms, tend to real-
ize the largest savings. On a national scale, DCV has an esti-
mated maximum energy savings potential of between 0.4 and
0.5 quads.
DCV reduces peak electricity demand when actual occu-
pancy levels fall below design occupancy levels during peak
demand periods. Lower levels of OA translate into decreased
cooling (and, to a lesser extent, ventilation) loads and, there-
fore, air-conditioning power draw. In some cases, DCV
may allow building operators to close fresh air dampers for
short periods during the hottest hours in the summer (typically
coinciding with peak electric load). In general, peak reduc-
tions vary from building to building, depending
on occupancy patterns. Consequently, the average peak de-
mand reduction likely will mirror the cooling energy savings
potential.
Market Factors
The CO2 sensors required to implement DCV cost approxi-
mately $400 to $500 a piece (installed), with typically one
sensor installed per zone (~2,000 to 3,000 ft2 [~185 to 280
m2]). Additional expenses likely will be required to integrate
the sensors into building controls.4,6 In practice, DCV has re-
duced annual energy costs by $0.05 to $1 per square foot
($0.54 to $10.75 per m2), with large variations reflecting the
range in building types studied. On average, DCV appears to
have a two to three year payback period, which should make it
attractive for many applications.
Currently, most buildings do not use DCV because of con-
cerns about nonhuman indoor pollutants mentioned previ-
ously. DCV is a new concept for standards, and local building
codes have been slow to adopt it. Once adopted, additional
The following article was published in ASHRAE Journal, July 2003. © Copyright 2003 American Society of Heating,
Refrigerating and Air-Conditioning Engineers, Inc. It is presented for educational purposes only. This article may not be
copied and/or distributed electronically or in paper form without permission of ASHRAE.
92
ASHRAE Journal
July 2003
permitting and verification often is required. Contractors and
designers have concerns about liability for systems that do not
meet IAQ standards, when designed to the IAQ procedure. This
could occur because of improper CO2 sensor installation or
sensor failure, or because one of the many non-human indoor
pollutants rises above acceptable levels. In contrast, the pre-
scriptive standard offers less room for liability because it is
deemed to provide acceptable IAQ.1 Other issues discouraging
widespread DCV adoption include the need for savvy system
installation and operational personnel, which cost more and
are hard to find; CO2 sensor maintenance issues; and the lim-
ited number of control systems that support CO2 sensor input
for ventilation control.
Clearly, there must be a substantial reduction in the real and
perceived risks of implementing the IAQ procedure for DCV to
realize its considerable energy savings potential.
References
1. ANSI/ASHRAE Standard 62-2001, Ventilation for Acceptable
Indoor Air Quality.
2. Brandemuehl, M.J. and J.E. Braun. 1999. “The impact of demand-
controlled and economizer ventilation strategies on energy use in build-
ings.” ASHRAE Transactions 105(2).
3. Roth, K.W., et al. 2002. “Energy consumption characteristics of
commercial building hvac systems – volume III: energy savings poten-
tial.” Final Report by TIAX LLC to U.S. Department of Energy, Office
of Building Technology, State and Community Programs.
4. Schell, M., S.C. Turner, and R.O. Shim. 1998. “Application of
CO2-based demand-controlled ventilation using ASHRAE Standard
62: optimizing energy use and ventilation.” ASHRAE Transactions
104(2):1213–1225.
5. Schell, M. and D. Int-Hout. 2001. “Demand control ventilation
using CO2.” ASHRAE Journal 43(2):18–29.
6. Turk, B.H., et al. 1987. “Indoor air quality and ventilation mea-
surements in 38 Pacific Northwest commercial buildings.” Lawrence
Berkeley National Laboratory Report LBL-22315.
Kurt W. Roth, Ph.D., is a project manager at the Appliance
and Building Technology sector of TIAX, Cambridge, Mass.
John Dieckmann, P.E., is a principal at the HVAC and Refrig-
eration Technology sector of TIAX. James Brodrick, Ph.D., is a
project manager, Building Technologies Program, U.S. Depart-
ment of Energy, Washington, D.C.

ASHRAE Journal ashrae.org
July 2003
By L. Alevantis, Associate Member ASHRAE, J. Wagner, W. Fisk, Member ASHRAE, D. Sullivan, D. Faulkner,
L. Gundel, J. Waldman, and P. Flessel
F
About the Authors
L. Alevantis, P. Flessel, J. Waldman, and J.
Wagner are with the State of California Depart-
ment of Health Services, Environmental Health
Laboratory Branch, in Berkeley, Calif. D. Sullivan,
W. Fisk, D. Faulkner and L. Gundel are with
Lawerence Berkeley National Laboratory, Indoor
Environment Department, Berkeley, Calif.
ollowing the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency’s classification
of Environmental Tobacco Smoke (ETS) as a Group A carcinogen
in 1992, California passed legislation in 1994 (Assembly Bill 131) pro-
hibiting most employers from exposing nonsmoking workers to ETS.
As a result of this legislation, workplace smoking restrictions were
added to the California Labor Code.2 This statute prohibits any em-
ployer from knowingly or intentionally permitting the smoking of to-
bacco products in enclosed places of employment.
a. Air from the room is exhausted di-
rectly to the outside by an exhaust fan;
b. No smoking room air is recirculated
to other parts of the building; and
c. Smoking rooms are in a non-work
area where employees are not required
to be present as part of their work respon-
sibilities other than custodial or mainte-
nance work when the room is unoccupied.
Criteria a and b are the major focus
of this article, and in particular,
we consider the level of negative pres-
Prohibition of smoking at the work-
place does not apply to breakrooms des-
ignated by employers for smoking, under
specified conditions. There are addi-
tional exemptions to specific workplaces
that are not related to the subject matter
in this article.
Smoking Breakrooms
Smoking is allowed in specially de-
signed and operated breakrooms that
meet the following criteria:
surization and other separation tech-
niques that are effective in achieving
the “no air … is recirculated…” crite-
rion of b. This article does not consider
any of the ventilation goals in the smok-
ing breakrooms themselves. Rather, our
focus is on minimizing leakage of air
from these breakrooms to non-
smoking areas.
California Study of Smoking Rooms
Phase I. From 1991 to 1994, prior to
the passage of AB13, we studied the ef-
fectiveness of various smoking-area de-
signs in containing ETS within smoking
areas in 23 public buildings.11,12 The
The following article was published in ASHRAE Journal, July 2003. © Copyright 2003 American Society of Heating, Refrigerating and Air-
Conditioning Engineers, Inc. It is presented for educational purposes only. This article may not be copied and/or distributed electronically or
in paper form without permission of ASHRAE.
July 2003
ASHRAE Journal
27
designs studied ranged from open, adjacent, and/or contigu-
ous smoking/nonsmoking areas to smoking rooms that were
completely isolated from adjoining areas with walls and doors.
We measured nicotine in the smoking and nonsmoking areas,
pressure differentials between smoking and nonsmoking ar-
eas, smoking room airflow rates, and building ventilation rates.
In addition, we tagged the air in the smoking room with a
tracer gas (sulfur hexafluoride [SF6]) and measured its concen-
tration in the smoking and nonsmoking areas.
Among the designs studied, we found that enclosed areas
with no air recirculation to nonsmoking areas and with exhaust
to the outside were clearly the most effective in reducing expo-
sure of non-smokers to ETS. Although only a small number (4
out of 23) of the smoking areas met the restrictions currently in
AB13, the study indicated that the most important variables
relevant to smoking room performance were room de-pressur-
ization, door opening patterns, and in the case of open ceiling
plenums between smoking and nonsmoking areas, leakage into
the return air ceiling plenum above the smoking room.
Phase II. The purpose of this study was to quantify the effect
of the variables identified during Phase I of the study relevant
to smoking room performance under controlled laboratory con-
ditions. This study was conducted from 1999 to 2002.
Twenty-seven experiments were conducted in a simulated
smoking room with a smoking machine and an automatic door
opener. The characteristics of the test chamber are described in
Table 1. Smoking room performance was quantified primarily
by tagging smoking room air with SF6 and monitoring its con-
centration in both the smoking and nonsmoking areas. Be-
cause the dynamics and transport of the various ETS
components can differ substantially from that of SF6 and from
each other, we measured three particle and two gas phase ETS
tracers in a subset of these experiments. The particle-phase ETS
tracers measured were: total particulate matter (PM), PM-phase
scopoletin, and optical absorption of PM at 370nm (UVPM).
The two gas-phase tracers measured were: nicotine and 3-
ethenylpyridine (3-EP).
Three potential air leakage mechanisms were investigated
in the chamber tests:
a. Through the gap under the door and wall cracks when the
smoking room was pressurized relative to the nonsmoking area;
b. Around the ceiling tiles in an open plenum that connected
with the nonsmoking area when the smoking room was pres-
surized relative to the plenum; and
c. Via the pumping action of the door as occupants enter and
exit the smoking room.
Data collected from the 27 laboratory experiments allowed us
to quantify the various types of leakage flows, the effect of these
leaks on smoking room performance and non-smoker exposure,
and the relative importance of each leakage mechanism.
The most important findings of interest to designers of smok-
ing rooms are summarized next. A detailed discussion of all
the experimental findings has been published elsewhere.13
Smoking Room Effectiveness
The impact of each leakage mechanism on smoking room
effectiveness was evaluated using the following performance
measure:
• The smoking room exhaust efficiency is the percentage of
smoking room air that is successfully exhausted to the out-
Many organizations have issued guidelines for negatively
pressurized rooms. These guidelines are based on field ex-
perience using smoke test methods.
1. A “rule of thumb” for designing negatively pressurized
rooms has been that a 10% differential between a room’s
supply and exhaust (or return) airflow is adequate to prevent
room air leakage to adjoining spaces.3 Guttman4 reported
that this 10% rule of thumb “is a hangover from an old
ASHRAE guide.”
2. The Francis J. Curry National Tuberculosis Center5 rec-
ommends that the negative pressure differential across Tu-
berculosis isolation rooms be approximately –7.5 Pa (0.03
in. H2O). The same Center recommends that exhaust should
exceed supply by at least 47 L/s (100 cfm).
3. CDC6 recommends 0.25 Pa (0.001 in. w.c.) negative
pressure for TB isolation rooms and that the exhaust should
be 10% or 24 L/s (50 cfm) greater than the supply.
4. The California Office of Statewide Health Planning and
Development (OSHPD7) and the California Mechanical Code
Existing Design Guidelines for Containment Rooms
recommend for TB isolation rooms the same pressure differ-
ential as CDC, but also specify that the exhaust should be 35
L/s (75 cfm) greater than the supply, that the room should be
under negative pressure, and that the velocity at the “trans-
fer opening” be 0.51 m/s (100 fpm).
5. For laboratories, the American National Standard for
Laboratory Ventilation8 specifies that where air must be con-
tained, the exhaust and supply airflow rates must be main-
tained through any opening between the controlled space
and adjoining areas, including open doorways, so that the
following velocities be achieved at the opening:
a. minimum velocity: 0.25 m/s (50 fpm); and
b. preferred velocity: 0.51 m/s (100 fpm).
6. For areas undergoing asbestos containment, OSHA9
recommends that these areas be negatively pressurized at
5 Pa (0.02 in. w.c.).
7. Wiseman10 recommends a minimum negative pressure
of 2.5 Pa (0.01 in. w.c.) and advices a pressure 12 Pa (0.05
in. w.c.) or higher for “critical areas.”
28

July 2003
doors by the ventilation system serving the smoking room.12,13
Smoking room air containing ETS that is not exhausted to the
outdoors can be sorbed on smoking room surfaces and/or leak
into adjoining, nonsmoking areas. The steady-state exhaust
efficiency, ηexh, is given by

where Qexh, SR is the smoking room exhaust flow in units of

smoking room exhaust duct at steady-state in units of mass per
volume, and S is the generation rate of ETS or SF6 in units of
mass per time. Higher exhaust efficiencies indicate that most of
the smoking room air is removed by the smoking room’s exhaust.
Flow Under Closed Door
We fitted the data for the flow under the door and the data for
the pressure differential between the smoking room and the ad-
joining nonsmoking room to the power law equation as described
in the 2001 ASHRAE Handbook—Fundamentals, Chapter 26:

Flow Around the Perimeter of Ceiling Tiles
Similarly, using Equation 2 and fitting the data for the experi-
ments where the ceiling plenum was open between the smoking
and nonsmoking areas, we obtained c = 28.5 L/s/(Pa)0.484 and n
= 0.484 for flow from the smoking room to the plenum.
Assuming that the flow coefficient is linearly proportional
to the total perimeter of ceiling tiles installed in a smoking
room (200 linear feet for our experiment), we produced the
following equation:

29
where QSR-cp is the flow through the perimeter of the ceiling
plenum tiles in units of L/s, Lcp is the perimeter of the ceiling
tiles in meters, and ∆Pcp is the pressure differential between the
smoking room and ceiling plenum in Pascals. The proportion of
ETS contaminants in this leakage flow that enters the nonsmok-
ing area of a building will depend on whether the plenum air is
recirculated or leaks into the nonsmoking areas, the percentage
that is recirculated, and on the extent to which the contaminants
deposit on surfaces in the return or leakage air path or are re-
moved by filters.
Pumping of Smoking Room Air by Opening and Closing
of Entry Door
Each opening and closing of the smoking room swing-type
door transferred approximately 24 ft3 (670 L) of smoking room-
laden air from the smoking room to the adjacent nonsmoking
area. The effective leakage rate in units of L/s can be deter-
mined by multiplying this volume by the number of door open-
ings per unit time, D
(5)
This volume was measured when door pumping was the
only leakage mechanism of the smoking room, i.e., the room
was not ventilated and not depressurized.
We anticipate that this leakage rate would scale approxi-
mately linearly with door size.
Equations 3 and 4 allow a designer to specify exhaust airflows
for a smoking room based on target values for ∆PSR and ∆Pcp
(target values are discussed later in this article). These equations
apply for the experimental setup that we studied and may not
fully describe other rooms with considerably different leakage
mechanisms.
Leakage Mechanisms and Pressure Gradient
Plotting the various leakage flows as a function of the ap-
propriate pressure drop (Figure 1) indicates that:
a. Depressurization eliminates undesirable leakages under door
gaps and around ceiling tiles (instead, leakage goes from non-
smoking to smoking area);
b. Pumping of smoking room air via door opening is the
only leakage mechanism in depressurized smoking rooms; and
c. In our experiments, leakage to the ceiling plenum was a
stronger function of ∆P than leakage through the door.
Since the quasi-steady state concentration of a pollutant
originating in an enclosed space is roughly inversely propor-
tional to the space’s pollutant-free ventilation rate, a higher
ventilation rate in a smoking room reduces concentrations of
ETS contaminants in the smoking room air, which, in turn,
diminishes the adverse effects of leakage from the smoking
room to the nonsmoking room. To first order, for the high ven-
tilation rates in smoking rooms, the concentrations of ETS
constituents in the smoking room air will increase in direct
proportion to ETS constituent production rate and decrease in
proportion to the reciprocal of the smoking room’s ventilation
rate. A more complete discussion of ETS concentrations dur-
ing and after smoking can be found elsewhere.14,15
Effect of Entry Door Type on Room Leakages
We conducted the majority of our experiments with a swing-
type entry door to the smoking room. In addition, we con-
Advertisement in the print edition formerly in this space.

door
30
ASHRAE Journal ashrae.org
July 2003
ducted a limited number of experiments with a sliding-type
entry door as well as with an open doorway (no door) in order
to compare the leakages of smoking room air to the nonsmok-
ing area of these configurations.
The “pumped out by eight door openings/hr” curve in Fig-
ure 1 was determined using a swing-type door. When we re-
placed this type of smoking room door with a sliding door, the
volume of air pumped out per opening was reduced by 77%.
Therefore, the volume shown in Equation 5 is reduced to only
5.4 ft3 (150 L) in the case of a sliding door.
Intuitively, using a smoking room with a fixed, open door-
way would be a way to completely eliminate smoking room air
leakage via door pumping. However, thermally-induced circu-
lation flows through the doorway can cause air from the smok-
ing room to leak into the nonsmoking room, even when the
net flow across the doorway is towards the smoking room. In
our “open doorway” test, SF6 concentrations in the nonsmok-
ing room were comparable to those found in our tests with a
door in place, but it required ventilation rates that were two to
four times higher to achieve the same results. Thus, using a
door and maintaining the smoking room depressurized was a
much more effective way to control leakage from the smoking
room. Open doorways with higher face velocities than ours
may be more protective, though they presumably would re-
quire even higher exhaust flows.
In our experiments, we were able to achieve 99 L/s (210 cfm)
of exhaust flow or velocities of 0.053 m/s (10 fpm) through the
open doorway. As was mentioned previously, for a laboratory
with open doorways, the American National Standard for Labo-
ratory Ventilation8 recommends a minimum velocity of 0.25 m/s
(50 fpm) with 0.51 m/s (100 fpm) being the desired velocity. For
a standard size door, these velocity requirements translate into
exhaust airflows of 470 L/s and 940 L/s respectively (1,000 and
2,000 cfm). These exhaust flows are unrealistically high, espe-
cially for smaller size rooms. For large smoking rooms with large
numbers of users, such as in some airports, the open-door smok-
ing room may possibly be more practical and superior to a smok-
ing room with a swinging door. Note that the open doorway
may be perceived as a hazard by some nonsmokers.
Recommended Pressure Differential
Figure 2 shows the ηexh as a function of the pressure differen-
tial between the smoking and nonsmoking room. The graph
shows that for pressure differentials of –5 to –7 Pa (–0.02 to
–0.03 in. W.C.), exhaust efficiencies of at least 90% were
achieved. This pressure difference will vary with the total
amount of leakage in the smoking room’s envelope. Tempera-
ture differentials of 2°C (3.6°F) did not result in measurable
additional leakages.
Correlation Between SF6 and Other ETS Markers
We used the exposure ratio to correlate SF6 to the other ETS
In 2001 our group conducted a telephone survey of all 62
local tobacco control jurisdictions in California regarding
their ordinances for the operation of smoking rooms. The
results indicated that 29% (N=18) have ordinances prohib-
iting smoking anywhere at the workplace, including smok-
ing rooms, while the remainder do not have any specific
ordinances more strict than the California Labor Code and,
therefore, do not prohibit the operation of smoking rooms.
The California Department of Health Services conducts
ongoing statewide surveys aimed at collecting information
about Californians’ smoking behaviors, including operation
of smoking rooms. Based on these surveys, we estimated
that about 122,000 California workers, or 0.8% of the
workforce were working in buildings where smoking rooms
were operating in 1999. For 2000, these estimates were
slightly lower (100,000 workers or 0.6% of the workforce),
indicating a decline in smoking at the workplace.

July 2003
ASHRAE Journal
31
tracers. The exposure ratio is the ratio of ETS or SF6 concentra-
tion in the nonsmoking area divided by the corresponding
concentration in the smoking area. Lower exposure ratios in-
dicate better protection for occupants of nonsmoking areas
As shown in Figure 3, all ETS tracer exposure ratios showed
good correlation with SF6 (i.e., all fluctuated together in re-
sponse to the various smoking room configurations). How-
ever, all ETS tracers exhibited lower-magnitude exposure ratios
than SF6, implying less leakage to nonsmoking-room air. 3-
EP showed the highest levels in nonsmoking room air, whereas
nicotine showed the lowest.
Other Considerations
There were many issues related to the health and comfort of
nonsmokers occupying areas adjoining smoking rooms that
our research did not intend to address. Some of these issues
include:
a. Health effects associated with low-level ETS exposures in
the nonsmoking areas;
b. Leakage of residual and sorbed ETS from a smoking room
when the room is unoccupied and its ventilation is turned off;
c. Ventilation rates for odor control in the smoking rooms; and
d. Transfer of ETS from smoking areas to nonsmoking areas
by occupant clothing.
Conclusions
Our test results indicate that designers of smoking rooms
should consider the following:
1. Maintain smoking rooms depressurized relative to the ad-
joining nonsmoking areas. Our results showed that for pressure
differentials between –5 to –7 Pa (–0.02 to –0.03 in. w.c.), ex-
haust efficiencies of at least 90% were achieved.
2. Air from the smoking rooms should be exhausted to the
outside without recirculation to other occupied spaces.
3. Figure 2 may be used to estimate the pressure differen-
tial to maintain a desired level of smoking room efficiency.
Equations 3 and 4 can then be used to estimate exhaust airflow
requirements to maintain the pressure differential.
4. Increasing the smoking room ventilation rate will dimin-
ish the concentration of ETS contaminants in any air that hap-
pens to leak from the smoking room to the nonsmoking area.
5. If a smoking room shares a common plenum with adja-
cent nonsmoking spaces, either block off plenum or ensure
that the smoking room is under slightly negative pressure rela-
tive to the ceiling plenum.
6. Even when smoking rooms are maintained under nega-
tive pressure, operating swing-type entry doors to enter and
exit smoking rooms results in pumping up to 10% of smoking
room air into adjoining nonsmoking areas.
7. Sliding-type entry doors minimize leakage due to the
“pumping” effect.

8. Automatic closure mechanisms are recommended for
swing-type and sliding-type doors to avoid leakage through

and is unlikely to be a practical configuration for the most
common, smaller size smoking rooms.

About the Author
Steve Kavanaugh, Ph.D., is a professor at the
University of Alabama, Tuscaloosa, Ala.
summary report of recent studies highlights the increasing energy
consumption of auxiliary equipment in building HVAC systems.1 It
was estimated that 1.5 quadrillion Btus (1.5 quads) were required to
operate auxiliary equipment compared in 1995 to 1.85 quads for the
primary space heating equipment and 1.87 quads for cooling. Supply,
return and exhaust fan consumption was 83% of the total. Many of
these studies used elaborate, hour-by-hour simulations to account for
and predict auxiliary equipment energy consumption.2
specific demand values (kW/ton) for sev-
eral types of water-cooled (WC) and air-
cooled (AC) chillers and direct expansion
(DX) compressors. Values from
ANSI/ASHRAE Standard 90.1-2001,
Energy Standard for Buildings Except
Low-Rise Residential Buildings also are
included. The standard conditions used
were 45°F (7°C) leaving chilled water
temperature, 55°F (13°C) return chilled
water temperature, 85°F (29°C) entering
condenser water and 95°F (35°C) leav-
ing condenser water temperature for
water-cooled chillers, and 95°F (35°C)
outdoor air temperatures for air-cooled
condensers. A value of 45°F (7°C) was
used for the evaporating temperature of
DX coils. The results were inserted into a
table on the score sheet that includes
kW/ton values for high-, medium-
Unfortunately, resources often are un-
available for conducting hour-by-hour
analysis. If energy analysis is performed,
it is often in the conceptual phase. Ac-
curate energy analysis is best conducted
after an initial detailed design is com-
pleted, several iterations that optimize
cost vs. efficiency vs. space vs. comfort
vs. code compliance are conducted, and
the design is “value engineered” to meet
the project budget. At this stage, energy
efficiency may have a low priority and
systems with high demand and energy
requirements could result. A score sheet
has been developed3 to quickly evalu-
ate the impact of individual components
on system demand (and efficiency)
when detailed energy analysis is not
possible. Table 1 is a representation of
the spreadsheet results for a central
chilled water system with a fan-powered
VAV air distribution.
Chillers and Compressors
Performance data from three major
manufacturers were surveyed to arrive at

The score sheet program is available at www.geokiss.com.
Supply and Return Air Fans
The fans in supply air-handling units must overcome fric-
tion losses in the ductwork, filters, coils, dampers, and the
AHU cabinet. The score sheet requires the total fan static pres-
sure in inches of water be entered. An airflow rate of 400 cfm
per ton (54 L/s per kW) is assumed but can be changed in the
spreadsheet cell. Values for fan4 and motor efficiencies5 are
entered. Equation 1 converts the fan pressure, flow rate, and
efficiency to motor output power in horsepower and then to
motor input in kW based on the motor efficiency.

The equations apply only to equipment driven by EPACT-
covered motors of 1 hp (0.75 kW) and larger. Other fans are
covered later that recognize the lower efficiency of this equip-
ment. Fortunately, these smaller fans and motors are not nor-
mally used in HVAC applications requiring high static
pressures.
Equation 1 also applies to return air fans if they are part of
the HVAC systems. However, the fan heat deduction for re-
turn fans must be adjusted in recognition of the fact that a
certain fraction of this air is exhausted and this portion does
not contribute to the cooling load. A multiplier should be
added to Equation 2 for return fans to recognize the percent-
age of air recirculated. The default value in the spreadsheet is
80%, which can be adjusted.
Pumps
The assumptions for the chilled water pumps are 2.4
gpm/ton (0.043 L/s per kW). Values for pump and motor effi-
ciencies are entered. Equation 3 is used to convert the pump
pressure, flow rate, and efficiency to motor output power in
horsepower and then to motor input in kW based on motor
efficiency.

The chilled water pump heat also should be deducted, al-
though it is often a minor contribution. If it is located outside
the conditioned space, Equation 4 should be multiplied by
the pump motor efficiency to assign the motor losses to venti-
lation air rather than to the building load.

The condenser water pump assumptions were similar except
that the flow rate is based on 3 gpm/ton (0.05 L/s per kW). Some
systems have optimum flows with lower values. However, the
chiller kW per ton values used previously assume 2.4 gpm CHW
and 3 gpm/ton (0.05 L/s per kW) CW and can be adjusted if
different water flows are used to optimize the system.

No capacity deduction is needed for the condenser water
pumps since the added heat is rejected in the cooling tower or

Cooling Fans
Specification and sizing procedures for five manufacturers
of cooling towers and fluid coolers were surveyed. Plots were
generated of required fan hp as a function of condensing ca-
pacity. The following equations resulted from a trend line of
the data assuming 90% efficient fan motors.

Unit and Series VAV Terminal Fans
The “wire-to-air” efficiencies of small fractional horsepower
fan motors are substantially lower than those in larger air-
handling units. Both the motor and fan efficiencies vary sig-
nificantly. Rather than attempting to predict these values

39
independently, the products of three manufacturers
were surveyed that provide actual fan wattage. Results are
shown in Figure 1, which is a plot of input fan wattage as a
function airflow rate for fractional horsepower fan motors as-
suming an ESP of 0.5 inches of water (125 Pa). When the
standard airflow rate of 400 cfm/ton (54 L/s per kW) is ap-
plied to this data, an average value of 180 watts per ton
(0.18 kW/ton) will result for fan coils and fan-powered VAV
terminals that employ permanent split capacity (PSC)
motors. This value will be 0.13 kW/ton for electrically com-
mutated motors (ECMs), which are known as brush-less dc
motors. The fan heat deduction is 0.05 ton/ton for PSC mo-
tors and 0.04 for ECMs.
Calculating System kW/ton, EER, and COP
The summation of the two columns in the score sheet pro-
vides the total electrical input and the corrected net output on
a per ton basis for the cooling system. The values in the sum-
mation sheet permit comparisons of various HVAC system types
when auxiliary equipment is included. The format also can
help identify the electrical demand and associated performance
penalties of individual components.
The recent advances toward lower values of chiller and com-
pressor kW/ton are important. However, the end results of these
advances are not usually compared with the performance of
unitary equipment that are rated by the energy efficiency ratio
(EER). The units for EER are Btu/watt-hour (= MBtu/kWh). To
convert kWSystem/ton to system EER,

sionless term COP, which is more common and useful in the

Example Results
Table 1 was prepared to demonstrate the use of the score
sheet and to highlight the critical importance of minimizing
auxiliary demand. Five systems are compared. The first system
is a chilled water VAV system with a 0.5 kW/ton centrifugal
chiller. The assumptions made for this system include a total
supply pressure (∆hfilter + ∆hcoil + ∆hcabinet + ESP) of 4 inches of
water (1 kPa), a return fan total pressure of 2 inches (0.5 kPa), a
chilled water pump head of 75 ft (225 kPa), a condenser water
head of 75 ft (225 kPa), an axial fan cooling tower, and series
fan-powered VAV terminals. The score sheet results indicate a
system kW/ton of 1.61, an EER of 7.44, and a COP of 2.18.
While this system can hardly be classified as optimum, none
of the values are out of line with common practice.
The second system is an air-cooled chiller with a reciprocat-
ing compressor. Although this chiller has more than twice the
demand of the centrifugal compressor, the resulting values for
the system are similar (kW/ton = 1.56, EER = 7.7, COP = 2.3).
The assumptions for the auxiliary equipment were based on a
smaller constant volume air delivery system that would not
require the higher pressures of a larger air-handling unit serv-
ing longer duct runs and multiple VAV terminals.
The third option is a constant air volume rooftop unit air-
cooled DX coil. Again the compressor kW/ton is high but the
auxiliary power requirement is much lower. The kW/ton is 1.37,
EER is 8.8, and COP is 2.6. These results are typical of the
expected values for air-cooled rooftop DX units. However, the
minimization of auxiliary demand provides energy efficiency
that exceeds that of the two chilled water systems.
The fourth example is a closed-loop ground source heat
pump system. Ground loops are sized to provide 85°F (29°C)
entering water to the heat pumps on the design day. The uni-
tary equipment has scroll compressors and ECM drives on the
indoor fans, a common practice. The loop pump head repre-

Compressor
sents the value of small ground loops, either individual to
each unit or several loops in a common area connected to
several units. This system combines the features of medium
efficiency compression (0.7 kW/ton) and minimal auxiliary
demand to arrive at a system kW/ton of 0.90, an EER of 13.3,
and a COP of 3.9.
Proponents of chilled water systems could argue that be-
cause the ground source heat pump system was optimized it is
not a good comparison to the chilled water VAV system. There-
fore, values for an optimized chilled water VAV system were
input into the score sheet. The FPVAV terminals were elimi-
nated and fan pressures and pump heads were reduced below
common practice for large central systems. The results improved
significantly (kW/ton = 1.04, EER = 11.4, and COP = 3.4).
However, the values indicate the negative impact of high aux-
iliary equipment requirements is difficult to overcome even
with the most efficient chillers. Table 2 summarizes the input
to the score sheet for all five alternatives. Figure 2 shows the
results for system kW/ton, EER, and COP.
Limitations
This article identifies the assumptions used to formulate the
simplified equations for component electrical demand and
cooling capacity correction. These equations are listed and
can be adjusted if equipment with different efficiencies is used.
Users are encouraged to adapt these into a spreadsheet format
so that assumed values can be adjusted, evaluated and opti-
mized. Clearly, this procedure is not intended to substitute for
detailed energy analysis and resulting economic evaluations
of heating and cooling alternatives. The analysis focuses on
design load and many strategies have been incorporated to
reduce consumption at part load.
Additionally, the procedure does not include several other
items that can be connected to building HVAC energy con-
sumption. These include water heating, ventilation air precon-
Figure 2: Score sheet results show system kW/ton, EER and COP for five different HVAC alternatives.
ditioning, heat recovery, exhaust air fan energy, and refrigera-
tion. However, design load system efficiency is one indicator
of seasonal efficiency. If demand at extreme conditions is mini-
mized, other measures to reduce energy consumption (heat re-
covery systems, variable speed drives, direct digital controls,
etc.) will be much more effective. Furthermore, electrical de-
mand in the United States is a critical issue in many areas.
However, the reader is cautioned not to directly infer that a
savings in demand at full-load can be translated into an equiva-
lent savings in seasonal energy consumption. The values of
kW/ton, total static pressure, condenser fluid temperature, and
other values in Table 1 must be modified to reflect local ambi-
ent temperatures and correlated with their frequency of occur-
rence before energy consumption can be predicted.
Many decisions regarding building efficiency need to be
made at the beginning of system design. This procedure is
recommended to conveniently allow the designer to determine
which paths are more likely to lead to the desired goal of lower
demand and efficiency. The application of this procedure high-
lights the impact of auxiliary equipment on system consump-
tion. Careless specification of primary and auxiliary equipment
can result in elaborate and costly systems with high demand
and poor efficiencies.
References
1. U.S. Department of Energy. 1998. A Look at Commercial Build-
ings in 1995: Characteristics, Energy Consumption, and Energy Char-
acteristics. DOE/EIA-0625(95).
2. Brodrick J. and D. Westphalen. 2001. “Uncovering auxiliary en-
ergy use.” ASHRAE Journal (43)2.
3. University of Alabama. 2001. “A score sheet for system kW/ton
and EER.” Energy Conservation Curriculum (skavanaugh@
coe.eng.ua.edu).
4. ANSI/ASHRAE/IESNA Standard 90.1-2001, Energy Standard
for Buildings Except Low-Rise Residential Buildings.
5. NEMA. 1997. National Electrical Manufacturers Association, Stan-
dard MG 1-12.59, Table 12-10.
Score Sheet System EER and COP

By Thomas G. Lester, P.E., Member ASHRAE
T
About the Author
Thomas G. Lester, P.E., is a senior mechanical
engineer at Bergmann Associates, Rochester, N.Y.
he article, “Calculating Pressure Drops in Piping Systems” in the Sep-
tember 2002 issue of ASHRAE Journal, presented a procedure that
used the built-in iteration function in spreadsheets to solve the Colebrook
Equation. This is fine if an engineer is using the spreadsheet as an alterna-
tive to pressure drop tables or graphs. However, there are times when
solving for the friction factor is part of a larger solution. That larger solu-
tion might benefit from using the iteration function. However, I know of
no way to have an iteration embedded in a second iteration. In such a
case, it would be helpful to have a direct solution for the friction factor.
This article describes two spreadsheets:
one used in developing a direct solution
and a second, an electronic duct designer,
used as a practical application of the pro-
cedure. The second spreadsheet will use a
user defined function to simplify friction
factor calculation and is an alternative to
the duct designer wheels for duct sizing.
Both spreadsheets can be downloaded from
http://www.ashrae.org/journal0403.
The prior spreadsheet was based on
splitting the Colebrook Equation into
two equations, calculating a difference
between the two and performing an it-
eration that varied the friction factor
until the difference in the two equations
was zero or close to zero. It is interesting
to see how the difference plots against
the friction factor at the four extremes of
the Moody Diagram. (Refer to Figure 1
or the spreadsheet, “Lester\_Colebrook\_
Eq\_Solver.xls” under the tab “Four
Corners.”)
To produce the plots, the formulas in
Row 15 of the spreadsheet were copied to
Rows 22 through 38. The difference for-
mula in Column H was modified for rea-
sons that will become apparent later.
Starting in Cell B22, a value of 0.005 was
entered and for each of the following rows
the prior value was increased by a multi-
plier of 1.2. In the spreadsheet, the differ-
ence values were saved for each of the
four cases tested in Rows 60 through 80.
The Reynolds Numbers and relative
roughness values used were:

Each of the plots represents a graphi-
cal solution for the friction factor for that
set of parameters. The intersection of the
plot and the zero difference line is the
true value for the friction factor. The pro-
cedure that follows has been developed
to accurately determine the value of the
friction factor at this intersection.
The following article was published in ASHRAE Journal, July 2003. © Copyright 2003 American Society of Heating, Refrigerating and Air-
Conditioning Engineers, Inc. It is presented for educational purposes only. This article may not be copied and/or distributed electronically or
in paper form without permission of ASHRAE.
42
ASHRAE Journal ashrae.org
July 2003
Step 1
Starting with the equations in Rows 22 to 38, a table can be
created and the VLOOKUP function used to identify the last
point below the zero difference line before the plot crosses
this line and the difference becomes positive. The correspond-
ing friction factor is returned by the VLOOKUP function. The
objective is to find a starting value for friction factor that
would be no less than 80% of the correct friction factor. (The
80% is based on 1.2 multipliers for the friction factor in Cells
B23 through B38.)
To create a table for the VLOOKUP function, the values in
Column B are duplicated in Column I. The table then be-
comes “H22:I38.” The VLOOKUP formula in Cell B44 is
“=VLOOKUP(0,H22:I38,2).” The value that is being searched
for is “0,” or as the procedure works, the last negative value
before the difference becomes positive. The “2” represents the
second column in the table which is the friction factor corre-
sponding to difference value in Column H. (Refer to refer-
ences on Excel or Lotus functions for a more detailed
explanation.) The values in the first column of a lookup table
must be in ascending order. That is the reason for modifying
the difference formula that was mentioned earlier. The net re-
sult is that the last value below the zero difference line is
returned by the use of this function.
Step 2
Row 22 is copied to Rows 47 and 48. Using the value of
friction factor from Step 1, a slope will be approximated by
multiplying the friction factor from Step 1 by 0.9999 in Cell
B47 and by 1.0001 in Cell B48. The corresponding differences
are used to calculate the slope. The intersection of a line with
this slope and the zero difference line can be calculated with
simple triangulation. The result is plotted in Figure 2 and under
the Tab “Mag Ex” in the spreadsheet. (Note that because of the
magnification used in the scaling of the axis, the following
parameters must be used: diameter = 12 in., absolute roughness
= 0.001 ft [0.3 mm] [giving a relative roughness of 0.001], and
Reynolds Number of 150,000.) While the friction factor calcu-
lated at the intersection is not as accurate as desired, it is closer
to the true value of the friction factor than the starting value.
Step 3
Using the friction factor calculated in Step 2, the procedure
is repeated. The equations are shown in Rows 50 and 51. This
result is plotted in Figure 3 and under the Tab “XXX Mag” in
the spreadsheet. As can be seen in the plot, the new friction
factor at this intersection is approaching an acceptable level
of accuracy.
Step 4
Repeats the procedure using the value produced in Step 3.
Step 5
Repeats the procedure once more. The result from the Step 5
projection is in Cell J56. The formula in Cell D10 is basically
the same as in the previous spreadsheet, returning the value
from J56 when all conditions are satisfied. If you examine Cells
H47 through H57, you will see the difference between the two
sides of the Colebrook equation becomes smaller and smaller
with each of Steps 2 through 5. The procedure as shown has
reached its maximum accuracy at this point. Additional steps
will not converge any further unless the formulas in Column B
are revised to finer increments. At this point, the difference in
Cell H56 is a negative number and the difference in Cell H57 is
a positive number. Remember this point later when the DO-
LOOP-UNTIL procedure is used in the “user-defined function.”
Experience has shown this friction factor will be accurate to
at least six significant digits compared to the GOAL SEEK
method from the previous spreadsheet. The GOAL SEEK result
is shown in Cell D9 for comparison.
Alternative Approach
I have experimented with several alternative approaches.
With the ultimate goal of simplifying the approach so that a
user defined function could be easily created, I have found
that using an approximate method, such as the Swamee and
Jain formula, in place of the Step 1 table is a good start. The
objective in Step 1 is to get an approximate value for friction
factor. Accuracy is achieved in subsequent steps.
The Swamee and Jain equation defines friction factor as:

= the Absolute Roughness in feet
D = the diameter in feet
Re = the Reynolds Number (dimensionless)
Additional information can be found at www.agen.okstate.edu/
darcy/DarcyWeisbach/Darcy-WeisbachEq.htm.
This formula, which can be placed in a single cell, is simpler
than the 17 rows of formulas used in Step 1. While the approxi-
mate formulas have limited ranges where they are considered
accurate, I have found Swamee and Jain to be accurate within
5% of the true value of friction factor, even at the very extreme
case of 0.05 Relative Roughness and a Reynolds Number of
2000. This makes for an acceptable substitute for Step 1. While
the graphs generated from the data in Step 1 provide a “pic-
ture” in developing the non-iterative solution, both accuracy
and simplicity are improved by substituting the Swamee and
Jain equation for the Step 1 procedure when only a mathemati-
cal solution is being sought.
Steps 2 and beyond can be used as they are, though fewer
steps are required for the same degree of accuracy because the

Friction Factor
value from Step 1 is more accurate than before. The result is a
procedure that is simpler and can be written into a user-defined
function with a little knowledge of Visual Basic programming.
This has been done in the working example. The user defined
function is named, “=fCalc” and uses two arguments,
relative roughness (RelRough) and Reynolds Number
(ReynoldsNum).” (Note that
relative roughness equals abso-
lute roughness divided by di-
ameter.
Using
relative
roughness reduces the number
of arguments to two and simpli-
fies the code.) Like all build-in
functions, it can be selected from
the list of functions, found un-
der the “user defined” category.
It can also be simply written out
as “=fCalc(RelRough,Reynolds
Num) where RelRough and
ReynoldsNum can be numbers
or Cell References.
The source code can be seen in the Visual Basic editor as
Module 11. It is beyond the intent here to explain Visual Basic
modules and user-defined functions. I hope, however, that suf-
ficient remarks are provided in the code to make it easily un-
derstood. Steps 2 and beyond are replaced with
a “DO-LOOP-UNTIL” statement. The loop ends when the
DiffLow is less than zero and the DiffHigh is greater than zero.
Refer back to the explanation in Step 5 where this situation
was discussed. At this point, a highly accurate calculation of
the friction factor has been completed.
Working Example
The benefits to this approach are best seen in an example.
HVAC engineers are familiar with duct design wheels for siz-
ing ductwork. A spreadsheet version can be created using the
above procedure. Three worksheets have been created in the
workbook “Lester\_ Duct\_Designer.xls.” The first two
worksheets use the user-defined function twice, once to cal-
culate friction factor before the iterative calculation of the
exact duct dimension and a second to calculate the pressure
drop of the rounded off dimensions that the engineer chooses.
The third example uses the
function in a duct system
where pressure drop in each
section of duct is computed.
The total loss of all sections
can then be added up. It would
not be possible to use the it-
eration function to find the di-
ameter or side of the
rectangular duct without solv-
ing for the friction factor di-
rectly. Likewise, without a
user-defined function, the sec-
tion by section analysis would
be overwhelming.
Description of the Duct Designer Spreadsheet
The spreadsheet requires the primary inputs for flow and
design static loss. secondary parameters for specific volume,
absolute roughness (see 2001 ASHRAE Handbook—Funda-
mentals Table 1 on Page 34.7 for a list), and kinematic viscos-
ity allows the user to input the appropriate values for these
variables. The Round Duct worksheet is shown in Figure 4 after
the iteration for diameter has been run. The calculated outputs
should be self-explanatory or you could refer to the first spread-
sheet. Note that the friction factor in Cell G9 has been calcu-
lated using the “user defined function,” “fCalc” with cell
references for relative roughness and Reynolds Number.
The unknown is the duct diameter required to have the ac-
tual pressure drop (Cell G11) equal the design pressure drop

44
ASHRAE Journal
July 2003
(Cell C5) for the set of input parameters
used. To calculate this with the iteration
function, the friction factor had to be cal-
culated directly and then the diameter is
calculated by iteration. The correct di-
ameter will yield a pressure drop (in Cell
G11) equal to the design pressure drop
entered in Cell C5. This is accomplished
by iteration driving the difference be-
tween Cells C5 and G11 (multiplied by
100 for greater accuracy in Cell G12) to
zero by changing Cell C12 using the
“TOOLS/GOAL SEEK” function. Row 12
has been hidden in the example spread-
sheet to make this less confusing. A
macro was written to perform the iteration and it can be run
with either the macro button or the hot keys “Ctrl-D.”
Alternatively, the GOAL SEEK procedure could be run from
the menu bar. Cell C13 alerts the user when the iteration must
be run and displays the value from Cell 12 after a successful
iteration. (Note that the iteration function will not always con-
verge on a solution. This was noted particularly when a very
large diameter found in one calculation was followed by a
second calculation with a small diameter. For this reason, the
macro begins by placing a value of 20 in Cell 12. This appears
to have corrected the problem. The possibility exists, how-
ever, that the iteration might not converge in all cases. If this
occurs, manually enter a value in Cell C12 close to what you
expect the iteration to return and execute the “TOOLS/GOAL
SEEK” from the menu bar.)
The second part of the Round Duct worksheet is in Rows 53
to 60. Here the designer can select an available duct size, close
the exact diameter in Row 13 and see the actual pressure loss
for the chosen duct size. Normally, this would be an even sized
duct diameter the next size smaller than the exact diameter.
This yields a somewhat higher loss than the design loss, or
diameter the next size larger, which yields a lower pressure
loss. Whatever is chosen, the correct loss can be used in sum-
ming the total loss for the duct system.
The Rectangular Duct worksheet is very similar except it
uses the concept of hydraulic diameter, which is explained in
2001 ASHRAE Handbook—Fundamentals page 34.8. With
the rectangular duct, one side must be inputted and the other
side will be calculated with the iteration routine. As before, a
macro was created, a button drawn on the spreadsheet and the
hot keys “Ctrl-S” assigned.
The Section x Section worksheet is simply a table of round
duct sections. This is provided to demonstrate how a table could
be created for a duct system. Each row contains the calculation
for friction factor from the parameters provided in that row.
Finally, a blank worksheet is provided. By deleting the three
previous worksheets, a blank worksheet is created that con-
tains the user defined function “=fCalc” that can be used for a
new project. Note that if a new blank spreadsheet is called up,
it will not contain the “=fCalc” function. You could export the
module from the duct designer and import it into your new
spreadsheet but this is more work than is necessary. For those
unfamiliar with the Visual Basic editor, simply delete the three
worksheets to create a blank with “=fCalc.”
Summary and Conclusion
The approach described in this article offers several advan-
tages to the user:
1. Actual values for specific volume, absolute roughness
and viscosity can be used. This can significantly improve ac-
curacy of the results.
2. Friction factor can be calculated to greater accuracy than
previously possible.
3. Experienced spreadsheet users will find it easier than other
methods.
4. An entire duct or pipe system can be evaluated with the
friction factor calculated directly from the user defined func-
tion, “fCalc.”
Figure 4: Round duct worksheet after the iteration for diameter has been run.
