August 2003
ASHRAE Journal
43
ing system control and space comfort. In addition, fan motor
energy and its related cost are reduced.
Small electronic variable speed fan drives are being installed
on residential heating and cooling units. Rather than shutting
off when the thermostat is not calling for heating or cooling,
the fan runs at low speed, which decreases the propensity for
air stratification. In two-story homes with the additional verti-
cally separated occupied spaces, continuous low airflow sig-
nificantly decreases stratification and provides more uniform
space temperature control, which increases comfort.
In another example of relationships, porous chilled water
pipe insulation thickness is sometimes increased in humid
environments not to save energy as much as to decrease the
propensity for condensation forming on the outer surface. If
moisture condenses, the insulating properties and resulting
insulation performance can be lost. If severe enough, this
affects the reliability and control of the end-use point equip-
ment. A better solution is to install zero permeability insula-
tion in this type of environment.
Many of Missouri’s larger state-owned facilities have cen-
tral primary/secondary chilled water systems with multiple
constant flow chillers in the primary system and a variable
flow secondary system. Providing the coldest available chilled
water to the secondary loop and their downstream coils re-
quires that the primary loop’s flow rate be equal to or greater
than the secondary loop’s flow rate. If the primary’s flow rate
is less than the secondary, mixing occurs, which results in
increased secondary chilled water supply temperature. Con-
sequently, when operating near full load conditions, air han-
dlers’ cooling coils may not have sufficient capacity to control
space humidity loads and temperatures.
Operators have, on occasion, run the least number of chillers
to save chiller compressor, condenser pump, chilled water pump
and cooling tower fan electrical energy. However, even with
these energy-saving intentions, the primary loop’s flow rate is
generally less than the secondary resulting in a supply water
temperature increase and potential loss of space humidity and
temperature control. In the need’s hierarchy, space humidity and
temperature control are a higher priority than energy efficiency.
Building envelope renovation projects, such as window re-
placements, generally are justified based on energy cost sav-
ings. Old windows that have lost their weatherproof seals can
cause moisture to penetrate the structure. In addition, infiltra-
tion air can make the occupied spaces uncomfortable. Long-
term building envelope integrity and room temperature control
are higher needs than energy efficiency. When requesting funds
for window replacement, the budget request can be based on
the building envelope’s integrity and environmental control
issues with the added bonus of energy and cost savings.
Sometimes, energy conservation projects can meet several
needs simultaneously. For example, in central steam heating
plants with low summer flow requirements, a new base load
boiler can provide all three needs. Adding boiler capacity in-
creases plant reliability, the smaller boiler more closely matches
the plant’s loads, resulting in better control and stabilization
of steam pressure while also increasing energy efficiency.
Project Budget and Other Design Issues
From a broader perspective, the hierarchy is secondary to
the project budget (Figure 2). In addition, the integrity of the
HVAC budget must be maintained throughout the design pro-
cess to ensure a successful project.
The extent and resulting cost of safety components in resi-
dential systems, for example, tends to be less sophisticated
than in large institutional or industrial systems. Regarding
reliability, a small residence generally has one space-heating
furnace, whereas an institutional heating plant probably has
Historical Perspective
One hundred and fifty years ago, boiler explosions were
a frequent occurrence. Over the following years with
better understanding of pressure vessel and other en-
ergy systems’ design methods along with better materi-
als, manufacturing methods and favorable economics,
coupled with inspection and operating procedures, boiler
explosions and major equipment failures are extremely
rare today. With safety no longer a dominant concern,
system reliability was further addressed. Again, as de-
sign knowledge and procedures evolved along with bet-
ter materials, manufacturing methods and the econom-
ics of the market place coupled with inspections and op-
erating procedures, reliable full load followed by modu-
lating part load system operation became the mainstay.
At the third level, system control started from the very
simple mechanical and with time led to the more com-
plex. As means and methods were developed, pneu-
matic, electric then electronic, leading to today’s Internet
enhanced, direct digital control systems evolved. As the
issues of safety, reliability and system control evolved,
closely behind was the energy efficiency issue.
For most of the first seventy plus years of the last cen-
tury, this country’s abundant and cheap fuels resulted in
little attention to this need. There were obvious excep-
tions with very large energy conversion systems, like coal-
fired electric generating plants. However, for the most
part in our industry, energy efficiency was a low priority.
In the 1960’s for example, a favorite HVAC system was
the reheat system because of its excellent space tem-
perature control capabilities. The 1973/74-energy crunch
quickly gave energy efficiency a much higher priority. A
second energy price shock wave occurred in 1979/80
further raising concerns about increasing utility costs and
better HVAC system energy performance. In response to
quickly rising building HVAC energy costs, ASHRAE Stan-
dard 90-1975, Energy Conservation in New Building De-
sign, was developed and followed by updated versions
in 1980, 1989, 1999 and 2001.
44
ASHRAE Journal ashrae.org
August 2003
firm capacity, i.e., with the loss of the largest unit; the remain-
ing units have the capacity to carry the peak load. Residen-
tial heating and cooling controls are generally two positions,
i.e., on or off. More complex and expensive institutional con-
trols are generally modulating with proportional action or
additional degrees of control.
Most safety needs are mandatory and are met by complying
with various building codes and regulations along with manu-
facturers’ industry standards. Reliability and control needs are
to a larger extent dependent upon the owners’ design guide-
lines, operating standards and maintenance requirements. Sys-
tem energy efficiency needs are generally based on the owners’
minimum energy efficiency standard or economic decisions
based on simple payback period, rate of return, saving-to-
investment ratio or, perhaps, life-cycle cost.
Design of energy conservation measures can be compro-
mised if the previous hierarchy issues are not properly under-
stood and implemented. From the more general design
perspective, HVAC systems can also be compromised during
the design process if several other issues are not considered.
The design must be integrated, incorporating the combined
requirements of the various systems and equipment needs.
Furthermore, HVAC system components must be appropriate,
suitable for the specific application and meeting the owners’
project requirements. The integrity of the HVAC budget must
be maintained to ensure that a reliable, functional and main-
tainable design is not placed in jeopardy. Value engineering
is an excellent tool for providing an effective and economi-
cal system. However, this tool can impact HVAC systems nega-
tively given minimal building codes and inadequate or poorly
documented planning and design criteria.
Other Supporting Issues
Providing a safe, reliable, controlled and energy efficient
design of energy conservation measures requires several other
supporting issues (Figure 2). Owners must have a planned main-
tenance program to ensure that equipment and systems per-
form their intended functions throughout their service life.
Without adequate maintenance, equipment performance de-
grades with time, decreasing reliability, reducing the prob-
ability of good system control and resulting in possible lower
energy cost savings. Adequate equipment access for routine
and other types of maintenance is essential.
Sufficient operating and maintenance (O&M) staff must be
available and capable of operating and maintaining the energy
conservation measures. A higher level of technology requires a
more knowledgeable staff. Accordingly, upgrading direct digi-
tal controls should be accompanied by additional staff training.
Ample automation hardware along with the appropriate soft-
ware needs to be available along with the proper tools and equip-
ment to complete the required tasks. O&M budgets must be
sufficient to attract, train and retain staff as well as management.
Continued management support is critical. Easy access to
relevant project information including: design data, as-con-
structed drawings, commissioning documents, testing, adjust-
ing and balancing reports, approved shop drawings of all
significant equipment components, performance tests and
O&M manuals should be provided.
Conclusions
Over the last 50 years, we have witnessed a steady increase
in our standard of living and resulting quality of life provided
in large part by various types of machines, our mechanical/
electrical “servants.” However, these machine servants, like
human servants, need food. The machines’ food is “energy.”
One method of making energy available for other and future
uses is by increasing the energy performance of existing sys-
tems. Actually realizing the calculated energy cost savings
when proposing energy conservation measures requires an
understanding and then the application of a specific set of
hierarchical design needs.
Turbulent times 60 years ago motivated Abraham Maslow
to think about a model of human needs and their hierarchical
relationships. The HVAC design needs model provides the
entry-level engineer with a simple structure for understand-
ing the hierarchical relationships of energy conservation mea-
sures. Additionally, the model provides the journeyman
engineer with a hierarchical concept to better understand,
and then apply, the more complex energy conservation de-
sign relationships. Better understanding will help to bring
about safe, reliable and in control “sustained energy effi-
cient systems.”
References
1. Maslow, A.H. 1954. Motivation and Personality. Harper &
Row Publishers, Inc.
2. ANSI/ASHRAE Standard 55-1992, Thermal Environmen-
tal Conditions for Human Occupancy.
3. Coad, W.J. 1998. “A fundamental perspective on chilled
water systems.” Heating/Piping/Air Conditioning (8):59–66.

About the Author
Rick VanSeters is a product engineer with Parker
Hannifin, Refrigerating Specialties Division, in
Broadview, Ill.
he efficiency and operating flexibility advantages of liquid recircula-
tion systems — relative to direct expansion — more than offset
their added complexity in larger refrigeration systems. The basic ele-
ments of a liquid recirculation system, sometimes referred to as an
overfeed system, are shown in Figure 1. As the term “overfeed” im-
plies, much more liquid is fed to the evaporator than actually is vapor-
ized. By overfeeding the evaporator, the coil inner surface is kept thor-
oughly wetted and has optimum heat transfer.
lead to serious operating difficulties.
Knowing a few basic principles about
their design and operation can keep these
systems operating satisfactorily and prof-
itably.
Selecting the Pump
Overfeed & Recirculation Rates
Overfeed & Recirculation Rates
Overfeed & Recirculation Rates
Overfeed & Recirculation Rates
Overfeed & Recirculation Rates
Overfeed rate is the ratio of the liquid
mass that exits the evaporator to the va-
por mass exiting. The recirculation rate
(not to be confused with overfeed rate) is
the ratio of the liquid mass that enters
the evaporator to the vapor mass that
exits. If all of the liquid is vaporized, the
overfeed rate is zero and the recircula-
tion rate is one. Recirculation and over-
feed rates vary for different systems as
shown in Tables 1 and 2.
Overfeeding also ensures the vapor ex-
iting the coil is close to saturation (not
superheated), permitting lower compres-
sor inlet temperatures. Furthermore, the
high and low sides are effectively
decoupled in recirculation systems,
which allows more efficient operation of
the condensers. This article focuses on the
installation and operation of centrifugal
pumps in a liquid recirculation system.
The advantages of this approach, com-
pared to direct expansion are summarized
in the sidebar, “Overfeed Advantages.”
The low-pressure receiver, sometimes
called an accumulator, contains saturated
liquid refrigerant. Using a pump to move
this saturated liquid from one place to
another is a difficult task. Even when the
low-pressure receiver and pump are sized
properly, interactions between them can

Types of Centrifugal P
ypes of Centrifugal P
ypes of Centrifugal P
ypes of Centrifugal P
ypes of Centrifugal Pumps
umps
umps
umps
umps
Centrifugal pumps are open or her-
metic. In hermetic pumps, liquid re-
frigerant circulates through the
motor’s rotating parts to lubricate
bearings and carry away heat. A thin
shell separates the rotor and stator
windings. Since bearings rely on liq-
uid refrigerant for cooling and lubri-
cation, they cannot be allowed to run
dry ever. If minimum flow through
the pump is not provided, the motor
heat vaporizes the refrigerant inside,
and the bearings run dry. However,
when properly installed and oper-
ated, hermetic pumps are leak-proof and dependable.
Open, or non-hermetic, pumps have a lower initial cost and are
repaired more easily in the field than hermetic pumps. Also, open
pumps are well-suited for applications with frequent starting and
stopping, such as liquid transfer. Starting and stopping a her-
metic pump can cause sporadic flow through motor windings.
The windings heat up and the bearings can run dry. Open pumps
are better suited for applications where pumping is erratic.
Selecting the Pump Size
Selecting the Pump Size
Selecting the Pump Size
Selecting the Pump Size
Selecting the Pump Size
Once the decision has been made to use either a hermetic or
open pump, the correct pump size must be selected for the
specific application (see sidebar “Defining ‘Head’”).
The dashed curve in Figure 2 shows how total head increases
with flow through a typical refrigeration system. The solid curve
in Figure 2 shows how much liquid a typical centrifugal pump
can move against different flow resistances. The intersection of
pump curve and system curve defines the system’s operating
point, which is how much flow circulates in a particular system
The following discussion briefly compares liquid overfeed
and direct expansion (DX) for ammonia and R-22 systems. The
advantages of decoupling the high and low sides of the system
by using liquid recirculation have been omitted because this
issue is better explained in an examination of condenser perfor-
mance. Also, exact energy and equipment costs are too vari-
able to be included here.
Ammonia liquid overfeed systems are more common than
halocarbon recirculation systems. One reason is the large dif-
ference in flow volumes between the two refrigerants. A 100-ton
(350 kW) system with a 0°F (–18°C) evaporator requires about
35 lb/min (0.85 cfm [0.4 L/s]) of saturated liquid ammonia to
provide the desired refrigerating effect. By comparison, an R-22
system requires 212 lb/min (2.5 cfm [1.2 L/s]) of liquid flow. As
the temperature drops, the mass and volume flow rates also
Advantages of Overfeed Systems
drop. For ammonia, accurately distributing this small flow among
several DX cooling units becomes problematic as orifices be-
come smaller. Therefore, liquid recirculation especially is help-
ful for ammonia, where system control is improved by increasing
refrigerant flow rate. However, regardless of the type of refriger-
ant, liquid overfeed can significantly reduce the total system
power requirements.
Consider the 100-ton (350 kW) R-22 system. With a 3:1 recir-
culation rate, a 10 hp (7.5 kW) pump is needed to move the
required 57 gpm (0.4 L/s) against a 50-ft (150 kPa) system
head. (For an ammonia system, only a 3 hp [2.2 kW] pump
would be required for a 19 gpm [1.2 L/s] flow rate.) The pump,
liquid accumulator, and associated piping represent the bulk of
the extra equipment cost of the overfeed system. The extra pump
horsepower represents a significant additional operating cost.
with a particular pump. Ideally, the operating point coincides
with the refrigerant flow needed to satisfy process loads, and
will be at or near the peak efficiency point of the pump.
If a pump was specifically designed for a particular appli-
cation, the goal of peak efficiency at the operating point
could be matched precisely. Unfortunately, the true system
curve cannot be determined precisely through calculation.
Even if the exact curve could be determined, the actual flow
rate in the system would vary depending on a number of fac-
tors. Refrigerant flow through individual evaporators is typi-
cally fine-tuned for a specific load with valves located just
upstream (Figure 1). The total load on the system can vary for
a number of reasons.
Even if the system flow could be determined with precision
during design, it is impractical to custom design a pump for
each new application. Although a pump curve can be varied
somewhat by the motor’s speed, this approach is somewhat
limited by the geometry of the pump impeller. Also, speed
controls add another level of complexity and expense.

ASHRAE Journal
37
Because it can be impractical to tailor a pump’s operating
curve to a particular system, it is more common for a designer
to select an off-the-shelf, fixed-speed pump. These pumps have
high operating efficiencies over a broad range of flows. The
designer selects a pump capable of moving the maximum an-
ticipated system flow at the highest total head. A small amount
of flow beyond what’s needed for process loads can be returned
to the low-pressure receiver via a bypass line. Naturally, if the
pump were grossly oversized, this bypass flow would represent
a significant amount of wasted energy.
Installing the Pump
Once the right pump has been selected for an application, it
needs to be connected to the system. Even a high-quality pump
can have operating difficulties if it is installed improperly.
Most operational problems are related to the flow of the refrig-
erant through the pump impeller. These problems often can be
traced to the size and arrangement of the equipment connected
The performance of an evaporator is based on two main
factors: 1) the temperature difference between the refrigerant
inside and the air (or other fluid) outside the coil, and 2) the
heat transfer rate. In a DX system the temperature difference is
reduced because of the need to superheat the refrigerant, and
the heat transfer rate is reduced as more refrigerant vaporizes
within the coil. The performance penalty can be substantial for
an evaporator operating in a DX system. One manufacturer
shows the capacity of a given evaporator can be 10% to 20%
less when used in a DX system, compared to liquid overfeed.
Thus, the DX system requires a larger evaporator, partially off-
setting the extra equipment cost of the overfeed system.
Larger evaporators frequently require a step up in fan horse-
power. This increases the total required refrigerating capacity
of the system. For example, a 100-ton (350 kW) DX system
might need evaporators with 45 total fan hp (34 kW). An over-
feed system needs less coil area and only 30 fan hp (22 kW)
because of the better heat transfer. The extra 15 fan hp (11 kW)
inside the refrigerated space for a DX system adds about 5 tons
(18 kW) to the system load. In addition to the extra equipment
cost of a larger evaporator with larger motors, the operating
cost increases because extra fan heat energy must be removed.
The net difference in equipment cost between a system with a
larger evaporator and a system with pump and accumulator
depends on details of the specific application. The tradeoff here
may or may not be in favor of liquid overfeed. As alluded to
earlier, decoupling the high and low sides of the system with
liquid recirculation provides operating flexibility to lower con-
densing temperature in cold weather. Again, this may or may
not be advantageous in light of hot gas needs. However, the net
5 hp (3.7 kW) reduction in power required for the R-22 system
(12 hp [9 kW] for ammonia), as well as the 5-ton (18 kW) load
reduction over the life of a 100-ton (350 kW) system, justifies the
liquid recirculation approach.
Pumps are rated in terms of flow capacity and flow resis-
tance. In the United States, capacity is given in gallons per
minute. Flow resistance is expressed in terms of pressure
loss (pump head), specified in feet.
To understand using units of feet to quantify pressure,
imagine a tall, empty vertical pipe. Plug the bottom of the
pipe with a piston. As the pipe gradually fills with water,
you would need to press harder and harder to hold the
piston in place against the weight of the rising column of
water. Since you’re not trying to move the piston, only hold
it in place, the height of the column of liquid is the “static
head.” You can relate the force needed to apply to the
height of the column and density of the liquid. This allows
you to relate the height of the column in feet to traditional
force per unit area.
Then imagine you push the piston upward and water
begins to flow out of the top of the pipe. The harder and
faster you push, the faster water flows out. The pressure
applied in excess of the static head is called the “dynamic
head.” Now, imagine the top of the pipe is connected to a
series of long horizontal pipes, elbows, and valves. You
can see that the relationship between the dynamic head
and the flow volume depends on flow velocities, friction
factors and valve characteristics. (Calculation of dynamic
head is a complex procedure and beyond the scope of
this article.) The sum of the static and dynamic heads is
total system head.
Defining ‘Head’
to the pump. A typical pump and receiver arrangement might
look like Figure 3 for a hermetic pump.
To understand the influences of the pump connections, it is
necessary to understand net positive suction head (NPSH).
Liquid inside the receiver is at saturated conditions, so any
drop in pressure causes it to partially vaporize. When the re-
frigerant enters the pump impeller, it is accelerated, and the
pressure necessarily drops temporarily. Any resultant vapor
can cause cavitation or partially block the impeller inlet. Cavi-
tation is the violent implosion of vapor “cavities” that can
damage the pump. Any partial blockage of the impeller inlet

August 2003
can reduce system flow and potentially damage a hermetic
pump. To prevent liquid from flashing to a vapor when it en-
ters the impeller, the pressure there needs to be increased suffi-
ciently above saturation. This is what NPSH accomplishes. By
mounting the pump some distance below the receiver, the
weight of the liquid column above increases the inlet pressure
sufficiently so cavitation will not occur. The connection from
the bottom of the receiver to the pump is called the drop leg.
The height of the liquid column head required to prevent cavi-
tation is sometimes called NPSHR.
Even with adequate NPSHR, if flow through the system is
stopped or drastically reduced, cavitation can still occur. For
example, evaporator shutdown or a blocked pump inlet
strainer, can reduce pump inlet flow to the point where some
of the refrigerant actually reverses direction and simply spins
in place at the impeller. The vorticity of this recirculation can
lead to cavitation. Protection against low flow can be pro-
vided by either a differential pressure switch installed be-
tween the pump inlet and discharge, or by a flow sensor
mounted in the discharge pipe.
Another method of protecting the pump against low-
flow/high-backpressure conditions is to install a bypass line
downstream of the pump. A pressure regulator in this line opens
if the pump discharge pressure rises too high, and the flow is
routed directly back to the receiver.
Preventing cavitation means vapor will not be generated
inside the impeller. However, remember that vapor and liquid
exist in equilibrium inside the receiver. So, even if vapor gen-
eration within the pump is prevented, it is possible for vapor to
flow with the liquid into the impeller from the receiver vessel.
As with cavitation, vapor ingestion can reduce pumping ca-
pacity, disrupting the system, and damaging a hermetic pump.
Operating conditions that can lead to vapor ingestion include:
• If liquid drains quickly at a time of low level in the re-
ceiver, a vortex can form similar to the whirlpool seen when
draining a bathtub. If the bottom of this whirlpool becomes
entrained by the liquid refrigerant moving into the drop leg,
vapor can be pulled into the pump. For this reason, a vortex
breaker typically is installed by the vessel manufacturer.
• Refrigeration load variations can result in pressure tran-
sients within the receiver. A pressure drop that is faster than
about 1 psi (6.9 kPa) per minute causes boiling to occur below
the liquid surface. Bubbles near the mouth of the drop leg can
be entrained by the flow into the pump.
• When the liquid level within the receiver drops low enough
so that the liquid makeup solenoid opens, new refrigerant en-
ters as a mixture of liquid and vapor. The vapor is drawn into
the compressor, and the compressor loads to a higher capacity.
When the proper liquid level is restored, the makeup solenoid
closes, but the compressor continues to run at the higher ca-
pacity for a short time. During this time, the receiver pressure
drops and liquid refrigerant begins to boil.
• During defrost, hot gas is allowed into the evaporator and
warms the coils. Eventually the pressure builds, the defrost
regulator opens, and the hot gas moves down the wet return
line into the receiver. The superheated gas causes the refriger-
ant to boil, forming bubbles below the liquid surface. Also, as
before, the compressors respond by loading to a higher capac-
ity. At the conclusion of defrost, the compressor continues to

Advertisement in the print edition formerly in this space.
August 2003
ASHRAE Journal
39
run at the higher capacity, resulting in the pressure drop de-
scribed previously.
Protection from vapor ingestion is largely provided by the
depth of the liquid inside the receiver. The liquid depth is influ-
enced by the construction of the receiver, for example, whether
it is horizontal or vertical. Also, the locations of the various
vessel connections can contribute to, or prevent, vapor inges-
tion. For example, a liquid makeup line that dumps directly
above the pump inlet will agitate the liquid and cause bubbles.
The total distance from the minimum liquid level in the vessel
to the pump is the actual net positive suction head, or NPSHA.
Because it is not always possible to avoid operating condi-
tions that can lead to vapor ingestion, arrange the system to be
as forgiving as possible. Avoid horizontal runs in the drop leg. If
there must be a horizontal section, angle the pipe slightly to
permit vapor bubbles to rise back into the receiver. Specify a
large diameter for the drop leg to keep the velocity of the refrig-
erant low. This allows any entrained bubbles to float easily back
into the receiver. Connect the drop leg directly to the pump
inlet with a low-pressure drop stop valve. The low-pressure drop
helps maintain maximum NPSH. A reducer connects the larger
diameter drop leg to the stop valve. On horizontal runs, this
reducer should be eccentric, and installed to allow bubbles to
return to the receiver easily.
Because the pumps discussed here were designed to move
refrigerant, not viscous oil, significant oil quantities can dam-
age a pump. The mouth of the drop leg should protrude about
2 in. (50 mm) into the receiver to prevent any oil collecting at
the bottom of the vessel from being ingested by the pump.
(The receiver also should have a separate oil trap.)
Summary
Liquid recirculation can provide an efficient and flexible
approach to large refrigeration systems. Centrifugal pumps are
only one of the elements in successful operation of these sys-
tems. Selecting the pump for an application is straightforward
once the system capacity and head are known. Even with prop-
erly selected components, improper arrangement can adversely
affect pump performance and possibly damage the pump.
While quality pump manufacturers are aware of the issues
discussed here, each manufacturer has its own approach to
addressing them. It is crucial to carefully follow specific manu-
facturer recommendations for NPSH, pipe sizes, and other
factors.

ASHRAE Journal
57
Air-To-Air Energy
Recovery Heat Exchangers
By John Dieckmann, Member ASHRAE, Kurt W. Roth, Ph.D., Associate Member ASHRAE,
and James Brodrick, Ph.D., Member ASHRAE
A
This is the sixth article covering a new energy-saving technol-
ogy evaluated in a recent U.S. Department of Energy report,
which is available at www.eren.doe.gov/buildings/documents.
ir-to-air energy recovery heat exchangers (ERHXs) include
heat recovery ventilators (HRVs) and energy recovery
ventilators (ERVs). They are placed in ventilation units,
unitary air conditioners, and air-handling units that take in
outdoor air (OA) while venting indoor air (Figure 1). Exhaust
air from the building interior passes through one side of the
exchanger, counter flow to the incoming makeup air, which
passes through the other side of the exchanger. During the cool-
ing season, the (cooler) indoor air precools the incoming OA.
The transfer of heat reverses during the heating season, i.e., the
exchanger transfers heat from the warmer indoor air to preheat
the incoming OA. The heat exchanger may transfer sensible
heat only (HRV) or both sensible and latent heat (ERV). Typical
heat and enthalpy exchange efficiencies range from 55% to
almost 80%.1 ARI Standard 1060 and ASHRAE Standard 84
form the basis for rigorous performance ratings of ERHXs.
Several configurations of air-to-air ERHXs exist. Plate-fin
arrangements transfer only sensible heat between the makeup
and exhaust airstreams. Plate arrangements constructed with
moisture permeable plastic can transfer latent heat as well,
although no product based on this principle has been commer-
cialized on a significant scale.
Heat and enthalpy wheels are slowly rotating discs made of
thin metal, plastic, paper or ceramic surfaces, such as honey-
comb or a random woven screen mesh, to create large surface
areas. Enthalpy wheels use the same types of heat transfer sur-
faces, but add desiccant material, typically silica gel or a mo-
lecular sieve (adhered to the matrix material), that enable total
enthalpy transfer, that is, both mass (moisture) and heat transfer.
Implementation of energy recovery wheels in rooftop units
is limited primarily to niche applications where the benefits
are obvious, e.g., exhaust fan unit replacement in high-humid-
ity locations and/or high makeup air applications. Air-to-air
ERHXs can be integrated with single package rooftop unitary
air conditioners (Figure 2). Alternatively, most major air-
conditioning manufacturers offer add-on accessory ERHX
packages for select AC unit models.
For an ERHX to operate effectively, the exhaust airflow must
meet two key requirements. First, the flow rate must equal a
significant fraction of the makeup airflow rate (>~75%). Sec-
ond, the temperature and humidity of the exhaust air must be
close to that of the conditioned space, i.e., heat losses or gains
in the return and exhaust ductwork must be small.
Energy Savings Potential
When OA is introduced into the interior space of a building at
a higher or lower temperature than the interior temperature, it
must be cooled or heated (respectively) to bring it to the space
temperature. By exchanging heat with the exhaust airstream to
precool (during cooling season) or preheat (heating season) in-
coming OA, heat exchangers reduce the sensible portion of the
ventilation-induced air-conditioning and heating loads. En-
thalpy exchangers also transfer humidity and thus diminish the
latent cooling and heating (dehumidification and humidifica-
tion, respectively) portion of the ventilation load.
On the other hand, ERHXs increase ventilation fan energy
because of the additional pressure drop posed by the devices.
ASHRAE’s Handbook1 indicates that wheel and flat-plate heat
exchanger pressure drops (in inches of water) range from 0.25 to
1.0 (62 to 250 Pa) and 0.1 to 1.5 (25 to 375 Pa), respectively. In
practice, the ERHX face velocity has a large impact on the mag-
nitude of the increase in ventilation fan energy consumption.
In a reasonably tight building, ERVs can reduce annual cool-
ing and heating energy consumption by about one-third.2,3
Taking fan energy into account, an ERV has a maximum na-
tional energy savings potential of 0.5 to 0.6 quads in commer-
cial buildings. In addition, ERVs can reduce peak heating and
cooling loads by up to one-third.2 Actual energy savings and
peak cooling load reductions for a specific building depend
greatly upon local climate and building OA requirements.
Market Factors
In addition to reducing HVAC energy consumption, ERHXs
also reduce the required air-conditioning capacity for the build-
ing because they decrease the contribution of the OA to the
design air-conditioning load. For instance, a simple calcula-
tion shows that roughly every 170 cfm (80 L/s) exhausted from
an ERV reduces peak air-conditioning tonnage by 1 ton (3.5
kW).\* The decrease in required air-conditioning tonnage off-
\* Assuming that the enthalpy exchange effectiveness equals 70% and
the following indoor and outdoor conditions: 75oF (24°C) at 50% RH,
95oF (35°C) at 67% RH.

58

Duct Wall (typ.)
sets the first-cost premium of the ERHXs and leads to attrac-
tive payback periods of about two years.
Although ERVs appear to have attractive economics in a size-
able percentage of applications, only about 1% of potential ap-
plications specify ERVs. ERHXs for ventilation suffer from a
perception of higher first cost in the marketplace, in some in-
stances because HVAC system designers do not take full credit
for the offset in chiller or unitary air-conditioner capacity (cost)
afforded by the device. The heat/enthalpy transfer surfaces of an
ERHX require periodic cleaning to maintain effectiveness. The
frequency depends on the cleanliness of the OA and exhaust air.
In colder climates during the heating season, frosting protection
may be necessary. Heat wheels and enthalpy wheels also are per-
ceived as having greater maintenance requirements than flat plate
heat exchanger devices, due to moving parts and past opera-
tional experiences with early equipment, although current de-
signs have corrected the reliability problems of early equipment.
References
1. 2000 ASHRAE Handbook—HVAC Systems and Equipment, Chap-
ter 44: Air-to-Air Energy Recovery.
2. TIAX. 2002. “Energy consumption characteristics of commercial
building HVAC systems – volume III: energy savings potential.” Final
Report to U.S. DOE, Office of Building Technologies, July.
Kurt W. Roth, Ph.D., is a senior engineer at the Appliance
and Building Technology sector of TIAX, Cambridge, Mass.
John Dieckmann is a principal at the HVAC and Refrigeration
Technology sector of TIAX. James Brodrick, Ph.D., is a project
manager, Building Technologies Program, U.S. Department of
Energy, Washington, D.C.

ASHRAE Journal ashrae.org
September 2003
he two essential requirements for the development of the North
American skyscraper are considered to be the elevator and air con-
ditioning. However, contemporary photographs of skyscrapers in the
1920s show they had opening sash windows indicating that, at least for
the upper floors, natural ventilation was used. This is despite air condi-
tioning becoming more common in industrial, commercial and enter-
tainment buildings from the early 1900s onwards.
building but according to architect Solis
Seiferth, constructing a skyscraper was
entirely the idea of Gov. Huey P. Long.
The building is still the tallest state capi-
tol building in the United States.1
The capitol was constructed during
1930–32 at a cost of $5 million. The me-
chanical design engineer was F.H.
Chisholm of Baton Rouge, La. The build-
ing includes two large assembly chambers
on the ground floor (Senate and House of
Representatives), two courts of law and
some 19 floors of offices. The original ser-
vices appear to have been as follows:
By the 1930s, a number of new sky-
scrapers claimed to be air-conditioned
including the Philadelphia PSFS build-
ing completed in 1932.
To see what part, if any, air condition-
ing played in the design of a North Ameri-
can skyscraper of this period, the author
visited the Louisiana State Capitol build-
ing in Baton Rouge, La., 70 miles (113
km) west of New Orleans.
Architects Weiss, Dreyfous and Seiferth
of New Orleans designed this distinctive
34-story art deco style skyscraper. The
Nebraska State Capitol inspired the
Heating. A 15 psi (104 kPa) steam sys-
tem that fed under-window radiators
served all of the office areas. Three natu-
ral gas-fired boilers generated the steam.
Ventilation. The offices had natural
ventilation by opening windows. The as-
sembly chambers and other areas used
100% outside air mechanical supply and
extract systems with filters and steam bat-
tery tempered supplies. These systems
tended to be very elaborate. Figure 1
shows some of the complex ductwork in
the basement necessary to supply and
extract the high air change rates called
for in the debating chambers above.
Air Conditioning?
One major service provided through-
out the building was an “ice water” sys-
tem for drinking fountains. The
building’s specification2 called for two
reciprocating refrigeration compressors
By J. Michael Barber
About the Author
J. Michael Barber is with the Liverpool School
of Architecture and Building Engineering, The Uni-
versity of Liverpool, England.
T
The following article was published in ASHRAE Journal, September 2003. © Copyright 2003 American Society of Heating, Refrigerating and
Air-Conditioning Engineers, Inc. It is presented for educational purposes only. This article may not be copied and/or distributed electronically
or in paper form without permission of ASHRAE.
September 2003
ASHRAE Journal
45
using carbonic anhydride (carbon dioxide?) refrigerant, one
of 4 in. (102 mm) bore × 6 in. (152 mm) stroke with a 50 hp
(37 kW) motor and the other 3¼ in. (83 mm) bore × 6 in. (152
mm) stroke with an unspecified motor. It was evidently de-
cided that this system was of sufficient cooling capacity to
also provide space cooling (i.e., air conditioning), and con-
sequently was used for cooling the supply air
to the Judge’s reception rooms on the 22nd
and 23rd floors, and to two restaurants on the
ground floor.
These cooling systems appear to have been
fresh air supply with filters, steam coils and
cooler batteries fed with chilled water from the
ice water system. The filters are reported to have
had a novel automatic self-cleaning system,
“Cleaning of the filters is automatic; when the
fan of the unit is shut down the deceleration
starts a motor which operates a washing and
cleaning unit in the filter apparatus.”3
Original mechanical services drawings, dated
November 1930,4 show the mechanical venti-
lation systems and the ice water system, but
show no trace of the air-conditioning systems
described earlier. However, these are described
in later contemporary publications,3,5,6 so it appears that the
air-conditioning systems were something of an afterthought.
A contemporary account written to mark the building’s open-
ing in 1932 reads:
“With hot and cold and ice water flowing on every floor
of the building, an air cooling system for the dining room,
lunch room and judges’ reception rooms and a combina-
tion supply and exhaust ventilation system for the five
large rooms in which large numbers of people
will be assembled, no detail for the comfort
of the users of the building has been over-
looked by Weiss, Dreyfous and Seiferth, Ar-
chitects, who designed the layout, and the
American Plumbing and Heating company,
and the Holzer Sheet Metal Company, who
have made the installations.” 7
The Capitol Today
The building continues to serve its purpose
as the state capitol, and is now fully air condi-
tioned (ca. 1955) with little appearing to remain
of the original 1931 HVAC systems.
Conclusions
The building as designed, was basically not
air-conditioned, using natural ventilation for of-
fice floors. Thermal comfort in the assembly chambers was
achieved by providing high air change rates of outside air (12
Louisiana State Capitol used mainly mechanical and natural ventilation.
1930s postcard of capitol.
...water that had
circulated through
air-cooling coils
was apparently
used for drinking!
No worries about
such things as
Legionella in
those days!
‘
’
Figure 1: The Louisiana State Capitol’s original basement plan showing ductwork.
Advertisement in the print edition formerly in this space.
September 2003
ASHRAE Journal
47
ach). To do this satisfactorily required extensive and complex
ductwork to get the air in and out without creating unaccept-
able air movement. However, three internal areas were belatedly
air-conditioned using chilled water taken from the extensive
‘ice water’ drinking fountain system. Hence, water that had cir-
culated through air-cooling coils was apparently used for drink-
ing! No worries about such things as Legionella in those days!
Thus, in this particular design of 1929–30, it appears that
mechanical refrigeration was considered best used for aiding
the occupants’ general comfort by providing them with copi-
ous supplies of chilled drinking water rather than a mechani-
cally cooled (i.e., air conditioned) environment.
An interesting feature described in a contemporary trade
magazine article3 is that the extract ventilation from the kitchen
was passed “through a radi-ion, which deodorises the air and
puts ozone into it before discharge” to outside. Treatment for
the extract air rather than the supply!
Therefore, in this particular example, air conditioning had a
modest role in the building’s design. Instead, reliance was
placed on natural ventilation or high air-change rate mechani-
cal ventilation systems for thermal comfort. One wonders if
design engineer Chisholm calculated the savings in ductwork
costs and fan power that would have occurred if he had made
more use of air conditioning in his building. If he did, he prob-
ably concluded the high cost of refrigeration at that time can-
celled out savings elsewhere.
Acknowledgments
The author would like to thank Pat Pickens, building man-
ager of the capitol, and Bill Le Blanc for help in facilitating
his research. Regretfully, lack of research time has precluded
this story being told in greater depth. It would be good if the
work could be continued to unearth the full story of the design
decisions by Chisholm and his team, who were working at that
exciting time when comfort air conditioning in the workplace
was ceasing to be a novelty.
References
1. Gorin, A.A. 2000. The Rivergate. Howard-Tilton Memorial Li-
brary, Tulane University. www.rivergate.tulane.edu/chapter1\_text.html.
2. 1930. Original specification for Louisiana State Capitol.
3. 1932. “Estimating ductwork for Louisiana State Capitol building
air-conditioning system.” Heating, Piping and Air Conditioning
4(6):418–419.
4. Weiss, Dreyfous, Seiferth (architects) and F.H. Chisholm (con-
sultant). 1930. Mechanical services drawings at scale ¼ in. to 1 ft.
5. 1931. “Hot and ice water to be provided in new capitol.” Morning
Advocate Dec. 12.
6. 1932. “Louisiana State Capitol.” The Architectural Forum (12):519.
7. 1932. Morning Advocate May 16.

By Robert S. Jones, P.E.
About the Author
A
t the Massachusetts Institute of Technology a few years ago, the
school wanted to convert basement storage and shops into class-
rooms. The steam flow noise from a 24 in. (0.6 m) diameter, 200 psig
(1380 kPa) steam distribution main carrying approximately 100,000 lb/h
(12 600 g/s), subjectively was judged to be excessive for classroom teach-
ing. Sound level measurements confirmed that the noise level was 79
dBA, which is well above the ASHRAE recommendation of about 35
dBA (25 to 30 RC) for classrooms.
Reducing Pipeline Noise
For this application, the entire steam
main and branches to other buildings
served by this main were wrapped with 2
in. (51 mm) thick fiberglass pipe insula-
tion. Other types of insulating material,
such as mineral wool or ceramic wool,
could have been used. Because fiberglass
was suitable for the temperatures in-
volved, it was deemed to be the best
noise transmission loss data for mass
loaded vinyl in octave band center fre-
quencies for different thicknesses. All
seams, joints and edges of the treatment
were overlapped a minimum of 2 in. (51
mm) and sealed airtight. This same treat-
ment was applied to the shutoff valves.
(The vinyl manufacturer should recom-
mend sealer or tape for the joints, seams
and edges that is compatible with its
product [Figure 1a].)
Where necessary, Velcro strips were
used for access to elements needing pe-
riodic servicing.
Make certain that materials are not
exposed to temperatures above the
manufacturer’s recommended limits.
Where unusually high temperatures ex-
decoupling agent between the pipe and
vinyl outer skin. Since fiberglass essen-
tially is transparent acoustically, its only
function was to decouple the pipe from
the vinyl outer skin. Calcium silicate
was not recommended due to its stiffness.
The outer skin in this case was mass-
loaded vinyl, with a surface weight of 1
lb/ft2 (4.9 kg/m2). Manufacturers publish

on acoustics.

The following article was published in ASHRAE Journal, September 2003. © Copyright 2003 American Society of Heating, Refrigerating
and Air-Conditioning Engineers, Inc. It is presented for educational purposes only. This article may not be copied and/or distributed
electronically or in paper form without permission of ASHRAE.
September 2003
ASHRAE Journal
29
ist, use ceramic insulation (although even this insulation has
limits).
For outdoor piping, the wrap must be further protected with
an aluminum jacket or other weatherproof material and then
secured in place with aluminum belt straps. This protects the
vinyl from physical damage and ultraviolet rays.
The expected noise reduction should be 10 to 45 decibels,
depending on the noise source’s magnitude and spectrum shape.
Flexible Pipe Connections to Vibrating Equipment
Flexible pipe connections to vibrating HVAC equipment,
especially when installed above ceilings in buildings, require
vibration isolation fittings. Neoprene can rupture due to ex-
cessive pressure or temperature (or even misapplication), mak-
ing it unfit for use above ceilings. In those cases, use
alternatives such as sections of corrugated metal bellows with
braided external cover. These connectors must be at least 18 in.
(0.46 m) long and kept in a slack position. For maximum vi-
bration isolation efficiency, smaller pipe diameters should be
looped a full 360°.
For larger diameter pipe sizes (i.e., in mechanical equipment
rooms), special vibration isolation fittings are recommended.
They should be of a reinforced flexible product with steel
flanges, preferably without steel or wire control snubbers across
the flanges. These connectors need to be specifically designed
to mitigate vibration transmission along the pipe wall and
dampen fluid pulsations in the pumped fluid. Manufacturers
specifically state when fittings are designed for vibration iso-
lation and provide certified ratings. Again, take care not to
exceed the fitting manufacturer’s recommended temperature
and pressure limits. The fittings should be installed to con-
form to the manufacturer’s installation instructions (Figure 1b).
Noisy Ductwork
In many situations, sheet metal ductwork requires external
acoustical treatment similar to that for piping. Where sound
attenuators are used, either inside or outside mechanical equip-
ment rooms, the intervening duct sections between the sound
attenuator and the room wall, floor, or ceiling needs to be
wrapped externally. Where the sound attenuators are in the
mechanical room, the wrap keeps the room noise from enter-
ing the duct after the sound attenuator. When the sound at-
tenuator is outside the mechanical room, the wrap keeps the
noise in the duct until it reaches the sound attenuator.
Semi-rigid fiberglass insulation (2 in. [51 mm] thick) may
be used for the initial wrap for rectangular or square ducts.
Apply over this a wrap of mass-loaded vinyl weighing not less
than 1 lb/ft2 (4.9 kg/m2). When the problem is noise transmis-
sion, fiberglass alone is rarely enough. Fiberglass is the
decoupling agent, which allows the mass-loaded vinyl to per-
form to its optimum ratings.
As an option to mass-loaded vinyl, two layers of 5/8 in. (16
mm) thick gypsum board may be used. The gypsum board
treatment requires a more substantial support method such as
forming a frame or using small metal studs around the duct to
be wrapped. The frame may enclose all four sides of the duct.
However, if the duct is reasonably close to the building con-
struction above, a three-sided frame can be used with the side
legs attached to the roof or slab construction (Figure 2). The
two layers of gypsum board can be fastened to the support
frame. All joints, seams and edges abutting the building con-
struction must be taped and/or sealed with a non-hardening
construction sealer (Figures 1c and 1d). Ordinary construc-
tion sealers are suitable if they clearly state that they are
permanently flexible.
For the fiberglass and mass-loaded vinyl treatment, it is
recommended to use weld nail pins for securing the wrap to
the duct. Do not use conventional adhesive clips, as the
weight of the treatment causes the adhesive to release after a
few days or weeks. If any other kind of adhesive clips is used,
be certain the clip manufacturer provides a written guarantee
that the clips will not release even with the added weight of
the acoustical treatment.
This treatment usually is recommended for sections of
ductwork in mechanical equipment rooms between the manu-
factured sound attenuators and where the duct leaves the
room. It is also recommended for similar sections of ductwork
when the sound attenuator is positioned outside the mechani-
Figure 1a: Seal or tape joints, seams and edges.
Practical Tips
For Avoiding
Noise & Vibration
Real-life examples of methods for
preventing noise and vibration in
HVAC systems.
September 2003
ASHRAE Journal
29
30
ASHRAE Journal
September 2003
cal equipment room. This treatment should cover all of the
intervening duct and at least two-thirds of the length of the
attenuator (Figure 3). The treatment must be applied cor-
rectly and completely, as described, to maximize its noise
control performance. A 10 to 40 dB reduction can be achieved
with greater reductions occurring at the higher frequencies.
In cases of fan breakout or break-in noise from duct sys-
tems, access doors, where required, should be tight fitting,
gasketed, and have cam-type latches. The cam-type latch,
when closed, draws the door tight against the gasket, ensur-
ing an airtight seal.
The term breakout noise is a bit misleading, as noise
inside an airtight sheet metal duct cannot actually breakout
through the duct walls. The duct system noise that the
previous treatment attenuates is actually noise from the fan
and air velocity noise in the duct that radiates off the outside
of the duct walls.
Flexible connections at fans, air-handling units, and other
pieces of vibrating equipment are another location where a
fair amount of noise can escape. To keep this noise in the duct
system, it is necessary to use flexible material that has mass
and is still resilient. I have tried mass loaded vinyl, which
works acoustically. Although mass-loaded vinyl is difficult to
fabricate into the ductwork, I have not found another product
as effective acoustically. Flexible connections should be lim-
ited in length to 4 in. (102 mm), for purposes of noise control.
Penetrations of the Building Structure
Penetrations of walls, floors, and ceilings by ducts, pipes, con-
duits, etc., need to be packed and sealed acoustically to control
sound transmission or “cross-talk” from one space to the next. It
is best to pack and seal around these penetrations resiliently.
This mitigates noise transmission and prevents vibration in the
penetrating member from entering the wall, floor or ceiling and
exciting these to the degree that audible sound occurs (Figure 4).
Provide a space of 0.5 in. to 0.63 in. (13 mm to 16 mm)
around the penetrating element. Pack this space firmly with
fibrous material to maintain a resilient separation. Then, seal
both sides with a non-hardening resilient sealer.
Where gypsum board walls extend to the underside of corru-
gated metal decks, the open space in the corrugations should be
closed with special fiberglass inserts or gypsum board cutouts
and seal with a permanently flexible sealer. This treatment con-
trols “cross-talk” between adjacent occupied spaces (Figure 1e).
Many people misunderstand that fiberglass has fine sound-
attenuating characteristics when used inside ducts and ple-
nums and HVAC equipment. It absorbs sound/noise, especially
as that sound/noise propagates along the inside of ducts. How-
ever, adding fiberglass insulation above a lay-in acoustic tile
ceiling to reduce downward directed noise from the cabinet
heater or fan coil unit will not work.
In the previous example, the fiberglass insulation was
used in an attempt to stop noise transmission through it. The
law of physics says that stopping noise requires adding mass.
Does 1.5 lb/ft3 (24 kg/m3) fiberglass have any significant mass,
or weight? Obviously, it doesn’t, which means it cannot stop

noise from HVAC equipment from coming through the duct
wall to the space below. Fiberglass is essentially transparent
acoustically because of its very light weight
(mass) and the porous nature of the material. Air and noise
easily pass through. Inside of ducts, fiberglass is great for
absorbing sound as it passes along the duct path. In that
application, the noise path through the fiberglass (along the
duct wall) is much longer than the path through the thickness
of the lining. Fiberglass alone is ineffective at reducing
breakout noise.
Plumbing System Noise Control
Plumbing system noise control often is neglected, especially
in hotels, motels, apartment complexes, and townhouses.

32
ASHRAE Journal
September 2003
One of the first steps in controlling plumbing noise is to
limit the system pressure to what is needed to provide satisfac-
tory flow to the last or topmost fixture. Water flow velocity
generally is designed to not exceed 8 to 10 fps (2.4 to 3 m/s).
Many problems can be avoided in plumbing piping noise con-
trol if the flow velocity does not exceed 8 fps (2.4 m/s).
When plumbing system noise is of concern, do not use thin
wall copper tubing. Similarly, do not use black steel or wrought
iron pipe less than Schedule 40.
Manufactured vibration isolation fittings may be used at all
support points and penetrations of walls, floors and ceilings,
as well as through metal or wood studs. The idea is to keep the
noisy and vibrating piping completely and resiliently sepa-
rated from building construction components. Fittings are de-
signed for nearly every application where plumbing piping
connects through the wall.
For noise control avoid flat plate or paddle orifices for vol-
ume control. Venturi type fittings generate less noise and vi-
bration. These fittings have gradually sloping walls that
transition the water flow smoothly into and out of the orifice.
An alternative to using manufactured vibration isolation fit-
tings is to use neoprene pad wraps that are available in small
sheets of ribbed or waffle pattern design about 0.25 in. to 0.63
in. (6.4 mm to 16 mm) thick. The neoprene should have a Durom-
eter (degree of hardness) of not more than 30 for this type of
application. The neoprene can be cut easily with regular scis-
sors into various size and width strips to wrap around the pipe at
each support point and wall penetration. Each wrap should be
carefully executed so as to maintain complete separation and
resiliency between the pipe and the building construction.
Noisy Rooftop Units
The problem on a recent large project in New Mexico was
caused by a rooftop HVAC unit on a curb on a steel frame
building with conventional bar joists, corrugated steel deck,
insulation and a membrane finish. The unit was positioned
directly above an occupied space where speech intelligibility
and telephone usage was vital. The workspace was finished
with a lay-in acoustic tile ceiling.
Absolutely no sound attenuation had been installed. The
supply air was via flat oval ductwork and the return was
through a large, short, rectangular “tee” fitting. The legs were
so short as to allow direct line of sight directly through the
“tee.” This configuration allowed noise to pass directly
through the tee, unattenuated. There was no internal duct
lining and no sound attenuators.
The solution was to install sound attenuators in both the
supply and return air ducts (both were significant contribu-
tors to the noise problem). In addition, all the intervening
ductwork between these sound attenuators and the roof pen-
etrations was wrapped externally. Fortunately, the building
had a high ceiling, in which to implement the recommended
treatments for noise control.
The mechanical engineer likes to keep pressure drop as low
as possible through sound attenuators. However, in many cases
added pressure drop is necessary to reduce the noise as needed
to achieve the acoustical design criteria. The amount of noise
reduction is calculated to satisfy the design, and this informa-
tion is included in the equipment schedules as dynamic inser-
tion loss (in decibels) at the critical frequencies, typically 125,
250 and 500 Hz.
While on the roof of this project, the author asked the unit
manufacturer’s representative if he could feel vibration stand-
ing next to the RTU. He appeared surprised, and answered “Yes,
definitely.” He added, “I don’t know why. All the equipment in
the unit is internally vibration isolated.” This kind of response
is nothing new, nor is it an isolated case.
Picture the fans inside this packaged equipment, ripping, tear-
ing, pushing and sucking some 40,000 cfm (18 900 L/s) of air
through dampers, coils, filters, mixing boxes and whatever else
is in the unit. All of this causes considerable air turbulence,
which easily excites or sets in motion the unit casing, even if the
unit happens to have double-wall construction. This casing vi-
bration energy passes easily into the roof below and then into
ceilings, walls, etc., with little or no attenuation. When vibra-
tion energy is of sufficient magnitude to move building con-
struction components, unwanted noise can result.
In buildings where low noise criteria are required, such as con-
cert halls, music buildings, conference rooms, executive offices,
and the like, this excess vibration has been, and in many cases,
still is, a serious problem. The ongoing nature of this problem
seems to indicate that RTU equipment manufacturers do not
believe this problem exists, or do not wish to acknowledge it.
Many acoustical consultants recommend alleviating this
persistent problem by manufacturing and shipping HVAC pack-
aged rooftop units with all internal rotating, reciprocating and
vibrating components securely fastened to the unit frame. Then,
it will be easier to vibration isolate the entire package on an
appropriate isolation system for each individual condition.
Vibration isolators can be provided to compensate for wind
loading effect and for seismic restraint situations when required.
Proper static deflections for the isolation system, which take
into account the natural deflection of the structure where RTUs
are mounted, can be correctly and professionally designed.
Vibration isolators for outdoor equipment are manufactured
with “travel limit stops” that limit the amount of movement of
the mounted equipment before snubbing that movement.
Proper static deflection is established by determining the
driving frequency of the vibrating equipment and the natural
frequency of the structure supporting the mounted equipment.
The natural frequency of the structure should be no more than
one-tenth of the driving frequency of the vibrating equipment.
This is a general recommendation. Some situations require a
more precise procedure.
RTUs almost always should be vibration isolated from the
structure on which they are mounted. As an acoustical consult-
September 2003
ASHRAE Journal
33
ant, the author prefers the RTUs above the roof on a steel dun-
nage frame with RTU on point vibration isolators. If the RTUs
are isolated on a manufactured vibration isolation curb, the
inside of the curb should be treated acoustically to limit the
amount of RTU noise coming through the roof, under the unit.
This treatment generally is about the same as the gypsum board
duct wrap, i.e., 2 in. (51 mm) fiberglass with two layers of 5/8
in. (16 mm) gypsum board on top with everything packed and
sealed airtight.
If RTU equipment manufacturers insist on providing vibra-
tion isolation devices with their units, then the shipping, or
hold down bolts, should be left tied down. Of course, the end
user pays for equipment that is not used.
Caution: it is not wise to have two vibration isolation sys-
tems mounted together. Should the two systems resonate at, or
near, the same frequency, then violent oscillation may occur
with disastrous results.
On a recent job in Santa Fe, N.M., a restaurant owner asked
if anything could be done to stop the restaurant lights from
shaking and flickering. Many patrons found it annoying and
could not enjoy their meal. After examining the HVAC sys-
tem, it was discovered that RTUs mounted on roof curbs di-
rectly above the dining room were causing the problem. The
units had been supplied with internal vibration isolation.
However, the vibration from the unit casing was sufficient to
shake the roof and cause the lights to shimmer. It was recom-
mended that a spring vibration isolation curb be placed un-
der each unit. The existing isolation devices were bolted tight
to the RTU frame. Problem solved.
Noisy Roof Exhaust Fans
On a slightly different matter, consider roof exhaust fans.
Many manufacturers of this equipment furnish “rubber grom-
mets” as an integral part of their package for standard vibra-
tion isolation. These fans are commonly set and fastened to
some sort of roof curb, which tends to direct the vibration
energy into the roof. Typically, the author has found that this
method for vibration isolation is not adequate for mitigating
the vibration energy generated by the equipment.
Thus, when one stands beside one of these fans typically in-
stalled on one of today’s lightweight roofs, one can commonly
feel vibration passing into the roof. In many cases, the vibra-
tion is significant due to the forces generated by the unbal-
anced configuration of the fan, motor and belt drive.
Simple rubber grommets generally do not isolate this low
frequency energy.
A practical solution is using a fan manufacturer’s acousti-
cally rated sound attenuating curb. Even a straight through
conventional sound attenuator can be used, but select an at-
tenuator with as low as possible pressure drop. Typically, roof
exhaust fans cannot accept much pressure drop and still de-
liver rated capacity.
Electrical Conduit Connections to Vibrating Equipment
For conduit connections up to and including 1 in. (25 mm)
diameter, it is recommended to use that a full 360° loop of
grossly slack corrugated metal conduit. For sizes that are more
than 1 in. (25 mm) in diameter, a manufactured fitting is
recommended.
Where electrical outlet boxes occur in walls separating
noise sensitive spaces or spaces requiring privacy or confi-
dentiality, the boxes should be separated in all directions by
at least 24 in. (0.6 m) and should be covered, airtight, with a
fire-rated putty pad.
Any and all of these items designed and manufactured for
controlling noise and vibration in buildings will work as pre-
dicted if installed completely and correctly and in conform-
ance with the manufacturers’ installation instructions.

sing thermal energy storage has shifted gigawatts of power off of daytime peaks in a cost-effective
manner. However, thermal energy storage (TES) market penetration is small in comparison to its
potential. Why? In TES’ infancy (early 1980s), a small number of manufacturers carefully researched the
technology and installed equipment. In the technology’s adolescent years (late 1980s and early 1990s),
dozens of manufacturers, chasing the new demand-side management rebate incentives, jumped into
the marketplace. These difficult adolescent years resulted in tarnished reputations and the spread of
misinformation about the technology.
Buildings, and the LEED rating system
are based on energy cost savings. Sev-
eral TES projects that have won
ASHRAE’s Technology Award2,3,4 detail
the cost-saving aspect. However, less
emphasis has been given to the reduc-
tions of equipment size and infrastruc-
ture that normally occurs.
The basic TES cooling systems that I
base most of my analysis on are:
Chiller-based systems. Throughout
the adolescent years of TES, a variety of
systems including site-built liquid over-
feed refrigeration systems, ice-harvesting
equipment and others, were used success-
fully in other applications. However, 99%
of commercial air-conditioning TES sys-
tems installed use a standard chiller to
By Mark M. MacCracken, P.E., Member ASHRAE
This article attempts to set the record
straight on the myths and reality of this
technology by demonstrating how TES is
well-positioned to help the move towards
more energy-efficient and environment-
friendly air-conditioning systems.
The obvious reason for installing TES
is to reduce energy costs. Although de-
regulation of the electric industry has cre-
ated localized anomalies in energy costs,
the basic reality of supply and demand is
that on-peak power is more expensive
than off-peak power.1 One consistently
proven aspect of TES is that it saves en-
ergy costs, which has more significance
now that ANSI/ASHRAE/IESNA Stan-
dard 90.1, Energy Standard for Build-
ings Except Low-Rise Residential
U
About the Author
Mark M. MacCracken, P.E., is president and CEO
of CALMAC Manufacturing in Englewood, N.J.
produce the cooling. Chillers are famil-
iar, reliable, capacity rated, and competi-
tively priced. They cool water or a glycol
water solution.
Ice-based storage. For projects where
space is not as much of a consideration,
chilled water storage is becoming widely
used.5 However, since so much HVAC
work involves retrofits where space is a
concern, ice is the likely choice.
Closed system. Large district cooling
systems use either water and/or ice as the
storage media and the heat transfer fluid.
These “open” systems create added hy-
draulic complications that need to be

carefully addressed. However, most TES systems now separate
the storage media from the heat transfer fluid, so the systems
are the same hydraulically as most chiller systems.
The Myths of TES
Uncommon and risky. If I said TES has 100% market penetra-
tion and that you use it today, you probably would say I was
crazy. Well, I would be right because a domes-
tic hot water heater is the best example for un-
derstanding the value of cool thermal energy storage. (When
applied to commercial air-conditioning applications, TES of-
ten is referred to as the more descriptive term “off-peak cool-
ing.”) To instantaneously heat water for a low-flow showerhead,
a simple calculation shows that 18 kW of power is required
(Equation 1) or 36 kW for two simultaneous showers.

Even a large capacity water heater (electric for simplicity)
has a 4.5 kW heater, at most. So the reduction in infrastruc-
ture for wiring and electrical power associated with it is a
minimum of 4 to 1.
Although no one sizes a domestic heating element to handle
instantaneous load, this is done regularly in the HVAC world.
Why install a chiller system that safely (with our understand-
able use of “safety factors”) meets a load that occurs a couple
of hours per year? A simple partial storage system reduces the
chiller size to something safely above the average peak daily
load, which normally reduces the chiller plant size by about
40% to 50%, with the proportional savings of infrastructure
that are similar to the water heater.
Too much space. Does the water heater in your
house take up too much space? In Equation 26
a quick calculation shows the space required for a full storage
system.

Storage systems in retrofit applications are usually “partial
storage” and normally are sized to handle about one-third of
the peak load, yielding 0.23% of occupied space needed for
storage. A 1,000,000 ft2 (93 000 m2) building needs only
about 2,300 ft2 (214 m2). For a 2,000 ft2 (186 m2) home, the
water heater takes up 5 ft2 (0.5 m2) or 0.25%. So for off-
peak cooling (OPC) using 33% partial storage, the space
needed is about as much proportionately as the water heater
in a house.
Too complicated. Let’s go back to the water
heater. Is it complicated? No. It has a reliable,
undersize heating element that creates heat whenever the in-
ventory drops below 95%.
In a partial storage OPC system, a reliable undersized cooling
element (chiller) runs whenever the inventory drops below 95%.
TES tanks are simply thermal capacitors with no moving parts.
With partial storage, no on-peak control malfunction can occur
because there is no full-size chiller to accidentally set a massive
electrical peak. What can complicate the system is mismatching
the control complexity with the aptitude of the eventual opera-
tor. Installing a 50% sized chiller creates a major advantage in
demand cost savings, which is a good goal for a small applica-
tion (such as a school with a janitor). A large OPC system can
handle a more complex control strategy, but that is where prob-
lems always occur. A design engineer spends hours figuring out
the precise, logical way to save the most money while working
with a complicated rate structure. Think how incomprehensible
the strategy will be to a third-shift operator when some sensor
fails (see sidebar “Designing for Success”).
Lack of redundancy (risk) with partial stor-
age. Almost any OPC system can meet the same
redundancy criteria as a conventional system at a comparable
cost. Obviously, in a conventional system with one chiller where
the chiller is inoperable, you are out of luck. It is the same with
a one chiller storage system. However, let’s look at a conven-
tional system with a calculated design day load of 1,000 tons
(3500 kW). A reasonable conventional design would be three
400-ton (1400 kW) chillers and an equivalent partial storage
system could be two 400-ton (1400 kW) chillers and 3,500 ton-

Instead of adding a safety factor of 20% to the cooling
plant on every job, and paying the price of oversizing
for the life of the building, downsize the actual size by
20% and add storage.

Ice
Discharging
The best example of a well-designed con-
troller for an operator (school janitor) is a
three-position switch, located next to the ice
inventory meter, labeled “cool day—warm
day—humid hot day.” The controller simply
changes the leaving chilled water tempera-
ture on the upstream chiller setting from
55°F to 50°F to 45°F (13°C to 10°C to 7°C),
which changes the control strategy from full
storage to partial storage-ice priority to par-
tial storage-chiller priority (Figures 1a–d). Be-
cause no penalty exists for starting with a full
charge of storage every morning (as with
other TES technologies outside of the scope
stated previously), there is no fear of
guessing wrong, just change the setting that
morning.
The main goal for this project was to take
advantage of a large difference in energy
charges ($0.12 on-peak/$0.06 off-peak).
Figures 1b, 1c and 1d illustrate the success
of the strategy. Although the strategy wasn’t
“optimal,” it was close. And, it worked like
a charm.
I can’t stress enough the “Keep It Simple
Stupid” (KISS) principle. My advice is to keep
the controls as simple as possible but to
install simple real-time feedback on energy
use information for the operators (for example,
real-time total building kW). (The real-
time game of “what is causing that electric
peak?” is fun. Getting a call from the boss
about an outrageous electric bill from two
months ago is not).
With feedback, operators stay interested
and tune up a simple system. OPC systems
are different, but not necessarily more
complex. Owners and operators who are
aware of the differences can save a lot of
money.

Figure 2a: Excess capacity on design day.
hrs (12 300 kWh) of ice storage. Figure 2a shows the maximum
capacity for both systems as compared to the design day load
profile. Though the 1,200-ton (4220 kW) system could create
more ton-hrs in the 24-hour period, it is clear that the storage
system is more than what is required even on a design day.
The next concern is equipment failure. Figure 2b shows if a
chiller failed on the conventional system, or storage was unavail-
able in the storage system, both would have two 400-ton (1400
kW) chillers to handle the load, and for six of the 11 hours, the
system would be short some capacity. If the component that failed
on the storage system were a chiller, the remaining chiller and
storage would be able to meet the full load for eight of 11 hours.
Therefore, the systems are quite similar and both systems would
need 500-ton (1760 kW) chillers (three or two), instead of the
400-ton (1400 kW) machines, to have “n+1” redundancy.7
Too expensive to install. It is difficult to ob-
tain real costs for equipment because com-
panies don’t want to publicly show their “hand.” Although
many documented case studies demonstrate lower first
cost,3 it is easier to look at it using simple numbers. In most
applications, the chiller system can be downsized by 40% to
50%. However, let’s use the 33% reduction in the earlier ex-
ample. In this case, we have downsized the refrigeration plant
by 400 tons (1400 kW) including chiller, cooling towers,
cooling tower pumps and electrical capacity to all units.
Using $900/ton ($256/kW) for an installed cost for a
system yields:

ASHRAE Journal
September 2003
experienced OPC contractors compete on new construction
projects (more than 400 tons [1400 kW]). In retrofit projects,
as with almost all energy efficiency upgrades (excluding light-
ing), there should be another reason to go forward for the
paybacks to be reasonable, i.e., aging chiller plant, CFC re-
placement, building expansion, strained electrical supply, etc.
Does not save energy. When analyzing en-
ergy savings with OPC, you must consider both
energy used at the building and energy used at the source of
generation at the power plant. The reason is simple. Most en-
ergy-efficient products reduce energy use but do not change
when energy is used. As an industry, we have done a poor job
of relaying the energy saving benefits of OPC beyond the
meter. Site energy savings may or may not occur. Source en-
ergy savings almost always occur.
Site Energy Savings
Is the goal to save the most energy or energy costs? Clearly
the owner’s answer is the latter. However, energy-efficiency
funding from most states is based on kWh saved. With thermal
storage, optimizing for energy savings can be done but often
is not the same as maximizing energy cost reduction. So let’s
review a design maximizing energy savings for air-cooled and
water-cooled applications.
First, an air-cooled chiller operating at ARI design condi-
tions, 95°F/45°F (35°C/7°C) (Point A in Figure 3), uses the same
kW/ton at ice-making conditions of 78°F/25°F (26°C/–4°C)
(Point B) (Figure 3). Therefore, a 17°F (9°C) change in dry bulb
gives equal efficiency for ice making. In much of the country,
the ambient day to night swing is 20°F (11°C). Because the
swing is sinusoidal, the average for the on-peak hours versus the
ice-making hours make the average temperature swing more
