ASHRAE Journal
October 2003
tally composed of the curtailment supply curve plus a profit or
other bidding constraints that the ISO may impose. For instance,
a bid curve with a 10% profit margin would elevate the price
thresholds by 10% for the same load reduction amount.
We recommend that customers test their load curtailment
strategy before committing to a DR program that has penalty
clauses for non- or partial performance. Some ISO programs
require customers to demonstrate that their subscribed or
claimed load curtailments can be achieved.
Potential Benefits from DR Programs
To illustrate this load curtailment assessment process and the
potential benefits of customers participating in ISO DR pro-
grams, we consider a hypothetical commercial building in New
York City with a summer daytime peak demand of 3 MW and is
considering enrolling in the New York Independent System Op-
erator (NYISO) DR programs. In this example, we consider only
usage in the discretionary and on-site generation categories.
The facility manager estimates that he has 700 kW of load cur-
tailment capability, about 23% of summer peak demand, com-
prised of 250 kW of discretionary load and 450 kW of self
supply (Table 2). The costs for executing individual curtailment
strategies include labor estimates to manually turn off equip-
ment, monitor load curtailment performance and restore the load
after the event. We estimated a labor rate of $60/hour. To evalu-
ate the benefits of curtailing, we assumed a five-hour curtail-
ment period (from noon to 5 p.m.) that is coincident with highest
electric demand period for the building. Table 2 shows total cost
of each measure as well as the cost per energy unit curtailed over
the five-hour event. Costs for individual load reduction strate-
gies range between $0.11 and $0.40 per kWh. Other impacts on
the facility such as valuing any reduction in service or amenity
levels, which are more difficult to quantify, are not included.
Figure 3 illustrates the supply curve of selected load reduc-
tion measures inventoried in Table 2.
Given the results of the load reduction assessment, it ap-
pears to be cost effective for the hypothetical facility to par-
ticipate in three DR programs: ICAP, day-ahead market and
emergency resources.§
To estimate the potential benefits, we examine two scenarios:
(1) manually executing and monitoring the measures and re-
storing the load after the event, and (2) a scenario in which the
customer invests and upgrades the facility’s energy manage-
ment and control system (EMCS). The EMCS upgrade reduces
the cost of dispatching load reductions by enabling the facil-
ity operator to remotely trigger and monitor the load reduc-
tion, and to restore the load after the event. To improve the
flexibility and cost effectiveness of the automated load reduc-
tion dispatch, we estimated that it would cost about $32,000
for automation that included the installed cost for lighting
and HVAC controls, and remote controls for the escalators.
Additional controls for the emergency generator would cost
$68,000. We assume that the customer has an interval meter
that is sufficient for settlement and billing with the ISO. The
future revenue estimates are based on price data from NYISO
markets for the summer of 2001.†
The day-ahead market program requires customers (or load
aggregators) to develop a bidding strategy based on curtail-
ment availability, costs, and the likelihood of being sched-
uled given their forecast of day-ahead electricity market prices.
For our example, the facility decides to bid curtailments at its
curtailment cost for a five-hour time block (noon to 5 p.m.).
Customers can bundle measures together and bid shorter time
blocks (e.g., two hours), particularly if the inconvenience to
the occupants is a significant concern, but that influences the
revenue potential. In our example, at a bid price at $0.05/kWh

§ The emergency program benefits are not estimated in the following ex-
ample because they are influenced by factors other than price. Because of
the expensive telemetry requirements and the very short notification for
the ancillary services and balancing, these two programs also are not con-
sidered in our example.

October 2003
(as used in the DR investment scenario), we estimate that the
facility’s bid would be accepted for 98 days based on 2001
NYISO prices in New York City. For a higher bid price, the
chance lessens that the wholesale prices will exceed the bid
price. As the facility’s bid price is reduced, additional curtail-
ments are scheduled, which increases their payments from the
ISO (i.e., revenues to the customer).
The revenue for the five-hour curtailment of more than 98
selected days is calculated by multiplying the curtailment
amount (kWh) by the hourly clearing price of electric energy
($/kWh) for the 98 curtailment periods. During these periods,
the hourly clearing price of electric energy is equal to or greater
than the bid price and changes every hour. The revenue esti-
mates were determined based on NYISO market clearing price
data of the day-ahead market for a six-month summer period in
2001.
The ICAP program requires 24/7 availability of the load
curtailment resource for a minimum contract period of one
month with two hours or less of response time. In our example,
the emergency generator is the only resource that can meet the
availability requirement since we assume that the facility can-
not meet the load curtailment obligation by reducing loads if
an event occurs outside of normal operating hours.
Table 3 summarizes estimated revenues and profits by the
curtailment strategy for the two DR programs (day-ahead mar-
ket program and ICAP). Results are provided for investment and
no investment scenarios during a six-month summer period in
2001. The results indicate that the DR investment significantly
lowers the curtailment cost, which permits the customer to lower
the bid price to $0.05/kWh.‡ Thus, the revenues and the profits
for the curtailment strategies are higher than those for manual
load curtailment scenario. The investment in the EMCS system
upgrade would increase annual profits by about $5,600. This
represents a five- to six-year payback on the investment of
$32,000. This payback time is at the lower end of the range and
generally acceptable to these kinds of customers. But the en-
hanced automation capability from the upgraded EMCS sys-
tem decreases the response time for these strategies. The customer
can now seriously consider participating in the ICAP program
and committing the 250 kW of load reduction measures, which
would generate additional revenue of about $13,000 ($52.5/kW
× 250 kW). Given the improved economics, the facility may be
more willing to accept the relatively low risk that the ISO may
call an ICAP event outside of the normal operating hours of the
facility (e.g., nighttime).
We conducted a sensitivity analysis and applied an addi-
tional inconvenience cost of $2/kW to the cost of the lighting
and thermostat reset measures. This inconvenience cost is based
on an outage cost estimate and represents 50% of what is gener-
ally accepted to reflect the cost to customers of a curtailment on
very short notice.2 An inconvenience cost of $2/kW for the ther-
mostat reset measure, on top of the operating cost for executing
the measure, significantly raises the customer’s curtailment costs

and bid price (from $0.05/kWh to $0.45/kWh), resulting in a
decrease of annual revenue and profits. Using the example of
Table 3 for the DR investment scenario, the profits for the light-
ing and thermostat reset measure would be reduced from $3,600
to $300.
This example shows that many commercial customers may
require either an up-front or ongoing payment to seriously con-
sider investing in enhanced automation to participate in DR
programs. Participating in ICAP-type markets provides the up-
front reservation payments, which are particularly attractive if
load aggregators or other third parties offer insurance products
to cover losses incurred when ICAP calls do not coincide with
the resource availability in the commercial buildings sector. In
such a market environment where risk can be managed, DR ac-
tivity and investment in DR automation will be more attractive
to facility managers.
The economics of the EMCS-upgrade investment further im-
prove when the entire value chain of the enhanced controls and
monitoring capabilities is considered. This includes improved
HVAC troubleshooting capabilities, energy efficiency monitor-
ing, improved operation and maintenance opportunities as well
as managing overall peak demand.
Conclusions
To assist facility managers in assessing whether and to what
extent they should participate in DR programs offered by ISOs,
we introduced a systematic process by which a curtailment
supply curve can be developed that integrates costs and other
program provisions and features. This curtailment supply curve
functions as a bid curve, which allows the facility manager to
incrementally offer load to the market with terms and condi-
tions acceptable to the customer. We applied this load curtail-
ment assessment process to a stylized example of an office
building, using programs offered by NYISO to provide detail
and realism.1,3
Based on our stylized representation of customer circum-
stances imposed upon an actual DR program, we offer the fol-
lowing conclusions:
• ISO DR programs offer facilities new and potentially at-
tractive opportunities to be compensated for load curtailments
at market price.
• Discretionary loads that typically represent between 5%
and 20% of summer peak demand using manual approaches,
offer limited benefits.
• But, by offering even modest curtailments in low-risk pro-
grams, customers gain experience that can lead to expanded
participation in programs with more attractive benefits, even
though they are accompanied by greater risks.
• Investments made to automate these curtailment actions
can enhance customer DR capability, but may have relatively
long payback times (five years or greater).
• Participation in ICAP-type programs that offer up-front, res-
ervation payments provides opportunities for a more certain
stream of benefits that can help justify and support investments
in upgraded EMCS systems. These enhanced automation capa-
bilities also may enable facility managers to realize other ben-
efits, such as the ability to manage and control peak demand,
receive near-real-time feedback on hourly energy usage, and
obtain improved energy information and general improved O&M

Table 3: Revenue and profit estimates for selected curtailment measures based on Summer 2001 NYSIO market data.
capabilities. However, ICAP-type programs impose performance
risks during off-hours when DR resources are not available.
• A facility that has a reliable on-site generator may recover
any required investments in controls or reduced emissions rela-
tively quickly in ISO-based capacity markets that offer up-
front reservation payments. But, participation may be restricted
by environmental regulations.
• Our research indicates that many facility managers are par-
ticipating in emergency DR programs and earning cash ben-
efits initially by using existing control systems, and
time-honored and low cost actions such as manually switch-
ing off lighting and other discretionary equipment. Over time,
we anticipate that this experience will provide them with the
wherewithal to incrementally expand their curtailment capa-
bility so they can participate in other ISO DR programs with
higher rewards.
In summary, facility managers should reevaluate the oppor-
tunities to turn their load management capabilities into cash.

About the Author
rchives can be as variable in the materials of their collections as
art galleries and museums. Paper documents, photographic
records (i.e., prints, sheet or roll film and movies), electronic media,
audiovisual recordings, paintings, medals, and coins are some ex-
amples of archive items that differ in their temperature and humid-
ity requirements for preservation.
much of their collections. Archives do
little of these. While the material sci-
ences of paper preservation and climate
control remain identical, the different
mandates of the library significantly shift
risk management decisions, such as ap-
propriate HVAC targets.
New Handbook Chapter
In the mid-1990s, a new ASHRAE tech-
nical subcommittee began work on a
chapter for the 1999 ASHRAE Hand-
book—Applications, Chapter 20, Librar-
ies, Archives, and Museums.1 Prior to
that edition, archives and museums were
only briefly mentioned in the handbook,
and the advice was simple: achieve 50%
RH and human comfort temperatures, and
keep fluctuations very small. This target
was consistent with much of the archive
and museum literature of the time, and
had its origins in conservative “best-
Table 1 shows approximate classes of
record stability, lifespan, and era of pro-
duction. Zones of control emerge based
on these classes. Rag paper and parchment
records last for centuries at moderate cli-
mates. Unfortunately, electronic data is
proving to be more ephemeral, having the
shortest lifetime of all of the classes.
Photographic records are more com-
plex to generalize because color images
usually have low stability and black and
white materials have medium to high sta-
bility (unfortunately, poorly processed
stock can be highly unstable and destroy
adjacent records).
Archives are funded with a variety of
A
By Stefan Michalski
budgetary resources and because their
mandate is to preserve diverse records as
long as possible, the HVAC engineer plays
a key role in balancing effective archive
preservation with limited resources.
Libraries
Libraries have much in common with
archives. Obviously, books and mono-
graphs are made of paper as are most
archive records. Much of the information
in this article can be transferred to librar-
ies. However, profound differences exist
in usage pattern and occupancy. Librar-
ies lend most of their items to many dif-
ferent users, and continually replace
Stefan Michalski is a senior conservation scien-
tist at the Canadian Conservation Institute, Ot-
tawa, ON, Canada.

guesses” driven by the building
boom of the 1950s and 1960s.
During the 1980s, preservation sci-
ence began to question much of this
received wisdom, noting its high costs
and dubious benefits for many types
of collections, especially archives. Al-
though preservation science was (and
still is) incomplete, the time was right
for a distinct and comprehensive chap-
ter. The first version of the chapter saw
the development of targets for RH and
temperature, but not pollutants. (Fil-
tration recommendations were
present, based on current best prac-
tice.) The committee work for the sec-
ond version2 has focused on the
inclusion of pollutant guidelines.
This article summarizes key points
of that chapter for archives and notes
issues peculiar to archives that space
did not allow in the handbook. Most
of these derive from a technical pub-
lication for archives by the author.3
The rest emerge from 20 years expe-
rience as a scientific advisor to both
clients and engineers.
Risk Management Approach to HVAC Control
Risk management has become the mantra of many profes-
sions concerned with preservation of assets (financial or other-
wise) and for good reason. “Best-available technology” fails as
a cost-effective strategy! Museums and archives have begun to
recognize that preservation, what they do, is “risk management”
of their collections. Not only in terms of mitigating catastro-
phes, but in terms of everyday mitigation issues, such as indoor
climate. Effective preservation is a logical balance between these
risks, over periods of time that can be as long as centuries.
The late 20th century tradition for museum and archive HVAC
targets was a single perfect target, and no discussion of the im-
plications of less-than-perfect control. In contrast, the 1999 hand-
book chapter contains a range of risks assigned to a range of RH
and temperature targets (Table 2 in that chapter). Basic (and
reliable) levels of climate control can achieve most preserva-
tion benefits. This is important for small archives and their con-
sultants to understand. Their systems must prevent all the highest
climate induced risks. Damp, of any duration, is the highest risk
to archives, followed by sustained warmth. Beyond these, the
chapter emphasizes that there are various levels of climate con-
trol, and that basic levels achieve much of the benefits. On the
other hand, flagship archives, such as national archives, can
justify the highest standards of control, since some remaining
risk has been mitigated by the high costs.
One neglected issue emerged with a vengeance as the systems
of the 1970s building boom reached the end of their life expect-
ancies: system reliability. It is counter-productive to make a
complex system that gives excellent performance for 10 or even
20 years, if it then crashes, often creating warm damp condi-
tions, with no easy local fix possible, financially or technically.
It is much better for preservation over the next century if ar-
chives build simple robust systems that can be serviced and
repaired locally, within the maintenance budget of the archive.
Colder, Dryer Means Lower Risk
Enough is known about chemical degradation rates to map
a generic set of relative record lifetimes on the psychrometric
chart (Figure 1).1,2 Thus the lifetimes of Table 1, benchmarked
at 50%rh and 20°C (68°F) can be multiplied by the relative
factors in Figure 1. It is clear that both RH and temperature
have a profound influence on record lifetimes. It is not sur-
prising that paper or film records in hot climates, as com-
pared to those from cold climates, are usually much browner
and more brittle.
A research institute in photographic preservation has devel-
oped two useful tools for archives based on extensive film
research data, which formed part of the data used to derive
Figure 1. The first tool was the IPI Storage Guide for Acetate
Film,4 a circular slide rule that estimated the effect of storage
RH and temperature on the onset of what is known as “vinegar
October 2003
ASHRAE Journal
27
Figure 1: Areas of less color equals area of less risk. Blue is
too damp. Red is too warm and humid. Orange is too dry for
some records. Heavy black lines are lifetimes, benchmarked
at "1" for 20°C and 50% RH. The factor given first is best fit to
all data, the value in brackets is pessimistic fit to data. The
dotted line following almost the line of 50% RH is a line of
constant moisture content for paper and photographic mate-
rials. (Abridged from ASHRAE Handbook, developed by the
author from a review of all data).
Humidity Ratio (grams moisture/kg dry air)
Temperature °C
syndrome” — the smell of rapidly deteriorating film. More
recently, they have developed the Preservation Environment
Monitor, a data logger with an algorithm to predict the relative
lifetime of the collection. It not only indicates the effect of
current conditions, but also calculates the cumulative effect of
variable conditions previously monitored.
Whether using computational tools such as those from IPI, or
Figure 1 from ASHRAE, all of which predict chemical deteriora-
tion phenomena, one must also be mindful that high RH, at any
temperature, must also be avoided due to biological effects, as
indicated by the blue danger region in Figure 1. Furthermore,
while it is true that mold will not grow below the freezing point,
one does not want records equilibrated to high RH in cold stores.
On retrieval they will immediately be at risk of mold, as well as a
host of mechanical problems such as sticking and deformation.
A common confusion about records and RH needs discus-
sion. An engineer at a film preservation conference some years
ago complained about the emphasis on relative humidity in
all the presentations by preservation scientists. Surely, he
asked, absolute humidity (dew point) was more relevant
to the science of deterioration, not to mention more
straightforward to a designer of storage systems. The
fact is relative humidity and dry bulb tempera-
ture are the best parameters to characterize
rate of chemical deterioration in records.
It is not the vapor pressure that
determines rates of mois-
ture-mediated deteriora-
tion (primarily acid
hydrolysis) it is the equi-
librium moisture content
(EMC) of the record. RH
is the best parameter to
predict EMC. On the other
hand, RH is not a perfect
predictor of EMC. There
is a small temperature cor-
rection, as characterized
in Figure 1 by the devia-
tion of the line of constant
EMC from the line of
50% RH. This correction becomes important in low temperature
storage, where film archivists may ask for slightly lower than
50% RH, to ensure that packaged records do not equilibrate
above 60%rh (and risk sticking to their envelope or neighbor)
when retrieved to a warm room.
In summary, and considering the implications of Table 1 and
Figure 1 together, high stability records only need to be kept
out of high-risk regions. Medium stability records need cool
and/or dry conditions to last many centuries. Low stability
records need cold and dry conditions to last even one century.
And some records, such as magnetic media, have specific prob-
lems like migration of lubricants at low temperatures that need
balancing with all other risks. Of course, it is not the engineer
who makes these climate control decisions, it is the client, the
archives. As with any profession, people look towards best
current practice, and to real examples, for guidance.
The fact is cool or cold stores are used primarily for photo-
graphic archives and audiovisual archives. (They are certainly
used for nitrate film archives, which pose spontaneous fire risk
issues as well.) A few paper archives
have implemented cool conditions
on a large scale. Records on magnetic
media, both analog (audio, video) and
digital (data), pose the greatest prob-
lem of instability but also suffer the
problem of rapid technical obsoles-
cence. These records are sometimes
given cool conditions, but more usu-
ally, their risk management is ap-
proached entirely through record
“migration,” i.e., copying onto new
and sometimes more stable media.
Fluctuation Specifications
“Permissible fluctuations” have
been an endless source of argument
within the preservation community,
and in turn with the engineering com-
munity that implements them. Or
doesn’t. Innumerable planning meet-
ings and post-project complaints have
stumbled on what was or wasn’t the
design criteria, what was or wasn’t un-
derstood about design conditions,
whose thermohygrograph data is or
isn’t reliable, and in the end, what risk,
seriously, does it mean to the collec-
tions anyway. This article cannot ad-
dress all those dilemmas, but it can
outline a common starting point, us-
ing the risk management approach
introduced in the handbook chapter.
Archive records are usually pack-
aged in moisture resistant boxes. Humidity response times of
the boxed record can reach weeks, even years, so the client can
incorporate box (and cabinet) design into the risk management
of rh fluctuations. In the handbook chapter, however, we deal
with climate conditions outside the record box, not inside.
In the handbook chapter, the committee adopted a risk man-
agement concept proposed by this author, that of quality levels
AA, A, B, C, D in target fluctuations. AA represents the narrowest
range of fluctuations, and D the widest. This would replace the
previous notion of a single “ideal” fluctuation target, typically
±3% RH and ±1°C (2°F), for which archives and museums either
passed (rare) or failed (everybody, eventually).

Table 3: Time-out-of-cold storage that cuts record lifetime in half.
The committee debated considerably about the merit
of this multilevel approach. The author responsible for
most of the system design sections of the chapter, with
long and successful experience in museum and archive
systems design objected strongly to the scheme. How
could one design to less and less controlled conditions?
He considered the scheme as suitable only for categoriz-
ing “as-is” projects. Other engineers expressed support
for the notion of variable “quality” performance targets.
The targets AA, A, B, C, D are not intended as criteria
simply for the system fluctuation under a fixed load, at
a single point in the design space. They are intended as
performance targets that incorporate the effects of many
other design parameters, such as air distribution, air
change rate, mixing effectiveness, building envelope
characteristics, and of course, movement of the measur-
ing point throughout the controlled space, and through-
out the seasons. In other words, they are written from the
client’s perspective, and thus incorporate parameters that
might well be outside the HVAC engineers control, or
even current design paradigms. Whereas the difference
between AA and A may seem subtle, A was reserved for what
seems a consensus among major researchers, such as those at
the Smithsonian,5 and at the Canadian Conservation Insti-
tute,3 as the most appropriate generic (and mercifully achiev-
able) design target for mixed collections. The more
“uncontrolled” level B incorporates operational parameters,
such as temperature setback for cold climate regions. Levels C
and D obviously make no sense for design of “precision envi-
ronmental control” archives, but they do make sense for simple
HVAC improvements within historic buildings that house ar-
chives, often the case for smaller clients.
A theoretical flaw in these targets as stated, as it was for the
single ideal target of the past, is that they do not deal rigor-
ously with design conditions, i.e., 3%, or 1.5%. For most cli-
ents, of course, this is a completely unexpected fact of life. It
always shocks museums and archives to be told by an engi-
neer that in the “fine print” it is understood that the system,
even when working exactly as designed, may be overloaded as
much as several days per year— longer in some bad years.
Specifying within such stochastic risk assessment parameters
is an unfinished element of current preservation research.
Another objection to the scheme is that the risks as identi-
fied for each level of fluctuation are not well substantiated.
Experimental data in this area is sketchy. The stated risks are
only estimates, based on interpolations from mechanics of ma-
terials, but benchmarked by practical experience of museum
workers with collections. Imprecision is common to most areas
of risk management, but as one thinker in the field has stated,
better to be approximately correct than precisely incorrect.
Cold Store Dilemmas
Archive projects with cold storage invariably provoke dis-
cussions on transition spaces, with intermediate climate con-
ditions, to lessen the “shock” on the record in transit. Such
transition spaces have been built, and are certainly conve-
nient, but opinion varies on the need for such spaces. This
author considers transition spaces, on balance, a negligible
improvement in risk management, and often a decline. In small
archives they represent a significant fractional loss of storage
space. In all projects, they become another potential for me-
chanical failure, and an operational burden. Appropriate pack-
aging and retrieval protocols can address the problem much
more cost-effectively. All records in cold storage should be in
moisture-proof packages anyway, and such packages can safely
be moved through wide extremes in temperature, without risk
of condensation on the record.
“Time-out-of-storage,” a term coined by McCormick-
Goodhart6 at the Smithsonian Museum, refers to the implica-
tions of retrieval on cold storage design. There is no point in
very cold storage if the record is retrieved often, since the
periods in the warm room determine the record lifetime, not
the cold storage temperature. Table 3 summarizes the break-
even points on cold storage temperatures. Clearly, extreme
cold temperatures have limited value if the record is retrieved
often to human comfort temperatures.
Lubricants incorporated in magnetic tapes will be
“squeezed” out of the binder at low temperatures, so a limit of
8°C (46°F), is placed on their cold stores.
One experienced film archivist (and skeptic) considers cool
storage better than cold storage in overall risk management
because he has seen too many mechanical failures in cold
stores (freezers) as compared to cool stores (refrigerators), and
because he has set a modest target lifetime for his records of a
century or so, before which he expects them to be copied.
Pollutant Control
For the last few decades when faced with a request for filtra-
tion specifications, archive preservation officers threw up their
hands at the lack of quantitative knowledge, and optimisti-
cally entered the phrase “best available technology.” Needless
to say, after feedback from the engineer and the costing expert,
some concrete target less than this emerged.
For particulates, the tendency was to settle somewhere at the
high end of the ASHRAE Atmospheric Dust Spot, sometimes as
high as HEPA filters. Good practice added prefilters to reduce
maintenance costs. The usual engineer’s plea to consider elec-
trostatic filters was always rejected due to the dangers of ozone.
Despite the continuing assurance from manufacturers that this
ozone problem “is solved” the risks remain unacceptable to
archives and museums. In small archives, filters beyond the
standard commercial level often were not even discussed. For
gaseous pollutants, a few archives used activated charcoal fil-
ters or potassium permanganate on alumina filters.
In the 2003 handbook, the new sections on pollutant speci-
fications introduce a risk management approach: Lowest Ob-
servable Adverse Effect Dose (LOAED). LOAED is the
cumulative dose of a pollutant at which the first signs of ad-
verse effects are observed on a material. This in turn is used to
derive three classes of pollutant control: collection safety over
a period of 100 years, 10 years, or 1 year. Detailed information
on LOAED of pollutants for various materials and control strat-
egies can be found in Tétreault.7
Table 4 summarizes the interaction of room control with en-
closure control (cases, cabinets, packages). For room values, the
preservation target of one year is easy to maintain without spe-
cial gaseous and particle filtration system, whereas the target of
100 years requires HEPA filters and sorbent filters, combined
with tightly sealed rooms with limited access. Enclosures are
often more cost-effective elements of pollution risk management.
In summary, filtration needs for archives are still difficult to
specify with certainty. Archives need to remove particulates to
a much higher level than commercial spaces. Effects of sul-
phur dioxide, the major gaseous pollutant of the last two cen-
turies, are shown by the brown brittle edges of old exposed
books, but ambient levels have decreased in most North Ameri-
can cities from the worst values of the past. Ozone and particu-
lates from automobiles probably represent the greater risks
now. For many archives, cost-effective control of gaseous pol-
lutants is not simply by building filtration, but by good bar-
rier packaging of records. Ironically, most other examples of
known archive record damage by gaseous pollutants have been
caused by the packaging itself, e.g., redox blemishes on mi-
crofiche by low quality paper boxes. Packages must be good
barriers and themselves free of volatiles.
Conclusions
The engineer does not make archive climate control deci-
sions. The archivist does. During work on the ASHRAE chap-
ter, the committee debated the balance between the first half,
the “why” for climate control in archives, and the second half,
the “how” on system design. Opinion varied on how much the
engineer really needed to know about the “why,” especially in
a volume called Applications. This article has emphasized the
“why,” and some of the larger questions of “how,” leaving the
important details of “how” to the handbook (and the continu-
ally growing competence of engineers in this specialty).
As with any profession, people look towards best current
practice, and to real examples. Both Canada and the United
States have recently completed new National Archives storage
buildings that use state-of-the-art systems, with a large portion
of the archive space controlled precisely to either cool or cold
conditions at level AA or A fluctuation control with elaborate
filtration. The exact targets for each space, each collection,
was the subject of months, if not years, of committee work and
debate within each organization. The HVAC engineering con-
sultants were large-scale companies asked to deal with large-
scale projects. They were certainly not asked to question the
client’s climate control targets. For each such national archive,
however, there are dozens of state or provincial archives, and

thousands of smaller archives, where in
practice, there is a dynamic balance and
open discussion of both why and how
with the engineer, who is likely on his
own, or part of a small consultancy. More
often than not, in fact, there will be situ-
ations where the archivist will look to
the engineer for at least comprehension,
if not guidance, on the “why” of climate
control as the escalating costs and un-
certain risks of an archive project are re-
examined yet again. Remember, the
engineer’s contribution to the preserva-
tion of a community’s archive is not just
the design of a system that delivers cer-
tain conditions next year, but a system
that reduces all the worst climate risks of
the next 100 years, as well as reducing
all collateral risks of the system such as
water leaks, fire ignition, security
breaches, and pest habitat. All within
budget.

ASHRAE Journal
67
Small Centrifugal Compressors
C
This is the eighth article covering one of several new energy-
saving technologies evaluated in a recent DOE report, which
is available at www.eren.doe.gov/buildings/documents.
By John Dieckmann, Member ASHRAE, Kurt W. Roth, Ph.D., Associate Member ASHRAE,
and James Brodrick, Ph.D., Member ASHRAE
entrifugal compressors are used in chillers with capaci-
ties in excess of 80 tons (280 kW).1 The trend has been
for screw compressor-based chillers to be used in most
applications below 300 to 400 tons (1055 to 1400 kW),
with centrifugal compressors used in larger capacities. Small
centrifugal compressors combine high-speed motor technol-
ogy with centrifugal compressor technology to lower the opti-
mum capacity range. This provides centrifugal compressors
for chillers and unitary air conditioners in smaller sizes (25 to
80 tons [88 to 280 kW]) currently served primarily by scroll
and reciprocating compressors.
Electronically driven high-speed (on the order of 50,000
rpm) motors can extend well-known centrifugal refrigerant com-
pressor technology down to the aforementioned capacity range.
Because the motors run at the speed of the impeller, they can
drive it directly without speed- increase gearing. United Tech-
nologies Research Center/Carrier worked on a four-year NIST
ATP project to develop a small, high-speed centrifugal com-
pressor for commercial HVAC systems. To obtain the necessary
lift (from 45°F [7°C] to almost 150°F [65°C] at high outdoor-
ambient temperatures), they selected a two-stage design. An
additional advantage to this design is that it uses a refrigerant-
economizer cycle, which increases the COP by 5% to 7%.
In the refrigerant-economizer cycle, the liquid refrigerant
from the condenser goes through a two-step expansion pro-
cess. First, the vapor expands to the pressure between the two
compressor stages, with the flashed vapor returning to the in-
let of the second compressor stage. This saves compression
power. The remaining liquid then expands to the evaporator
pressure, with a decreased volume of vapor flashed.
Relative to similarly sized reciprocating and scroll com-
pressors, small centrifugal compressors offer the potential for
increased efficiency, efficient capacity modulation, lower size
and weight, and reduced noise.
Energy Savings Potential. High-speed centrifugal compres-
sors can reduce energy consumption in three ways:
1. Higher full-load efficiency. The efficiency levels mea-
sured at 45°F/130°F (7°C/54°C) (standard compressor rating
conditions) — aerodynamic 84%, motor 94%, drive 97% —
and allowing 2% for bearing losses equates to a standard con-
ditions compressor EER of 12.4 Btu/W-hr. This compares fa-
vorably to 11.5 for the best scroll and semi-hermetic recipro-
cating compressors in this capacity range.
2. Variable-speed motor operation: This enables close match-
ing of capacity to part-load demands, which reduces cycling
losses and potentially increases seasonal efficiency by about
20% compared to a single-speed, single compressor, and by
about 5% compared to a multiple compressor system.
3. Refrigerant-economizer cycle: This increases the COP by
5% to 7%.
In sum, small centrifugal compressors could achieve about
a 15% increase in full-load efficiency, plus the increase in
seasonal efficiency or IPLV. Analytical assessments and pro-
totype testing have confirmed these estimates.3,4 Applied pri-
marily to packaged rooftop units and reciprocating chillers,
small centrifugal compressors have a national energy sav-
ings potential of about 0.15 quads.
Market Factors. Market projections suggest that small cen-
trifugal chillers can attain cost parity with reciprocating chill-
ers at at a volume of about 25,000 units/year.4 The high-speed
motor and inverter combine to account for 75% of the total
system cost, which indicates a major opportunity for overall
system cost reduction as these fast-moving technologies fur-
ther decrease in cost.
A developer and manufacturer of turbomachinery is in the
midst of a multi-year program to develop a 50-ton (175 kW)
centrifugal compressor using R-134a intended for use in pack-
aged rooftop units and chillers.2 Their centrifugal chiller uses a
two-stage design that operates on foil-type gas bearings. At maxi-
mum speed, the device operates at 40,000 rpm, while a variable-
speed drive provides capacity modulation. Ultimately, the de-
veloper anticipates that the device will achieve efficiency gains
comparable to those outlined above. Upon completion of the
development program, this manufacturer intends to produce the
centrifugal compressor for OEM customers.
References
1. 2002 ASHRAE Handbook—Refrigeration. p. 43.8.
2. Burr, D. 2003. Personal Communication, R&D Dynamics Corpo-
ration, Bloomfield, Conn., August.
3. Brondum, D.C., et al. 1998. "High-speed, direct-drive centrifugal
compressors for commercial HVAC systems." Proceedings of the 1998
International Compressor Engineering Conference.
4. Biancardi, F. 2001. Personal Communication, Independent Con-
sultant to United Technologies.
John Dieckmann is a principal at the HVAC and Refrigeration Technol-
ogy sector of TIAX, Cambridge, Mass. Kurt W. Roth, Ph.D., is a senior
engineer at the Appliance and Building Technology sector of TIAX.
James Brodrick, Ph.D., is a project manager, Building Technologies
Program, U.S. Department of Energy, Washington, D.C.

November 2003
C
About the Author
Craig DeWitt, Ph.D., P.E., is president of RLC
Engineering, Clemson, S.C.
rawlspace foundations are common in houses built in a band that
stretches from the Carolinas across the center of the U.S. to
Washington and Oregon. Basements dominate the foundation type
in houses north of this line, partly because of deep frost. Slabs domi-
nate in areas below this line. Crawlspace foundations therefore oc-
cupy much of the middle of the mixed climate zone, but can be
found in all four U.S. climate zones.
Crawlspace foundations make up a
higher percentage of foundations in South
Carolina than in any other state. Yet South
Carolinians are not alone in continuing
to have problems with crawlspaces. These
problems include mold and decay, el-
evated radon levels, and termite and other
pest concerns. Condensation forms on
ductwork. Mold grows on joists. Termites
and wood boring beetles cause damage.
Hardwood floors cup. The historic and
current widely recommended solution to
crawlspace moisture problems is to in-
crease or provide ventilation of the
crawlspace.1–10 However, the National As-
sociation of Home Builders (NAHB) ap-
pears to be presenting a different
recommendation. The NAHB now recom-
mends11 closing vents in the summer as a
method of mold prevention.
Is venting a crawlspace necessary for
moisture control? If a crawlspace is
vented, are there other impacts or ramifi-
cations of the venting? Are there better
ways to address crawlspace issues, such
as moisture, pests, soil gasses, etc.? I be-
lieve venting can cause more problems
that it solves, and that other, more bene-
ficial ways exist to address crawlspace
problems. This article discusses these is-
sues to encourage further discussion and
research to ultimately change the way
we look at crawlspace foundations.
Myth 1: A research basis for current
crawlspace ventilation guidelines exists.
Crawlspaces are vented to control mois-
ture. That is how it started; that is why we
still do it. Looking back through histori-
cal documents, researchers12,13 have
found several documents that discuss
venting crawlspaces. In 1939, the Forest
Products Lab published Use and Abuse of
Wood in House Construction, which states,
“Screened vents totaling 3% of the house
are best, with a thoroughly insulated
floor…. One small ventilator in each wall
is hardly enough in the damp South.”
The Federal Housing Administration’s
Property Standards and Minimum Con-
struction Guidelines in 1942 contained
the first requirement for ventilation of
By Craig DeWitt, Ph.D., P.E., Member ASHRAE
The following article was published in ASHRAE Journal, November 2003. ©

November 2003
ASHRAE Journal
21
crawlspaces in regulatory literature.14 It predates any known
research on crawlspace performance. These requirements state
in part: “Provide a sufficient number of foundation wall vents
to assure a total ventilating area equivalent to ½ percent of the
enclosed area plus ½ ft2 (0.05 m2) for each linear feet (0.3 m) of
wall enclosing that area.”
The Housing and Home Finance Agency (HHFA) published
Crawlspaces: Their Effect on Dwellings15 in 1949. This docu-
ment contains a discussion of investigative work done by R.R.
Britton on several housing complexes. Britton said, “when ven-
tilation to the extent of 1:1,500 of the building area was cut into
the crawlspace walls, in conjunction with ventilation of ap-
proximately 1:500 of the building area in the loft space walls
and the covering of the crawlspace ground with 55 lb (25 kg)
mineral surfaced roofing, all trouble was apparently eliminated.”
An interesting note with this discussion was that Britton was
investigating attic moisture problems.
The Minimum Property Standards of 1958 states “At least
four foundation wall ventilators shall be provided, one located
close to each corner of the space, having an aggregate net free
ventilating area not less than 1:150 of the area of the
basementless spaces, or ground surface treatment in the form
of a vapor barrier material…plus at least two foundation wall
ventilators having an aggregate net free ventilating area not
less than 1:1,500 of the area of the basementless space.”16 The
only difference between this 1958 code and the 2000 Interna-
tional Residential Code (IRC) is that today we require a mini-
mum of four vents at the 1:1,500 ventilation level.
From my investigations, research to support these recom-
mendations and the code does not exist. What I can find in the
literature appears to be limited to a field investigation with
several moisture control steps happening at once. I do not see
an evaluation of the effectiveness of each step, that is when
attic ventilation and foundation ventilation and a soil cover
were added, the attic moisture problem was fixed. These pa-
pers contain good information, but I do not think they contain
enough information to support existing building codes and
ventilation requirements.
Following a symposium on crawlspaces,17 ASHRAE has es-
sentially removed crawlspace ventilation guidelines from the
ASHRAE Handbook—Fundamentals18 beginning with the
1997 edition.
Myth 2: We build houses the same today as when current
crawlspace ventilation guidelines were established. Many
things have changed in the houses built today vs. what was
built in the 1930s–1950s when the ventilation guidelines were
established. Houses often are built on wetter sites (because
many of the high-and-dry sites are gone). Houses are built
deeper into the ground. Smaller overhangs are used, often with-
out gutters and downspouts. Proper drainage around the foun-
dation often is neglected, or changed by the occupants.
The most significant change we have made in the last 50
years, in my opinion, is air conditioning. In many parts of the
country, creating artificially cooler temperatures in our homes
is now standard practice. Indoor temperatures are easily and
often created that are near or even below the dew-point tem-
perature of the surrounding air. Condensation occurs on sur-
faces that historically have never experienced condensation.
Air conditioning has upset the balance houses used to experi-
ence, and the balance we were using when the ventilation
codes were created.
One example of the impact of these changes is on our recom-
mendations for ground covers in crawlspaces. The U.S. Forest
Service19 published a map showing where ground covers are
recommended to reduce the potential for “winter” condensa-
tion problems in crawlspaces. This map is based on climatic
conditions. The map basically leaves out the southeastern U.S.,
where we may not have “winter” crawlspace condensation prob-
lems, but we can have summer condensation problems, espe-
cially in an air-conditioned house. Verrall and Amburgey20
mention that “this type of condensation occurs in hot weather
and may involve the entire floor area under cooled rooms rather
than just peripheries…and it develops much more rapidly than
with cold-weather condensation.”
Myth 3: The 1:150 or 1:1,500 ventilation area requirements
mean something. I used an ANSI/ASHRAE Standard 51-1985,
Laboratory Methods of Testing Fans for Rating, airflow test de-
vice to measure flow through foundation vents ranging from 24
in.2 (0.02 m2) of net free area (NFA) to 75 in.2 (0.05 m2) of NFA.21
Table 1 shows airflow data from three vents, a 24 in.2 (0.02 m2)
NFA vent, a 65 in.2 (0.04 m2) NFA thermostatically controlled
vent, and a 75 in.2 (0.05 m2) NFA vent. The 75 NFA vent had a
larger flow at a given pressure, but the flow was about 1.75 times
that of the 24 vent rather than three times the flow as would be
... venting can cause more problems that it solves, and other,
more beneficial ways exist to address crawlspace problems.
‘
’
22
ASHRAE Journal ashrae.org
November 2003
expected from the size difference. The 65 NFA automatic vent
has an airflow much closer to the 24 NFA vent than the 75 NFA
vent. Apparently the additional screen on the inside of the
vent, which is not used in the calculation of NFA for the vent,
provides additional restriction to airflow. Therefore, the actual
airflow achieved when meeting the 1:150 requirement appears
to depend on the total aggregate ventilation area more than on
the NFA of the vents used. (In addition, the flow through a vent
with more than one layer of protective screen, such as the 65t
automatic vent, has significantly different airflow characteris-
tics than a similar manually operated vent.)
Air changes per hour also were estimated assuming a 3 ft
(0.9 m) tall crawlspace of a 1,500 ft2 (139 m2) house using the
data for these same vents. At current IRC 2000 required venti-
lation rates of 1 ft2 (0.09 m2) of NFA per 150 ft2 (14 m2) of soil
area (a vent ratio of 1:150), 60 of the 24 NFA vents would be
required. The 60 vents would yield an air change rate of about
6.3 air changes per hour (ACH) at a pressure difference of 0.0005
in. w.g. (0.12 Pa), assuming half would be for infiltration and
half for exfiltration. In contrast, only 20 of the larger 75 NFA
vents would be required and provide only 3.4 ACH. The rela-
tively large 65 NFA thermostatically controlled vents would
only provide 2.6 ACH at the 1:150 ratio.
The IRC 2000 allows a reduction in ventilation to 1:1,500 if
the soil in the crawlspace is covered with an approved vapor
retarder. The number of vents required drops to six for the
small 24 in.2 (0.02 m2) vents, and four for the other vents (due
to a minimum four-vent requirement in the code.) The air change
rate for the 24 NFA vents at this ratio at 0.0005 in. w.g. (0.12
Pa) pressure is 0.63 ACH, 0.46 ACH for the 65 NFA vent and
0.70 for the 75 NFA vent. In this case, a larger NFA actually
provides a higher air-change rate, except when an automatic
vent is used. In a larger house where the code 4-vent minimum
would not apply for the 75 NFA vent, the small vent would
provide more airflow.
This investigation has shown that specifying a NFA for
crawlspace ventilation does not logically indicate the amount
of ventilation that can or will occur in a crawlspace. Using smaller
NFA vents will provide more ventilation than when using larger
NFA vents, when installed to the same ventilation requirement
ratio. Thermostatically controlled vents do not provide flow cor-
responding to a similar-sized manually operated vent. In prac-
tice, debris, shrubbery, vent wells and other obstructions also
will affect the actual amount of airflow through a vent.
Myth 4: Venting will reduce crawlspace moisture levels
to non-harmful levels. Excess moisture in a crawlspace can
cause three primary problems: 1) High relative humidity can
lead to fungal growth and increased insect activity in
crawlspaces; 2) High dew-point temperatures can lead to con-
densation on cool surfaces, which can lead to fungal growth,
increased insect activity and increased energy use; and 3)
High crawlspace moisture levels can increase latent loads,
and contribute to fungal growth and wood expansion prob-
lems in the living space. Therefore, crawlspace moisture con-

November 2003
ASHRAE Journal
23
latent load can be huge. In a sealed crawlspace, a small dehu-
midifier or other means can be used to address the latent loads.
A System View. So far, I have addressed some of the overall
myths about venting crawlspaces, using a mixed, humid cli-
mate. The original intent of venting a crawlspace is to prevent
conditions for fungal growth and peeling paint. Since a
crawlspace is an integral part of the building, venting a
crawlspace can affect other aspects of the building.
Much progress has been made recently in reducing the infil-
tration of outside air into buildings. Infiltration of summer air
increases both latent and sensible loads, while infiltration of
winter air increases heating loads. Infiltration of summer air
increases indoor humidity levels, which increases wood mois-
ture.22 Hardwood flooring can cup, and windows and doors can
swell and stick. In winter, infiltration decreases indoor humid-
ity levels, often to the point where additional humidification is
needed. Hardwood floors develop gaps, and windows and doors
warp. Framing shrinks, resulting in cracks in walls, ceilings and
panels. If we put so much effort into reducing infiltration into
the living space, why shouldn’t we do the same to the crawlspace
since it is also an integral part of a building?
From an energy standpoint, venting a crawlspace does not
make sense in any climate zone. In the winter, an uncondi-
tioned crawlspace is warmer than outside. Bringing in addi-
tional cold outside air only tends to make the crawlspace and
everything in it colder, and increase heat loss. Many people
install automatic vents that close during the winter just for this
reason. The opposite situation occurs in the summer: warm
outside air will add heat to a crawlspace and increase both the
latent and sensible cooling load. Where ducts are installed in
crawlspaces, venting increases the energy loss from the ducts.
These loads are now presented in Tables 7G – 7I of the Air
Conditioning Contractors of America’s Manual J 8th Edition.23
Summertime condensation that wets duct insulation will also
significantly increase the energy losses from ducts.
Summary
A research basis for current crawlspace ventilation require-
ments does not exist. The houses we build today are not the
same as when current crawlspace ventilation guidelines were
established. Using vent net free areas to provide code-required
ventilation openings doesn’t provide anything close to a con-
sistent ventilation rate. Venting will reduce crawlspace moisture
levels only within significant constraints. Venting in the sum-
mer can easily add moisture or increase relative humidity in a
crawlspace (and the whole house). In winter, venting will help
dry a crawlspace (and the house), sometimes to a detrimental
extreme. Venting a crawlspace will increase energy loads.
Venting may help address elevated crawlspace moisture con-
ditions in some climate zones. But the potential energy penal-
ties, effects of summer wetting vs. winter drying, the potential for
extended period of time in any climate zone where venting may
not suffice, and the ease at which closed crawlspaces can func-
trol has two objectives: maintaining a low relative humidity
in the crawlspace and reducing the potential for condensa-
tion on surfaces in the crawlspace.
The moisture content of wood is related to the relative hu-
midity of air in contact with that wood. Wood that is below
20% moisture content is generally considered safe, according
to pest control and home inspection industries. Wood will
reach 20% wood moisture content when exposed to air at ap-
proximately 90% relative humidity.21 Therefore, a primary ob-
jective is to keep the crawlspace air below about 90% RH.
The other primary objective is to prevent condensation on
surfaces in the crawlspace. This can be accomplished by either
warming surfaces (typically by adding insulation), or by low-
ering the dew-point temperature of the air. Adding insulation,
such as to a band joist, will sometimes suffice in raising sur-
face temperatures. Insulation that is incorrectly installed un-
der an air-conditioned floor only serves to further cool a surface
in contact with high dew point crawlspace air. In other in-
stances, adding enough insulation to an air-conditioner blower
cabinet or crawlspace ductwork is not an economical option.
Therefore, drying the air, rather than warming a surface is often
necessary to prevent condensation.
Can venting keep the crawlspace dry? Venting can accom-
plish this, but only under one condition: When the mixture of
ventilation air and crawlspace air results in a crawlspace RH
below 90% at virtually every surface in the crawlspace. This
entails more than just a psychrometric-mixing situation since
surface temperature effects are involved. For example, venting
a 65°F (18°C)/95% RH crawlspace in the winter with 50°F
(10°C)/90% RH air may result in a RH of only about 70% on
the bottom side of the 70°F (21°C) floor sheathing. On the
other hand, venting a 70°F (21°C)/95% RH summer crawlspace
even with 90°F (32°C)/40% RH air could still result in con-
densation on the underside of a 68°F (20°C) subfloor.
In fact, at crawlspace conditions of 70°F (21°C)/95% RH, con-
densation will occur on the 68°F (20°C) subfloor if any crawlspace
air contacts the subfloor. In this case, venting with a sufficient
quantity of 90°F (32°)/40% RH air could help. What about at
night, when all conditions are the same except that the outside
air is now 62°F (17°C)/100% RH (same absolute humidity)? No
amount of venting will pull the crawlspace RH below 90%.
Determining how much ventilation air is needed would re-
quire knowing moisture entry rates from the soil, foundation
walls and outside air, and doing a moisture balance. The “exit-
ing” half of this equation would be constrained by absolute
humidity levels equivalent to 90% RH at the coldest surface
in the crawlspace. Further complicating this is the coldest sur-
face may often be determined or affected by the inside thermo-
stat setting.
A crawlspace often exhibits all of the symptoms of a room
with an oversized air conditioner. The conditioned floor above,
cool soil and cold ductwork can address the sensible load. But
what is handling the latent load? With a vented crawlspace, the
24
ASHRAE Journal
November 2003
tion all weigh against continuing to build vented crawlspaces.
Handling Crawlspaces. A crawlspace should intentionally
be made as either part of the inside or part of the outside. If left
outside, the floor system over the crawlspace should be ad-
dressed just like walls: fully insulated with moisture and air
barriers. If made as part of the inside, the building envelope
gets pushed outward to include the crawlspace. The soil and
foundation walls then become the plane for insulation and
moisture and air barriers.
Addressing moisture issues is the first priority in a crawlspace
whether it is open or closed. Exterior water must be directed
away from the foundation with proper grading of the lot and
proper handling of roof runoff. Crawlspace soil should be com-
pletely covered with a vapor retarder. Capillary moisture move-
ment should be restricted using either capillary breaks under
piers and foundation walls, or by covering foundation walls
and piers with a vapor retarder. In some instances, a dehumidi-
fier is needed in the crawlspace because of the complexity of
home designs and the psychrometrics involved. Note: Natu-
rally vented combustion devices cannot be used in sealed
crawlspaces.
My proposed building code for crawlspaces would include:
• “Where AC system evaporator coils are installed in an en-
closed crawlspace, an auxiliary drain pan and float kill-switch
are to be installed under the system.”
• “The space under attached decks and porches with non-
watertight decking cannot be open to the crawlspace.
• “Where the crawlspace is enclosed by continuous wall
sections, the soil must be covered 100% with a 6-mil poly or
better vapor retarder that is continued up the exterior founda-
tion walls to the level of the outside soil.
• “Where the house is to be air conditioned, the crawlspace
must be unvented or the underside of the floor system must
be airtight with an air retarder system such as spray foam,
sealed rigid sheathing, air barrier membrane or similar sys-
tem. (This would cover houses on open piers as well, and
allow vented crawlspaces.)
• “Where air-conditioning ducts are run below the level of
the floor joists, the underfloor plenum must be unvented.
• “An alarm system must be provided in the sealed plenum or
sealed floor system to alert to elevated moisture levels due to
plumbing leaks, etc.
• “Where expected crawlspace moisture cannot be handled
by convection or diffusion through the floor over the
crawlspace, a mechanical system to control crawlspace humid-
ity levels is required. Other circumstances may warrant a hu-
midity control system as well.
ASHRAE is to be commended for removing crawlspace ven-
tilation guidelines from ASHRAE Handbook—Fundamentals.
Now we need to push further towards making crawlspaces into
controllable environments that allow better protection of the
structure and better, more economical control of the indoor
environment.
References

By Roy Ahlgren, Member ASHRAE
D
esigners often find it economical to generate and distribute steam
at higher pressures, then reduce the pressure close to the point
of use. If high-pressure steam is required for some other purpose in
the facility, then a pressure-reducing station to supply the lower pres-
sure loads makes sense.
than the high-pressure alternative. Of
course, the steam pressure and its result-
ing temperature have to be high enough
to transfer heat without using excessively
large heat exchangers.
If several levels of steam pressure are
required, then several pressure-reducing
stations will be needed. Each of these con-
tains many components as shown in Fig-
ure 1. If these components are not
provided, poorly selected or improperly
High-pressure steam has lower specific
volume compared to low pressure steam,
so a given size pipe can carry more pounds
of steam at high pressure, saving on dis-
tribution piping costs. Low-pressure
steam has a greater enthalpy of evapora-
tion, so it provides more useable heat per
pound compared to higher-pressure
steam. It’s also easier to control tempera-
ture in typical applications since lower
pressure steam has a lower temperature
installed, plant operators will face con-
tinuing headaches trying to provide the
steam required by this process. This ar-
ticle provides some recommendations for
proper selection and installation of these
components. The pressure-reducing sta-
tion is the primary example, but the same
principles can also be applied in other
systems where these components are used.
Pressure-Reducing Valves
The pressure-reducing valve, or pres-
sure regulator, is at the heart of the re-
ducing station.
Manufacturers provide a variety of
steam regulating valves. Proper selec-
About the Author
Roy Ahlgren, Member ASHRAE, is director of
the Bell & Gossett Little Red Schoolhouse in
Morton Grove, Ill. He is a past chair of TC 6.1,
Hydronic and Steam Systems and Equipment.
The following article was published in ASHRAE Journal, November 2003. © Copyright 2003 American Society of Heating, Refrigerating and Air-
Conditioning Engineers, Inc. It is presented for educational purposes only. This article may not be copied and/or distributed electronically or in
paper form without permission of ASHRAE.
38
ASHRAE Journal ashrae.org
November 2003
tion of different external pilot valves will allow this type of
regulator to serve as a temperature regulator, controlling the
steam flow to a heat exchanger to automatically maintain the
temperature of a liquid leaving the heat exchanger. In this
article, our emphasis is on the use of pilot valves that control
steam pressure.
The main valve is installed in the pipe to regulate steam
flow. A pilot valve that reacts to the steam pressure feedback
signal coming from downstream of the main valve controls the
main valve.
The main valve shown in Figure 2 is a single seat, normally
closed valve. The replaceable valve seat and plug are made
of materials designed to withstand high velocity and
abrasive flow. The valve is held closed by a spring that is
compressed between the valve body partition and the “but-
ton” fixed to the end of the valve stem. That button rests on
a stainless steel diaphragm. To open the valve and allow steam
flow, steam pressure must be applied underneath the dia-
phragm. As the pressure difference builds across the fixed
area of the diaphragm, an upward force is exerted on the main
valve stem, further compressing the spring, and opening
the valve. Conversely, if the steam pressure under the dia-
phragm is vented away, the spring force overcomes the pres-
sure on the diaphragm, closing the main valve, and the steam
flow stops.
Pilot Valve
The pilot valve supplies steam to the bottom of the main
valve diaphragm case. This pilot is installed on the upstream
side of the main valve, so it always has full upstream pressure
at its inlet, even if the main valve is closed (Figure 3). The
operator can adjust the spring compression setpoint, loading a
given force on top of the pilot valve diaphragm. A copper tube
connected to the system well downstream of the main valve
conducts the downstream steam pressure to the bottom of the
pilot valve diaphragm. If that pressure is lower than the operator’s
setpoint on top, a net force acts on the pilot valve stem, open-
ing it, allowing steam pressure to build under the main valve
diaphragm and opening the valve.
As downstream steam pressure builds toward setpoint, the
forces acting on the pilot valve stem equalize and the pilot
closes, cutting off the steam supply to the main valve dia-
phragm. It would take a small amount of time for the steam
trapped under the main valve diaphragm to condense and
allow the main valve to close, but during that time, the main
valve would be held open, and steam pressure would rise
above setpoint. A small “bleed orifice” is included in the
main valve to allow the main valve to close. The bleed ori-
fice vents off the residual steam from under the main valve
diaphragm very quickly, allowing the main valve to close at
the desired setpoint. If that small orifice fitting is not in-
stalled, the main valve may not open fully. If the orifice is
clogged, the main valve won’t close promptly at setpoint and
the regulated pressure will rise too high.
These main valves are always installed in horizontal pipes,
always with the diaphragm case at the six o’clock position,
and the flow arrow cast into the valve body must always point
downstream!
Sizing the Main Valve
Manufacturer’s capacity tables or computer applications
should be used to select the correct size valve. The following
information is required in order to use those applications:
1. Supply pressure at the main valve inlet. This pressure
must be within the temperature and pressure limits for the valve
component materials;
2. Desired downstream (reduced) pressure; and
3. Flow rate required by the application.
Avoid oversizing the regulator. Oversized valves don’t regu-
late pressure very well, often “hunting” around the setpoint
pressure. Because they operate much of the time with the
plug very close to the seat, they are much more likely to fail
due to “wire drawing” compared to a valve of the proper size
for the application. Besides, the oversized valve costs more
than a properly sized valve. There’s really no advantage in
buying an oversized valve.
Two variables determine the regulator capacity: body size
and port size within that body.
The cost of the main valve body varies with size, so it’s
advantageous to use the smallest main valve possible. There-
fore, it’s often useful to begin the valve sizing process by as-
suming the largest port. That results in the lowest cost valve.
On the other hand, designers sometimes want to anticipate a
future increase in steam demand, so they buy a larger valve
body now, but equip it with a smaller port to avoid all the
problems associated with valve oversizing. In the future, when
the steam flow increase is required, they simply replace the
small port with a larger one.
‘
’
The cost of the main valve body
varies with size, so it’s advan-
tageous to use the smallest
main valve possible. It’s often
useful to begin the valve siz-
ing process by assuming the
largest port.
November 2003
ASHRAE Journal
39
Piping
The pipes carrying steam to and from the main valve will
probably not be the same size as the regulator! This may seem
strange, because all three components are carrying exactly the
same steam flow. Remember that the steam upstream of the
main valve is at higher pressure, therefore lower specific vol-
ume than the downstream steam, so the upstream pipe can be
smaller than the downstream pipe. Steam distribution pipes
are selected to limit steam velocity or steam pressure drop rate
by choosing a large enough pipe size. The main valve is se-
lected to avoid all the problems associated with over-sizing. In
general, if the pipes and the valve are chosen correctly, the
valve will be the smallest of the three, the upstream pipe will
be next largest and the downstream pipe will be the largest of
them all. Tapered concentric reducers and a small straight sec-
tion of valve-sized pipe right at the main valve form a smooth
transition from one component to the next (Figure 1).
Hand-Operated Bypass Valve
Most installations include a manual bypass valve to provide
flow if the automatic regulator is out of service. During the short
period that the main valve is out of commission, the operator
can crack open the small bypass valve to provide at least some
steam, but that steam pressure will not be automatically regu-
lated. The downstream pressure will rise and fall with changes in
demand or supply pressure. Use a small valve, no larger than the
main valve size, suitable for throttling the steam, and make sure
that it can be closed tight during normal operation.
Pressure Gauges
A good quality pressure gauge is required on the down-
stream side of the regulator system to establish the setpoint on
the pilot valve. Keep the operator in mind when you install the
system — he must be able to read that gauge when he’s adjust-
ing the pilot. If he can’t, the pilot won’t get set properly. He
also has to see that gauge to adjust the manual bypass valve.
A pressure gauge, or at least a provision for installing one,
on the upstream side of the regulator system is helpful during
troubleshooting. Significant pressure losses probably exist in
the piping between the boiler and the main valve inlet, so full
boiler pressure probably will not be available for sizing the
main valve. Over time, the steam pressure at the main valve
inlet may decrease due to changes in piping or steam demand
upstream of the regulator. Low supply pressure will affect the
outlet pressure in many valve designs.
Isolation Valves
Good quality, full flow, isolation valves are required to service
the main valve. Small systems often use full port ball valves. In
larger systems, gate valves are common. Without isolation valves,
the entire system must be shut down for routine service. These
valves also can be very useful in troubleshooting the system, so
it’s good practice to include them in the reducing station.
Strainers
Steam often carries a lot of solids. Rust particles, water treat-
ment chemicals, and pipe mill scale can clog the internal strain-
ers in the pilot valve, and may even damage the main valve
port as high velocity steam drags them across the valve seat
and plug. Select a strainer mesh small enough to remove the
particles you expect to see in the system. “Y” strainers in hori-
zontal piping should be mounted so the strainer basket is hori-
zontal, not vertical. A vertically oriented strainer acts as a
condensate collection pocket during low flow periods, allow-
ing high velocity steam to pick up the liquid and throw it into
the main valve, causing wear and damage. In larger systems, a

40
ASHRAE Journal ashrae.org
November 2003
small steam trap on a vertically mounted strainer basket will
remove any condensate that collects there.
Steam Traps
Steam traps must be installed at any change in piping el-
evation, as shown in Figure 1, on the upstream and the down-
stream sides of the main valve. It’s also a good idea to install
one just upstream of the main valve, so any condensate that
collects there while the valve is closed will be drained out
instead of being forced into the main valve. A steam line
“drip trap” always requires a collection pocket. If the trap
were connected directly to the steam pipe, high velocity steam
would carry most of the condensate past the trap without
separating it.
Rough rules of thumb suggest that this piping pocket should
be the same diameter as the pipe up to 6 in. (152 mm). For
larger pipes, a collection pocket one-half the pipe diameter is
sufficient. The depth of the pocket should be one and one-half
times the main diameter, and a dirt leg should be provided to
keep solids out of the trap.
Safety Valve
If the main valve leaks, full upstream pressure will enter the
downstream portion of the system. If there are components in
that section that are not rated for full upstream pressure, a safety
valve is required to pop open and vent steam to the atmosphere
before the pressure builds high enough to damage any of those
downstream items. Be careful to select a safety valve that can
protect the downstream components while remaining closed
during small, short-term variations in pressure at the regulator as
it reacts to load changes. Some main valves will provide a slight
rise in outlet pressure just as they close. This “lock-up” pressure
is not large, but if the safety valve setting is close to the normal
regulator setpoint, it could result in nuisance popping of the
safety every time the regulator shuts down. This is annoying, it
wastes steam, and it puts excess wear on the safety valve.
The safety valve must be able to discharge enough steam
to limit the rise in downstream pressure. That exact limit is
spelled out in the American Society of Mechanical Engi-
neers (ASME) Code for different classes of systems. Earlier,
sizing of the main valve and its port were considered from an
economic point of view. There’s an aspect of that selection
that affects the safety valve too. The safety valve has to be
selected assuming that the largest possible port has been in-
stalled, even though a smaller port was actually used. That’s
because it’s possible for a larger port to be installed, possibly
making the existing safety valve too small.
Advertisement in the print edition formerly in this space.
November 2003
ASHRAE Journal
41
The manual bypass valve should also be chosen with the
safety valve in mind. If a large manually operated valve were
installed as the bypass, its capacity would govern safety valve
sizing. Some designers opt to avoid the complications pre-
sented by the bypass, and simply acknowledge that the system
will be down until the regulator can be fixed.
Pipe Insulation
It probably should go without saying that all steam
pipes must be properly insulated to minimize heat loss.
Unfortunately, there are many pressure-reducing stations
where the insulation was removed “temporarily,” and never
re-installed. This wastes energy, increases condensate flow
through the drip traps,
and may even pose an
additional load on the
building air-condition-
ing system. Insulating
pads attached by wiring
hooks are ideal for re-
ducing heat loss from
valve bodies, since they
can readily be replaced
after the service is com-
plete.
Noise
The main valve ac-
complishes its pressure
reduction by throttling
the steam flow. A by-
product of the throttling process is aerodynamic noise in
the downstream pipe as the high velocity steam jet leaving
the valve slams into the larger steam volume of the down-
stream pipe.
Regulator noise may be a non-issue, it might be a nui-
sance, and it may even be hazardous. Dealing with noise
after the system is installed can be expensive, so it is a good
idea to anticipate and avoid the problem. Computer applica-
tions exist which can predict the noise level 3 ft (0.9 m)
downstream, and 3 ft (0.9 m) off the centerline of the valve.
That’s the location used by the U.S. Occupational Safety and
Health Administration (OSHA) to define noise hazards. If it
looks like noise might be a problem, hazardous or not, the
designer may choose to use two regulators in series, taking a
part of the total pressure drop across each valve, thus reduc-
ing the noise level. Other noise abatement measures such as
using schedule 80 pipe, acoustic insulation, orifice plates
and diffusers are also available. Even if noise is not an issue,
taking a large pressure drop across two valves in series can
reduce wear and maintenance requirements at the valve. A
common upper limit for pressure drop across a single valve is
100 psid (690 kPa). An upper limit of 150 psid (1034 kPa)
may be allowed, but at the cost of accelerated wear.
Regulators in Parallel
A single valve used to control highly variable steam flow
rates must be sized for the largest flow required. But if the
valve is big enough to handle that maximum flow, it could be
too big during periods of smaller steam demand.
The solution is to use two regulators in parallel. Each sees
the same inlet and outlet pressure, and each gets its feedback
pressure from the same point. One valve will be sized for one-
third of the total flow while the other is sized to handle two-
thirds of the total. If the pilot valve on the smaller regulator
is set at about 2 psi (13.8
kPa) higher than the pi-
lot on the larger valve,
then the smaller regula-
tor will handle small
flow demand by itself.
The larger regulator will
stage on as the load in-
creases, and stage off as
the load decreases. It
doesn’t take any compli-
cated control system to
achieve this staging ef-
fect; the regulators are
simply following their
pressure-capacity curve
that droops slightly in
outlet pressure as the
flow through the valve increases. Arranging regulators in this
way allows both of them to handle full load, and the smaller
one to handle the low flow load by itself, avoiding all the
consequences of “oversized” valves.
Summary
Properly designed pressure regulating stations can provide
useful, economical service in hospitals and manufacturing
facilities that produce high-pressure steam. Like any engi-
neering problem, it is wise to anticipate and avoid the prob-
lems this article has identified before the system is installed,
rather than trying to deal with them after the fact. Using the
design tools provided by steam equipment manufacturers
along with your own good engineering judgment, you can
provide economical and reliable solutions in the design of
your pressure-reducing station.
Additional Sources
• 2000 ASHRAE Handbook—Systems and Equipment, Chapter 42
• Hoffman Steam Regulating Valves Manual, TES 492, ITT Fluid
Handling Training & Education Department.
