By Jeff Gollnick
H
eightened homeland security, the increasing
value of information, and the need to com-
municate faster and more accurately are driving
the creation of mission critical data centers. Once
contained in single rooms or floors within exist-
ing structures, data centers now require increased
security, access, functionality, and capability—far
beyond the original purpose or capacity of their
host buildings.
BACnet
® Exceeds Needs at
‘Because the BMS
needs to perform to
the same standards as
the data systems,
BACnet was used to
integrate and
coordinate all of the
building systems onto
a single network.’
Mission Critical Data Center
The following article was published in ASHRAE Journal, November 2003. © Copyright 2003 American Society of Heating, Refrigerating and
Air-Conditioning Engineers, Inc. It is presented for educational purposes only. This article may not be copied and/or distributed electronically
or in paper form without permission of ASHRAE.
November 2003 BACnet ® Today|A Supplement to ASHRAE Journal
B9
Today’s data center is not only housed within a secure build-
ing—it is the building. Any company whose business is con-
trol of and access to its data must ensure every system in the
building—from heating and cooling to lighting and ventila-
tion—runs perfectly at all times in conjunction with its data
service systems. For this reason, companies are placing build-
ing management systems (BMSs) and control devices onto the
enterprise network to simplify control of all operations.
Atlanta Data Center
An Atlanta-based identification and credential verification
services company recently built its new data center from the
ground up to provide mission critical data storage, access, and
distribution. While 99.999% high availability—where annual
downtime doesn’t exceed five minutes per year—may suffice
for most companies, the Atlanta company required 100% high
availability.
The organization handles massive amounts of information
through the identification, retrieval, storage, analysis, and
delivery of data. Some of its services include:
•
Providing credit check information,
•
Tracking the results of DNA testing on death row
inmates,
•
Conducting high level background checks for gov-
ernment and corporate agencies,
•
Creating biometric and authentication services, and
•
Maintaining a fax-based alert system that identi-
fies likely communities where a missing/abducted
child might be recovered then transmitting data
and photos of the child to get a search underway.
Because the BMS needs to perform to the same standards as
the data systems, BACnet was used to integrate and coordinate
all of the building systems onto a single network.
Mission Critical Systems
The non-negotiable 24/7/365 operation of the data center
requires a specialized infrastructure to handle its mission criti-
cal nature. Because redundancy is the key to 100% uptime,
the BMS contractor used BACnet technology to create a solu-
tion that met the needs of the mechanical, electrical, HVAC,
and data systems to achieve it.
Power
Power
Power
Power
Power
The data center has 4160 vac emergency generators in case
main power ever fails. The generators feature two 20,000
gallon fuel tanks and four day tanks, allowing the center to
operate for 50 hours without main power. The company in-
stalled power distribution units (PDUs) that clean and distrib-
ute the power from an uninterruptible power supply. The PDUs
can page data center staff if any alarm is activated within the
BACnet BMS. The BMS contractor monitors the PDUs and the
static transfer switch issues, which transfer one feed to another
B10
BACnet ® Today| A Supplement to ASHRAE Journal
November 2003
in the event of a power surge or outage,
so that none of the mission critical com-
puters ever go down.
Water
Water
Water
Water
Water
The company’s own water well sup-
plies its mechanical systems. The well is
the primary source for the data center’s
makeup water, with the City of Atlanta’s
water well serving as backup. Conversely,
if the city’s water supply is disrupted, the
well serves as a source for potable water.
Tying in HV
ying in HV
ying in HV
ying in HV
ying in HVAC
AC
AC
AC
AC
Every device running the data center—
from the building’s standard HVAC opera-
tions to the chillers, generators, utilities,
incoming power, breakers, and fuel oil—
had to be integrated with the BMS. The
center’s HVAC system runs on a native
BACnet/IP BMS operating on the
building’s Ethernet local area network.
System Components
System Components
System Components
System Components
System Components
The BMS contractor installed three
global controllers to manage the 15 es-
tablished zones in the building. An MS/
TP network connects the global control-
lers to nearly 300 field level controllers
that oversee all HVAC devices, includ-
ing main air-handling units, cooling
towers, variable air volume terminals, fan
coil terminals, and exhaust fans.
For secondary control of the variable
frequency drive (VFD), which controls
the average of hot and cold duct static
pressures, the contractor installed
MODBUS communication wires directly
to the drive so the BMS could retrieve
data from the VFD.
BACnet extends redundancy capabili-
ties to the HVAC system’s cooling proto-
cols. The center hosts one stand-alone
chiller plant with two expandable con-
trollers that act as redundant panels. If one
panel fails, the other carries the load and
no change is noticed when the switch oc-
curs. The BMS monitors all chiller data
and operating information.
BACnet/Software Features
BACnet supplies the interface between
the company’s building controls and its
engine and compressor controls by ty-
ing many MODBUS points to 12-cylin-
der diesel-fueled engines. This way
engineers can see real-time data from
each engine, including voltage and cur-
rent for the entire building.
Using BACnet, the BMS transmits the
status of each breaker position from 54
third-party vendor distribution boards
throughout the data center.
The BMS tracks the company and
other building tenants’ main power us-
age to accurately capture and bill energy
costs. The additional monitoring capa-
bilities of the BMS allows tracking and
justifying the electrical and mechanical
costs of running their businesses.
BACnet operator workstations allow
the data center’s facility manager to view,
monitor, and adjust all system functions,
Screenshot of HVAC control shows an air-handling unit.
including time schedules, trend logs,
alarms, and alarm histories. Vivid, ani-
mated 3-D graphics display zones down
to the deepest levels, allowing complete
and detailed views of every zone and
mechanical device. Operators can also
create global control strategies or modify
existing control algorithms for any con-
troller using graphical DDC logic pro-
gramming software, then quickly
download the program to individual con-
trollers on the network.
BACnet, BMS and the Future
The BACnet BMS within the data cen-
ter is the latest example of how compa-
nies are protecting their mission critical
data within mission critical buildings.
New BMS technologies are beginning to
converge with existing enterprise systems
for exponentially increased benefits to
building owners and property managers.
By providing interoperability between
disparate equipment, the BACnet proto-
col standard leverages a company’s total
technology investment for better system
performance and a greater return on in-
vestment. Already accepted industry-
wide, BACnet is proving to be a long-term
solution to building management issues.
Jeff Gollnick is communications man-
ager at Alerton, Redmond, Wash.
Screenshot of generators.
B24
BACnet ® Today| A Supplement to ASHRAE Journal
November 2003
By David G. Holmberg, Ph.D., Member ASHRAE
T
he building automation industry is moving away from isolated building control systems toward building
automation systems (BAS) integrated with business applications on a shared “IT” network. This open
shared architecture allows not only for integration of diverse building control systems, but also for connec-
tions to corporate business applications as well as off-site business partners.
For at least one large BAS vendor, 50% of new jobs share
Ethernet wiring with the corporate local area network (LAN).
This LAN is connected to the Internet, typically behind a
firewall. Another large vendor reports a significant number of
jobs where the workstation is a Web server sitting behind a
firewall and accessible via any browser using secured commu-
nication (https, the secure form of the Internet’s hypertext trans-
fer protocol). Many vendors have workstations and even
controller level devices that run common operating systems
and sit on the corporate LAN. One consequence of this trend is
many new BAS are exposed to network attacks that previously
were reserved for Web servers, and have resources that make
them nearly as attractive to hackers as a desktop PC.
And it is not as if one can seal the doors and forbid outside
communication. Networking technology development, cus-
tomer demands, innovation in services, and open communica-
tion standards are driving the building control industry toward
inter-networked buildings with ever-increasing services. The in-
formation flow is not just between equipment on a BAS subnet-
work, or between the BAS subnet and other building equipment
across the corporate wide area network (WAN), but is (or soon
will be) between the building and off-site service partners: equip-
ment vendors; gas, electric, and water utilities; security contrac-
tors; energy service contractors; telecommunication service
providers; financial service providers; government regulating
agencies; etc.
The following article was published in ASHRAE Journal, November 2003. © Copyright 2003 American Society of Heating, Refrigerating and
Air-Conditioning Engineers, Inc. It is presented for educational purposes only. This article may not be copied and/or distributed electronically
or in paper form without permission of ASHRAE.
November 2003 BACnet ® Today|A Supplement to ASHRAE Journal
B25
What threatens a networked BAS? This article aims to:
• Increase awareness of the threats associated with connect-
ing the building control system (specifically a BACnet sys-
tem) to a wider network,
• Encourage development of security policies to address
building security,
• Present countermeasures available for addressing security
threats, and
• Introduce future security direction planned for BACnet.
BAS Network Security
Many different kinds of BAS communication might cross
the public Internet: BACnet messages, proprietary http com-
munication between vendor workstation and an operator in-
terface, XML-encoded data from a building controller to a
service provider, etc. The messages vary, but all are passed
using the Internet Protocol (IP). The BACnet standard provides
two ways to pass a BACnet message over an IP network: (1)
BACnet/IP (Annex J of the BACnet standard), and (2) Annex
H. In both cases a BACnet message is wrapped up in an IP
packet for delivery. A good tutorial on this can be found at
www.bacnet.org.
For BACnet messages, the BACnet Network Security clause
(Clause 24) provides optional security measures such as data
origin and operator authentication, and data confidentiality and
integrity. However, issues such as key distribution and access
control are not addressed. In addition, implementation hurdles
due to complexity and other issues have pushed vendors toward
other technologies to secure BACnet traffic if needed. As part of
the effort to address evolving security concerns in BACnet, the
Network Security Working Group (NS-WG) identified the need
for a complete BACnet threat assessment. That report1 presents a
detailed review of Clause 24.
BAS communications over IP are not just for new build-
ings; as existing systems are upgraded to allow “single-seat”
control of diverse BAS subsystems, and as connections to
outlying equipment become desirable, it is more common to
connect separate BAS networks using existing cables and IP
protocol. So, BACnet standard network security measures are
not applied, even as BACnet IP traffic increases. How then is
current BAS traffic secured? If it is secured, it is most com-
monly done by using virtual private networking (VPN) tech-
nology from building firewall to building firewall across the
Internet (see “Firewalls & VPNs” sidebar). In other words,
‘...many new BAS are
exposed to network
attacks that previously
were reserved for Web
servers, and have
resources that make
them nearly as
attractive to hackers
as a desktop PC.’
B26
BACnet ® Today| A Supplement to ASHRAE Journal
November 2003
security is provided by the IT department and
is layered on top and independent of BACnet
communication.
Threats
What are the dangers to today’s building
control system? If the system is not connected
to the WAN, the dangers are fairly well known:
• Human error leading to a potentially inse-
cure, incorrectly configured system,
• Faulty equipment such as a failed cooling
system in a room housing vital control or net-
working equipment,
• Physical break-ins—vandalism, theft, and
• The insider threat—a facility operator per-
forming unauthorized actions.
But what are the threats when we share ca-
bling with the LAN, and through that have a
connection to the Internet? What if we have
the BAS network sitting right on the Internet
with no firewall? Are we opening our build-
ing to attack by terrorists, hackers, and other
misfits, or are we no less secure since “most
attacks come from the inside” anyway?
The ‘Who’ and ‘What’ of an Attack
Let’s assume for now that someone with ill
intent has access to the BAS network. Who
are they, and what might they do?
• Hackers. They could be students out to
play (e.g., turning lights off), or criminals.
• Criminals (thieves, terrorists, competitors,
etc.). Criminal scenarios include gathering
information to gain knowledge of the build-
ings to break in, or compromising the secu-
rity system and having doors open. Denial of
Service (DoS) attacks could be used for a vari-
ety of purposes including: making a political
statement, and interfering with business. Ter-
rorists could use low security on a network to
shut down facility operation (i.e., as a
smokescreen or disruption) to facilitate other
destructive activity.
• Competitors. Monitoring the network
could be used for corporate research. What
kind of building control system are you us-
ing? How efficiently are you using power?
The curious party could be a utility or a con-
trols manufacturer/equipment manufacturer.
• Disgruntled Employees. Actually, this
threat exists apart from outside access to the
network. Various reports have indicated that
the majority of damage inflicted on compa-
nies is due to unauthorized actions of employ-
ees with ready access to systems and informa-
tion, and results in loss of valuable
information, loss of reputation, financial loss,
and legal action. Opening the same network
to the outside may give a means for a dis-
gruntled employee to hide his tracks or give
access to a former employee who has no physi-
cal access.
Assuming that the intruder is not an em-
ployee with easy access, how would he or she
gain access to the BAS network? The most
prevalent attacks today are allowed by known
vulnerabilities in popular software packages
(e.g., operating system, e-mail) or known vul-
nerabilities in common protocols (e.g., SNMP)
that result in allowing outsiders to: get valu-
able information (credit card numbers, per-
sonal information, company proprietary
information, etc.), gain access to system re-
sources (e.g., storage space, CPU power, entire
machine), and use those resources for such
things as launching distributed denial of ser-
vice (DDoS) attacks on other networks, stor-
ing MP3 files, propagating viruses, or gaining
privileged access to service partner networks.
And while a limited number of applications
run on BAS field controllers (operating sys-
tem: yes, e-mail: no), these BAS devices share
the network with other devices that can be
• Password Attacks: Using
guessing, brute force, or pro-
tocol attacks to gain access to
a workstation or secure device.
• Data Confidentiality: Most
BACnet BAS data is not pro-
tected and is available to the
outsider who gains access to
the network.
• Data Integrity: In most
BACnet networks, device
properties can be modified,
data changed or erased,
router (or BBMD or B/IP PAD)
tables modified, device con-
figuration altered, etc.
• Denial of Service (DoS):
This is a big one. Many IT at-
tacks flood networks with use-
less packets. Within the BACnet
protocol exist many ways to
overload the network with use-
less valid and invalid packets.
Both IT and BACnet DoS at-
tacks can shut down the BAS.
Firewalls and intrusion detec-
tion can help prevent this.
• Spoofing Attacks: Several
IT spoof attacks exist (forging
the source address so that an
attack looks like it was initiated
by another machine). Only au-
thentication of BACnet mes-
sage sources can keep one
BACnet device from spoofing
another.
• Eavesdropping, Snoop-
ing and Port Scanning: A
passive category of attacks—
the attacker does not actively
alter or bring down a network.
He uses tools for scanning or
wiretapping to gain informa-
tion being passed over the
network. He may then use this
information for active attacks.
Within BACnet, one can read
properties to gather informa-
tion on network architecture,
devices, objects, services,
system status, security, etc.
• Exploitation Attack: IT at-
tacks that exploit flaws in soft-
ware, such as buffer overruns.
• Telephone Line Scanning
(war dialing): Scanning for
open modems to gain back-
door entrance to the network.
IT/BACnet
Threats
Firewalls & VPNs
A firewall sits between trusted and not trusted net-
works, or between two distinct internal networks, to pro-
vide access control. A typical corporate firewall
configuration is shown at right.
Here we see an external firewall (FW-1) facing the
Internet that has a rule-based access list (acceptable IP
source/destination addresses for DMZ services on a
given port) and logging capabilities. It filters incoming
packets and disallows any direct communication with
the intranet. Behind this first firewall is the “Demilitarized
Zone” (DMZ, a standard IT term) where Web servers
(including any BAS Web server), ftp servers, e-mail,
DNS, and other external-facing servers are located. An
intrusion detection system (IDS) would also be located
here to monitor the gateway as well as many other net-
work appliances.
All traffic headed for the internal network would be
routed through a proxy gateway (in the DMZ). The
proxy keeps track of which external server is connected
at which IP address and port number and assigns
these dynamically to internal hosts as needed. The
proxy can look beyond packet headers into packets
and is stateful so that it can provide application level
November 2003 BACnet ® Today|A Supplement to ASHRAE Journal
B27
compromised. A compromised device might
be used, for instance, to spoof a BAS control-
ler speaking BACnet or to launch denial of
service attacks on the BAS.
The ‘How’ of an Attack
Many common IT threats can affect the net-
worked building control system. In addition,
security vulnerabilities within the BACnet
protocol can be used to compromise the BAS.
An overview of these is given in the sidebar,
“IT/BACnet Threats.” A more detailed list, with
applicable protocols, and recommended coun-
termeasures, is given in the NIST internal re-
port referenced earlier.1 These attacks might
be used directly on a BAS server or controller
device, or might indirectly impact the BAS
by shutting down the corporate network.
Countermeasures
So, given these threats, how do you protect
your networked BAS? To a large degree, com-
mercially available measures can be taken to
increase network security. For example, the
Network Reliability and Interoperability
Council (www.nric.org) publishes “best prac-
tices” guidelines for dealing with various
known IT threats.
If you are located in a corporate setting with
an IT department, then working with them to
Intrusion
Detection
address security is the best approach, espe-
cially since they regard the LAN as “their net-
work.” They own the firewalls, and they can
set up VPNs as needed for devices on the BAS
to securely access distant BACnet devices, or
for non-BACnet traffic needed for communi-
cation with off-site service partners.
If you are located in a facility without IT
support, BAS security may fall into your hands.
Setting up a simple firewall on a dial-up con-
nection along with anti-virus protection might
be sufficient, but how do you know? A good
approach is to go through the process of de-
veloping a security policy (see “Security Poli-
cies” sidebar). A security policy will help you
follow these principles of network security:
• Know your system (what is your exposure
to the not trusted network, what services are
you running, and what are the associated
risks?),
• Assign least privilege (use access control),
• Apply defense-in-depth (multiple security
layers), and
• Use intrusion detection (since prevention
is never 100% [see sidebar at right “Intrusion
Detection.”]).
After assessing and addressing security
you’ll be confident that you’re doing the right
thing and aware that security is an ongoing
battle. The reality is that security will continue
to become more of an issue as the BAS is con-
nected to outside partners’ systems. Soon you
may be required to have a security policy sim-
ply to meet legal due diligence requirements
of service providers. Until then, you have time
to educate yourself and start the process of
addressing security.
BACnet Protocol Security Enhancements
And soon you will have available more than
standard IT security products to address
BACnet security—the BACnet committee’s
Network Security working group is actively
working on building secure messaging into
the BACnet protocol, adopting and adapting
existing security measures (Kerberos, SHA
hash, etc.) when possible. This BACnet proto-
col security will serve better for addressing
BACnet-specific threats such as those given
in the sidebar, “IT/BACnet Threats.” For in-
stance, message authentication will help pro-
tect BAS communication from hostile
users/devices on the LAN.
filtering. The internal firewall (FW-2) only allows traffic
originating in the DMZ.
Because all traffic passes through the firewall(s), this
“security center” is often used as the location for imple-
menting encryption via a Virtual Private Network (VPN).
A VPN is essentially an encrypted connection between
two boxes on either end of an untrusted network. VPNs
protect traffic and provide privacy, authentication and
data integrity. While they are often implemented at the
firewall (the secure VPN or customer premises based),
they may also be managed by an Internet service pro-
vider (trusted VPN or network based).
While Trusted VPNs are not encrypted, the service
provider routes the connection over a trusted line and
also can provide service guarantees (i.e., availability).
In contrast, Secure VPNs provide better security and
can support remote users.
Typical corporate network interface to Internet.
An intrusion detection sys-
tem (IDS) can be imple-
mented to increase network
security. There are two primary
types of IDS: network based
(e.g., in the DMZ) and host-
based (i.e., software running
on each desktop PC). The net-
work based system acts simi-
larly to a network level firewall.
It cannot detect most internal
attacks since it only examines
packets at the IP level. The host-
based IDS supports the au-
thentication and authorization
mechanisms by watching the
activities of users and devices
and looking for pre-pro-
grammed misbehavior. It can
look for users exceeding au-
thorization, and log activities of
devices and users. The IDS
can then implement rules for
certain infractions such as shut-
ting out a device or user and
sending audit reports to the
network administrator via e-
mail or other method.
One of the first steps in de-
veloping a security policy is a
careful risk assessment of both
the probability and impact of
various threats. For help in risk
assessment, see for instance
NIST SP 800-30 at http://
csrc.nist.gov/publications/
nistpubs/index.html. The risk
assessment will guide individual
policy development, generally
classified by program or by is-
sue. Program-level policies: es-
tablish the security program,
assign responsibilities, state or-
ganization security goals, and
provide a basis for enforce-
ment. Issue-specific policies de-
fine areas of concern (e.g.,
e-mail, off-site login, personnel,
physical security) and state the
organization’s position and ex-
pectations on these issues.
Some Web sites to visit in-
clude: http://csrc.nist.gov,
http://secinf.net, www.sans.org,
and www.windowsecurity.com.
Security
Policies
Internet
FW-1
FW-2
DMZ
Intranet
B28
BACnet ® Today| A Supplement to ASHRAE Journal
November 2003
Current proposals define a new type of network layer or
BVLL (BACnet Virtual Link Layer, defined as part of
BACnet/IP) message used to securely communicate messages
or session login information—the “BACnet Secure Message.”
Both session based and non-session-based security configura-
tions are being considered. The session-based scheme would
look similar to Clause 24 authentication and encryption, us-
ing BACnet secure messages to login, challenge, and then
pass messages between two devices. Session-less (or static)
security would be enabled by a group of secure devices on a
given insecure network sharing a key that is used to pass BACnet
secure messages. This could be applied to many situations,
such as allowing routers in separate facilities to wrap insecure
messages prior to passing across the Internet—a BACnet VPN-
like tunnel.
Implementation of BACnet protocol security will add an-
other level of security to the BAS that addresses BACnet vul-
nerabilities unrecognized by traditional IT security products.
The committee has yet to address details of access control in
BACnet, and key exchange guidelines. Farther down the road
are firewalls designed specifically for protecting BACnet com-
munication and intrusion detection products to monitor
BACnet networks and log and report suspicious traffic.
Conclusion
While BACnet secure messages are likely several years away,
the time for addressing security is now. Take stock of your
current security vulnerabilities and develop a security policy.
Then implement that security policy using the many security
products currently available. And when the efforts of the NS-
WG are realized with the capability in BACnet for secure mes-
saging, future buildings will have a means to address BACnet
threats and add another layer of security. Pencil it into your
security policy now!
References
1. Holmberg, D.G. 2003. “BACnet wide area network secu-
rity threat assessment.” NIST Internal Report 7009.
David G. Holmberg, Ph.D., is a mechanical engineer in the
Building and Fire Research Laboratory, Building Environ-
ment Division at the National Institute of Standards and Tech-
nology (NIST), Gaithersburg, Md. He serves on the Network
Security and Utility Interaction Working Groups of ASHRAE
Standing Standards Project Committee 135 (BACnet).

November 2003
ASHRAE Journal
65
Saving Energy
With Building Commissioning
By Kurt W. Roth, Ph.D., Associate Member ASHRAE, Detlef Westphalen, Ph.D., Member ASHRAE,
and James Brodrick, Ph.D., Member ASHRAE
M
This is the ninth article covering one of several energy-saving
technologies evaluated in a recent DOE report, which is avail-
able at www.eren.doe.gov/buildings/documents.
any HVAC systems depart from intended performance
due to a wide range of “faults” that compromise their
effectiveness, degrade climate control, and increase
building energy consumption. Building commission-
ing can effectively address these issues. It is the process of ob-
serving, measuring and evaluating the performance of building
systems to identify substandard function of all equipment, sen-
sors and systems. Commissioning also can include tuning the
equipment and systems and their controls to enhance perfor-
mance above and beyond “as-intended” levels. In addition, com-
missioning usually incorporates documentation of the evalua-
tion process, and of system design and intent, operational pro-
cedures, training plans, and maintenance requirements. ASHRAE
Guideline 1-1996, The HVAC Commissioning Process, explains
commissioning in detail.
Typically, a specialized third-party contractor carries out
building commissioning. For new buildings, the commission-
ing process plays a quality assurance role that should ideally
begin during the project design phase and extend beyond the
completion of construction. Retrocommissioning denotes a
one-off process for an existing building to restore performance.
Ongoing commissioning (also know as Continuous
Commissioningsm) continuously monitors equipment and sys-
tem operations to evaluate building performance in nearly
real time. As such, it overlaps with building diagnostics.1
By ensuring the proper function of building systems, com-
missioning improves climate control, reduces complaints and
increases occupant comfort. Proper building function also de-
creases building operating costs by reducing energy consump-
tion. Commissioning may also lower maintenance expenses,
help find billing errors and identify water leaks.2
Energy Savings Potential
The actual energy savings potential from commissioning
any given building varies greatly and depends on the types of
systems in place, how well the building operators maintain it,
and what failures occur. A significant volume of literature sug-
gests that commissioning typically reduces annual energy
consumption by 5% to 20%, with higher values (up to 30%) in
some buildings.2–7 On a national basis, commissioning could
reduce commercial building HVAC energy consumption by
about 0.5 quads (primary energy).
In practice, however, the energy savings initially gained from
commissioning do not always persist because problems arise
over time from aging equipment, usage changes, alteration of
control settings, etc. For example, a study of 52 economizers at
22 sites, which had been commissioned when built, found that
29 economizers had stopped working after “a year or two.”8
The limited, publicly available, quantitative information on
commissioning suggests that many buildings and building
systems maintain energy savings while others see energy sav-
ings degrade by several percent each year.9 Hardware modifi-
cations tend to exhibit greater persistence than modifications
to control strategies, presumably due to changes made by op-
erators (such as setpoints and scheduling).
Market Factors
Large variations in energy savings and the cost of commis-
sioning exist between buildings. This reflects differences in
building size, system complexity, building maintenance prac-
tices and the problems that arise in each building. Data from
case studies — which tend to have a focus on larger and institu-
tional buildings — suggest that commissioning existing com-
mercial buildings costs between $0.20 to $1.00 per square foot
($2.15 to $10.76 per square meter).2,7–8,10–13
Similarly, reported payback periods exhibit broad variations
from one building to another. Average payback periods appear
to be on the order of two years,7,13 without taking into account
persistence effects. For new buildings, commissioning costs ap-
proximately 2% to 4% of the initial HVAC system costs,13–14
with energy-based payback periods on the order of 10 years.10
The longer payback periods of new buildings mainly reflects
higher costs resulting from its integration into the design and
building acceptance processes. In addition, commissioning tends
to save less energy in new buildings than existing buildings.
Commercial buildings have very low commissioning rates
The following article was published in ASHRAE Journal, November 2003. © Copyright 2003 American Society of Heating, Refrigerating and Air-
Conditioning Engineers, Inc. It is presented for educational purposes only. This article may not be copied and/or distributed electronically or in
paper form without permission of ASHRAE.
66
ASHRAE Journal
November 2003
— less than 5% for new buildings and less than 0.03% for
existing buildings in a given year. Public buildings appear to
have higher commissioning rates due to mandates and/or prac-
tice.15 A general lack of awareness of commissioning and con-
cerns about its cost appear to be responsible for low commis-
sioning rates.10,15
For new construction, building owners often assume that
systems installation, testing and balancing eliminates the need
for independent commissioning.15 This basic system testing,
however, does not incorporate the full range of testing included
in commissioning. In existing buildings, the operators may be
reluctant to make changes because they do not want to alter
current practices and, thus, risk receiving complaint calls.2
References

4. Claridge, D., et al. 1996. “Implementation of continuous commis-

December 2003
By Don Beaty, P.E., Member ASHRAE,
and Tom Davidson, P.E., Member ASHRAE
T
he nature of electronics cooling for data centers is
changing. For example, ultra-compact blade servers
take up less space than traditional rack-mounted servers
but greatly increase heat density. ASHRAE is moving to
bring together manufacturers, the design community and
facility owners to find common solutions and standard
practices that facilitate interchangeability while preserving
industry innovation.
The initial focus of ASHRAE’s new Technical Committee 9.9, Mis-
sion Critical Facilities, Technology Spaces and Electronic Equipment,
has been on thermal issues relating to information technology (IT)
equipment, especially in mission critical data center environments.
The result of the committee’s work is ASHRAE’s publication Thermal
Guidelines for Data Processing Environments.1
This article introduces a few of the central concepts of this guideline,
and shows some of the ramifications of the guideline as they relate to
facility design.
New Data Center Thermal Guideline
The thermal guideline covers four primary areas:
• Equipment environment specifications,
• Facility temperature and humidity measurement,
The following article was published in ASHRAE Journal, December 2003. © Copyright
2003 American Society of Heating, Refrigerating and Air-Conditioning Engineers, Inc.
It is presented for educational purposes only. This article may not be copied and/or
distributed electronically or in paper form without permission of ASHRAE.
December 2003
About the Authors
Don Beaty, P.E., is president, and Tom Davidson, P.E., is a senior mechanical engineer
at DLB Associates Consulting Engineers in Ocean, N.J. Beaty is chairman of ASHRAE
TC 9.9, Mission Critical Facilities, Technology Spaces and Electronic Equipment.
• Equipment and facility layout, and
• Equipment manufacturer’s heat and airflow reporting.
A brief overview of these four areas follows.
Equipment Environment Specifications
Equipment Environment Specifications
Equipment Environment Specifications
Equipment Environment Specifications
Equipment Environment Specifications
The driving force behind the environmental specifications section of
the guidelines is to provide a harmonized set of conditions that IT equip-
ment manufacturers can try to design to, and that data center designers
and operators can strive to maintain. A total of four environmental classes
are defined in the guideline.
The most stringent environmental class, designated as Class 1, has a
recommended IT equipment inlet temperature range of 68°F to 77°F (20°C
to 25°C), and relative humidity in the range of 40% to 55% RH. This
recommended envelope is a subset of a larger allowable envelope, with
an inlet temperature range of 59°F to 90°F (15°C to 32°C) and a relative
humidity range of 20% to 80% (dew point is also limited to 63°F [17°C]).
Both of these envelopes are shown on the psychrometric chart in Figure 1.
If manufacturers design to the allowable conditions, and operators
can maintain the narrower recommended conditions (with only occa-
sional excursions into the allowable range), the area between the two
envelopes provides a buffer that should result in extended IT equipment
operating life, with all other factors being equal.
One additional environmental parameter that affects the ability of elec-
tronic equipment to be adequately cooled is air density. The new guide-
30
ASHRAE Journal ashrae.org
December 2003
line suggests that data center products
be designed to operate up to 10,000 ft
(3050 m) altitude, but recognizes that
there is reduced mass flow and convec-
tive heat transfer associated with lower
air density at higher elevations. To ac-
count for this effect, the guideline in-
cludes a temperature derating (for the
maximum allowable temperature in
Class 1 and Class 2 environments) of
1°F/550 ft altitude above 2,950 ft
(1°C/300 m above 900 m).
The recommended envelope for Class
1 data centers, the most stringent class,
is actually more relaxed than the rec-
ommended data center envelope listed
in Chapter 17 of the 2003 ASHRAE
Handbook—HVAC Applications (72°F
[22°C] ±2°F [1.1°C] and 50% ±5%
RH),2 which is also shown for reference
in Figure 1. This relaxation should al-
low for energy savings through greater
use of economizer cycles, higher dis-
charge air temperatures, less latent dehumidification, etc. An
important difference between the two specifications is that while
the ASHRAE Handbook’s environmental envelope refers to nomi-
nal space conditions, the new guideline envelopes refer specifi-
cally to IT equipment inlet conditions.
Facility T
acility T
acility T
acility T
acility Temperature and Humidity Measurement
emperature and Humidity Measurement
emperature and Humidity Measurement
emperature and Humidity Measurement
emperature and Humidity Measurement
The facility temperature and humidity measurement section
of the new guidelines helps verify that facilities are generally
operating in the proper environmental range, and also pro-
vides protocols to allow for verifying proper inlet conditions
with new equipment installations, and in troubleshooting cases.
Field measurements have determined that in many cases envi-
ronmental problems are highly localized, and as many as nine
monitoring points per inlet may be needed to pinpoint the
source of a localized problem. The measurement protocols are
outlined in detail in the guideline.
Equipment and Facility Layout
Equipment and Facility Layout
Equipment and Facility Layout
Equipment and Facility Layout
Equipment and Facility Layout
It was difficult to reach a consensus on the equipment and
facility layout section of the guideline because of the absence
of a standard to date. The primary driving force behind a recom-
mended standardization is that the typical life of computer
equipment is short due to continuous innovation, and in a com-
petitive environment products from one manufacturer often are
replaced with those of another. “Plug and play” interchange-
ability is important to the long-term thermal health of a data
center. Otherwise, facility operators will be confronted con-
tinually with custom “fixes” to cope with one manufacturer’s
hot exhaust air being adjacent to another’s inlet, or a special
Figure 2: Hot-aisle/cold-aisle airflow with underfloor supply.
temperature and humidity requirement.
The primary recommendation of the layout section is stan-
dardization on three IT equipment airflow protocols for equip-
ment (front to rear, front to top, and front to top and rear) and on
a single a hot-aisle/cold-aisle arrangement for rows of equipment
in the facility. The airflow protocols align well with a standard
adopted within the Telecommunication Industry, Telcordia NEBS
GR-3028-CORE.3 A hot-aisle/cold-aisle arrangement for rows of
equipment is a natural match to the equipment airflow protocols
from a facility perspective. This type of cooling scheme, illus-
Ceiling
Floor Tiles
Computer
Cabinet
Computer
Cabinet
Perforated
Floor Diffuser
Floor Slab
Flow of Cold
Supply Air
Flow of Hot
Return Air
Figure 1: Comparison of Class 1 data center recommended and allowable operat-
ing conditions with existing ASHRAE envelope.
December 2003
ASHRAE Journal
31
trated in Figure 2, has adjacent equip-
ment aisles arranged front-to-front and
back-to-back, with cool air entering
the front of the IT equipment from a
common cold aisle, warming up as it
passes through the equipment, and
discharging from the rear of the IT
equipment into a common hot aisle.
Looking toward the possible future
of high-density data centers, the
reader may be interested in knowing
that the hot-aisle/cold-aisle arrange-
ment has been experimentally tested,
with new overhead cooling technol-
ogy, and has been found to effectively
cool data centers with an equipment
heat release density exceeding 500
W/ft2 (5382 W/m2).4 This airflow pro-
tocol allows for considerable growth
of IT equipment heat densities above
levels currently seen in the industry.
It is recommended that equipment
which cannot conform to the hot and
cold aisle configuration be placed
in a dedicated section of the data
center, with close attention paid to
exhaust and inlet locations to avoid
heat recirculation.
Equipment Manufacturers Heat, Airflow Reporting
Equipment Manufacturers Heat, Airflow Reporting
Equipment Manufacturers Heat, Airflow Reporting
Equipment Manufacturers Heat, Airflow Reporting
Equipment Manufacturers Heat, Airflow Reporting
The final section of the guideline covers equipment
manufacturer’s heat and airflow reporting. Reports of data cen-
ters designed for 200 W/ft2 (2153 W/m2), and operating at less
than 10% of this level (20 W/ft2 [215 W/m2] or less), have been
frequent in the past few years. In most cases this over-design
results in capital costs that cannot be recovered either by the
facility owner or the electric utility providing the infrastruc-
ture for this service. Heat density design based on nameplate
ratings has been one source of over design in the past, but in
many cases this was the only data available.
The new guideline calls for IT equipment manufacturers to
list typical heat release values at minimum, typical and maxi-
mum configurations, and also both the nominal and maxi-
mum airflow through the equipment. The combination of
available and accurate heat release and airflow data should
allow facility designers to provide cold aisle entering condi-
tions and airflows to more effectively and efficiently cool the
IT equipment in the racks.
Impact of Guidelines on Data Center HVAC Design
Impact of Guidelines on Data Center HVAC Design
Impact of Guidelines on Data Center HVAC Design
Impact of Guidelines on Data Center HVAC Design
Impact of Guidelines on Data Center HVAC Design
One of most important concepts to understand with the new
guidelines is that the hot-aisle/cold-aisle facility design re-
sults in a unique condition. Assuming an underfloor supply
(one of many possible hot-aisle/cold-aisle supply air configu-
rations), the supply temperature in the underfloor plenum is
the nearly the same as the supply temperature in the cold aisle,
and also the same as the air inlet condition to the IT equip-
ment. Refer again to Figure 2 for a theoretical depiction of this
configuration. It is interesting that a typical office supply air
condition of 55°F (13°C) and 90% RH, if injected directly into
IT equipment, not only fails to meet the recommended range,
but even fails to fall within the allowable (typically warranty)
inlet condition envelope for the equipment (Figure 1).
Fortunately, meeting the recommended conditions of the guide-
lines is fairly easy with some tweaking of the HVAC system
design, and can result in significant energy savings per unit heat
removal compared to an office environment. There are two de-
sign points that we wish to cover here: meeting the IT equipment
inlet conditions, and maintaining reasonable energy efficiency.
Figure 3a shows a simplified HVAC configuration of a fairly
typical HVAC system for underfloor distribution. A single air-
handling system (or a set of similar units operating in parallel)
performs all the air conditioning functions (cooling and dehu-
midification, reheat and humidification). The supply air re-
quirement of 68°F (20°C) (minimum) and 55% RH (maximum)
requires a unit discharge temperature of 52.5°F (11.4°C) to
remove the absolute humidity of the outdoor air, and also re-
quires a considerable amount of reheat so that the minimum IT
equipment inlet air temperature of 68°F (20°C) is met. A calcu-

lation of the HVAC energy consumed by this
conditioning cycle is sobering: under reason-
able sets of assumptions, the amount of reheat
needed to meet the IT equipment design condi-
tions is greater than the heat release of the IT
equipment itself.
Figure 3b shows a relatively minor modifi-
cation to the system described earlier. This time,
the data center is under slight positive pres-
sure, and the outdoor air is preconditioned. This
allows the dehumidification to occur in an in-
dependent outdoor air dehumidification coil,
and the primary cooling coil provides only sen-
sible cooling. Instead of the primary cooling
coil discharging at 52.5°F (11.4°C), it can now
discharge at 67°F (19°C), assuming that 1°F
(0.6°C) of fan reheat is available to achieve the
minimum IT equipment inlet condition of 68°F
(20°C). The change in design cooling and heat-
ing loads is dramatic: the design HVAC cool-
ing load drops 47% and the heat released in
the facility drops by 53%. Preconditioning the
outdoor air to remove humidity, allowing for
the elimination of reheat, is all that is required
to achieve this dramatic reduction. Table 1 sum-
marizes the state points and processes of the
two cases shown in Figures 3a and 3b. Figure 4
in turn shows the psychrometric conditions cor-
responding to Table 1.
Initial Commissioning Pointer
Initial Commissioning Pointer
Initial Commissioning Pointer
Initial Commissioning Pointer
Initial Commissioning Pointer
One last point that should be considered is
that of air recirculation within the data center,
and the initial setpoint that a facility operator
may wish to work with using this knowledge.
Figure 5 is a CFD simulation of the hot aisle/
cold aisle concept under a specific set of condi-
tions, showing one of the most typical design
problems: air recirculation from the hot aisle to
the cold aisle. This problem can occur, such that
the supply temperature at the base of the cold
aisle is within the recommended spec, but the
temperature at the top of the cold aisle is “con-
taminated” by recirculated hot air, and is thus out of spec.
Quite often the temperature gradient between the acceptable
and unacceptable conditions is quite dramatic, as Figure 5
shows. This could result in a premature temperature-related
failure of a server located at the top of a rack.
To address this problem, the author recommends to initially
start up the HVAC systems with a 68°F (20°C) supply air tem-
perature to the cold aisle, at 55% RH. This will allow up to a
9°F (5°C) rise in temperature from the floor to the highest rack
elevation in the cold aisle, and still maintain all inlet air tem-
peratures below the upper recommended limit of 77°F (25°C),
as well as above the minimum recommended relative humid-
ity of 40%. After the preliminary commissioning is complete,
it may then be evident that the inlet air temperatures to the IT
equipment at the tops of the racks are less than 77°F (25°C). In
this case, it would be possible to increase the supply tempera-
ture even higher than 68°F (20°C) to take advantage of the
associated energy savings. If a cold aisle temperature of 77°F
(25°C) or higher is encountered during commissioning, air
balancing and possibly other measures will be needed to bring

Conclusions
Thermal Guidelines for Data Processing Environments has
as a primary purpose bringing together IT equipment manu-
facturers, the design community and facility owners to ensure
that common solutions are found for the problems that are
occurring in data centers today and into the future.
This publication recommends a new set of environmental
envelopes that is based on inlet conditions to the IT equip-
Table 2: Summer design load ratio (Case 2/Case 1).

ment, rather than on nominal space conditions. While at first
glance the inlet conditions may seem restrictive, with some
minor design tweaking major energy savings can result, espe-
cially since supply air conditions do not need to fall below
34
ASHRAE Journal
December 2003
68°F (20°C). It is hoped that the energy efficiency ramifica-
tions of these new recommended conditions can be studied in
more detail by the ASHRAE community and others with a
strong interest in energy efficient data centers.5
A recommendation to start up systems with a 68°F (20°C)
supply air temperature and 55% RH is made by the authors to
minimize potentially high temperatures and low relative hu-
midity in localized hot spots prior to completion of the com-
missioning process.
Acknowledgments
The authors would like to acknowledge the contributions of
all members of TC 9.9 for their hard work and dedication this
past year in bringing the thermal guidelines document to the
point of publication. The assistance of Jim Van Gilder in pre-
paring Figure 5 for publication is especially appreciated.

Keep in mind that this analysis is not a life cycle cost, but a
simple payback. As changes in the natural gas cost occur
monthly, predicting the rate increases accurately over a 10 or
15-year period is impossible. That is why this analysis is a simple
payback only. If load-matching features were added to the equip-
ment, one of the cogeneration units could operate nearly 100%
of the time. This would change the entire analysis.
Simple Payback of Cogeneration System
The installation of the 350 kW system installed was priced
at $985,000 with the 95 ton (334 kW) absorption chiller. The
cost is $2,814/kW including the absorption chiller. This amount
may seem high, but the complexity of the system described
was high with considerable work required in noise control and
retrofitting the existing mechanical rooms to fit the new equip-

ment. The incentive program available
by the utility company represents 30%
of the cogeneration system installation
cost (not including the absorption
chiller) for a one-time incentive of
$210,000. The customer’s actual cost was,
therefore:
$985,000 – $210,000 = $775,000 (15)
and the simple payback with mainte-

$775,000/$103,447 = 7.49 years
(16)
Items To Consider
During Construction
It typically takes less time to install
the equipment and related systems than
it takes to have the utility company com-
plete the required testing (this depends
entirely upon the utility company). Even
if the system is not connected to the grid,
the testing may be completed by one de-
partment and signed off. Then, another
department in the same utility company
may require the same testing to be re-
done at the cost to the installing contrac-
tor/engineer. After this testing has been
completed, if one individual does not like
one particular contactor or relay, the en-
tire system is rejected until this one part
is replaced. The utility companies have a
monopoly in the business of generating
power and have little incentive to work
with cogeneration customers depending
upon the utilities strategic plan. A sec-
ond project took five weeks to engineer,
eight weeks to install and nearly 20
weeks for the utility company testing and
acceptance of the system.

Table 6: Demand savings from cogeneration.
The recommendations for items to
consider prior to and during construc-
tion include, but are not limited to:
1. Engineering of the heat recovery
system,
2. System design that uses as much of
the waste heat as possible,
3. Engineering of the electrical switch
gear that satisfies the protocol for the
utility company,
4. Unit selection to satisfy customer’s
needs including delivery time of the
equipment,
5. Air quality management district
documentation and testing,
6. Utility company rebate documen-
tation,
7. Careful consideration of the noise
level of the equipment (whether indoors
or out),
8. Siting of the equipment to reduce
the length of the waste heat piping,
9. Siting the equipment to minimize
the distance to the building’s electrical
service entrance, and
10. Scheduling the utility company
testing.
It has been proven that working
closely with your electric utility com-
pany early in the project can be very
helpful. The opportunities are tremen-
dous, and when the finished product
fires up and does exactly as it was in-
tended, the excitement is infectious.
References
1. 2000 ASHRAE Handbook—HVAC Sys-
tems and Equipment. Chapter 7, Cogenera-
tion Systems and Engine and Turbine
Drives.

ASHRAE Journal
35
By Philip Bartholomew, P.E.
A
About the Author
Philip Bartholomew, P.E., is an associate at
CUH2A in Princeton, N.J.
lthough a new science facility at a nearby college was only a frac-
tion of the total conditioned building area on campus, it doubled
the campus’s central plant utility output. To avoid a similar problem
with the new Integrated Natural Science Center at Haverford College
in Haverford, Pa., the designers developed an approach that incorpo-
rates heat recovery devices into the makeup air units.
Approximately 40% of Haverford’s
188,000 ft2 (17 465 m2) science center is
lab space that includes 110 fume hoods.
The large number of hoods made it chal-
lenging to meet the college’s energy-ef-
ficiency goals. Although traditional
HVAC systems were considered for this
project, the design team recognized that
some of the most aggressive approaches
did not provide the desired energy effi-
ciency.
A traditional solution for this type of
application is a VAV fume hood system
with a VAV supply air system (that may
be enhanced by sensible heat recovery).
Air is delivered to the lab space through
VAV reheat terminal boxes, and its air
quantity is controlled to match the vari-
able exhaust quantity of the fume hood
system. A constant differential between
the supply and exhaust is provided to
maintain the lab at a negative pressure.
These VAV systems have complicated
operational schemes for both comfort
and fume hood exhaust operation, and
yet, they still have severe thermal inef-
ficiencies. Some of the reasons that a
standard VAV lab system wastes energy
as compared to a system that provides
comfort include:
1. Outside air provides all summertime
cooling of the laboratory. In the eastern
two-thirds of the country, where humid-
ity levels are high, providing air condi-
tioning with outside air can require three
times the refrigeration energy of recircu-
lated air at design conditions. This as-
sumes the supply air quantity needed to
match the exhaust air also exactly meets
the quantity required to meet the ther-
mal load. In reality, this does not occur
most of the time. Sensible heat recovery
only reduces this penalty by 10% to 20%.
2. The air distributed from the air-han-
dling systems must be cooled to approxi-
mately 52°F (11°C) to properly
dehumidify the outside air. If the quan-
tity of supply air needed to match the ex-
haust air provides too much cooling to
the space, the supply air needs to be re-
heated before being supplied to the space.
A large amount of refrigeration energy is
Saving
Saving
Saving
Saving
Saving
Energy
Energy
Energy
Energy
Energy
In Labs
In Labs
In Labs
In Labs
In Labs
Makeup Air Heat Recovery

Makeup Air Heat Recovery
The following article was published in ASHRAE Journal, February 2004. ©
Copyright 2004 American Society of Heating, Refrigerating and Air-Condition-
ing Engineers, Inc. It is presented for educational purposes only. This article
may not be copied and/or distributed electronically or in paper form without
permission of ASHRAE.
36
ASHRAE Journal
February 2004
expended to subcool outside air in the
summer, only to have heat returned to it
before being supplied to the space.
3. When the supply air quantity re-
quired to match the exhaust air is not
enough to provide sufficient summer-
time cooling to match the space load,
the system again reacts in an ineffi-
cient mode. High energy outside air is
used to generate more supply air to the
space. Then, this already treated, un-
polluted air is directly exhausted from
the space to outside.
4. In the winter, the outdoor air first
must be heated to 70°F (21°C) before it
provides any beneficial heating to the
space. This could take five to 10 times
the heat input energy as compared to a
recirculated air system that provides the
same space heating capacity.
Because of these four reasons, it be-
came apparent that the standard labora-
tory system fails to both effectively and
efficiently meet the needs of the mod-
ern laboratory. This is due in large part
to forcing one HVAC system to meet all
functional requirements of the lab. There-
fore, the design team provided separate
makeup air and comfort conditioning
systems. This separation led to improved
thermal performance, a highly flexible
system, a simpler control scheme and
reduced supply ductwork. It also resulted
in easier design coordination, quiet op-
eration and lower construction costs.
The System
In the approach used at Haverford Col-
lege, the makeup air unit is designed to
produce essentially room temperature and
humidity neutral air to replace the exhausted air in as efficient a
manner as possible. The use of room temperature neutral air
eliminates reheat energy and allows the simple and efficient
delivery of air to the space as discussed later. The makeup unit
also provides all building humidification and dehumidifica-
tion. A cooling coil leaving temperature of 52°F (11°C) fully
dehumidifies the outside air to an absolute humidity level
slightly below the desired space conditions.
The makeup/exhaust air unit operation is illustrated in Fig-
ure 1d. The total energy heat wheel recovers heat from the
exhaust airstream and preconditions the outside air by trans-
ferring the desired temperature and humidity conditioning.
The sensible heat wheel eliminated the need for dehumidifi-
cation reheat and assists in the unit’s heat recovery efficiency.
Individual room heating and cooling fan coil units provide
space sensible comfort conditioning. This approach allows for
the efficient cooling and heating provided by full return air to
the unit. A return air unit for multiple spaces is not appropriate
because of the cross contamination of fumes from lab to lab.
The cooling is sensible only because the coil leaving tempera-
ture is 57°F (14°C), well above the space dew point set by the
makeup air unit. No condensation is present to deteriorate the
fan coil equipment or to cause indoor air quality problems
associated with drain pan condensate.
Figures 1a–1d show four different systems operating under
the most demanding summertime conditions. This demonstrates

37
how these systems consume energy and
indicates how the refrigeration plant ca-
pacity was substantially reduced. They
also indicate why the space reheat bur-
den is eliminated with both of the double
heat exchange air unit approaches. The
spaces are based on a lab of 650 ft2 (60
m2), four VAV fume hoods each operating
at an open 900 cfm (425 L/s) exhaust, four
people working in the space, an operat-
ing internal lighting and equipment load
of 5 W/ft2 (54 W/m2) and a summer enve-
lope cooling load of 2,400 Btu/h (703 W).
This description gives a single op-
erational condition that demonstrates
only the most important benefit of the
separate makeup system operation. To
estimate the yearly energy consumed
by these systems, a bin hour analysis
was performed. This was necessary since
energy analysis programs cannot ad-
equately model these different systems.
They do not correctly account for the
operation of the dual heat exchanger
air units and do not allow for proper
control simulation.
To try to give a balanced representa-
tion of varied laboratory operations
with VAV systems, the following
space conditions were modeled. An es-
timate of the average actual operating
equipment loads was used—not com-
mon design condition loads. For
most labs, a wide difference exists be-
tween these values. The variation in
fume hood exhaust rate is an estimate
of actual hood usage and exhaust
associated with the corresponding sash
position.
Space Conditions
Space Conditions
Space Conditions
Space Conditions
Space Conditions
• Four spaces: each 650 ft2 (60 m2).
• Occupied hours: nine hours per day,
Monday through Friday.
• Unoccupied hours: 15 hours per day
(Monday through Friday), 24 hours on
Saturday and Sunday.
• Room total fume exhaust rate of 2,400
cfm (1132 L/s) for three hours per oc-
cupied workday.
• Room total fume exhaust rate of 800
cfm (378 L/s) for six hours per occu-
pied workday.
• Room total fume exhaust rate of 600
cfm (283 L/s) for unoccupied periods.
• Low internal equipment and lighting
load (occupied) of 5 W/ft2 (54 W/m2)
for three of the rooms.
• High internal equipment and lighting
load (occupied) of 12 W/ft2 (129
W/m2) for one of the rooms.
• Low internal equipment and lighting
load (unoccupied) of 1 W/ft2 (11 W/m2)
for three of the rooms.
• High internal equipment and lighting
load (unoccupied) of 4 W/ft2 (43 W/m2)
for one of the rooms.
• Four people per space (occupied only).
• Exterior wall heat transmission of one
wall, variable based on ambient tem-
perature.
• Occupied space conditions: 72°F
(22°C), 50% RH.
• Unoccupied space conditions: 75°F to
65°F (24°C to 18°C).
Based on these space conditions, the
yearly boiler and chilled water utility
energy consumption for the four systems
are shown in Figure 2.

The previous comparisons do not ac-
count for the differences in fan energies
required by the operation of the system’s
components and the air pressure drops
imposed by the heat exchange equip-
ment. It is not within this article’s scope
to give an accurate accounting of this
difference. However, establishing the
magnitude does give useful insight to
the actual energy savings and costs.
The systems defined in Figures 1a and
1d are used for comparison. The added
static pressure of the two heat wheels is
1.6 in. static pressure on both the supply
and exhaust airstreams. The makeup de-
livery described next eliminates almost
all of the supply ductwork distribution
pressure requirements and the systems’
differences in supply air pressure require-
ments are eliminated from the analysis
since they are nearly equal.
For ease of comparison, component
energy consumption is reported in terms
of watts required to support the single
space. The cooling load is converted to a
chiller electrical input based on a full
plant (chiller, pumps and tower) effi-
ciency of 0.9 kW/ton. A boiler plan effi-
ciency of 75% is assumed.
1. Increased exhaust fan energy required
due to 1.6 in. additional static with flow
rate of 3,600 cfm (1700 L/s): 750 W.
2. Added fan coil operation require-
ments: 220 W.
3. Reduced chiller energy requirements:
15,600 W.
4. Reduced reheat energy requirements:
24,180 W.
In winter, the combined heat recovery
effectiveness is 80%. On an arbitrarily se-
lected outdoor condition of 20°F (–7°C),
the saved fuel energy related to supplying
65°F (18°C) air to the space would be
63,000 W as compared to the 970 W of
added fan energy established previously.
The conditions used for the compari-
son are extreme. The system dynamics
caused by occupied/unoccupied opera-
tions, fume hood use and weather con-
ditions create variations in fan capacity
as well as thermal energy requirements.
With the reduced airflows associated
with these system dynamics, a rapid non-
proportional reduction in fan energy is
realized. Reductions in thermal energy
are realized because of both the reduc-
tion in demand associated with the air-
flow, as well as the additional reductions
associated with milder weather.
Because of the combined airflow and
weather effects, the reduction in thermal
energy likely will be proportionately
greater than the reduction in fan energy
on a yearly basis. However, even with
these part-load considerations, it is clear
the ratio of saved thermal to increased
fan energy will remain high.
Use of Heat Wheel Technology
As demonstrated previously, the oper-
ating costs associated with the different
system operational characteristics justify
separating the makeup air unit function
from the space conditioning function.
The decision to use a total energy heat
wheel exchange device in lieu of a sen-
sible energy heat recovery device re-
quires care and consideration of the
operational impacts of the wheel. If in-
corporated, the increased system effi-
ciency is substantial as indicated earlier.
Heat wheels can allow an extremely
small amount of the fume hood exhaust
airstream to be returned to the space by
the supply airstream. If applied cor-
rectly, this heat wheel cross contamina-
tion is considerably less than that
caused by the typical arrangement of
fume hood discharge stack and air-con-
ditioning system fresh air intake. Nei-
ther of these conditions creates a health
hazard with anything but the most toxic
of substances combined with high lev-
els of release.
The proper application of the heat wheel
with laboratory systems is imperative and
the operation and functional requirements
must be understood fully before design-
ing a system around their use. A critical
factor is the proper selection of the desic-
cant media of the wheel. For ideal opera-
tion, only water molecules should be
allowed to be adsorbed into the media to
allow for latent heat transfer. Other mol-
ecules should not be transferred into the
media by adsorption. The three angstrom
molecular sieve approaches this ideal.
Additionally, the wheel purge opera-
tion is affected by a variable volume ap-
proach. It is necessary to compensate for
this. This project required placing con-
trolled back pressure dampers in the sup-
ply airstream so that the supply to
exhaust stream air pressure differential
at the wheel remains constant. This level
of sophistication is unnecessary with a
constant volume approach.
Also, investigate the possibility of
frosting on the media in severe winter
conditions. Consult the equipment
vendor’s engineer to establish the need
for a specific remedy for the actual
project requirements, if necessary.
Passive Air Delivery
The conditioning of makeup air to room
neutral conditions allows air to be deliv-
ered to the space without concern for space
subcooling, dumping or creating drafts.
This allows for an approach that results in
considerable supply ductwork distribu-
tion cost savings, quiet operation, and
much simpler control, balancing, and
startup, as well as simpler operation and
maintenance requirements.
The makeup distribution design in-
volves air being delivered to the labo-
ratory area hallway ceiling, and from
there passively drawn into the space by
the action of the fume hood exhaust. A
fabric back draft damper is installed in
Can a Science Center with 110 F
Can a Science Center with 110 F
Can a Science Center with 110 F
Can a Science Center with 110 F
Can a Science Center with 110 Fume Hoods Be
ume Hoods Be
ume Hoods Be
ume Hoods Be
ume Hoods Be
Comfortable, Quiet and Energy Efficient?
Comfortable, Quiet and Energy Efficient?
Comfortable, Quiet and Energy Efficient?
Comfortable, Quiet and Energy Efficient?
Comfortable, Quiet and Energy Efficient?
February 2004
ASHRAE Journal
39
the partition above the corridor ceiling to maintain the lab
space at the desired negative pressure of approximately 0.03
in. negative pressure with respect to the hallway.
The damper’s openness is self-adjusting to the fume hood
exhaust quantity and naturally maintains a fume containment
air velocity at the transfer opening to the space and a relatively
constant lab pressure. An added benefit of this approach is that
the back draft damper instantly shuts when a lab door opens and
provides a gentle inward fume containment velocity through
the open door. This containment velocity typically is more ef-
fective than that provided by a constant supply to exhaust air
differential provided by traditional laboratory systems.
This passive makeup air delivery eliminates many of the
complicated flow measurement and tracking controls required
for a traditional VAV lab system. It is necessary to maintain
global building pressure conditions by synchronizing the
makeup supply air quantity with that exhausted from the
spaces. This is done at one location, within the makeup unit,
with a standard flow tracking technique.
The simplicity of the passive air delivery allows for a
simpler system startup and air system balancing. The air bal-
ance procedures only required calibrating the fume hood
exhaust controls and properly setting up the makeup air unit
flow tracking.

Air AHU
A goal of building codes is the containment of fire and
smoke within a portion of a building to allow occupants to
safely exit the building and to protect property. These regu-
lations require the building systems do not degrade the ar-
chitectural barriers established to provide this containment.
A building’s corridors are a major component of the safe
egress provisions. Most codes relax the fire protection re-
quirements of the corridor system when the building is fully
sprinkled, but the importance of providing a smoke free exit
path is maintained.
The approach of using the corridor as a slightly pressur-
ized plenum both exceeds the intent and meets the letter of
most codes. This approach ensures the corridor, in all oper-
ating conditions, will have a positive pressure with respect
to the adjacent occupied spaces with airflow from the corri-
dor to the spaces. It also provides a smoke free source of
non-recirculated, outside air to dilute any smoke that does
enter the corridor. If the makeup unit does start circulating
smoke, the unit smoke detector will shut the unit down and
the corridor will revert to the code standard level of prevent-
ing smoke migration into the corridor.
The following discussion is based on the 2000 Interna-
tional Building Code (IBC) and International Mechanical Code
(IMC) since these are likely the most commonly invoked codes
nationwide. The IMC has a strong statement as follows:
“601.2 Air movement in egress elements. Exit and exit
access corridors shall not serve as supply, return, exhaust,
relief or ventilation air ducts or plenums except as permitted
by the International Building Code.”
The IBC allows two methods that permit the corridor ceil-
ing plenum approach presented in this article. The first and
most direct approach is to take advantage of the definition of
the extent of corridor as follows:
“708.4.3 Where the corridor ceiling is constructed as re-
quired for the corridor walls, the walls shall be permitted to
terminate at the upper membrane of such ceiling assembly.”
This allows the space above the corridor to be considered
not part of the corridor and allows its use as a supply ple-
num. Where this one hour rated ceiling may be considered
undesirable, a second approach to code compliance is avail-
able. This is provided by a subparagraph under 715.5.4 Fire
partitions, stating:
“7.15.5.4.1 Corridors. A listed smoke damper designed to
resist the passage of smoke shall be provided at each point
a duct or air transfer opening penetrates a corridor enclo-
sure required to have smoke and draft control doors in ac-
cordance with Section 714.2.3.”
This subparagraph allows the corridor to be considered
as a plenum since the air transfer openings directly relate to
the IMC definition of a plenum. It also provides for a means
of ensuring safety with that condition.
Safety Concerns & Code Compliance
Figure 2: Relative yearly system utility plant energy consumption (each pie segment is the percentage of yearly utility
energy consumed as compared to the total utility energy consumed by the 100% outside air AHU system.
40
ASHRAE Journal
February 2004
Because the fan coil and makeup air systems operate at low
pressures and velocities, the spaces are quiet. The only percep-
tible noise is that contributed by the fume hood exhaust sys-
tem. This factor was particularly important because of the
acoustically hard surfaces and the low sound levels required
by the teaching environment within the labs.
The fume hood controls consisted of a face velocity sensor
modulating an electronic motor-operated exhaust damper.
This control loop was local to the hood, and an interfacing
with a room controller was unnecessary. Any of the com-
monly available fume hood controllers based on velocity
sensing or sash position can be used because it is not neces-
sary to the exhaust volume from the lab (as with a flow track-
ing supply air delivery system).
The project’s design was under the 1993 BOCA code. This
code allowed the corridor ceiling plenum delivery system pro-
vided that the makeup to the corridor met or exceeded that
drawn into the spaces (as is always the case with this design).
With many of the more current codes, the design frequently
needs to incorporate a one-hour rated corridor ceiling or smoke
dampers on the transfer openings to the ceiling plenum. For
further discussion, see the sidebar on code compliance.
Conclusions
Incorporating heat recovery devices into the makeup air units
nearly doubles the equipment cost as compared to a simpler
heating and cooling 100% outside air-handling unit. However,
this cost is more than offset by three major areas of construction
savings. The size of the heating and refrigeration plant was re-
duced by almost 60%, the supply air ductwork was substan-
tially reduced and the control system is much simpler than that
required by a traditional VAV laboratory system. The final con-
struction costs of the mechanical HVAC systems for this facility
were under 90% of comparable facilities built in this area.
The heat wheel was a key component for the project’s
high level of energy efficiency. Initially, the purge section of
the wheel allowed cross contamination. This condition was cor-
rected by resetting the purge to match the actual controlled
differential pressure from the makeup to the exhaust airstream.
The system has operated for one year and has provided stable
and efficient operation through all operating conditions. The
only identifiable drawback of the approach appears to be the
high number of fan coils and the required maintenance associ-
ated with filter change out. This drawback is insignificant com-
pared to the other benefits of the system.
