The 7 Methods
Variable speed drive retrofit.
S14
HVAC Retrofit | A Supplement to ASHRAE Journal
perature adjustments has enabled this chiller to achieve a re-
markably low 0.19 kW/ton (0.054 kW/kW) at 50% load, contrib-
uting to a chiller-plant energy reduction approaching 460,000
kWh per year and annual savings to Sony of more than $20,000.
Today’s generation of VSDs designed specifically for cen-
trifugal chillers incorporate adaptive capacity control (ACC)
that learns optimum compressor speed for all chiller operating
conditions. This control logic creates its own compressor map
during the first year of operation, and then continuously refines
VSD operation based on chiller operating conditions. The ACC
adjusts compressor speed and prerotation vane settings to deter-
mine how low the VSD speed can go (using the least possible
amount of electricity), while keeping the chiller in a safe operat-
ing mode (out of surge).
On a call for cooling, the VSD
initiates a soft start of the chiller,
never exceeding 100% full-
load amps. The chiller initially
ramps up to full speed with
prerotation vanes set to match
load requirements, quickly es-
tablishing control of the
chilled-water loop. The ACC
evaluates operating parameters
and determines the appropriate
speed reduction. The ACC then
steps down the chiller speed,
while adjusting the vanes as
needed to maintain the
LCHWT setpoint, until the
minimum operating speed for
that condition is reached. The ACC memorizes the best speed for
each combination of conditions. When those conditions occur
again, the ACC tells the VSD to initiate the proper speed and vane
position.
Method 4: Modernize Chiller Controls
The operation of an older chiller that is in good mechanical
condition may be improved through a control center retrofit.
Modern graphic control centers provide operators with real-
time data using a graphical interface, availing quick access to
all operating parameters and control variables. Operators can
fine-tune chiller operation more easily, and become aware of
maintenance or service needs before they cause unscheduled
downtime or escalate repair costs. Log-keeping is simpler, and
operating trends can be viewed, printed and stored.
Modern control centers also automate many functions, if
desired. For example, control algorithms include surge detec-
tion, soft-start options and demand limiting, which serve to
lower operating costs and reduce chiller downtime without
compromising safety parameters. Communications options can
provide complete integration of the chiller’s controls into the
existing automation system and permit remote communica-
tion and monitoring of chiller operation. Updated controls
also may be part of a chiller plant automation package, as
described in Method 5.
Method 5: Add Chiller Plant Automation
A chiller plant automation system is an energy management
system designed specifically to optimize operation of the chiller
plant—chillers, pumps, and towers—often in coordination with
a larger facility or building automation system. These automa-
tion systems have grown in power and sophistication in recent
years while shrinking in physical size and operating complexity.
A basic chiller plant automation system can control one or more
of the following functions:
1. Demand limiting: monitors overall building demand and,
according to a preset strategy,
automatically limits chiller
demand;
2. Chilled water reset: resets
LCHWT automatically, when
conditions permit, to reduce
energy use (see Method 1);
3. Time-of-day start/stop:
controls equipment startup
and shutdown by using out-
door temperature and other
factors to anticipate the cool-
ing requirement and operate
the plant in the most effi-
cient manner;
4. Optimized sequencing:
operates the proper mix of
chillers, pumps and towers based on load conditions;
5. Maintenance needs: identifies and signals maintenance
needs based on performance records so the chiller plant can be
maintained at peak efficiency.
Thomas Hartman, P.E., Member ASHRAE, suggests his “pre-
ferred solution is to design and implement a simple and eco-
nomical chiller plant control network for the chillers, pumps
and tower fans, that automatically operates and sequences all
equipment to meet the load and optimize efficiency.”4
Hartman describes his approach as “an efficiency-focused
plant operations strategy” that in smaller plants can be imple-
mented manually. In either case he suggests it “will easily
reduce annual chiller plant energy costs by $20 to $100 or
more per installed ton ($5.69 to $28.43 per kW), depending on
climate, application and utility rates. Such an improvement
almost always will be cost effective and at the same time con-
tribute considerably to a greener environmental future.”
Method 6: Retrofit Chiller with
a New Compressor-Motor Driveline
Equipping an older chiller with a new driveline while re-
taining the heat-exchanger shells is not a common retrofit

HVAC Retrofit | A Supplement to ASHRAE Journal
June 2004
S15
technique, but merits consideration un-
der one of the more of the following cir-
cumstances:
1. Chiller capacity is greater than
present maximum building load, due to
energy conservation measures in the fa-
cility. In this case, downsizing the driv-
eline matches chiller capacity to actual
load, resulting in more efficient opera-
tion. Retaining the existing heat ex-
changer shells with the new, smaller com-
pressor boosts efficiency further because
the now-oversized shells provide greater
heat-transfer surface, resulting in lower
compressor head. The new motor also will
be more efficient than its predecessor.
2. The chiller is aged and maintenance
costs including service and parts are es-
calating. Since the compressor drive sys-
tem contains the majority of mainte-
nance-intensive parts, a new driveline
alone can significantly upgrade a chiller
plant at less cost than a completely new
chiller system. For example, some new
compressors eliminate oil management
from chiller operation, enhancing the
economic benefits of the retrofit. A new,
modern control center is generally part
of the retrofit as well (see Method 4).
3. The equipment room is not easily
accessible. Many older chillers can only
be removed from subbasements or other
difficult locations through extensive
work that may include partial building
demolition and reconstruction. Since the
heat exchanger shells are the largest com-
ponents of the chiller system, leaving
them in place may provide a solution.
New driveline components usually can
be delivered through standard doorways.
4. The chiller needs to be converted to
a new HFC refrigerant. A refrigerant con-
version can be accomplished on older
centrifugal chillers with a driveline ret-
rofit at less cost than a total chiller re-
placement. This approach merits consid-
eration especially if any of the other three
circumstances above must be addressed
at the same time.
Method 7: Replace Chillers
Ultimately, when desired performance
cannot be obtained from an antiquated
The Hilton at Sandestin Beach Golf Resort & Spa, located in Destin, Fla., along
Florida's Northwest Gulf Coast between Pensacola and Panama City, typically
has 3,000 guests on site. Over the last 20 years, the demand for cooling has
grown from 650 tons (2300 kW) over 325,000 ft2 (30 200 m2) of air conditioned
space to 1,200 tons (4200 kW) over 750,000 ft2 (69 700 m2). As a result, the
original chiller plants could no longer provide sufficient cooling. Reliability and
efficiency of the aging HVAC equipment was also at issue, as was the control
system responsible for plant operation.
A thorough analysis of the existing HVAC system was conducted to determine
the best methods for improving the resort's three chiller plants. Ultimately, the
resort elected:
1. Replacing three air-cooled chillers with three larger water-cooled screw
chillers.
2. Retrofitting two 20-year-old centrifugal chillers with graphic control centers.
3. Adding an electronic VSD to the lead chiller
4. Tying the entire HVAC system together into one network.
Replacing the three air-cooled chillers (one 135-ton [475 kW] and two 200-ton
[700 kW]) with three 200-ton (700 kW) water-cooled screw chillers enabled the
chillers to be installed inside, so they were no longer exposed to corrosive coastal
elements. In addition, the new chillers provided much-needed additional cooling
capacity as well as the improved efficiencies associated with water-cooled units.
Operation of the remaining two 277-ton (975 kW) water-cooled chillers also
improved with the addition of the control centers. Designed to update and
upgrade older chiller technology, the control centers provide chiller operators
with a real-time, graphical interface. Graphical trending and historical reports
enable operators to optimize adjustments, schedule maintenance and pinpoint
potential problems before they cause downtime.
The retrofit of the existing chillers included the installation of a VSD on the lead
chiller. VSD are efficient at off-design conditions, efficiently decreasing the chiller's
power draw in response to off-design loads and water temperatures. The addition
of the VSD doubled the efficiency of the chiller plant, reducing the amount of
energy required to operate the chillers from approximately 0.85 kW/ton to 0.4
kW/ton (0.24 kW/kW to 0.11 kW).
Installation of the control network linked chiller control to broader building
control, enabling all vital elements of the cooling system to be shown on one screen.
The improvements were put to the test during the hot, humid Florida summer.
In contrast to previous summers, complaints about the HVAC system disappeared.
Also, the HVAC system with energy (kW) use is down approximately 9%, which
translates into an estimated $375 per day savings.
The 7 Methods at Work
The Hilton at Sandestin Beach Golf Resort & Spa
S16
HVAC Retrofit | A Supplement to ASHRAE Journal
chiller system, consider replacing the entire chiller with a new
one. Chiller efficiency has improved significantly in recent
years, with higher-efficiency heat exchangers, compressors,
motors, and new control systems described earlier. Current
designs have been optimized for non-ozone-depleting refrig-
erants such as HFC-134a.
In addition, a wide selection of nonelectric chillers is avail-
able for engineers considering alternative energy sources or
hybrid chiller plant designs. Nonelectric chiller choices in-
clude steam- and direct-fired absorption units as well as gas-
engine and steam-turbine-drive centrifugals.5
Of course, a replacement chiller represents a significant capi-
tal expense that must be carefully evaluated. An analysis of
the present operating cost per ton of cooling should be per-
formed and compared with the anticipated costs of a new sys-
tem. Then, the initial capital expense must be compared with
the reduced costs of operation.
In summary, it’s essential to view any proposed changes in
chiller plant operation, including retrofits and new equip-
ment, in the context of the overall operating needs and goals
of the facility being served. Owners, in counsel with engi-
neers, should ensure that fundamental maintenance and op-
erating practices are in place. Then, engineers should evalu-
ate both opportunities to improve system operation and to
upgrade components and equipment. The best approach is
usually a mix of these techniques, and it is up to the engineer
to find the optimum combination.6

2. Barr, R. October 1986. “18 Ways to Improve Chiller Efficiency.”
Engineered Systems p. 29.
3. Avery, G. June 2003. “Operating Chillers in the Max-Cap Range.”
Engineered Systems p. 66.
4. Hartman, T. January 2001. “Improve Chiller Plant Efficiency.”
Automated Buildings.com.
5. Spanswick, I. September 2003. “Advances in steam cooling.”
ASHRAE Journal p. 20.
6. Lawrence Berkley National Laboratory. July 2001. “Integrated
Chiller Retrofits.” Cool Sense http://ateam.lbl.gov/coolsense/icr.html.
Andrew K. Burkhart is service marketing manager for York
International, York Americas in York, Pa.
